item.title: Poland says foiled cyberattack on nuclear centre may have come from Iran
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Poland has foiled a cyberattack on its nuclear research centre and is examining signs that Iran may be behind it, ŌĆŗthe government said on Thursday, cautioning the indicators might ŌĆībe a deliberate misdirection to hide the attackersŌĆÖ true location.
Poland says it has been the target of numerous cyberattacks since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in ŌĆŗ2022. Moscow has repeatedly denied involvement.
Minister for Digital Affairs ŌĆŗKrzysztof Gawkowski told private broadcaster TVN24+ that the attack on ŌüĀPoland's National Centre for Nuclear Research had taken place "in the ŌĆŗpast few days".
"The attack may not have been on a huge ŌĆŗscale, but there was an attempt to break through the security that was stopped. Appropriate services are already working", Gawkowski said, adding that the centre ŌĆŗwas safe.
"The first identifications of the entry vectors, i.e. those places ŌĆŗfrom which (the centre) was attacked, are related to Iran," he said. "When there is ŌĆīfinal ŌüĀinformation and the services will check it, we will verify it, but there are many indications that it took place on the territory of Iran."
The Iranian embassy in Warsaw did not immediately ŌĆŗreply to an ŌĆŗemailed request for ŌüĀcomment.
The centre conducts research into nuclear energy, subatomic physics and related fields. Poland has no nuclear ŌĆŗweapons and is building its first nuclear power plant.
The ŌĆŗU.S. ŌüĀand Israel unleashed coordinated airstrikes on Iran on February 28, killing its Supreme Leader , Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting ŌüĀU.S. ŌĆŗmilitary installations, effectively halting oil and gas ŌĆŗshipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the ŌĆŗworldŌĆÖs LNG and petroleum.

item.title: Explainer: Who might the US be talking to in Cuba?
item.description:
HAVANA, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump has said repeatedly the United States is in talks with high-level ŌĆŗCuban representatives, and that Cuba is eager to reach a deal to defuse tensions between the two neighbors that have deepened the island's economic crisis .
The Cuban government ŌĆīhas denied any official talks are under way, but has yet to explicitly deny press reports that U.S. officials were in talks with Raul Guillermo Rodriguez Castro, the grandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro, who is 94 and still wields great influence.
If talks are taking place, they come at a time when Washington has tightened its economic sanctions on Cuba by imposing a near-total oil blockade, and after the U.S. ŌĆŗcaptured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3, removing a crucial Cuban ally from power.
Raul Castro fought alongside his older brother Fidel in ŌĆŗthe revolution that toppled a U.S. ally in 1959 and served as his brother's loyal defense minister for decades. He ascended to the ŌüĀpresidency, first on an interim basis when Fidel got sick in 2006, then took over definitively when Fidel retired in 2008. With Fidel's death in 2016, Raul assumed the mantle ŌĆŗas the single unifying leader among those loyal to the Cuban revolution.
He still wields influence even after stepping down as president in 2018 and as leader of the Communist Party in 2021, ŌĆŗsince then carrying the honorific title army general.
At his 2018 swearing-in ceremony, current President Miguel Diaz-Canel said Raul Castro "will lead the most important decisions for the present and future of the nation."
That was on public display as recently as December 2025. As Cuba's Communist Party prepared to choose Diaz-Canel's successor at an all-important party congress this year, Raul Castro proposed postponing the congress indefinitely, given Cuba's economic crisis. The party's ŌĆŗCentral Committee approved the proposal unanimously.
Known as "El Cangrejo," or "The Crab," because of his hand, Rodriguez Castro, 41, is widely seen as one of his grandfather's closest ŌĆŗconfidants. As former bodyguard to Raul Castro, he was at his grandfather's side, sometimes dressed in the military uniform of the Interior Ministry, throughout Raul Castro's presidency. He has been reported to hold the ŌĆīrank of ŌüĀlieutenant colonel.
The Cuban government did not immediately respond to a query about Rodriguez Castro's biography or possible role in negotiations.
Axios, citing three unnamed sources, reported on February 18 that Rodriguez Castro was in secret talks with U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio. The Miami Herald, citing unnamed sources, reported on February 26 that officials close to Rubio met with Rodriguez Castro on the sidelines of a regional Caribbean Community conference in St. Kitts and Nevis.
The Trump administration has been having secret, high-level conversations with several people in Ra├║l CastroŌĆÖs inner circle, similar to the ŌĆŗdiscussions held in Venezuela before the U.S. captured ŌĆŗMaduro in a military raid earlier this ŌüĀyear, U.S. Representative Mario D├Łaz-Balart told the Miami Herald.
Rodriguez Castro is the son of Raul's daughter Deborah Castro Espin and
That lineage places Rodriguez Castro at the intersection of Cuba's political leadership and its most powerful economic institution, making him a potentially significant go-between for Washington.
Rodriguez Castro has never spoken publicly nor given interviews.
If "El Cangrejo" is acting as a back-channel connection between Havana and Washington, he would be playing a similar role to ŌüĀthat of his ŌĆŗuncle and Raul Castro's son, Alejandro Castro Espin, who conducted then-secret negotiations marking a major shift by Washington ŌĆŗtoward Cuba during Barack Obama's presidency.
Family ties appear to matter. Neither of the younger Castros held a diplomatic job. Raul Castro in turn had been a confidant and adviser to older brother Fidel.
Alejandro Castro Espin, 60, is believed to ŌĆŗhave been sidelined after his father left office, though the Interior Ministry colonel may have ascended to one-star general.

item.title: Russia's Rosatom says it will stay in Iran despite war
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - RussiaŌĆÖs state nuclear corporation Rosatom will stay in Iran despite the conflict in the Middle East and ŌĆŗis committed to its agreement to build two ŌĆīmore units at the Bushehr nuclear plant, its head Alexei Likhachev said on Thursday.
Rosatom, which built the first 1ŌĆægigawatt unit of ŌĆŗIranŌĆÖs sole nuclear power plant at Bushehr, evacuated ŌĆŗsome of its staff and suspended construction work on ŌüĀthe new units after the United States and Israel launched ŌĆŗstrikes against Iran on February 28.
Around 450 Rosatom employees remain ŌĆŗat the site, Likhachev said, after 150 returned to Russia via Armenia this week.
"The construction of the second and third units ŌĆŗremains among the corporationŌĆÖs priorities. It is definitely not ŌĆŗthe time to leave. What is happening in the Middle East is ŌĆīonly ŌüĀpart of a global mosaic," Rosatom quoted Likhachev as saying.
On Monday, Likhachev said that the situation around Bushehr remained tense but that there had been no strikes on ŌĆŗeither the plant ŌĆŗor the ŌüĀconstruction site.
A bilateral agreement between Russia and Iran allows for the construction of up ŌĆŗto eight nuclear units, four of them at ŌĆŗBushehr.
Last ŌüĀautumn, Iran said it had signed a $25 billion agreement with Rosatom to build four nuclear power units with a capacity ŌüĀof ŌĆŗ5 gigawatts at another site in the countryŌĆÖs ŌĆŗsoutheast. The parties also signed a memorandum on developing small nuclear power ŌĆŗplants in Iran.

item.title: Morning Bid: Triple digit crude
item.description:
March 12 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Crude oil is testing $100 per barrel once again, briefly topping that important threshold overnight after rising nearly 5% on Wednesday, as more ŌĆŗattacks on ships in the Gulf outweighed the impact of the International Energy AgencyŌĆÖs plan for a record release of ŌĆīcrude reserves. Oil market volatility by one gauge recently hit levels not seen since 2020.
Iran, clearly aware that an oil shock is one of its biggest defensive weapons, warned yesterday that the conflict could send oil to $200 per barrel - and the Islamic Republic seems in no hurry to free Gulf shipping as long as the war rages.
IŌĆÖll ŌĆŗget into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why the lessons of the Ukraine war may now embolden ŌĆŗhawks on the ECB.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss the longer-term implications of ŌüĀthis oil shock. Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Stock markets ŌĆŗare down across the world again on the latest jump on oil, with major U.S. indexes finishing flat to lower on Wednesday and Asian indexes giving up ŌĆŗsome recent gains on Thursday. European and U.S. stock futures were down before the bell.
The oil marketŌĆÖs shrug at the record 400-million-barrel reserve release is worrying and an indication of the timeline traders are now contemplating.
The 400 million barrels - more than twice the release seen after the Ukraine invasion in 2022 - would cover about 2-4 weeks of global ŌĆŗdemand. If the war is still shutting down the Gulf after that, markets could tighten further.
With that timeline in mind, financial markets are already ŌĆŗturning attention to potential inflation impacts and central bank responses.
A second U.S. interest rate cut has all but disappeared from the Fed futures curve, with markets now ŌĆīpricing in ŌüĀbarely one for 2026. February CPI inflation numbers came in as expected , but that data is from before this oil shock.
Looking ahead, thereŌĆÖs a sweep of central bank decisions next week. The Reserve Bank of Australia is expected to raise interest rates again. Markets are already fully priced for a European Central Bank rate rise by July, and mortgage rates are rising again in Britain. No central bank is likely to be brave enough to ŌĆŗcut in this environment.
Meantime, U.S. Treasury ŌĆŗyields hit their highest in almost ŌüĀsix months heading into Thursday. Soft debt auctions havenŌĆÖt helped restore confidence this week.
And the dollar continued to firm on the dwindling rate cut expectations, holding near its strongest levels of the year so far, weighing down gold ŌĆŗin turn.
Chart of the day
The IEA has coordinated emergency stock releases only four times since it was created ŌĆŗby OECD members ŌüĀin the midŌĆæ1970s in response to the 1973 OPEC oil embargo. The infrequency underscores the gravity of the current situation, writes ROI Energy Columnist Ron Bousso .
Today's events to watch
* U.S. January trade balance (8:30 AM EDT), January housing starts (8:30 AM EDT), weekly jobless claims (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 30-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* ŌüĀInternational Energy ŌĆŗAgency releases its March 2026 Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your ŌĆŗinbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They ŌĆŗdo not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Brazil's dreams for industrial-scale cocoa farms fading after price crash
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 12 (Reuters) - Cocoa farmers in Brazil have slammed the brakes on new planting projects following a 70% plunge in cocoa prices from their 2024 record high, stalling growth that investors had expected would make the country a ŌĆŗmajor supplier of the main ingredient in chocolate.
At current prices of around $3,000 per metric ton , farmers and analysts told Reuters they expected around half the projects in Brazil to ŌĆīgrow cocoa on an industrial scale could be canceled.
The projects, centered in Northeastern Brazil, would have added at least some 75,000 hectares of growing area, according to an estimate by supply chain services provider Czarnikow, enough to supply nearly 5% of the global demand for cocoa.
"I think Brazil expansion plans have had a massive cold shower," said Paulo Torres, a London-based cocoa industry advisor and cocoa farmer in Brazil's Bahia state. Torres himself canceled a plan for an additional 30 hectares of cocoa at his farm ŌĆŗin Bahia.
Current prices do not cover the investment or production costs of new fields, making the Brazilian projects unfeasible, he said.
The farmers and investors that planned the giant cocoa farms in Brazil , backed ŌĆŗby big players in the industry such as Cargill (CARG.UL) and Barry Callebaut (BARN.S) , opens new tab , saw them as a solution to years of supply shortages that led to skyrocketing prices.
The planned farms would ŌüĀhave provided an alternative to the main growing region in West Africa. Ghana and Ivory Coast produce nearly 50% of global cocoa, which has left the market vulnerable to production problems there.
Unfavorable weather, illegal mining and diseases ŌĆŗreduced West Africa production in 2023 and 2024, causing prices to spike from an average $2,500 per ton to more than $11,000 per ton and generating panic in the industry.
Cocoa traders scrambled to source beans. Smuggling rose sharply in Africa ŌĆŗwith people bypassing official government buying to sell across the border at higher prices. The chocolate industry raised prices to offset spiraling production costs.
African production has since recovered, and other geographies such as Ecuador have increased production. At the same time, consumers have cut back on buying expensive chocolate, and small cocoa farmers in Brazil have not been making money. A group of them last month blocked a road leading to the Ilheus port in Bahia, setting old tires on fire, to protest ŌĆŗthe arrival of imported cocoa from Africa.
After that, Brazil's food supply agency Conab suspended any imports of cocoa from Ivory Coast.
Weight-loss drugs have further curbed demand, and the industry has reduced package sizes and turned to non-cocoa ingredients, ŌĆŗsuch as artificially flavored palm oil butter, hitting demand for the beans and exacerbating the cocoa price crash.
Moises Schmidt, one of the biggest cocoa farmers in Northeast Brazil, said the current price of cocoa would not cover the investments in irrigation ŌĆīand machines for ŌüĀcrop care and post-harvest processing typical of the region's farms.
"If the market remains below $5,000 per ton, more than 50% of the projects are gone," he said.
Schmidt had expected to oversee as much as 10,000 hectares (24,710 acres) of cocoa plantations, roughly the size of Manhattan. He declined to comment on what the price crash means for his own plans.
Two sources, one a large farmer in the region and the other an executive at an agricultural equipment supplier, told Reuters that a large project by Switzerland-based investment company NewAg Partners , opens new tab , for up to 22,000 acres (8,900 hectares) of cocoa fields, had been suspended.
"At this stage we prefer not to comment," said NewAg chief executive Detlef ŌĆŗSchoen.
There are other initiatives in the area whose future ŌĆŗis uncertain.
Copa Investimentos, a Sao Paulo-based asset manager ŌüĀwith investments in agriculture and forestry, was also planning an industrial-scale cocoa farm, but one of its partners, Apolonio Sales, said it is now "evaluating" the investment.
Sales said he visited some of the new cocoa farms and also held talks with cocoa traders and chocolate companies, but the company has not yet reached a conclusion ŌĆŗabout whether to go forward.
Brazil cocoa production could still grow, experts said, but at a much slower rate, mostly as a diversification strategy in farms ŌĆŗwhere the trees would grow ŌüĀalongside other crops.
The big Brazilian farms, however, had intended to deliver quick and massive production growth. An additional area of 75,000 hectares of irrigated fields could deliver 225,000 tons of beans in four years, or 4.5% of global production.
"If some big farm had plans for 400, 500 hectares, now it will do 80 or 100, only to start learning about the crop," said Emerson Silva, a sales executive at irrigation equipment maker Netafim.
Brazilian co-op Cooabriel, which announced a cocoa initiative ŌüĀwith Cargill last ŌĆŗyear , said it would continue developing its small-scale project.
Projects from Sao Paulo's state government, which include using cocoa trees to reforest ŌĆŗdegraded parts of farms, will also go ahead, said the coordinator Ricardo Pereira.
"In some areas, we already have seedlings almost ready to go into the field," he said, adding that as with any commodity market, cocoa prices would eventually rebound.

item.title: Iran war unsettles India's packaged water makers as bottles, caps get pricey
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 12 (Reuters) - The Iran war is rattling India's $5 billion packaged water market just ahead of the sweltering ŌĆīsummer season.
One of the world's fastest growing bottled water markets is seeing some manufacturers hike prices for distributors, as supply disruptions linked to the war fuel higher costs in everything from plastic bottles to caps, labels and cardboard boxes.
Though retail prices are yet to feel the heat and bigger ŌĆŗcompanies are absorbing the pain, about 2,000 smaller bottled water makers have increased rates for their resellers by around ŌĆŗ1 rupee per bottle, a 5% hike, which will rise by a further 10% in coming ŌüĀdays, according to the Federation of All India Packaged Drinking Water Manufacturers' Association.
Consumers usually pay less than 20 rupees, or ŌĆŗaround 20 U.S. cents, for a one-litre bottle.
"There is chaos and within the next 4-5 days, this will start impacting customer prices," ŌĆŗsaid Apurva Doshi, the federation's secretary general.
Rising oil prices have increased the cost of polymer, which is made from crude oil and is a key material for the industry's plastic bottles.
The cost of material used in making plastic bottles has risen by 50% to 170 rupees per kilogram, ŌĆŗwhile the price of the caps has more than doubled to 0.45 rupees apiece. Even corrugated boxes, labels and adhesive tape ŌĆŗare costing much more, industry letters showed.
Clean water is a privilege in the country of 1.4 billion people where researchers say 70% of the ŌĆīgroundwater ŌüĀis contaminated, leaving people reliant on bottled water. Companies including Bisleri, Coca-Cola's (KO.N) , opens new tab Kinley, Pepsi's (PEP.O) , opens new tab Aquafina, billionaire Mukesh Ambani's Reliance (RELI.NS) , opens new tab and Tata all compete for a share of the $5 billion market.
The companies did not respond to Reuters request for comment.
Within the broad bottled water market, natural mineral water is a $400 million business in India and a new, fast-growing wellness product for India's wealthy.
The ŌĆŗpremium water segment accounted for ŌĆŗ8% of the bottled water ŌüĀmarket last year in India, compared to just 1% in 2021, Euromonitor says.
Aava, which sells mineral water sourced from the foothills of the Aravalli mountains, has increased prices of its water bottles ŌĆŗby 18% for resellers, Shiroy Mehta, CEO of the company, told Reuters.
"Most manufacturers are absorbing ŌĆŗ40-50% of the ŌüĀcost to ensure that they don't lose clients. It's a poor situation for the beverage industry ahead of the summer season," he said.
The mass market, however, is dominated by companies that produce "drinking water" to be sold in 1-litre bottles to customers.
Clear Premium Water, a ŌüĀbrand of ŌĆŗIndia's Energy Beverages, said in a notice to its distributors there had been ŌĆŗan "unprecedented and continuous surge" in prices of key raw materials used in packaging and production.
"It is no longer possible for us to absorb the escalating costs ŌĆŗwhile maintaining existing product prices," the notice said.
(This story has been refiled to fix the date in the dateline to March 12)

item.title: China's mediation eases fighting between Pakistan, Afghanistan, sources say
item.description:
ISLAMABAD/BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - Chinese mediation efforts, including ŌĆŗa message from President Xi Jinping, have helped ease the worst fighting between Pakistan and Afghanistan since ŌĆīthe Taliban returned to power in 2021, three Pakistani government officials said.
The officials said a meeting between the Chinese ambassador to Pakistan, Jiang Zaidong, and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif late last month included a message from Xi to cease hostilities.
Neither side has reported any Pakistani air strikes on ŌĆŗAfghanistan in recent days and ground fighting along the 2,600-km (1,600-mile) border has tapered off, although daily clashes continue ŌĆŗto be reported.
China has said it is in contact with both countries about ending hostilities but ŌüĀMosharraf Zaidi, a spokesman for Sharif who has previously said there would not be any talks with the Taliban, did ŌĆŗnot respond to questions about Beijing's efforts.
Pakistani security officials have said the military campaign will continue until desired goals were achieved, ŌĆŗwhich was to prevent militant attacks in Pakistan launched from Afghan soil.
Pakistan's foreign ministry and military did not respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Islamabad launched air strikes on Afghanistan on February 26, saying the Taliban were providing a safe haven to militants carrying out attacks in Pakistan. ŌĆŗKabul denies the charge and says militancy in Pakistan is an internal problem.
The Chinese efforts came as Qatar, Saudi Arabia ŌĆŗand Turkey, who hosted talks between Pakistan and Afghanistan during previous clashes in October, have been embroiled in the war in the Middle ŌĆīEast following ŌüĀthe U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran .
"China's Special Envoy for Afghanistan Affairs is currently shuttling between the two countries to mediate, while Chinese embassies in both nations maintain close communication with the respective parties," the Chinese foreign ministry told Reuters in an e-mail.
"The most urgent task is to prevent the fighting from expanding and for the two countries to return to the negotiating ŌĆŗtable as soon as possible."
The ŌĆŗforeign ministry added that Foreign ŌüĀMinister Wang Yi held telephone talks with Pakistan's Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar on Tuesday to discuss the conflict.
China's ambassador to Kabul, Zhao Xing, and the special envoy ŌĆŗYue Xiaoyong met Afghanistan's acting Foreign Minister Amir Khan Muttaqi this week, the Afghan foreign ŌĆŗministry said in ŌüĀa statement.
Afghanistan and Pakistan have said they inflicted heavy damage on the other in the conflict and killed hundreds of opposition troops, without providing evidence. Reuters has not been able to verify the reports.
Beijing, a longtime Pakistani ally, has invested heavily in mines ŌüĀand minerals ŌĆŗin both nations.
The investments include over $65 billion in road, rail and other ŌĆŗdevelopment projects in Pakistan, part of Beijing's Belt and Road Initiative to expand land and sea trade routes to Europe and Africa.

item.title: Thailand seeks apology from Iran after vessel hit in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 12 (Reuters) - Thailand's Foreign Ministry has demanded ŌĆŗan apology from Iran ŌĆīover damage caused to a Thai vessel that ŌĆŗwas hit by ŌĆŗa projectile in the ŌüĀStrait of Hormuz ŌĆŗon Wednesday, which led ŌĆŗto a fire and forced the crew to abandon ŌĆŗship.
The ministry said ŌĆŗit was seeking clarity from Iran's ŌĆīambassador ŌüĀon Thursday on the facts surrounding the incident.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ŌĆŗsaid in ŌĆŗa ŌüĀstatement carried by the Tasnim ŌĆŗnews agency that ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀship was "fired upon by Iranian fighters".

item.title: Russia calls on Israel and US to end the Iran war
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russia on Thursday called ŌĆŗon Israel and the ŌĆīUnited States to end their attacks on Iran ŌĆŗand come to ŌĆŗthe negotiating table.
"Russia will continue ŌüĀto take steps ŌĆŗto end the escalation ŌĆŗin the Middle East as soon as possible and ŌĆŗresolve any contradictions ŌĆŗby peaceful means," Foreign Ministry spokeswoman ŌĆīMaria ŌüĀZakharova told reporters.
"The number of victims of the illegal military ŌĆŗaction of ŌĆŗWashington ŌüĀand Tel Aviv among the civilian ŌĆŗpopulation of Iran, ŌĆŗaccording ŌüĀto the authorities, is in the thousands," ŌüĀshe ŌĆŗsaid.

item.title: RussiaŌĆÖs Iran setback comes with consolation prize
item.description:
BERLIN, March 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Russia is reaping a hefty reward from the loss of its influence in the Middle East. The bombing of Iran by the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israel has boosted oil prices just as MoscowŌĆÖs finances are crumbling. That will give President Vladimir Putin some ŌĆīfinancial firepower to fund his war in Ukraine and postpone painful fiscal decisions.
The Russian government built its 2026 budget based on Urals crude trading at $59 a barrel. That was a realistic forecast ŌĆö and just about the price its oil was trading at the end of February. But in the ŌĆŗ10 days since the war in the Middle East erupted it has shot up to more than $80. That ŌĆŗhands Russian President Vladimir Putin a helpful windfall given taxes and levies on oil and gas account ŌüĀfor about 20% , opens new tab of the central governmentŌĆÖs revenue.
The boost is also timely. Oil and gas receipts were down 47% , opens new tab in the first ŌĆŗtwo months of 2026 compared to the same period in 2025. The Kremlin had already exhausted its planned annual deficit by ŌĆŗthe end of February.
If the Russian government abides by its own fiscal rules, the windfall won't flow into the budget. Anything above the $59 threshold must be saved in the countryŌĆÖs National Wealth Fund. But the fund acts as a rainy-day facility that can cover budget shortfalls ŌĆö the situation Russia ŌĆŗis in now. The liquid assets of the fund (held in foreign currency or gold) have already been depleted from $150 billion to $50 ŌĆŗbillion since the war started.
If the prices of oil and gas remain at their current level for a few months, Russia can expect ŌĆīa significant ŌüĀfinancial boost. According to an estimate , opens new tab from investment banker Yevgeniy Kogan, a $1 change in the Urals price triggers a variation of up to $1.9 billion in government revenue. Maintained over a whole year, higher oil prices could then deliver some $40 billion in extra revenue to Moscow.
And Putin may also sell more oil and gas. Donald Trump last week granted India a 30-day waiver from Russian oil sanctions, ŌĆŗand the U.S. president hinted ŌĆŗhe could go further to ŌüĀmake up for the impact of the Strait of Hormuz blockade on world oil markets.
Moscow refrained from condemning the U.S. when it launched its recent Iran bombing campaign. It already lost a ŌĆŗkey ally in 2024 in the region with the end of SyriaŌĆÖs Assad regime. Many ŌĆŗgovernments in the ŌüĀMiddle East and beyond may soon wonder what is the point of having Russia as a supposed ally. But Putin has his consolation prize ŌĆō an unexpected windfall to fund his war.
Follow Pierre Briancon on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Indian source says Iran to allow India-flagged tankers through Hormuz as first tanker arrives
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 12 (Reuters) - Iran will allow Indian-flagged tankers ŌĆŗto transit the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit for 40% of the South Asian nation's crude imports, an Indian government source said ŌĆīon Thursday, but an Iranian source outside the country denied any such deal was reached.
India, the world's No. 3 oil consumer, said on Thursday the foreign ministers of the two countries have had three conversations in recent days and the latest one this week focused on "issues pertaining to the safety of shipping and India's energy security".
"Beyond that, it would be premature ŌĆŗfor me to say anything," foreign ministry spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal told a weekly media briefing.
The Indian source, who was briefed on the matter, ŌĆŗdeclined to be identified, citing a lack of authorisation to speak to media, while the Iranian source said the matter was ŌüĀa sensitive one.
On Thursday, the Suezmax tanker Shenlong, carrying Saudi crude, arrived at a port in Mumbai after transiting the strait. The Liberia-flagged vessel was ŌĆŗthe first crude carrier to reach India from the Middle East since the war between Iran and the United States and Israel broke out in late February, ŌĆŗaccording to LSEG data. The customer is state-run Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab , said a source with direct knowledge of the matter.
The company did not immediately respond to a request seeking comment.
The Indian source said two other foreign-flagged tankers believed to be bound for India had recently transited the Strait, and that Iran had given assurances of safe passage for Indian-flagged vessels after ŌĆŗthe foreign ministers of both countries spoke by telephone late on Tuesday.
The source said the situation remained fluid, with limited clarity on how instructions were being ŌĆŗrelayed across different layers of IranŌĆÖs administration.
In a statement after the talks between India's S. Jaishankar and his counterpart, Seyed Abbas Araghchi, Iran's foreign ministry said the United States ŌĆīshould be ŌüĀheld accountable for the "insecure situation and problems arising for shipping in the Persian Gulf".
Neither side mentioned any agreement on allowing safe passage for Indian vessels.
On Wednesday, India said 28 Indian-flagged vessels were operating west and east of the Strait with 778 Indian sailors aboard.
"Authorities, ship managers and recruitment agencies are coordinating closely with Indian embassies and local authorities to ensure safety and provide assistance to Indian seafarers," the Indian petroleum ministry said in a statement.
India has given ŌĆŗsafe harbour to 183 Iranian sailors ŌĆŗfrom a vessel that docked after ŌüĀthe war broke out between Iran and the United States and Israel.
New Delhi had allowed three Iranian ships that departed following a naval exercise in the Bay of Bengal to dock, but one was later sunk by a U.S. submarine ŌĆŗin international waters, and another sought assistance from Sri Lanka.
Iran has attacked at least 16 ships in the ŌĆŗStrait since the war ŌüĀbegan in late February. Tehran has warned that oil prices could nearly double to $200 a barrel as it retaliates.
A Thai vessel bound for India's western port of Kandla was attacked in the Strait on Wednesday, prompting criticism from New Delhi.
"India deplores the fact that commercial shipping is being made a target of military attacks in the ongoing ŌüĀconflict," the ŌĆŗforeign ministry said on Wednesday, adding that its citizens had also died.
The near-closure of the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz has compelled India to scramble for alternatives, such as buying more from Russia.

item.title: Bombing won't overthrow ruling clerics, Iran dissidents say
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - A senior official from ŌĆŗa Paris-based Iranian opposition group said on Thursday that the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran ŌĆīwould not topple the clerical leadership, arguing that only a popular uprising backed by internal resistance could do so.
Almost two weeks of bombing have killed around 2,000 people in Iran including supreme leader Ayatollah ŌĆŗAli Khamenei, and damaged much of its military and security apparatus.
Iran has responded in ŌĆŗkind, throwing global energy markets and transport into chaos and spreading the ŌüĀconflict across the Middle East, while the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has tightened its grip ŌĆŗon power and threatened to crush any unrest.
"The 12-day war in June, and the current ŌĆŗwar, now in its 12th day, proved that bombings cannot overthrow the regime," Mohammad Mohaddesin, head of foreign policy at the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI), told a news conference.
"Even if you have 50,000 ŌĆŗarmed soldiers on the ground, you need the support of Iranian people. You need a ŌĆŗpopular uprising. The combination of this 50,000 or 20,000 or any other number with a popular uprising, ŌĆīthen ŌüĀyou have this power to overthrow the regime."
Mohaddesin said he did not consider a deployment of U.S. ground troops realistic.
The NCRI, also known by its Farsi name Mujahideen-e-Khalq, was listed as a terrorist organisation by the United States until 2012.
It is banned in Iran, and it ŌĆŗis unclear how much ŌĆŗsupport it has there. ŌüĀHowever, along with its bitter rival , the monarchists backing Reza Pahlavi, exiled son of the toppled shah, it is one of the few ŌĆŗopposition groups able to rally supporters.
Mohaddesin acknowledged that his group alone could ŌĆŗnot bring ŌüĀdown the system. But he said mass protests, like those that raged in January until they were bloodily quashed, would resume once bombing stopped, and could eventually shift the balance.
"I cannot say ŌüĀhow ŌĆŗmany months or a year, but ... this is the ŌĆŗtrack of overthrowing the regime," he said.
Israeli officials have said that one of their objectives is to weaken the ŌĆŗsecurity apparatus so that Iran's people can take control of their own destiny.

item.title: In Norwegian Arctic, civilian doctors, volunteer 'casualties' rehearse for war
item.description:
NARVIK, Norway, March 12 (Reuters) - In Narvik harbour in the Norwegian Arctic, the city's chief medical officer watches as dozens of "casualties" are unloaded from ŌĆŗa train and get triaged before being transported in ambulances to nearby hospitals.
Thomas Hultstedt is here to observe and learn ŌĆīduring an exercise that is part of NATO's biennial drills in the European Arctic, Cold Response, which started on Monday and which this year places more focus on the role of civilians, businesses and public institutions in supporting the military in case of war.
He and other health personnel are rehearsing a scenario in which war has broken out ŌĆŗin Finland and U.S. and Norwegian soldiers fighting on front lines, as well as civilians, have to be transported to Norway for ŌĆŗmedical treatment.
Under the simulation, some 1,200 people are meant to be shipped over the next 10 days to Narvik, ŌüĀthough the actual exercise lasts just one day - Thursday - and involves about 100 volunteers, including students, playing the "casualties".
"I have never done this type of ŌĆŗexercise before," Hultstedt said minutes before the train arrived at the quay.
"It is good in the sense that you prepare yourself for things that are ŌĆŗout of the ordinary. This is very different from normal life. This is a war situation."
The train has come from Finland via Sweden to Narvik, where the volunteers will be "treated" in civilian hospitals in the region. In a real-life situation, they would then be sent on to southern Norway or abroad.
Hultstedt says this type of exercise was unthinkable five years ago.
"Then Sweden and Finland were not part of NATO... Now Norway has to step ŌĆŗup. We must get equipment in and the injured out," he told Reuters.
Finland and Sweden joined the Atlantic military alliance in 2023 and 2024, respectively, following Russia's full-scale ŌĆīinvasion of ŌüĀUkraine on February 24, 2022. Norway joined NATO in 1949 as a founding member.
While NATO military planners never refer to Russia by name, the scenario they train for involves an attack from an enemy in the east. Russia lies roughly 600 km (372 miles) to the east, as the crow flies.
Moscow has repeatedly dismissed as baseless fear-mongering suggestions that it might attack a NATO member in the future.
Narvik, an industrial city nestled between a 1,006-metre (3,300 ft) ŌĆŗsnow-capped mountain and the pristine Ofot ŌĆŗfjord, is one of the ŌüĀkey places along Norway's coast where NATO allies plan to land if Finland, Sweden or Norway comes under attack.
The train line from Narvik to Kiruna in Sweden, and further east to Finland, is the main way to ŌĆŗship equipment and troops between east and west in Arctic Scandinavia.
Narvik was the site in 1940 of an ŌĆŗearly battle of ŌüĀWorld War Two, when Norwegian, British, French and Polish forces pushed back Nazi German troops, before withdrawing when Germany invaded France.
Norway has declared 2026 the year of "total defence", the latest move by a Nordic nation to boost civilian preparedness.
"It is very important to narrow the gap between military forces and civil society ŌüĀbecause in ŌĆŗa really critical situation - war - there is a need for a lot of resources," ŌĆŗsaid Elisabeth Aarsaether, outgoing director of the Norwegian Directorate for Civil Protection.
While Norwegian planners are confident most households are better prepared to cope with war, she said, they are concerned that ŌĆŗlocal authorities are not ready and so are preparing new guidelines.
"We would really like to help them," she said.

item.title: Ghana to submit UN resolution on slavery reparations; eyes broad support
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Ghana ŌĆŗintends to propose a United Nations resolution recognising transatlantic slavery as ŌĆīthe "gravest crime in the history of humankind" and calling for reparations, and expects broad support despite resistance in Europe.
The West African nation, a prominent advocate of reparations , opens new tab on the continent, plans to ŌĆŗtable the proposal at the U.N. General Assembly, possibly as early ŌĆŗas this month, its Foreign Ministry said in a statement to ŌüĀReuters.
The plan marks a new step in Africa's efforts to seek accountability for ŌĆŗhistorical injustices by former colonial powers, and the country hopes it will "enjoy the support ŌĆŗof many member states".
"The proposed resolution seeks to recognise transatlantic slave trade as the gravest crime in the history of humankind, taking into account its scale, duration, legalisation and enduring consequences," ŌĆŗit said.
While calls for reparations have gained momentum in recent years, there is also a growing backlash .
Several ŌĆŗEuropean leaders have opposed even discussing the subject, with critics arguing today's states and institutions ŌĆīshould not ŌüĀbe held responsible for historical wrongs.
Asked if it expected opposition, the ministry said: "A backlash against truth is one that we hope would not occur. Ghana is not seeking to reopen old wounds but to heal those wounds with truth."
African and Caribbean nations ŌĆŗhave been seeking to ŌüĀestablish a special U.N. reparations tribunal, with lawyers noting previous tribunals had been created by resolution or by the Security ŌĆŗCouncil.
The African Union (AU) last year set out to create a "unified vision" ŌĆŗamong its ŌüĀ55 member states on what reparations may look like, from financial compensation and formal apologies to policy reforms. AU leaders endorsed the proposed resolution at a summit last ŌüĀmonth.
Member ŌĆŗstates of the Caribbean Community, which has outlined its ŌĆŗown reparations plan , are also expected to support it.
"We remain optimistic that others would equally be able ŌĆŗto do so," the ministry said.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz issue must be resolved through diplomacy, says German foreign minister
item.description:
BERLIN, March 12 (Reuters) - A solution to security issues in the strategically important Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz can only be achieved ŌĆīdiplomatically, German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul said on Thursday.
"A reliable and sustainable solution can ŌĆŗonly be achieved through diplomatic ŌĆŗchannels, and that is why I ŌüĀbelieve ... that we should pool our ŌĆŗcommon interests from the Gulf region, but ŌĆŗalso here in the neighbourhood," he said during a visit to Turkey.
Iran's retaliation to U.S.-Israeli ŌĆŗhas included strikes on ships ŌĆŗin the Strait of Hormuz, bringing non-Iranian vessel ŌĆītransits ŌüĀthrough the main gateway for much of Middle Eastern oil exports to a near standstill and forcing producers in ŌĆŗthe region ŌĆŗto ŌüĀcut output.
"Together, we must find a way out of this ŌĆŗwar and, at the same ŌĆŗtime, ŌüĀdevelop an initial idea of what a future security architecture for the region ŌüĀmight ŌĆŗlook like," Wadephul said ŌĆŗduring a news conference alongside his Turkish counterpart.

item.title: Oil unlikely to hit $200 a barrel, US energy chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said ŌĆŗon Thursday global oil prices are unlikely to hit $200 a ŌĆībarrel even as crude tankers remained stalled in the Strait of Hormuz and the U.S. and Israeli war with Iran widened.
"I would say unlikely, but we ŌĆŗare focused on the military operation and solving a problem," Wright ŌĆŗtold CNN when asked if prices would reach $200 a ŌüĀbarrel - a level prices could hit if the war continues to escalate, an ŌĆŗIranian official said on Wednesday.
"Get ready for the oil barrel to be ŌĆŗat $200 because the oil price depends on the regional security which you have destabilized," Ebrahim Zolfaqari, the spokesperson for Tehran's Khatam al-Anbiya military command headquarters, said on ŌĆŗWednesday.
Oil prices jumped 6% to nearly $100 on Thursday as two ŌĆŗtankers blazed in an Iraqi port after a hit by suspected Iranian explosive-laden boats.
The ŌĆīrise ŌüĀcame despite more than 30 countries in the International Energy Agency announcing a day earlier the biggest-ever coordinated drawdown of global oil reserves of 400 million barrels, about 40% of which will come from the U.S., the ŌĆŗworld's largest oil ŌĆŗproducer.
The war ŌüĀhas forced Middle East Gulf countries to cut total oil production by at least 10 million barrels ŌĆŗper day, about 10% of world demand. The IEA ŌĆŗsaid on ŌüĀThursday that is the biggest oil supply disruption in the history of the global market.
Wright also told CNBC on Thursday that the U.S. Navy cannot ŌüĀescort ships ŌĆŗthrough the Strait of Hormuz now but ŌĆŗit was "quite likely" that could happen by the end of the month.

item.title: EU sees no immediate oil supply concerns despite Iran disruption
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - The European Union does not see any ŌĆŗimmediate concerns regarding the ŌĆīsecurity of its oil supplies as a result of the ŌĆŗIran war, an ŌĆŗEU Commission spokesperson said on ŌüĀThursday.
The EU's top oil ŌĆŗsuppliers are Norway and the ŌĆŗUnited States. The spokesperson added that EU countries would notify the ŌĆŗInternational Energy Agency (IEA) of ŌĆŗtheir planned oil reserve releases by ŌĆī1700 ŌüĀGMT on Thursday.
The IEA had on Wednesday agreed to release a record 400 million ŌĆŗbarrels of ŌĆŗoil ŌüĀfrom strategic stockpiles to combat a spike ŌĆŗin global crude ŌĆŗprices ŌüĀsince the start of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, ŌüĀwith ŌĆŗthe U.S. contributing ŌĆŗthe bulk of the supply.

item.title: Police say drone found at mine in western Poland
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Police in ŌĆŗwestern Poland were investigating on Thursday the origin of ŌĆīa drone discovered by a worker at a lignite mine and it did not appear to be a civilian model, a ŌĆŗpolice spokesperson said.
NATO member Poland has been on ŌĆŗhigh alert for airspace incursions since more than ŌüĀ20 Russian drones entered its airspace on the night ŌĆŗof September 9-10, 2025, and some of them were found ŌĆŗin the east of the country.
"It doesn't look like a civilian drone, but there's no information about the model," police spokesperson ŌĆŗMaciej Swiecichowski told Reuters by phone from the western city ŌĆŗof Poznan.
State broadcaster TVP Info cited sources as saying it ŌĆīwas ŌüĀa Gerbera military drone . Gerbera drones are produced in Russia and are used as decoys.
Local police said on X earlier they had been alerted to an "Unmanned ŌĆŗAerial Vehicle ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀdrone type" found by an employee in Galczyce, Konin County, on the premises ŌĆŗof the lignite mine.
Lignite, also known as 'brown ŌĆŗcoal', ŌüĀis a combustible sedimentary rock.
"At the scene, police officers from Konin and Poznan are securing the area," ŌüĀthey said, ŌĆŗadding that nobody had been ŌĆŗinjured in the incident.

item.title: ICC opens probe into alleged crimes against humanity by Belarus
item.description:

Belarus is not a member of ŌĆŗthe ICC, ŌĆŗbut the case was ŌĆībrought ŌüĀby Lithuania, which is.

item.title: Nepal to ration cooking gas due to panic over shortage
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 12 (Reuters) - Nepal will begin rationing cooking gas amid fears that the Middle ŌĆŗEast conflict could create a nationwide shortage, ŌĆīan official said on Thursday.
Chandika Prasad Bhatta, executive director of state-run Nepal Oil Corporation (NOC) said authorities would refill only ŌĆŗhalf of consumers' empty cylinders starting from ŌĆŗFriday to make its liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) stocks ŌüĀlast longer.
The supply squeeze comes as the ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's retaliation across the ŌĆŗregion effectively halts shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, raising energy and transport costs and reducing the output of Middle ŌĆŗEast oil and gas producers .
Bhatta said consumers were ŌĆŗpanicking despite repeated assurances that Nepal had enough supplies ŌĆīof ŌüĀLPG.
"The rationing is expected to end the panic and rush for hoarding," Bhatta told Reuters.
Consumers carrying empty cylinders have formed long queues outside refilling ŌĆŗplants throughout ŌĆŗthe country, ŌüĀwhich is completely dependent on India for its supplies of fuel, ŌĆŗincluding cooking gas.
Officials said Nepal requires about ŌĆŗ45,000 ŌüĀcylinders each containing 14.2 kg of cooking gas every month and there was no disruption to ŌüĀsupplies.
ŌĆ£We ŌĆŗare getting regular supply of ŌĆŗfuel including the cooking gas according to our demands," Bhatta ŌĆŗsaid.

BEIRUT, March 12 (Reuters) - The Israeli military ŌĆŗissued new evacuation ŌĆīorders for southern Lebanon on ŌĆŗThursday which ŌĆŗnearly doubled the zone ŌüĀit said ŌĆŗresidents should leave, ŌĆŗsaying they should move north beyond the ŌĆŗZahrani river.
The ŌĆŗnew zones, marked in ŌĆīred ŌüĀin a map published on X by ŌĆŗa military ŌĆŗspokesperson, ŌüĀmean that the Israeli ŌĆŗmilitary has ŌĆŗnow ŌüĀordered people out of 10% ŌüĀof ŌĆŗLebanese territory.

item.title: Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei says Strait of Hormuz should remain shut
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba ŌĆŗKhamenei said on ŌĆīThursday the Strait of Hormuz should ŌĆŗremain closed as ŌĆŗa tool of pressure.
In ŌüĀa message ŌĆŗread out by ŌĆŗa state TV broadcaster, the first the new ŌĆŗsupreme leader ŌĆŗhas issued since he was ŌĆīappointed ŌüĀto succeed his slain father, Mojtaba said all ŌĆŗU.S. bases ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe region should be ŌĆŗclosed as they ŌĆŗwould ŌüĀbe attacked.

item.title: Iran war is one 'TACO' too far
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - The "Trump always chickens out" investment strategy - buying beaten-down stocks on the assumption that the U.S. president will ultimately back down from his more extreme policies - has, for the most part, been a profitable one. But the Iran war may change that.
The joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran on February ŌĆŗ28 has sparked war across the Middle East, triggering the biggest energy shock since the 1970s and record volatility in oil and gas.
So far, U.S. equities have been ŌĆīremarkably calm, likely, in part, on the assumption that Trump will pull back if necessary. Since February 28, European, Asian and emerging stocks have lost 4-7%. The S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab is down 1% and the Nasdaq is up 0.5%.
But this conflict may be a "TACO" too far.
Even if Trump were to declare an end to the war tomorrow, damage has been done that cannot be quickly reversed, least of all by him.
Shuttered oil production and refining capacity, bombed-out infrastructure, ŌĆŗand the upending of global energy flows cannot be made right at the stroke of a pen ŌĆō or a Truth Social post. It will take months, possibly years, ŌĆŗto get everything back to its pre-war state.
Similarly, the unprecedented surge in the cost of shipping out of the Middle East and insuring ŌüĀvessels in the region is unlikely to return to "normal" levels any time soon, even after hostilities officially end.
"While global financial markets have been able to shake off the volatility and uncertainty caused ŌĆŗby Trump's mercurial policies, the economic and geopolitical ramifications of the Iran war are likely to prove far more consequential," says Eswar Prasad, professor of economics at Cornell University.
This is the first time the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz ŌĆō the vital waterway through which 20% of the world's daily energy use passes ŌĆō has effectively been closed. No previous conflict in the region, including the Iran-Iraq War of the 1980s, the first Gulf War in the early 1990s or the Iraq War of the 2000s, has brought oil tankers to a virtual standstill.
Even in the market's optimistic scenario, where the U.S. wraps up its attacks quickly, instability that is almost entirely ŌĆŗbeyond Trump's control would likely remain. And who knows how long that could persist, or how bad it could get.
Trump's economic and geopolitical playbook during his second term has been ŌĆŗto push the boundaries of accepted norms, rules and protocols to breaking point ŌĆō or sometimes beyond ŌĆō then wait and assess the market fallout. If investors barely blink, it's a green light to carry on, even ŌĆīaccelerate. If ŌüĀmarkets crater, Trump steps back from the brink.
Since Trump returned to the White House 14 months ago, he has unraveled America's 80-year alliance with Europe, imperiled the existence of NATO, threatened to annex Greenland and Canada, deposed Venezuela's president, and threatened to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.
To varying degrees, markets wobbled in response to each of these episodes, forcing Trump to tone down the rhetoric. That, in turn, gave rise to the so-called "TACO trade."
Despite the geopolitical tumult of the last year, U.S. equities have continued to appreciate, volatility has mostly remained subdued, and risk premia have stayed relatively ŌĆŗwell-contained.
The biggest "TACO" of them all ŌĆō at least until now ŌĆō ŌĆŗrevolved around the "Liberation Day" tariffs last April. After ŌüĀtrillions of dollars were wiped off U.S. stock markets in a couple of days, Trump backed down on the most punitive duties.
But today's conflict in the Middle East is different, and potentially riskier than anything seen in recent decades. If investors are looking for a historical precedent, ŌĆŗthe oil shock of 1973-74 may be a useful guide in terms of its lasting economic impact.
Oil prices are unlikely to quadruple, as they ŌĆŗdid then, especially now that ŌüĀthe U.S. is the world's biggest energy producer and exporter. But bear in mind, although the OPEC oil embargo lasted only six months, the inflation it spawned haunted the U.S. and other industrialized economies for the rest of the decade and into the 1980s.
A persistent surge in energy prices and disrupted global supply chains could easily light the stagflationary fuse. Investors may now start asking whether this ŌüĀcrisis is one ŌĆŗTrump can't relieve, even if he does "chicken out."
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for ŌĆŗReuters)
