item.title: Helium prices soar as Qatar LNG halt exposes fragile supply chain
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Disruptions to Qatar's natural gas processing from the Iran war have driven helium prices sharply higher, exposing the fragility of a small but critical market that supports industries from semiconductors to medical imaging.
Helium spot prices have doubled since the Middle East crisis began, according to Phil Kornbluth, ŌĆŗpresident of Kornbluth Helium Consulting, as buyers scramble to secure supply.
State energy giant QatarEnergy, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced a production halt at its 77 ŌĆīmillion tons per annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure on LNG shipments , amid the conflict.
Because helium is extracted as a byproduct of natural gas processing, any disruption to LNG output directly cuts helium supply.
Qatari Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi told the Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" for deliveries to return to normal even if the conflict ended immediately.
Qatar is a pivotal ŌĆŗsupplier rather than a marginal one.
Data from the U.S. Geological Survey shows the country produced about 63 million cubic meters of helium in 2025, out of ŌĆŗroughly 190 million cubic meters globally, accounting for close to one-third of world supply.
"If those conditions (supply disruption) persist, the market is ŌüĀeffectively missing about 5.2 million cubic meters of helium per month," said Aleksandr Romanenko, CEO of market research firm IndexBox.
The disruption is reverberating through a market with little ŌĆŗspare production capacity and limited storage, leaving buyers with few short-term alternatives.
Japan's top helium supplier Iwatani (8088.T) , opens new tab said it had so far maintained stable supply to customers including semiconductor ŌĆŗmanufacturers, partly because it also sources helium from the United States and maintains stockpiles in both Japan and the U.S.
Helium markets operate very differently from most commodities.
Most supply is sold through long-term contracts rather than a transparent spot market, meaning price signals often emerge slowly even as supply tightens.
That opacity makes price discovery difficult, but signs of tightening supply have ŌĆŗalready begun to emerge.
"Early indications show about 50% spot price increases already," said Anish Kapadia, CEO of market research firm AKAP Energy.
"In a sustained disruption, prices could ŌĆŗrise sharply and potentially retest past shortage peaks of more than $2,000 per thousand cubic feet."
Romanenko said a 30-day disruption could lift delivered helium prices by 10% to 20%, while a 60-to-90-day outage ŌĆīcould push ŌüĀprices higher by 25% to 50%, particularly for buyers without long-term supply contracts.
Helium's physical properties add another constraint. The gas is typically shipped in liquid form and gradually evaporates during transport.
"It's a commodity, but it also has a shelf life," said Chris Bakker, CEO of helium developer Avanti (AVN.V) , opens new tab .
"So when you liquefy it, and that's how they tend to ship it worldwide, you've got notionally 45 days to get it to the end-user."
If supply tightens further, suppliers typically prioritise key sectors when allocating volumes ŌĆŗduring force majeure events.
Kornbluth said industries such ŌĆŗas medical MRI systems and rocket ships would ŌüĀprobably get 100% of their needs, while semiconductor manufacturers might receive 95%.
Lower-priority uses, including welding, diving equipment and party balloons, would likely face deeper cuts.
Last week, South Korea's ruling party lawmaker Kim Young-bae warned the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could disrupt supplies of ŌĆŗkey semiconductor manufacturing materials, giving helium as one example.
The hierarchy reflects helium's role in technologies where substitutes are limited.
Industrial gas ŌĆŗcompanies sourcing helium from Qatar, ŌüĀincluding Air Liquide, Linde (LIN.DE) , opens new tab and Air Products and Chemicals (APD.N) , opens new tab , are among those most exposed to the supply shock, said Kapadia.
Air Products said it was taking steps to ensure continuity of supply but did not give further details.
Linde declined to comment, while Air Liquide said it relied on multiple sources in different continents and on its storage cavern in Europe.
Iwatani Japan is ŌüĀalso exposed, ŌĆŗKornbluth and Bakker said.
Producers outside the region could benefit if disruptions persist.
Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is the largest helium ŌĆŗproducer outside Qatar, while Canada-based North American Helium and smaller developers such as Helix Exploration (HEXH.L) , opens new tab and Blue Star Helium (BNL.AX) , opens new tab could see stronger demand, Kapadia added.

item.title: Trump: US benefits from high oil prices, but priority is stopping Iran
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. ŌĆŗPresident Donald ŌĆīTrump said ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗThursday that rising ŌüĀoil ŌĆŗprices ŌĆŗmean greater profits for ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗUnited States, but ŌĆīhis ŌüĀpriority is stopping ŌĆŗIran ŌĆŗfrom ŌüĀhaving nuclear ŌĆŗweapons.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ŌĆītaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ŌĆŗfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ŌĆŗChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil ŌüĀand gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ŌĆŗUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ŌĆŗa summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ŌĆŗundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ŌĆŗsystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ŌĆīneeded ŌüĀto reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ŌĆŗup about 18% ŌĆŗof industrial power ŌüĀbills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ŌĆŗgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ŌĆŗincluding state ŌüĀaid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must ŌüĀbe ŌĆŗready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ŌĆŗuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ŌĆŗimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: EU to soften gas authorisation rules to help secure LNG supplies, diplomats say
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - The European Commission will instruct governments to be flexible in enforcing EU rules on gas imports, diplomats told Reuters ŌĆŗon Thursday, amid fears they could hold up liquefied natural gas ŌĆīdeliveries needed to stabilise supplies during the Iran crisis .
The Commission plans to issue guidance before March 18 on how to apply rules within the European Union's law to ŌĆŗphase out Russian gas, the diplomats said.
The guidance marks an attempt ŌĆŗby Brussels to prevent its Russia phaseout rules from inadvertently ŌüĀchoking Europe's gas supply at a moment of vulnerability. It would not ŌĆŗaffect the EU's phase-out of Russian gas.
With the Iran conflict rattling global LNG ŌĆŗflows and raising the risk of cargo diversions, EU officials fear rigid enforcement could delay or strand shipments, undermining the bloc's ability to keep storage adequately stocked and ŌĆŗsafeguard energy security heading into next winter.
The rules in question require ŌĆŗthat shipments of LNG from certain non-Russian countries receive "prior authorisation" - meaning companies must provide European ŌĆīcustoms ŌüĀauthorities with proof of the gas's origin five days before it arrives in the EU.
The main country affected by a softening of the "prior authorisation" rules would be Azerbaijan, which last year supplied 4% of EU gas ŌĆŗimports.
The EU has ŌĆŗalready said its ŌüĀmain gas suppliers, including Norway and the U.S., will not face the prior authorisation rules since the EU deems ŌĆŗthe risk of Russian gas entering their exports to ŌĆŗbe low.
Europe's ŌüĀgas industry had already asked Brussels to temporarily suspend the rules on prior authorisation.
"We simply cannot afford that a single flexible LNG cargo is delayed ŌüĀin port, ŌĆŗstranded at sea, or rerouted to Asia ŌĆŗbecause prior authorisation is unclear or pending," industry group Eurogas said in a statement on ŌĆŗThursday.

item.title: Russia says eight medics killed in Ukrainian drone attack in Donetsk region
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - The Russian Defence Ministry said on ŌĆŗThursday that eight medics ŌĆīwere killed and 10 people were wounded in a ŌĆŗUkrainian drone attack ŌĆŗthis week on a medical ŌüĀfacility in Ukraine's ŌĆŗDonetsk region.
There was no immediate ŌĆŗcomment from Ukraine.
Reuters could not independently verify the incident. The Russian ŌĆŗministry said it ŌĆŗtook place on Tuesday in Donetsk ŌĆīregion, ŌüĀwhich is mostly controlled by Russian forces, but it did not state ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗprecise ŌüĀlocation.
"This facility has never been used for ŌĆŗmilitary purposes, and ŌĆŗits ŌüĀdeliberate destruction by the Kyiv regime constitutes a ŌüĀgross ŌĆŗviolation of international ŌĆŗhumanitarian law and human morality," ŌĆŗthe ministry said.

item.title: Morocco plans repatriation of IS-linked citizens from Iraq, official says
item.description:
RABAT, March 12 (Reuters) - Morocco is developing plans to repatriate its ŌĆīnationals who fought for Islamic State in Syria and were transferred by the United States into Iraqi detention, a senior security official said on Thursday.
The U.S. began moving detained IS members out of Syria in ŌĆŗJanuary after the collapse of the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), which had been guarding around ŌĆŗa dozen facilities holding IS fighters and affiliated civilians, including foreigners.
Last month, ŌüĀthe U.S. Central Command said the U.S. military had completed its mission in Syria after transferring ŌĆŗ5,700 adult male Islamic State detainees to Iraq.
Iraq has since then urged Muslim and Western ŌĆŗnations to repatriate their nationals.
Morocco "is currently developing an action plan on this matter ... taking into consideration the diversity of the population targeted by the procedure, namely the fighters, as well as the women who lived ŌĆŗin the SDF camps in Syria, in addition to their children," a security official close ŌĆŗto the matter told Reuters.
There were 1,667 Moroccan foreign fighters in Syria and Iraq, of whom 244 ŌĆīwere ŌüĀdetained in prisons controlled by the SDF in northeastern Syria, while 279 former fighters have returned to the Kingdom, the official said.
The official added that 269 Moroccan women remain in the conflict zone along with 627 minors, while 134 women and 354 children are being held ŌĆŗin SDF-run camps in ŌĆŗnortheastern Syria. So far, ŌüĀ125 women have returned to Morocco since the beginning of the Syrian crisis.
In March 2019, Morocco repatriated eight fighters detained by the ŌĆŗSDF, who stood trial in Morocco and now are serving sentences ranging ŌĆŗfrom 13 ŌüĀto 18 years on terrorism charges.
Joining jihadist groups abroad is punishable by up to 10 years in jail under Moroccan law. In recent years, IS branches in Africa have recruited more than 130 ŌüĀMoroccan ŌĆŗfighters, the head of Morocco's counter-terrorism agency, the Central ŌĆŗBureau of Judicial Investigations (BCIJ), Haboub Cherkaoui said last year.
Since its establishment in 2015, the BCIJ has dismantled dozens of militant ŌĆŗcells and arrested more than 1,000 suspected jihadists.

item.title: UK exploring additional Gulf deployments after minehunter withdrawal
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Britain is exploring additional options for deployments to the Gulf after Iran stepped up attacks on ŌĆŗvessels, defence minister John Healey said on Thursday, ŌĆīadding that the UK already has autonomous mine hunting systems in the region.
The Royal Navy (RN) said last week its last minehunter ŌĆŗHMS Middleton, which was based in Bahrain, had ŌĆŗarrived back in Britain on March 1. The vessel, ŌüĀwhich was more than 40 years old, was ŌĆŗno longer certified to sail, it said.
"We do have some ŌĆŗautonomous mine hunting systems already in the region," Healey said. "There are additional options we're starting to consider that we could deploy alongside ŌĆŗallies."
Iran has deployed about a dozen mines , opens new tab in the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, two sources familiar with the matter told Reuters on ŌĆīWednesday, ŌüĀin a move likely to complicate any reopening of the narrow waterway, an important route for shipping oil and LNG.
The U.S. military says it has targeted Iranian mine-laying ŌĆŗvessels, eliminating 16 ŌĆŗof them ŌüĀon Tuesday.
Two tankers were ablaze in an Iraqi port on Thursday after a hit by ŌĆŗsuspected Iranian explosive-laden boats. Hours earlier, three ŌĆŗother ships ŌüĀhad been struck in the Gulf. Healey described the situation as a "major escalation from Iran".
"It is a breach of ŌüĀinternational ŌĆŗlaw," Healey said. "It's a very serious ŌĆŗsituation that is having an impact on oil prices and the cost ŌĆŗof living for everyone."

item.title: Caribbean islands seek $200 million for regional biodiversity push
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - A group of Caribbean countries plan to raise $200 ŌĆŗmillion for a joint bid to boost biodiversity, in a push for more influence ŌĆīon internationally financed environment projects to maximise their impact, Grenada's climate ambassador has told Reuters.
The 30X30 initiative by the Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States (OECS) and its 12 members is part of a broader global drive towards targets, agreed in ŌĆŗ2022, that include protecting 30% of the world's land and sea by 2030.
The hope is ŌĆŗthat such cross-border initiatives, with government oversight from the start, will be more ŌüĀeffective as they harness the power of multilateral, bilateral, philanthropic and private sector funding along with ŌĆŗfinancial tools such as debt-for-nature swaps, Safiya Sawney said.
While around $650 ŌĆŗmillion was raised to fund conservation in member states over the 20 years to 2024, governments often had to consider the priorities of multilateral donors before their own. For lack of coordination, some donors duplicated efforts.
"From the outside looking ŌĆŗin, you say, 'Well, that's a lot of money. Why do you need more?'. The issue ... is ŌĆŗthat we're not seeing that in the impact," she added, in an interview last week.
A report , opens new tab from the Back ŌĆīto Blue ŌüĀinitiative, which focuses on ocean sustainability policy, suggested regional approaches such as "OECS 30X30" could be transformative in closing a huge funding gap.
Small Island Developing States (SIDS) worldwide - which will bear the brunt of rising sea levels - need around $12 billion , opens new tab a year to adapt to the effects of climate change, and get just $2 ŌĆŗbillion, according to the ŌĆŗGlobal Center on Adaptation.
"There's ŌüĀa huge disconnect between what the governments have invested time and effort into planning for themselves, based on their own indigenous circumstances, and what the ŌĆŗdonor is funding," Sawney said.
"We're challenging them to say 'No, you have to ŌĆŗdo a better ŌüĀjob if you really want to ensure that your money stretches. You have to trust us, trust that we know what would we need to do for ourselves'."
Sawney said that, as development aid budgets are ŌüĀcut in ŌĆŗricher countries, it was important to scale projects regionally and ŌĆŗattract more private sector investors.
"For us, the programme is really, really important because it's really on us to be able to ŌĆŗshow investors, particularly non-traditional investors, that we're a good bet."

item.title: Ukraine opens battlefield data access to allies' AI models
item.description:
KYIV, March 12 (Reuters) - Ukraine is opening up access to its battlefield data for its allies to train drone AI software, the defence minister said ŌĆŗon Thursday, as Kyiv seeks to harness the experience it has garnered ŌĆīfending off Russia's four-year, full-scale invasion.
The move comes as militaries across the globe start to use automated systems which can guide drones to their targets without a pilot, or ŌĆŗquickly analyse vast pools of data.
Foreign allies and companies have sought ŌĆŗaccess to Ukraine's datasets, as these are crucial for training models to ŌüĀrecognise patterns, shapes, and the behaviour of people and machines on the ŌĆŗbattlefield.
Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov said a platform had been created to safely train ŌĆŗAI models without giving away sensitive data, but which nevertheless provides constantly updating datasets and large quantities of photos and video footage.
"Today, Ukraine has a unique array of battlefield data ŌĆŗthat is unmatched anywhere else in the world," he wrote on Telegram.
"This ŌĆŗincludes millions of annotated images collected during tens of thousands of combat flights."
Fedorov, a tech-savvy ŌĆīally ŌüĀof President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, said Ukraine would benefit from speeding up the development of AI models which it can then use in its war against Russia.
"We are ready to work with partners on joint analytics, model training, and the creation ŌĆŗof new technological solutions," ŌĆŗhe said, adding ŌüĀthat Ukraine wants to increase the role played by autonomous systems in the war.
When he was appointed in January, ŌĆŗFedorov laid out his plans to conduct a broader data-driven overhaul ŌĆŗof ŌüĀUkraine's vast defence ministry.
Ukraine is keen to maximise its advantage from the experience gained from Europe's largest conflict since 1945, as it strives to retain its allies' ŌüĀinterest and ŌĆŗfunding in the fifth year of full-scale ŌĆŗwar.
It has sent anti-drone specialists to four Middle Eastern nations this week after they requested Kyiv's help in ŌĆŗdowning Iran's vast barrages of Shahed UAVs.

item.title: European rights body says abortion refusal complaints from Poland have ceased
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - The number of legal abortions in Poland doubled in 2024, while complaints to the European Court of Human Rights over conscience clause refusals in ŌĆŗthe country have ceased, the Council of Europe said on Thursday.
Poland, a ŌĆīpredominantly Catholic country, introduced a near-total abortion ban in 2021 under the previous nationalist government after pregnancy termination due to foetal abnormalities was ruled unconstitutional.
Prime Minister Donald Tusk's pro-European coalition won the ŌĆŗ2023 election partly on the promise it would ease the restrictions, but it ŌĆŗhas so far failed to agree on a bill. Nationalist President ŌüĀKarol Nawrocki has signalled he will block any attempt at liberalisation.
However, Tusk's government ŌĆŗhas taken steps to enforce the existing law. Ministers issued recommendations to hospitals and ŌĆŗprosecutors clarifying that abortion on the grounds of a woman's mental health is legal, and that all hospitals are obliged to provide the procedure.
The Council of Europe's Committee of Ministers, which oversees ŌĆŗthe implementation of European Court of Human Rights rulings, said on Thursday the ŌĆŗgovernment's actions "appear to bring results in practice."
"The number of lawful abortions has doubled year to year ŌĆīand ŌüĀno complaints have recently been received about the refusal of abortion based on the conscience clause", the committee said in its decision on Poland's compliance with judgments concerning access to legal abortion.
It also noted "that since the introduction of the unconditional obligation of ŌĆŗhospitals to provide abortion ŌĆŗas a contracted ŌüĀmedical service, no complaint has been reported about a refusal of lawful abortion based on the conscience clause."
The committee, however, expressed ŌĆŗregret over the lack of progress in adopting legislation on ŌĆŗthe safe ŌüĀtermination of pregnancy.
Health ministry data shows that nearly 900 legal abortions were performed in Polish hospitals in 2024, compared with 425 a year earlier. There were 411 legal terminations ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe first half of 2025, with full-year data ŌĆŗnot yet available.
Before the 2021 ruling that tightened the law, more than 1,000 abortions were performed annually in ŌĆŗPoland, nearly all attributed to foetal abnormalities.

item.title: Eight countries warn EU not to weaken carbon market, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - Spain, the Netherlands and six other governments have urged the European Union not to dismantle or suspend the bloc's emissions ŌĆŗtrading system, its main climate change policy, as Brussels hunts for ŌĆīways to curb energy prices.
With energy prices surging on disruption to Middle Eastern oil and gas supplies, Brussels is facing calls from governments including Italy to suspend its ETS, which ŌĆŗrequires power plants to buy permits to cover their CO2 emissions.
"Making ŌĆŗfundamental changes to the ETS, calling into question the ETS ŌüĀinstrument itself, or suspending it, would constitute a very worrying step backwards," ŌĆŗa group of eight EU countries said in a joint paper, seen by ŌĆŗReuters.
Weakening the scheme would "dramatically penalise early movers who have already invested and innovated in decarbonisation", said the paper, which was also signed by Denmark, Finland, Luxembourg, Portugal, Slovenia and Sweden.
By putting ŌĆŗa price on pollution, the ETS aims to provide a financial incentive ŌĆŗto companies to invest in lower-carbon production.
The Commission has so far held off calls from ŌĆīother ŌüĀgovernments, including Slovakia and the Czech Republic , to weaken the ETS.
But officials are hunting for quick fixes to avoid the Iran conflict triggering a repeat of 2022, when Europe faced record-high energy prices after Russia invaded Ukraine and slashed ŌĆŗgas deliveries to ŌĆŗEurope.
The European Commission ŌüĀhas said it will provide options for EU leaders to consider at a summit on 19 March. European Commission ŌĆŗPresident Ursula von der Leyen said on Wednesday Brussels was ŌĆŗexploring ŌüĀa gas price cap , but did not mention changes to the ETS.
The ETS cost comprises around 11%, on average, of electricity bills in Europe, according to EU data. ŌüĀIt ŌĆŗresults in higher costs for countries like Poland, ŌĆŗwhere the ETS's contribution to power bills is 24% due to the dominant role of coal ŌĆŗpower plants in the power mix.

item.title: Foreign investors threaten legal action against Vietnam over renewables, document says
item.description:
HANOI, March 12 (Reuters) - Foreign investors may take legal action if Vietnam does not fully pay electricity tariffs agreed with multiple wind and solar projects, according to ŌĆŗa document signed by five chambers of commerce seen by Reuters.
The dispute ŌĆībegan last year when Vietnam cut previously agreed subsidised prices for electricity from some solar and wind farms, citing irregularities. The move comes as Vietnam's energy sector struggles with higher prices and risks of shortages ŌĆŗlinked to the conflict in Iran.
Lengthy negotiations with affected investors, who run ŌĆŗprojects with a combined capacity of 12 gigawatts, failed to produce a ŌüĀcompromise, prompting the chambers of commerce in Vietnam from the European Union, Britain, Japan, ŌĆŗSouth Korea and Thailand to send a joint letter to the government on Thursday.
If ŌĆŗpayment obligations are not met, electricity producers "may seek to enforce remedies, including pursuing dispute resolution in Vietnam or other jurisdictions," said the document, dated March 12.
The letter urges authorities to find an amicable ŌĆŗsolution to a dispute that the chambers said could lead to defaults and significant ŌĆŗlosses on multibillion-dollar investments in Vietnam's renewable energy sector.
Vietnam's industry ministry, which received the letter, did ŌĆīnot ŌüĀimmediately respond to a request for comment.
In recent years, the Southeast Asian country saw a boom in renewable energy investments driven by generous subsidised, or feed-in, tariffs under which the state committed to buying electricity for 20 years at above-market prices.
However, the high ŌĆŗtariffs increased losses for ŌĆŗstate-owned power utility ŌüĀEVN, the sole buyer of the generated electricity, and pushed up power prices for households and factories.
The retroactive change to tariffs, which ŌĆŗtook effect in January 2025, followed investigations into alleged abuses ŌĆŗin accessing ŌüĀthe preferential rates.
EVN had no immediate comment.
The letter follows several coordinated actions aimed at persuading Vietnam to change course. These included a document in May signed by 16 affected firms, ŌüĀamong them ŌĆŗprivate equity fund Dragon Capital, the Vietnamese subsidiary ŌĆŗof Philippines' ACEN ACEN.PS energy group, and investors from Thailand, Portugal, the Netherlands, South Korea, Singapore and China.

item.title: US sues California over zero emission vehicle, greenhouse gas rules
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Thursday sued the state ŌĆŗof California, claiming the state's zero-emission vehicle and tailpipe greenhouse gas emissions ŌĆīrules are illegal and preempted by federal law.
The U.S. Transportation Department sued the California Air Resources Board in U.S. District Court in California over vehicle rules that remain in place after President ŌĆŗDonald Trump signed legislation last year to overturn CaliforniaŌĆÖs Advanced Clean Cars ŌĆŗII rules that aim to phase out new gasoline-powered cars by 2035.
The ŌüĀlawsuit wants a ruling declaring that all zero-emission vehicle mandates by California are ŌĆŗunlawful and unenforceable.
"This litigation will help automakers design and produce cars and trucks to ŌĆŗmeet one federal fuel economy regulation," said Jonathan Morrison, who heads the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration.
California won approval in 2022 from the Environmental Protection Agency for its current vehicle ŌĆŗrules known as Advanced Clean Cars I, which the state says are still ŌĆŗin effect. The California Air Resources Board did not immediately comment.
This is the latest in a ŌĆīseries ŌüĀof efforts by the Trump administration to bar California from setting vehicle rules. In August, it sued California to stop the state from enforcing stringent emissions standards for heavy-duty trucks.
California's rules require automakers to sell a rising number of electric ŌĆŗvehicles and meet ŌĆŗincreasingly stringent limits ŌüĀon tailpipe emissions.
Those rules are much stricter than those imposed by the Trump administration, which plans to roll back federal fuel economy rules.
California contends ŌĆŗthe fuel savings for consumers from the rules far exceed ŌĆŗthe higher ŌüĀupfront costs of EVs.
Congress rescinded authority for California to outlaw traditional gas-powered vehicles after 2035 after Toyota and the Detroit Three lobbied Congress and the White House for significant relief ŌüĀfrom ŌĆŗCalifornia's emissions regulations.
The White House also significantly weakened ŌĆŗfederal tailpipe rules and Congress passed legislation to stop collecting penalties for not meeting vehicle tailpipe standards.
A group ŌĆŗrepresenting major automakers did not immediately comment.

item.title: Hungary returns seized Ukrainian bank vehicles, withholds cash and gold
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 12 (Reuters) - Hungary returned two seized armoured bank vehicles to Ukraine on Thursday but withheld cash and gold worth approximately $82 million, ŌĆŗciting an investigation into alleged money laundering, a move that ŌĆīKyiv has denounced as theft.
Relations between Hungary and Ukraine, already strained by Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban's close ties to Russia, reached a new low ŌĆŗlast week when Hungary detained seven Ukrainians transporting cash and gold .
Kyiv ŌĆŗaccused Budapest of taking the bank employees, engaged in ŌüĀa legitimate transfer, hostage in order to pressure Ukraine into restarting ŌĆŗsuspended oil shipments.
Those detained were expelled by Hungary and crossed into ŌĆŗUkraine on Friday.
Ukraine has demanded the return of the seized assets, with Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha on Sunday describing Hungary's actions as an "unprecedented act of state ŌĆŗbanditism and racketeering".
The detainees' lawyer, Lorant Horvath, told reporters on Thursday ŌĆŗthat the Ukrainian bank believed the money transport was "entirely lawful".
"Oschadbank will use all ŌĆīlegal ŌüĀmeans at its disposal to recover its assets," he said.
Hungary's parliament passed a law proposed by Orban's Fidesz party on Tuesday, allowing the tax authority to retain the cash and gold for 60 days while ŌĆŗit investigates the origin ŌĆŗand destination ŌüĀof the assets and evaluates any implications for Hungary's national security.
The tensions come as Orban faces a serious ŌĆŗelectoral challenge to his 16-year rule on April ŌĆŗ12. He ŌüĀhas made the war in Ukraine a central plank of his campaign , saying the centre-right opposition would drag Hungary into the conflict, which the ŌüĀopposition ŌĆŗhas repeatedly denied.
Orban has also vetoed new ŌĆŗEU sanctions on Moscow as well as a huge loan for Ukraine over the ŌĆŗoil dispute.

item.title: Gunman at Virginia university┬Āshot dead after injuring two
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - A ŌĆŗgunman opened fire on ŌĆīThursday morning at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, injuring two ŌĆŗpeople before being shot dead, ŌĆŗaccording to the university.
In an ŌüĀemergency alert, the university ŌĆŗsaid the two injured individuals were ŌĆŗtransported to hospital and classes and campus operations were cancelled for the ŌĆŗrest of the day.
The ŌĆŗuniversity's alert said that the gunman opened ŌĆīfire ŌüĀshortly before 10:49 a.m. in Constant Hall, the center of the university's college of business.
By ŌĆŗmidday ŌĆŗon Thursday, ŌüĀpolice vehicles with flashing lights had blocked ŌĆŗoff streets around the ŌĆŗNorfolk ŌüĀcampus. The university serves some 24,000 students, according to the ŌüĀschool's ŌĆŗwebsite.

item.title: FBI alert on possible Iran retaliation based on unverified tip, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - An FBI alert to law enforcement agencies last month warning of the possibility that Tehran ŌĆŗmight try to retaliate for any U.S. strikes ŌĆīon Iran by launching drone attacks in California was based on a single unverified tip, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said ŌĆŗon Thursday, adding that there has never been such ŌĆŗa threat to the U.S. from Iran.
The confidential alert , ŌüĀissued by the FBI through the multi-agency Los Angeles Joint ŌĆŗRegional Intelligence Center, surfaced publicly on Wednesday as the war ŌĆŗthat began on February 28 with massive U.S. and Israeli bombardments of Iran stretched on.
The alert cited FBI information that, as of early ŌĆŗFebruary, Iran "allegedly aspired to conduct a surprise attack using ŌĆŗunmanned aerial vehicles" launched from a sea vessel against targets in California "in ŌĆīthe ŌüĀevent that the U.S. conducted strikes against Iran."
Leavitt in a post on X on Thursday said that the alert was based on one email sent to local law enforcement ŌĆŗin California containing ŌĆŗa single, ŌüĀunverified tip.
"TO BE CLEAR: No such threat from Iran to our homeland exists, and it ŌĆŗnever did," Leavitt wrote.
ABC News first broke ŌĆŗnews of ŌüĀthe FBI security bulletin.
U.S. President Donald Trump has shrugged off the notion of Iran-backed attacks on the U.S. homeland.
Asked on ŌüĀWednesday ŌĆŗif he was worried that Iran ŌĆŗmay ramp up its retaliation to include strikes on U.S. soil, Trump told ŌĆŗreporters, "No, I'm not."

item.title: Israel drops charges against soldiers accused of abusing Gaza detainee
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - Israel's military on Thursday dropped charges against five soldiers accused of torturing ŌĆŗa Palestinian detained during the Gaza war , in a case that exposed divisions within Israel ŌĆīover whether soldiers can abuse enemy prisoners with impunity.
The military announced the decision at a time when much of the country's attention is focused on the war with Iran.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu praised the decision. "The State of Israel must hunt ŌĆŗdown its enemies, not its own heroic fighters," he said in a statement.
The case gained ŌĆŗinternational attention after right-wing protesters, including members of Netanyahu's cabinet, stormed military compounds in ŌüĀprotest of the probe against the soldiers.
It drew further attention when the military's chief legal officer leaked ŌĆŗvideo of the alleged abuse to local media.
The advocate general, Yifat Tomer-Yerushalmi, resigned in October and was later ŌĆŗarrested over the leak, an action she said she took to fend off propaganda against the military's legal department entrusted with upholding the rule of law.
Her successor, Major General Itai Ofir, was withdrawing indictments against the soldiers in part due to "exceptional ŌĆŗcircumstances that negatively affected the ability to prosecute the case while also preserving the right for a ŌĆŗfair trial of the defendants in the case," the military said in a statement.
Those circumstances include the fact that the ŌĆīPalestinian ŌüĀsubjected to alleged abuse "is currently present in the Gaza Strip and the implications this has on the evidentiary basis," the statement said.
The leaked security camera footage from the Sde Teiman military detention camp for Palestinians arrested during the Gaza war shows soldiers taking a prisoner aside and crowding around while holding a dog and blocking ŌĆŗvisibility of their actions with ŌĆŗtheir riot gear.
The soldiers ŌüĀfaced charges relating to causing severe abuse and injury. The indictment against them said that one of the soldiers stabbed the detainee with a sharp object, ŌĆŗcausing a tear near his rectum.
Neither the Palestinian detainee, who was released back ŌĆŗto Gaza as ŌüĀpart of the Israel-Hamas ceasefire deal in October, nor the Israeli soldiers involved have been identified by name.
There was no immediate comment from Palestinian leaders on the decision.
The Association for Civil Rights in Israel (ACRI) previously filed a ŌüĀpetition ŌĆŗfor the closure of Sde Teiman over alleged abuse of Palestinian ŌĆŗdetainees.
The Israeli military began phasing down use of the facility in June 2024, although accusations from rights groups and former Palestinian detainees of torture ŌĆŗand abuse in Israeli detention facilities remain widespread.

item.title: US has arranged nearly 50 charter flights from Middle East amid Iran strikes, State Dept says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Trump administration has organized nearly 50 flights to return U.S. citizens from the Middle East since the U.S.-Israeli war ŌĆŗwith Iran began, a State Department official said on Thursday, and ŌĆīofficials said demand for the flights has declined.
The State Department was criticized in the days after the conflict began on February 28 for its late preparations to relocate diplomats and ŌĆŗoffer assistance to U.S. citizens as Iran's strikes led to ŌĆŗairspace closures and flight cancellations across the region.
"By the end of ŌüĀtoday, we'll have completed approximately four dozen flights and safely evacuated ŌĆŗthousands of Americans from the Middle East on those flights," said the official, ŌĆŗwho briefed reporters on condition of anonymity.
The department, which waived the cost of government-assisted flights, has declined to give detailed numbers for how many people benefited from them. ŌĆŗThe official said that, as of Thursday, a total of 47,000 U.S. ŌĆŗcitizens had returned from the region to the United States since February 28, mostly ŌĆīon ŌüĀcommercial flights, and that the State Department had provided guidance or assistance to thousands of Americans.
"Through the department's Task Force, we have directly provided security guidance and travel assistance to about 32,000 impacted Americans," the official said. "Most ŌĆŗAmericans who have ŌĆŗrequested assistance have ŌüĀdeclined seats when offered, opting to remain in country or take commercial flight options, which offer greater flexibility in ŌĆŗterms of destination and luggage," the official said.
The department ŌĆŗsaid on ŌüĀWednesday it would scale down charter flights and ground transport options because commercial flight availability is improving.
U.S. facilities in the region have continued to be ŌüĀhit ŌĆŗby Iran. A drone struck a major diplomatic facility ŌĆŗin Iraq on Tuesday, amid U.S. warnings that Iran-backed militias in the country may target ŌĆŗAmericans and U.S. interests.

item.title: Romania signs deal with Ukraine on joint defence production including drones

item.title: US and allies clash with Russia and China over Iran nuclear program
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Western allies clashed with Russia and China on Thursday ŌĆŗover Iran's nuclear intentions, as Washington sought at the United Nations to further justify the war it launched ŌĆīon Iran two weeks ago.
At a meeting of the 15-member U.N. Security Council, which is chaired this month by the U.S., Russia and China moved unsuccessfully to block a discussion about a committee established to oversee and enforce U.N. sanctions on Iran. They were overruled 11-2 with two abstentions.
Addressing ŌĆŗthe council, U.S. envoy to the United Nations Mike Waltz accused Moscow and Beijing of seeking to ŌĆŗprotect Tehran by blocking the work of the so-called 1737 Committee.
"All member states of the ŌüĀUnited Nations should be implementing an arms embargo against Iran, banning the transfer and trade of missile technology, and ŌĆŗfreezing relevant financial assets," Waltz said.
"The U.N. provisions to be re-imposed are not arbitrary, but instead, narrowly scoped to ŌĆŗaddress the threat posed by Iran's nuclear, missile and conventional arms programs and Iran's ongoing support for terrorism," he said.
Waltz charged that both China and Russia did not want a functional sanctions committee "because they want to protect their partner, Iran, and continue to maintain defense ŌĆŗcooperation that is now once again prohibited."
Waltz noted that last week the U.N.'s International Atomic Energy Agency had reiterated ŌĆŗthat Iran was the only state in the world without nuclear weapons to have produced and accumulated uranium enriched up to ŌĆī60 percent, ŌüĀand had refused to provide the IAEA access to this stockpile.
Russia's U.N. ambassador Vasily Nebenzya charged that the U.S. and its allies had "whipped up hysteria surrounding supposed plans Iran had to get a nuclear weapon" that were never corroborated by IAEA reports.
"This was done in order to undertake yet another military venture against Tehran and to ensure great ŌĆŗescalation of the situation in ŌĆŗthe Middle East and beyond," ŌüĀhe said.
China's representative, Fu Cong, called Washington the "instigator" of the Iranian nuclear crisis and said it had "resorted to blatant use of force against Iran during the negotiation process, which ŌĆŗrendered the diplomatic efforts futile."
U.S. President Donald Trump has used Iran's nuclear program to justify ŌĆŗhis war ŌüĀon Iran. He said this month that Iran would have had a nuclear weapon within two weeks had the U.S. not struck three key nuclear sites in June, a claim sources have said was not supported by U.S. intelligence assessments.
Britain and France ŌüĀtold the ŌĆŗSecurity Council that re-imposing sanctions on Iran was justified by Tehran's ŌĆŗfailure to address concerns about its nuclear program. France said the IAEA was no longer able to guarantee the peaceful nature of the program ŌĆŗand Tehran's nuclear stockpile was sufficient for 10 nuclear devices.

item.title: Israeli military says it struck Basij checkpoints in Tehran

The Basij militia is a part-time paramilitary force under the control of the ŌĆŗIslamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) that is often used ŌĆŗto quell protests inside Iran.

Both allies have called on Iranians to rise up and topple ŌĆŗtheir clerical rulers. Many Iranians want change ŌĆŗand ŌüĀsome openly celebrated the death of Khamenei, after his forces had killed thousands of anti-government protesters in January.
But there ŌüĀhas ŌĆŗbeen no sign of organised dissent ŌĆŗwhile the country is under attack, and no sign of Iran's ŌĆŗrulers relinquishing power.

item.title: Senegal's PM says contract for BP-operated GTA gas project is unfair

item.title: Netanyahu threatens Iran's new supreme leader, defends joint assault with US

JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu ŌĆŗon Thursday issued a veiled threat to kill ŌĆīIran's new supreme leader as he sought to use his first press conference since the start of the war to defend ŌĆŗhis joint military assault with the U.S. against ŌĆŗIran.
Netanyahu said that Iran was "no longer the same" after ŌüĀnearly two weeks of U.S.-Israeli air bombardment and ŌĆŗthat Tehran had suffered blows to its elite Revolutionary Guards ŌĆŗCorps and Basij paramilitary force.
Standing between two Israeli flags and taking questions via video link, Netanyahu vowed to keep hitting Lebanon's Hezbollah ŌĆŗafter the Iran-backed group opened fire on March 2 ŌĆŗto avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader at the start ŌĆīof ŌüĀthe war.
Asked about what actions Israel might take against Iran's new Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei and Hezbollah chief Naim Qassem, Netanyahu said:
"I wouldnŌĆÖt issue life insurance policies on any ŌĆŗof the leaders ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀterrorist organization ... I don't intend to give an exact message here about what we ŌĆŗare planning or what we are going ŌĆŗto do."
The ŌüĀIsraeli leader said that Iran and Hezbollah no longer pose the same threats that they once did.

item.title: US Department of Energy to invest $1.9 billion for power grid upgrades

March 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. Department of Energy will invest ŌĆŗabout $1.9 billion to accelerate upgrades of ŌĆīpower grids in the country, it said on Thursday.
U.S. power consumption is set to rise ŌĆŗsharply this year and the ŌĆŗnext, driven by rapid growth in AI ŌüĀand cryptocurrency data centers and ŌĆŗincreased electrification of heating and transport. However, ŌĆŗthat demand has also led to higher electricity prices for a large swath of the ŌĆŗcountry.
The DOE said the funding would ŌĆŗbe used to prepare for the rise in ŌĆīdemand ŌüĀand to lower electricity costs for American households and businesses.
The selected projects for the funding will need to showcase ŌĆŗhow reconductoring ŌĆŗor ŌüĀreplacing existing power lines with higher-capacity conductors, combined with transmission ŌĆŗtechnologies, can bolster the nation's ŌĆŗelectric ŌüĀgrid.

item.title: TotalEnergies output down 15% due to US-Iran war; confirms UAE outages

item.title: Iran's Shi'ite allies step up strikes despite weakened hand
item.description:
BEIRUT/BAGHDAD/JERUSALEM, March 12 (Reuters) - Shi'ite Muslim armed groups in Lebanon and Iraq are stepping up their role in the war with the U.S. and Israel, showing the Iran-backed "Axis of Resistance" can still wage attacks despite damage inflicted on the alliance during the Gaza conflict.
Groups that have long been armed and financed by Iran and loyal ŌĆŗto its Shi'ite Islamist rulers are now helping Tehran intensify the war around the region, strikes in recent days show.
Hezbollah and Iran's Revolutionary Guards on Wednesday launched their first-ever simultaneous rocket barrage on Israel, with the ŌĆīLebanese group firing 200 missiles. Israel reported that only two of these hit its territory.
Iraqi Shi'ite militants have also picked up the pace of drone and missile attacks on U.S. interests in Iraq in the last 3-4 days, according to three Iraqi security sources and two sources close to the groups.
One group yet to enter the fray are Tehran's Houthi allies in Yemen, heavily armed and capable of disrupting maritime navigation around the Arabian peninsula, as shown during the Gaza war when they fired at Red Sea shipping and Israel. Houthi attacks could further disrupt oil markets because Saudi Arabia diverted exports ŌĆŗto the Red Sea after Iran shut the Hormuz straits.
Last week, Houthi leader Abdul Malik al-Houthi said the group had its "fingers on the trigger" and was ready to act militarily when developments warrant it.
The alliance that Tehran calls the Axis of ŌĆŗResistance suffered major blows after Hamas - one of its key members - attacked Israel on October 7, 2023, igniting a war that decimated the Palestinian group and pummelled Hezbollah, with Israel killing the ŌüĀLebanese group's leader Hassan Nasrallah. The ripple effects helped topple Bashar al-Assad in Syria, knocking away a pillar of the Axis.
"Iran built the Axis for a moment like this," said Mohanad Hage Ali of the Carnegie Middle East Center think-tank, describing it as an "existential ŌĆŗwar" for Iran and Hezbollah, which joined the fight even though its military power remains well below levels seen in 2023.
"If the Iranian regime is destroyed, there would be nothing left" of the Axis, he said.
Israeli military spokesman Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani said the ŌĆŗHezbollah attack on Wednesday comprised 200 rockets and 20 drones.
"There's no contradiction between the fact that we heavily, heavily diminished Hezbollah in the last three years, and the fact that they are still a relevant, dangerous force," he told reporters on Thursday.
Washington has long demanded that Iran end its support for the proxies. The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei thanked "the fighters of the Resistance Front", according to a statement issued on Thursday and read out by a state TV announcer.
"We consider the countries of the Resistance ŌĆŗFront as our best friends," he said in the statement, the first issued in his name since he was named as leader on Sunday.
Hezbollah, founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, entered the Iran war on March 2, saying it aimed to avenge the ŌĆŗkilling of Mojtaba's father, former Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed on the first day of the war.
Israel has retaliated with a new offensive against the group in Lebanon, just 15 months after the last one, killing more than 600 people, forcing more than 800,000 from their homes.
Hezbollah's ŌĆīrocket barrage on ŌüĀWednesday night - its heaviest during this war - was launched at the same time as Iran fired ballistic missiles at Israel, according to the Israeli military and two Lebanese sources familiar with Hezbollah operations.
The Lebanese sources said the coordinated strikes were part of Iran's plan in the event of a major war, aiming to confuse Israeli air defense systems.
Despite the volleys of rockets, Hezbollah's attacks have caused relatively little damage so far. Two Israeli soldiers have been killed in Lebanon.
No fatalities have been reported in Israel as a result of Hezbollah rocket attacks.
Hezbollah played a major part in Iran's regional strategy under the leadership of Nasrallah, the secretary general killed in 2024, backing Shi'ite factions in Iraq, Hamas, and the Houthis.
In Iraq, not all the IranŌĆæbacked armed groups appear to support attacks ŌĆŗon U.S. interests. Reuters reported last week that many of the fighters ŌĆŗand militia groups the Iranians cultivated in Iraq had ŌüĀnot entered the fight.
But analysts and officials say a core group of TehranŌĆæaligned factions remains active and capable of exerting pressure.
Operating under the umbrella of the Islamic Resistance in Iraq, these groups said on Thursday they'd carried out 31 attacks over the past 24 hours using dozens of drones and rockets against what they described as ŌĆ£occupation basesŌĆØ in Iraq and the region
Security officials say the militias have also sought ŌĆŗto extend pressure to energy projects and oilfields in southern Iraq, where several U.S. companies and U.S.ŌĆælinked service firms operate alongside international partners.
Among the claimed attacks, two security sources said two ŌĆŗdrones hit the southern Majnoon oilfield ŌüĀon Wednesday, where U.S.-based KBR is the operator. No casualties were reported. The attack was corroborated by a field engineer who said there had been five such strikes in less than a week.
On Tuesday a U.S. diplomatic facility near Baghdad International Airport was struck by a drone, according to the U.S. State Department, which said there were no injuries and everyone was accounted for.
Four security sources told Reuters the same site has come under repeated attack and was also hit on Wednesday.
Separately, two drones targeted a U.S. military base near Erbil airport in Iraqi Kurdistan on Wednesday, ŌüĀthree Kurdish security ŌĆŗsources said.
In northern Iraq, a drone attack struck an oilfield operated by U.S. firm HKN Energy in IraqŌĆÖs Kurdistan region on March 5, causing a fire ŌĆŗand halting production, two security sources and an oilfield engineer said. The sources said the drones belonged to Iran-backed militias and came from an area they controlled.
Reuters could not independently verify who was behind the attacks.
Andreas Krieg, a lecturer at King's College London's security studies department, said that while the Axis of Resistance had ŌĆŗbeen degraded since 2023, Hezbollah, Iraqi Shi'ite militias, and the Houthis were "very much operational".
"They still retain capabilities, they still show very strong intent, and they remain well resourced," he said.

item.title: Canada, Mexico say trilateral deal is key ahead of talks to review USMCA

MEXICO CITY, March 12 (Reuters) - Canadian and Mexican officials on Thursday reaffirmed the importance of ŌĆŗmaintaining the trilateral free trade agreement between their ŌĆīcountries and the U.S., amid signals from Washington it could be interested in bilateral deals.
Canada's ambassador to Mexico Cameron MacKay ŌĆŗand Mexican deputy trade secretary Luis Rosendo Gutierrez ŌĆŗboth told a conference in Mexico City that ŌüĀmaintaining the trilateral U.S.-Mexico-Canada agreement was a priority for ŌĆŗtheir nations.
The meetings follow an announcement by U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump's administration on Wednesday of two new trade investigations into alleged forced labor and industrial overcapacity among 16 major trade partners.
The move ŌĆŗseeks to restore tariff pressure after a Supreme ŌĆŗCourt ruling last month struck down significant portions of the administration's ŌĆītrade ŌüĀprogram.
Mexico is among the countries that could be targeted by the investigation into excess capacity under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, a legal provision that ŌĆŗallows the ŌĆŗU.S. to investigate ŌüĀforeign trade practices deemed unfair.
However, Mexican Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard downplayed the potential ŌĆŗfallout, saying the bulk of Mexican trade falls ŌĆŗunder ŌüĀthe USMCA pact and is not subject to Section 301.
The U.S. did not list Canada, the United States' second-largest ŌüĀU.S. ŌĆŗtrade partner after Mexico, as ŌĆŗamong the countries that could be subject to investigation.

item.title: UN mission says Venezuela's repressive apparatus persists after Maduro ouster
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - A United Nations fact-finding mission said on Thursday that Venezuela's "repressive state" remains fully operational following the removal of former President Nicolas Maduro by U.S. forces in early ŌĆŗJanuary.
The mission told the U.N. Human Rights Council in a report ŌĆīthat "institutional structures facilitating human rights violations have not been dismantled", adding that 87 politically motivated detentions had taken place since Maduro's ouster.
Venezuela's government has long denied accusations of human rights abuses and ŌĆŗthe existence of political prisoners, saying those jailed committed common crimes.
The Venezuelan communications ŌĆŗministry, which handles all press queries for the government, did not ŌüĀimmediately respond to a request for comment.
The report said senior government and military ŌĆŗofficials previously identified as responsible for crimes against humanity continue to hold significant power.
Washington ŌĆŗhas long criticized Venezuela's democratic record, accusing its socialist government of electoral fraud and political repression.
The U.S. announced it would "take control" of Venezuela but has not pushed for new elections, touting good relations ŌĆŗwith Maduro's vice president Delcy Rodriguez - who has taken over as interim president ŌĆŗand has backed major changes to oil and mining regulations, meant to stoke investment.
Rodriguez's government also passed ŌĆīan amnesty ŌüĀlaw as part of an agreement to free thousands of prisoners. Her administration said in late February that nearly 2,200 people have been released under this law, but local NGOs have criticized a lack of transparency and estimated that the number of freed ŌĆŗprisoners is much ŌĆŗlower.
Alfredo Romero, the director ŌüĀof legal rights NGO Foro Penal, said at a press conference on Thursday that "there is still a repressive system, a justice ŌĆŗsystem that is used to persecute."
The U.N. mission called for ŌĆŗthe immediate ŌüĀrelease of all remaining political detainees and unhindered access to the country to continue its investigations. It did not provide an estimate for the number of people still in ŌüĀprison.
"Venezuela cannot ŌĆŗbe said to truly be on the road ŌĆŗto human rights reform unless and until that repressive apparatus is dismantled," said lawyer Maria Eloisa Quintero, a ŌĆŗrepresentative of the mission.

item.title: Exchanges oppose potential US Treasury intervention in oil futures market
item.description:
BOCA RATON, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - The heads of a number of top exchanges, including CME Group (CME.O) , opens new tab and Toronto Stock Exchange parent TMX Group (X.TO) , opens new tab , oppose any potential ŌĆŗintervention from the U.S. government involving the oil futures market, amid ŌĆīrising energy prices in the aftermath of the Iran conflict.
The latest comments come as reports have emerged that the U.S. Treasury is weighing potential measures involving oil futures to combat rising prices. On ŌĆŗWednesday, the U.S. government announced it would release 172 million barrels of oil ŌĆŗfrom its strategic petroleum reserve to reduce oil prices that have ŌüĀsurged due to supply disruptions from the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"Markets do not like ŌĆŗit when governments intervene on oil prices," said Terry Duffy, Chief Executive Officer of ŌĆŗCME, during a panel discussion earlier this week. The CME, which is the world's largest derivatives exchange, is among a group of U.S. exchanges that trade energy futures.
The White House and the ŌĆŗU.S. Treasury Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Another CEO of ŌĆŗa leading exchange, who requested anonymity to discuss the matter candidly, echoed similar sentiments, saying that ŌĆīan intervention ŌüĀfrom the U.S. Treasury risked aggravating the problem, as it could raise the risk of hefty losses for the government if energy prices continue to rise.
Oil prices jumped nearly 5% on Wednesday as fresh attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz further ŌĆŗaggravated supply shock fears. ŌĆŗSeveral analysts said ŌüĀthe International Energy Agency's proposal for a record release of oil reserves is inadequate to quell those concerns. The IEA the ŌĆŗrelease of 400 million barrels of oil to try to ŌĆŗcombat a ŌüĀsurge in energy prices, which are now up more than 25% since the war broke out.
"I usually I find those things (potential government intervention in markets) lead to unintended consequences. ŌüĀYou create ŌĆŗa different problem by trying to solve the ŌĆŗfirst problem. The market will sort this out itself," said John McKenzie, CEO of TMX Group.

item.title: Iran not going to close Strait of Hormuz, Iran UN envoy says

March 12 (Reuters) - Iran's U.N. Ambassador said on Thursday Tehran was not going to close ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz, but added that it ŌĆīwas Iran's right to preserve the security of the key shipping route.

In a prepared statement he read to ŌĆŗreporters before responding to questions, Iravani said that "Iran fully ŌĆŗrespects and remains committed to the principle of freedom of navigation under ŌĆīthe ŌüĀlaw of the sea.
"However, the current situation in the region, including in the Strait of Hormuz, is not the result of Iran's lawful exercise of its right of self-defense.

He said he had no response ŌĆŗto ŌüĀa comment by U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who told Sky News in an interview on Thursday that the U.S. ŌüĀNavy, ŌĆŗperhaps with an international coalition, would escort ŌĆŗvessels through the Strait of Hormuz when it is militarily possible.

item.title: Colombia's Petro, Trump spoke on phone, Trump said Petro welcome in U.S.
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 12 (Reuters) - Colombia's President Gustavo Petro and his U.S. counterpart President Donald Trump spoke on the phone on Thursday, Petro's office said on ŌĆŗX, ahead of Petro's meeting with Venezuela's acting President Delcy Rodriguez.
The two ŌĆīmen discussed the economy along the Colombia-Venezuela border, which is expected to be the top topic at the Petro-Rodriguez meeting on Friday, the statement said, and Trump said Petro, who he ŌĆŗsanctioned last year, is welcome in the U.S. and apologized for not ŌĆŗinviting the Colombian leader to a meeting in Miami last week with ŌüĀother Latin American heads of state.
Petro and Trump have had repeated squabbles, though ŌĆŗthe two men were each positive after a face-to-face meeting in Washington last month.
Trump has ŌĆŗrepeatedly demanded more cooperation from Colombia in the fight against drug trafficking and has accused Petro, without evidence, of being an "". Petro meanwhile, claims record drug seizures during his tenure ŌĆŗand has said deadly strikes on alleged drug boats amount to war crimes.
"During ŌĆŗthe call, the two leaders discussed energy issues, hydrocarbons, security, illicit crops, eradication efforts and the ŌĆījoint ŌüĀfight against drug trafficking. They also talked about economic reactivation along the border and other matters of bilateral interest," the statement said.
"In the course of the conversation, the Colombian president extended an invitation to his U.S. counterpart to visit Cartagena, which ŌĆŗwas warmly received. President ŌĆŗTrump, in turn, ŌüĀreiterated that President Petro will always be welcome in the United States and expressed apologies for any previous inconvenience related to ŌĆŗan invitation to Miami," the statement said, adding Trump wished ŌĆŗPetro success ŌüĀin his meeting with Rodriguez.
The Rodriguez-Petro bilateral is Rodriguez's first presidential-level in-person meeting since she took power following her predecessor's ouster by the United States. Trump has supported a ŌüĀseries ŌĆŗof moves by Rodriguez, formerly vice president, to ŌĆŗattract investors in oil and mining and stabilize the country since the January raid that captured President Nicolas Maduro, with ŌĆŗTrump repeatedly praising Rodriguez for her cooperation with the U.S.

item.title: Pakistan's PM Sharif discussed regional developments with Saudi crown prince

item.title: Drone attack wounds at least six French soldiers at base in IraqŌĆÖs Makhmour, Erbil governor says

item.title: Brazil's top diplomat raises concerns over Trump aide's planned visit to Bolsonaro

item.title: Ships must coordinate with Iran's navy to pass through Strait of Hormuz, foreign ministry says
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - Ships must coordinate with Iran's navy to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, the country's ŌĆŗforeign ministry spokesperson said in comments carried by ŌĆīMehr news agency on Thursday.
Iran will fight on and keep the Strait of Hormuz shut as leverage against the United States and ŌĆŗIsrael, new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei said on ŌĆŗThursday in defiant first comments attributed to him since he ŌüĀsucceeded his slain father.
"The security of the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz is of vital importance to Iran, because the countryŌĆÖs ŌĆŗsecurity is tied to the security of the region. With the longest coastlines on the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman, ŌĆŗIran has always borne costs to protect this strategic ŌĆŗwaterway," said Esmaeil Baghaei, the spokesperson.
Khamenei also called on Iran's neighbours to ŌĆīshut ŌüĀU.S. bases on their territory and warned that Iran would continue to target them .
"The insecurity created in the region by the United States and the Zionist regime can affect the movement of ŌĆŗships. However, Iran ŌĆŗdoes not ŌüĀwant this strait to become insecure, and ships must coordinate with the Iranian navy ŌĆŗwhen passing through so that maritime security is ŌĆŗmaintained," ŌüĀBaghaei added.
The prospect that one of the most severe disruptions ever to hit global energy supplies could drag on sent oil prices ŌüĀsurging ŌĆŗback above $100 a barrel, after falling ŌĆŗearlier in the week on hopes of a swift end to the conflict.

item.title: Polish president vetoes EU defence loan bill
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Poland's president said on Thursday that he would not sign legislation creating a mechanism to spend 43.7 billion euros ($50.30 billion) in European Union loans to boost the ŌĆŗmilitary, triggering condemnation from the government.
The clash between nationalist President Karol Nawrocki and ŌĆīthe pro-EU government of Prime Minister Donald Tusk over the 150 billion-euro Security Action for Europe initiative is the latest example of the deep divisions complicating policymaking at Poland's highest levels of state.
"The SAFE mechanism ŌĆŗis a massive foreign loan taken out for 45 years in a foreign ŌĆŗcurrency, with interest costs that could reach up to 180 billion zlotys," ŌüĀNawrocki said in a televised address to the nation.
"Poles will therefore have to repay twice ŌĆŗthe value of the loan, while Western banks and financial institutions will profit from it."
He ŌĆŗadded that Brussels could halt the payout of funds arbitrarily through deploying conditionality rules, thus limiting Poland's sovereignty, and questioned its constitutionality.
"Poland's security cannot depend on foreign decisions," he said.
Warsaw was the biggest beneficiary of ŌĆŗSAFE, but the nationalist opposition party Law and Justice labelled it a German plot to ŌĆŗmeddle in Polish affairs that would saddle the country with debt and limit its flexibility regarding arms ŌĆīpurchases.
Tusk's ŌüĀgovernment says the cheap financing provided by SAFE is essential to Poland's security in the face of what it views as a rising threat from Russia.
"The President has lost the chance to act like a patriot. Shame!," Tusk wrote on X, adding that the government would ŌĆŗpresent its response at ŌĆŗan extraordinary council ŌüĀof ministers on Friday morning.
Nawrocki and Central Bank Governor Adam Glapinski have proposed an alternative that would involve using unrealised profits from the ŌĆŗincreasing value of the National Bank of Poland's gold reserves to ŌĆŗfinance military ŌüĀspending.
In his address, Nawrocki called on the government to cooperate on the alternative, national proposal.
However, Glapinski says the government has expressed " zero interest " in the proposal. The bill proposed by Nawrocki is unlikely ŌüĀto ŌĆŗbe approved by parliament, where Tusk's coalition has a ŌĆŗmajority.
The government has previously said it is preparing a "plan B" that would still allow Poland to access SAFE funds, ŌĆŗwithout giving further details.
($1 = 0.8687 euros)

item.title: TRADING DAY The destructive power of $100 oil
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - Global stocks fell on Thursday, slammed by a 10% spike in oil prices, spiking bond yields and a stronger dollar , all of which point to a deteriorating outlook for consumers, businesses and economic growth.
In my column today I argue that, although the "Trump always chickens out" strategy of buying beaten-down ŌĆŗstocks on the assumption that the U.S. president backs down from his more extreme policies, the Middle East war may be a "TACO" too ŌĆīfar .
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* 2026 Fed rate cut bets evaporate
And just like that, it was gone. Not so long ago - i.e., only a few weeks ago, before the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran - many analysts were predicting three interest rate ŌĆŗcuts from the Fed this year. As of Thursday, not even one U.S. rate cut in 2026 is fully priced in at all.
Traders have made it ŌĆŗclear where they think the stagflation risks lie with oil at $100 a barrel, and the 'transitory' lessons of 2021-22 are weighing heavily too. No rate hikes are priced in yet though, ŌĆīand next ŌüĀweek would be far too soon. Right? Let's see what tomorrow's PCE inflation figures hold.
* Global bond rout
Zoom right out the curve, and geographically, and inflation fears really are intensifying, as the accelerating global bond market selloff shows. Investors are fleeing fixed income everywhere.
On Thursday, the two-year U.S. yield hit its highest since August, and the 2s/10s curve flattened the most since April; Germany's 10-year yield is near 3% and the highest since October 2023, and UK yields are up 60 ŌĆŗbps in two weeks.
* Central bank ŌĆŗbonanza
Central bankers are in an unenviable ŌüĀposition, and could be forgiven for just wanting to close their eyes, sit on their hands and wish the unfolding crisis away. But many of them are under the spotlight next week in what will be one of the busiest ŌĆŗweeks of central bank meetings in a long time.
Here's a rundown of who's meeting next week: the central banks ŌĆŗof Australia , Canada, Brazil, Japan , Sweden, ŌüĀSwitzerland, the euro zone , the UK and, of course, the Fed. The most likely to hike rates is the RBA, then possibly the BOJ, with the rest on hold. But if oil's at, say, $120 or higher, you never know.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want ŌĆŗto receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust ŌĆŗPrinciples , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US carrying out rescue effort after losing aircraft in Iraq, US military says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - A U.S. military refueling aircraft crashed in western Iraq on ŌĆŗThursday, in an incident U.S. Central ŌĆīCommand said involved another aircraft but was not the result of hostile or ŌĆŗfriendly fire.

Since ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀU.S. and Israel started carrying out strikes against Iran on ŌĆŗFebruary 28, seven U.S. troops ŌĆŗhave ŌüĀbeen killed.

item.title: North Korea says Japan's missile expansion is raising regional security risks
item.description:
SEOUL, March 13 (Reuters) - North Korea accused Japan of ŌĆŗheightening regional security risks by accelerating ŌĆīthe deployment and development of long-range missiles, saying Tokyo's military buildup amounted to preparations for a ŌĆŗfuture attack, state media KCNA said ŌĆŗon Friday.
KCNA said in a commentary that ŌüĀJapan had begun deploying an upgraded ŌĆŗversion of its Type 12 surface-to-ship missile in ŌĆŗKumamoto and planned to position other long-range systems across multiple prefectures, putting neighbouring countries within range.
The commentary ŌĆŗsaid Japan was pouring resources into ŌĆŗextending missile ranges and adapting them for launches from ŌĆīland, ŌüĀsea and air, calling the systems "clearly long-range offensive weapons."
KCNA dismissed Japan's claim that the buildup was for self-defence, saying it reflected ŌĆŗambitions to secure ŌĆŗattack ŌüĀcapabilities and was part of "war preparations for re-invasion."
It added Japan's expanded ŌĆŗpurchases of long-range strike weapons and ŌĆŗparticipation ŌüĀin joint military drills showed the country was moving closer to using those capabilities ŌüĀand warned ŌĆŗTokyo risked serious consequences ŌĆŗif it continued on its current path.

item.title: Canada to boost Arctic defenses, says it can no longer rely on others
item.description:
YELLOWKNIFE, Northwest Territories, March 12 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Mark Carney unveiled a C$35 billion ($25.7 ŌĆībillion) plan on Thursday to boost Canada's defenses in the giant Arctic region as it tries to decrease its reliance on the United States.
Canada has traditionally relied on U.S. help to monitor the Canadian Arctic, which covers 4.4 million square km (1.7 million square miles) of land and ŌĆŗsea - larger than India - and is almost completely uninhabited. President Donald Trump's tariffs and musings about annexing Canada ŌĆŗhave put ties under strain.
"We will no longer depend on any one nation, and instead build ŌüĀa stronger, more independent country. With this new plan, Canada is taking full responsibility for defending our Arctic sovereignty," Carney ŌĆŗsaid.
Even before Trump re-entered the White House last year, Canada had long been under pressure from the United States to ŌĆŗincrease defense spending and vowed last June to boost funding for the armed forces. It is promising to hit NATO's 2% military spending target five years earlier than planned.
In January, Carney said the United States and other major nations were eroding the traditional rules-based order that had long ŌĆŗbenefited Canada.
"The assumptions that shaped decades of Canadian defense and security are being upended," he said on Thursday.
"Climate change is ŌĆŗcausing our Arctic region to warm nearly three times faster than the global average, a shift that great powers are actively looking to ŌĆīexploit," Carney ŌüĀsaid in a speech in Yellowknife, capital of the Northwest Territories and home to Canada's Arctic military command.
The plan laid out specifics of how previously announced funding for the Arctic would be spent. In 2022, Ottawa announced a C$38.6 billion plan to modernize its defenses and the joint North American Aerospace Defense Command that Canada operates with the United States.
Canada has four ŌĆŗrudimentary Arctic airfields that can ŌĆŗaccommodate six fighters each, ŌüĀand around 2,000 soldiers dotted around the region.
Carney's plan calls for investing C$32 billion to expand the military airfields in the region and to build four operational support hubs.
The plan would ŌĆŗalso upgrade two commercial airports and fast-track two proposed roads from the Arctic to Canada's ŌĆŗsouthern regions.
Trump has ŌüĀexpressed keen interest in the Arctic and its mineral potential. In addition to commenting about annexing Canada, he has insisted that the U.S. needs Greenland to fend off threats from Russian and Chinese interests in the region.
Canada's Arctic region comprises approximately 25% of ŌüĀthe global ŌĆŗArctic. Although the region is rich in rare minerals, it has very ŌĆŗlittle infrastructure which, combined with the extreme cold, makes mining operations extremely complex and costly.
Later on Thursday Carney is due to fly to northern Norway ŌĆŗto observe biennial NATO drills .
($1 = 1.3620 Canadian dollars)

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards second stateless ship in a week

STOCKHOLM, March 12 (Reuters) - The Swedish coast guard said on Thursday it boarded ŌĆŗand took control of a suspected ŌĆīstateless vessel in Swedish waters of the Baltic Sea, in the second such incident in ŌĆŗless than a week.

item.title: Six French soldiers wounded in Iraq after drone attack, army says
item.description:
BAGHDAD/PARIS, March 12 (Reuters) - Six French soldiers providing counter-terrorism training in northern Iraq were wounded after a drone attack in the Erbil region of Iraq, ŌĆŗFrance's army said on Thursday, just hours after an Italian base was ŌĆīalso targeted in the area.

"Today, six French soldiers providing counter-terrorism training to Iraqi partners were wounded after a drone attack in the Erbil ŌĆŗregion of Iraq," the French army said in a statement, adding that ŌĆŗthey had been taken to the nearest medical facility.

An overnight airstrike that hit an Italian ŌĆŗmilitary base in Iraqi Kurdistan was deliberate, the Italian ŌĆŗdefence ministry ŌüĀsaid earlier on Thursday, targeting a facility hosting NATO personnel.
France is deploying about a dozen naval vessels, including its aircraft carrier strike group, ŌüĀto the ŌĆŗMediterranean, Red Sea and potentially the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz as part of defensive support to allies threatened by the conflict in the ŌĆŗMiddle East.

item.title: Trump nominates State Dept official to lead diminished US global media agency
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump nominated a senior State Department official to take on the ŌĆīadditional role of leading the U.S. Agency for Global Media, the department said on Thursday, after a federal judge invalidated the previous Trump-appointed CEO's staff cuts at the agency.
Under Secretary of State for Public Diplomacy Sarah Rogers, who ŌĆŗhas led the administration's campaign against European Internet regulations that Washington says constitute censorship , was named as ŌĆŗTrump's pick to take over the agency that oversees U.S.-funded media outlets like ŌüĀVoice of America that are aimed at populations in oppressive countries and reach hundreds of ŌĆŗmillions of people.

They had argued that Lake's ŌĆŗappointment as acting CEO and actions she took in that role ran ŌĆŗafoul of ŌüĀthe Federal Vacancies Reform Act and the Constitution's Appointments Clause, dealing another blow to the Trump administration's attempts to diminish various government agencies.
Lake, in a post on X, said what she called her efforts at "right-sizing" ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗagency were "so effective over the last year that the Deep ŌĆŗState has done everything they can to stop us, including launching malicious lawsuits at me and the agency."

item.title: Venezuela signs deal with Repsol for gas production

March 12 (Reuters) - Venezuela and Spanish energy group Repsol ŌĆŗhave signed "strategic agreements," the government of the ŌĆīSouth American country said in a statement on Thursday.

item.title: Both sides dig in, as Iran war approaches two-week mark
item.description:
DUBAI/BEIRUT, March 13 (Reuters) - The leaders of Iran, Israel and the United ŌĆŗStates all voiced defiance and vowed to fight on as the Middle East war approached the two-week mark on Friday, killing thousands of people, disrupting the lives of millions of others and ŌĆīshaking financial markets.
New Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued his first comments, read out by a television presenter on Thursday, vowing to keep the Strait of Hormuz shut and calling on neighboring countries to close U.S. bases on their territory or risk Iran targeting them.
"I assure everyone that we will not neglect avenging the blood of your martyrs," said the hardline cleric, who is close to Iran's top military force. It was not clear why he did not appear in person.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu held his first news conference since the ŌĆŗU.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran started on February 28, taking questions via video-link and issuing a veiled threat to kill Khamenei and defending the military assault.
"I will not detail the actions we are taking. We ŌĆŗare creating the optimal conditions for toppling the regime but I won't deny that I can't tell you with all certainty that the people of Iran will topple ŌüĀthe regime - a regime is toppled from the inside," Netanyahu said.
"But we can definitely help and we are helping."
The prospect that one of the most severe disruptions ever to global energy supplies could endure sent oil prices up about 9% to $100 ŌĆŗa barrel on Thursday, helping drive down U.S. stocks.
The S&P 500 notched up its biggest three-day percentage drop in a month, and shares in Asia were also under pressure on Friday.
In an effort to stabilize global energy markets, the U.S. on ŌĆŗThursday issued a 30-day license for countries to buy Russian oil and petroleum products currently stranded at sea.
"The temporary increase in oil prices is a short-term and temporary disruption that will result in a massive benefit to our nation and economy in the long-term," Bessent said in the statement, echoing earlier comments from President Donald Trump.
Trump, who has already declared that the U.S. and Israel won the war, said the United States stood to make significant money from oil prices driven higher because of supply issues tied to ŌĆŗthe closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of global oil normally passes.
"The United States is the largest Oil Producer in the World, by far, so when oil prices go up, we make a lot of ŌĆŗmoney." Stopping Iran from having nuclear weapons was far more important, he said on social media.
Trump's comments angered opposition Democrats, who accused the Republican president of caring too little about the war's impact on average Americans and demanded more information about civilian casualties, particularly ŌĆīa strike that ŌüĀkilled dozens of children at an Iranian girls' school .
Trump's administration has not provided a public assessment of the expected cost or duration of the war, which is unpopular with the American public, or a strategy for Iran after the fighting stops. The president and top aides have also given conflicting reasons for starting to fight.
The death toll has risen to more than 2,000 people, most in Iran. Almost 700 have died in Lebanon, where Israel has targeted central Beirut and ordered residents out of a swathe of the south in an offensive against the Iran-backed Hezbollah group.
Drones have been reported flying into Kuwait, Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain and Oman, undermining U.S. and Israeli claims to have knocked out much of Iran's stock ŌĆŗof long-range weapons.
In Iraq, U.S. Central Command said it ŌĆŗwas carrying out rescue efforts after one of its refueling ŌüĀaircraft went down in an incident that involved another aircraft but was not the result of hostile or friendly fire. The Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of Iran-backed armed factions, claimed responsibility for downing the aircraft.
France's President Emmanuel Macron said one soldier had died and several were wounded during an attack in northern Iraq, hours after an Italian ŌĆŗbase was also targeted in the area.
Two tankers were set ablaze in the Iraqi port of Basra earlier this week after being hit by suspected Iranian explosive-laden boats and ŌĆŗother ships have been struck in ŌüĀthe Gulf near the Strait of Hormuz.
Inside Iran, residents said security forces were increasing their presence to demonstrate continued control.
"Security forces are everywhere, more than before. People are afraid to come out, but supermarkets are open," teacher Majan, 35, said by phone from Tehran.
Israel and the United States have called on Iranians to rise up and topple their clerical rulers.
