"We have significant electricity needs - powering ŌĆŗships at dock, shipŌĆærepair facilities - and these data centres consume a large share of available power," said Barles, who is running with the France Unbowed ŌĆŗlist.
In Wissous, south of Paris, ŌüĀmunicipal candidate Philippe de Fruyt is bringing a legal challenge due to be heard in court next week to block the extension of an existing data centre used by Amazon. Amazon did not comment.
Connecting a new data centre in Europe's major markets can take on average seven to 10 years, though access to the French grid is relatively unconstrained compared to some of its neighbours, according to climate think tank Ember.
A draft law ŌüĀproposes classifying data ŌĆŗcentres as ŌĆ£projects of national interestŌĆØ, reducing legal and environmental hurdles and allowing the state to bypass local authorities. The bill is ŌĆŗcurrently stuck in France's parliament.
Some academics and politicians say the measures would weaken public input.
"We've seen what happened in the U.S., where so many data centres were built that serious opposition emerged," said Anthony Devillet, of The Cloud Is Under Our Feet advocacy ŌĆŗgroup. "With the rise of AI, I think the debate will become omnipresent here."
($1 = 0.8651 euros)

item.title: Global EV sales fall again in February
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Global EV registrations fell 11% in February, dragged by ŌĆīChina's largest sales drop since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020, data by consultancy Benchmark Mineral Intelligence (BMI) showed on Friday.
As governments worldwide hit the brake on policies designed ŌĆŗto encourage purchases of electric cars, China has pulled funding for auto trade-ins, while ŌĆŗa tax exemption on EV purchases in the country expired ŌüĀat the end of last year.
China, the world's largest EV market, recorded in ŌĆŗFebruary a 32% year-on-year drop in new battery-electric and plug-in hybrid car registrations, a ŌĆŗproxy for sales, to less than 500,000 vehicles, BMI said.
That is in line with a 34% drop in overall car sales in the month recorded by the China Association of Automobile ŌĆŗManufacturers .
"Consumers are very price sensitive", BMI data manager Charles Lester said.
Worldwide, registrations fell ŌĆŗfor a second month in February to just over one million cars sold, their lowest since ŌĆīthe ŌüĀsame month of 2024.
The North American market contracted by 35% to under 90,000 EVs sold, falling for a fifth consecutive month after the end of an EV tax credit scheme in the United States last September and proposals by President Donald ŌĆŗTrump's administration to further ŌĆŗcut Co2 emission ŌüĀstandards .
Trump's policies and cooling global demand for electric cars have forced some of the carmakers with the largest exposure to ŌĆŗthe U.S. market to book over $70 billion in writedowns .
Europe has also ŌĆŗbacktracked on ŌüĀemission targets. The continent's EV sales however rose by 21% in February, maintaining growth despite a slowing pace compared to most of last year.
EV registrations in the rest ŌüĀof ŌĆŗthe world grew by 78% to over 180,000 ŌĆŗcars, as Chinese carmakers continued to expand their presence in Asian markets and Australia, as well as ŌĆŗEurope, while fending off fierce competition domestically.

item.title: 'Dream holidays' in disarray, trips diverted as travellers count cost of Iran war
item.description:
SYDNEY/LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - In the remote Western Australian town of Dardanup, accountant Natasha Earle and her family are feeling the financial pain of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
Their "once-in-a-lifetime" five-week trip to Europe - booked last May on Emirates and ŌĆŗtaking them to London, Paris, Berlin, Vienna and Rome - has been upended by the conflict, and is costing roughly A$10,000 ($7,000) more as they reroute to avoid disruptions from drone and missile fire in the Middle East.
"We've ŌĆīspent tens of thousands of dollars on this holiday," said Earle, who is due to fly at the end of this month amid the biggest disruption to global travel since the pandemic. "We should get at least half of that back from Emirates eventually."
With the Gulf serving as a global crossroads for commercial aviation, the Iran conflict underscores how quickly a problem in a single region can paralyse travel worldwide, driving up prices, squeezing capacity and throwing holiday plans into disarray.
Drone and missile fire have regularly left aircraft circling near Dubai as the war enters its third week, heavily impacting Middle East tourism worth some $367 billion annually to the region.
Combined, ŌĆŗEmirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad Airways normally fly more than half of all passengers between Europe and Australia, New Zealand and the nearby Pacific Islands, according to Cirium data.
The war has already led to the closure of much of the ŌĆŗMiddle EastŌĆÖs airspace due to the risk of missiles and drones, leading to tens of thousands of flight cancellations, reroutings and schedule changes worldwide, disrupting the lives of millions of people.
New Zealander ŌüĀJacob Brown, 34, who lives in the Qatari capital of Doha, drove through the desert of neighbouring Saudi Arabia to Riyadh where he caught a plane to London after his Qatar Airways flight from Doha to New Zealand - where he was due to be ŌĆŗbest man at a wedding - was cancelled.
A journey that should have taken him less than 24 hours ended up taking several days due to closed airspace, additional flight chaos, delays and lost baggage.
"It was pretty nerve-wracking flying out of Riyadh just considering that that morning, there ŌĆŗhad been a few missile interceptions south of the capital," he said.
Aditya Kushwaha, an Australian disability support worker living in Orange near Sydney, has booked a family holiday to London and Paris from April 13 to 29, with Emirates through Dubai. He is unsure if it will go ahead.
"We are very much in a dilemma of what to do," Kushwaha said, estimating he might lose more than $10,000 if he cancels the trip and only be able to afford it again in a few years.
For Australian Kellee Smith, her "dream holiday" to Europe at the end of March - planned 12 months ŌĆŗago - with her husband and two children has also turned into a "nightmare" and left her roughly A$5,500 out of pocket.
"I've had many sleepless nights as I was stressed thinking IŌĆÖm going to lose my dream holiday ... and all the money we paid," said Smith.
She is ŌĆŗwaiting for a refund from Emirates of more than A$4,000 after securing back-up flights with Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab and Qantas (QAN.AX) , opens new tab to fly through Asia rather than the Middle East.
The war has narrowed an already-slim flight corridor for long-haul flights between Europe and Asia, complicating operations for global air carriers ŌĆīand sending ticket prices sky-high .
As ŌüĀthe conflict rattles businesses worldwide and drives oil prices higher, concerns over jet fuel costs and supplies are also weighing on airlines, with many raising fuel surcharges and some, such as Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab , cutting flights.
The International Energy Agency has said the war is creating the biggest oil supply disruption in history, while some analysts have warned it could be only a matter of weeks before airlines run out of jet fuel. Vietnam said this week it could face aviation fuel shortages as soon as April due to the conflict.
In the British city of Bath, John Moore, 81, and his wife Pauline - who describe themselves as "travel virgins" - forked out "a couple of hundred pounds" to switch their flights to the Australian city of Brisbane from Qatar Airways to Qantas.
They will transit through Singapore instead of the Middle East.
"We decided ŌĆŗwe'd rather pay the extra to book via Singapore, which is ŌĆŗno guarantee, but clearly it's likely to be safer than ŌüĀthe current route," Moore told Reuters.
For others, the prospect of travel disruptions due to the war is not worth the risk.
Sumit Sharma, who lives in Sydney and works at Westpac Banking Corp., had planned to travel on Etihad Airways with his family to Dubai, but changed his plans after Etihad confirmed he was eligible for a refund.
"We changed the plan from the Middle East to Hong Kong," ŌĆŗSharma said, adding that he was now flying with Cathay Pacific and looking forward to taking his son to Disneyland.
Shobana Gopal, a senior consultant with Alliance Insurance in Sydney, has switched her ŌĆŗfamily's travel plans to China instead ŌüĀof flying through Dubai to get to Austria.
"We're going to three cities in China," Gopal said.
Qantas said more passengers were choosing to travel to Europe via the U.S., other Asian cities and Johannesburg in South Africa, using its partner network.
Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific, said this week the airline was seeing "drastic changes in terms of demand patterns due to the Middle East situation," while budget carrier Ryanair (RYA.I) , opens new tab highlighted a surge in bookings to European destinations as travellers avoided the Middle East.
Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab said demand for routes through Europe had surged, with the German carrier flagging that 12-month forward bookings for ŌüĀdirect flights to ŌĆŗAsia were up 75% year-on-year.
"The large Middle Eastern airlines like Emirates and Etihad can't fly the routes they should, and therefore the European and Asian airlines have ŌĆŗto pick up that traffic," aviation specialist Hans Joergen Elnaes said.

item.title: Police investigate overnight fire attack on Rotterdam synagogue
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 13 (Reuters) - Dutch police open an investigation ŌĆīinto an arson attack on a synagogue in Rotterdam on Friday, in the latest incident of suspected antisemitic ŌĆŗviolence since the start of the U.S-Israeli ŌĆŗwar on Iran .

"Around 3:40 a.m., a fire was started at the synagogue," Rotterdam police said in a ŌĆŗpost on X. "The fire burned for a ŌĆŗshort time before extinguishing," the police added, calling for any ŌĆīwitnesses ŌüĀto come forward with evidence.
Another synagogue, 200 km (124 miles) away in neighbouring Belgium, was hit by an explosion on Monday in what authorities said ŌĆŗwas an ŌĆŗantisemitic attack. ŌüĀThat attack caused damage but no injuries.
In Michigan in the U.S., ŌĆŗa Lebanon-born U.S. citizen crashed his truck ŌĆŗinto ŌüĀa synagogue and its preschool and was shot dead.
Concerns about possible attacks against Jewish communities around the ŌüĀworld ŌĆŗhave risen following U.S. and ŌĆŗIsraeli airstrikes on Iran and a subsequent response by Iran.

item.title: D8 summit in Indonesia delayed due to Middle East war, Indonesian foreign ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 13 (Reuters) - A summit of eight major Muslim ŌĆŗdeveloping countries, including Iran, that ŌĆīwas to be held in Jakarta next month has been delayed due ŌĆŗto the war in the Middle ŌĆŗEast , an official with Indonesia's foreign ŌüĀministry said on Friday.
The D-8 ŌĆŗOrganization for Economic Cooperation, which ŌĆŗgroups Bangladesh, Egypt, Indonesia, Iran, Malaysia, Nigeria, Pakistan and Turkey, was established in 1997 ŌĆŗto improve cooperation between the ŌĆŗcountries stretching from Southeast Asia to Africa.
A ŌĆīrevised ŌüĀdate for the conference has not been discussed, senior foreign ministry official Tri Tharyat told journalists.
It ŌĆŗwas originally ŌĆŗset to ŌüĀbe held on April 13 to 15.
The U.S. ŌĆŗand Israel began attacks on Iran ŌĆŗon ŌüĀFebruary 28. Iran has responded with strikes on Israel and Gulf ŌüĀcountries ŌĆŗwith U.S. bases.
Egypt ŌĆŗhosted the previous D-8 summit in 2024.

item.title: NATO-partner Serbia admits buying Chinese missiles after photos leaked
item.description:
BELGRADE, March 13 (Reuters) - Serbia recently purchased Chinese CM-400AKG air-to-surface ballistic missiles for its air force, becoming the weapon's first European operator, Serbian President Aleksandar Vucic ŌĆŗsaid late on Thursday.
Serbia is striving to balance its partnership ŌĆīwith NATO and aspirations to join the European Union with its centuries-old religious, ethnic and political alliance with Russia and strategic ties with China, a major investor.
"We have a ŌĆŗsignificant number of those missiles, and we will have even more," ŌĆŗVucic said in a live broadcast by Serbia's state RTS ŌüĀTV, days after the first images of the missiles mounted on a ŌĆŗSerbian plane leaked online.
Vucic said the Serbian air force had adapted its ŌĆŗSoviet-made MiG-29 fighter jets to carry the CM-400AKG.
Croatia - an EU and NATO member, and Serbia's foe during the wars of the 1990s - has criticised the missile purchase as a ŌĆŗthreat to regional stability, an attempt to alter the military balance, and ŌĆŗa sign of a growing arms race in the Balkans.
The CM-400AKG, manufactured by China Aerospace ŌĆīScience ŌüĀand Industry Corporation (CASIC), is a supersonic air-to-surface ballistic missile. It can carry either a 150 kg (330 lb) blast warhead or a 200 kg (440 lb) penetrator warhead and has a range of up to 400 km (248 miles).
It saw ŌĆŗits first combat use ŌĆŗduring the 2025 ŌüĀIndiaŌĆōPakistan conflict, when Pakistan's air force targeted an Indian S-400 air defence system.
Vucic declined to disclose the price ŌĆŗSerbia paid for the missiles, saying only it received a "slight ŌĆŗdiscount".
Serbia has ŌüĀallocated around 2.6% of its GDP for military expenditures this year.
In recent times, Serbia has purchased the FK-3 surface-to-air defence system - similar to Russia's S-300 or ŌüĀthe U.S. ŌĆŗPatriot system - and CH-92A combat drones from ŌĆŗChina, while at the same time buying 12 new Rafale fighter jets from France's Dassault along with ŌĆŗhelicopters and cargo planes from Airbus.

item.title: Oil heads for weekly gains despite US sanctions waiver on Russian oil
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices headed for weekly gains as ŌĆŗof Friday, despite the U.S. trying to ease supply concerns by issuing a 30-day license for countries ŌĆīto buy Russian oil and petroleum products stranded at sea.
Brent futures for May rose $1.02, or 1%, to $101.48 a barrel at 0730 GMT, heading for a weekly increase of nearly 10%. U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude for April was up by 94 cents, or 1.0%, at $96.67 a barrel, ŌĆŗpoised for more than a 6% uptick for the week.
The license was issued in what Treasury Secretary Scott ŌĆŗBessent said was a step to stabilise global energy markets roiled by the U.S.-Israeli war on ŌüĀIran , but analysts said this has failed to resolve wider supply constraints.
"ICE Brent futures have already breached $100 per barrel and ŌĆŗare still supported today, despite moves to calm the markets with the Russian oil waiver and the unprecedented release of ŌĆŗemergency stockpiles," said Emril Jamil, senior analyst at LSEG.
"The market sees this as a short-term solution that does not address the crux of the supply disruption. The crude intermonth spreads for future months indicate an unresolved and continued tightness in supply," Jamil said.
Brent is more supported than ŌĆŗWTI as Europe is more susceptible to energy security issues, while the U.S. is able to stave off its exposure ŌĆŗdue to its domestic output, Jamil said.
Yang An, analyst at Haitong Futures, said: "Issuing the license has eased market concerns, but it won't ŌĆīresolve the ŌüĀmost fundamental issue. The most important thing is the restoration of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz."
The announcement on Russian oil came a day after the U.S. Energy Department said the U.S. would release 172 million barrels of oil from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve to help curb skyrocketing oil prices.
That plan was coordinated with the International Energy Agency, which has agreed to ŌĆŗrelease a record 400 million barrels ŌĆŗof oil from strategic stockpiles, ŌüĀincluding the U.S. contribution.
Fleeting relief sparked by the IEA release, however, was shattered by a re-escalation of Middle East risks, IG analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note.
Both benchmark prices ŌĆŗsurged more than 9% on Thursday and hit their highest levels since August 2022.
Iran's new ŌĆŗsupreme leader Mojtaba ŌüĀKhamenei said Iran would fight on and keep the Strait of Hormuz shut as leverage against the United States and Israel.
Two fuel tankers in Iraqi waters were struck by explosive-laden Iranian boats, Iraqi security officials said on Thursday. An Iraqi official told state media ŌüĀthat the ŌĆŗcountry's oil ports have completely stopped operations.
Meanwhile, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ŌĆŗtold Sky News in an interview that the U.S. Navy, perhaps with an international coalition, would escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz when it is ŌĆŗmilitarily possible.

item.title: Analysts reassess oil price estimates as Iran conflict disrupts markets
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Major brokerages, including Goldman Sachs and Bank of America, have revised their average oil price forecasts for 2026 ŌĆŗas the war in Iran approached the twoŌĆæweek mark ŌĆīon Friday.
Analysts expect oil prices to remain elevated in the near term as they assess the impact of supply disruptions through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, a vital conduit for more than 20% ŌĆŗof global oil flows. However, they broadly anticipate prices ŌüĀstabilizing later in the year.
Brent futures and U.S. West ŌĆŗTexas Intermediate (WTI) futures rose to their highest since June 2022 this ŌĆŗweek, and were headed for more than 10% and 7% weekly rises respectively.
IranŌĆÖs new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, vowed on Thursday to keep the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz shut as leverage against the United States ŌĆŗand Israel, amid a Middle East conflict that continues to disrupt millions of ŌĆīlives ŌüĀand rattle energy and financial markets worldwide.

item.title: Exclusive: Philippines looks to regulate power market as LNG prices surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE/MANILA, March 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines plans to rein in power bills as Middle ŌĆīEast shipping disruptions drive up LNG prices, its energy secretary told Reuters on Friday, by boosting coal-fired power output and by regulating electricity prices, maybe as soon as next week.
Shipping in the Gulf and through the narrow Strait of Hormuz has slowed sharply as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran ŌĆŗescalates, driving LNG prices to their highest since 2022, after an output halt by Qatar, which provides a fifth ŌĆŗof global supply.
The Southeast Asian nation could face a rise of 16% in power prices by ŌüĀnext month unless the government intervenes, Energy Secretary Sharon Garin told Reuters, adding that Manila was in talks with Indonesia ŌĆŗto ensure steady supplies of coal.
"The basic idea is to ramp down liquefied natural gas and ramp up coal and renewables," ŌĆŗGarin said, flagging that LNG-fired power plants now supplying the grid can readily be replaced with electricity generated from coal in the short term.
"We're asking for emergency powers in order to regulate the market," she said, adding that the government's effort to limit power prices at current levels ŌĆŗcould begin as soon as next week.
"Because the cost of living will increase, we are trying to do some temporary ŌĆŗrelief."
In addition to measures such as speeding renewable linkages and rescheduling maintenance, the Philippines's return to coal will follow its first annual decline in ŌĆīcoal-fired ŌüĀoutput in nearly two decades last year.
That now stands to be reversed, highlighting Asia's struggle with LNG price swings and supply disruptions.
After Qatar's production halt, spot liquefied natural gas prices more than doubled to their highest in over three years.
The Philippine government's plan to intervene in one of the few Asian markets where power prices are not regulated could begin as early ŌĆŗas next week, Garin said.
Power tariffs ŌĆŗin the archipelago of ŌüĀmore than 100 million are already the second highest in the region, behind Singapore.
"With the exaggerated increase in fuel transportation costs, there's a multiplier effect," Garin said.
Some market rules might have ŌĆŗto be suspended to afford temporary relief, Garin said, adding that distribution utilities had offered to ŌĆŗboost coal-fired power ŌüĀin place of LNG.
"We'll intervene in the market, or whatever is allowed by law, especially for Meralco (MER.PS) , opens new tab , which is the biggest distribution utility we have," she said.
The government was in talks with First Gas Power (FGEN.PS) , opens new tab to supply any unused domestically extracted gas to ŌüĀpower plants ŌĆŗrunning purely on LNG, she added.
First Gas did not immediately respond to a ŌĆŗrequest for comment.
In an email, Meralco told Reuters it supported the energy department's initiatives to rein in prices, had sufficient contracted coal, and was coordinating with ŌĆŗpower suppliers to hold down generation charges.

item.title: Bodies of 84 Iranian sailors to be repatriated from Sri Lanka, Iran embassy source and local media say

item.title: Trump claimed in G7 call that Iran is "about to surrender," Axios reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Trump ŌĆŗtold G7 leaders in a ŌĆīvirtual meeting on Wednesday that Iran is "about to surrender," Axios reported on Friday citing three officials ŌĆŗfrom G7 countries briefed on ŌĆŗthe contents of the call.
According to ŌüĀthe report, Trump told allies that ŌĆŗhe "got rid of a cancer that ŌĆŗwas threatening us all," while boasting about the results of Operation "Epic Fury" on the G7 ŌĆŗcall Wednesday morning.
Trump said that "nobody ŌĆŗknows who is the leader, so there is ŌĆīno ŌüĀone that can announce surrender," Axios reported.
The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment ŌĆŗoutside regular ŌĆŗbusiness hours.
Trump ŌüĀon Friday derided Iran's leaders as "deranged scumbags" and said it ŌĆŗwas his great honor to ŌĆŗkill ŌüĀthem as the war in the Middle East approached the two-week mark ŌüĀwith ŌĆŗheavy exchanges of drone ŌĆŗand missile strikes across the region.

item.title: Thai family waits for news of missing relative on ship attacked in Straits of Hormuz
item.description:
BAN SAI, Thailand, March 13 (Reuters) - Thousands of miles from the Middle East in Thailand's rural northeast, Sommai Butdee waits for news of her nephew, one ŌĆŗof three crew members missing from the Thai-flagged vessel after it was struck with ŌĆītwo projectiles earlier this week as it travelled through the Strait of Hormuz.
On Friday, Sommai, 58, gathered with other relatives under their wooden stilt home in Surin province's Ban Sai village. Holding out her phone, she ŌĆŗshowed a photo of her nephew, 27-year-old Panupong Muentan, on his graduation from ŌĆŗa merchant marine training centre.
"He didnŌĆÖt say much. I wished him a ŌüĀsafe journey. He had gone to work at sea once or twice before. He told ŌĆŗme, ŌĆśDonŌĆÖt be afraid. ThereŌĆÖs nothing to worry about'," Sommai said, recalling the last time they had ŌĆŗseen each other in February.
Sommai raised her nephew and described him as a good and devoted man, who hoped to help pay off the family's debt and his sibling's tuition fees through his work as ŌĆŗan engine room mechanic.
"They only went there to make a living, to work. They didnŌĆÖt ŌĆŗknow anything about the bigger things happening in the world. We never thought it would turn out like ŌĆīthis. ŌüĀHis parents and relatives are all sad," said his uncle, Dechawat Ratanapakul, 70.
Bangkok has demanded an apology and clarification from Tehran over the incident.
"Thailand is not a party to the conflict, the ship was not carrying anything controversial and there have been ships that have had safe passage ŌĆŗand our vessel should ŌĆŗhave been treated ŌüĀthat way also," Thai Foreign Minister Sihasak Phuangketkeow told reporters in Bangkok on Friday.
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has threatened Gulf ports and disrupted global ŌĆŗtrade through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said in a statement ŌĆŗcarried by ŌüĀthe Tasnim news agency that the ship was "fired upon by Iranian fighters".
Twenty other crew members abandoned ship in a lifeboat before being rescued by the Omani navy.
The three missing crewmen are believed ŌüĀto be ŌĆŗin the vessel's engine room, the ship's owner, Precious ŌĆŗShipping (PSL.BK) , opens new tab said previously.
ŌĆ£I just want to hug him if he survives. I hope he is safe. I would be ŌĆŗso happy," Sommai said.

item.title: Cuban president, under pressure from economic crisis and from Trump, to address media
item.description:
HAVANA, March 13 (Reuters) - Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel is scheduled to address Cuban media on Friday in a rare appearance that comes amid a ŌĆŗsevere economic crisis and as the Communist government has come ŌĆīunder increasing pressure from U.S. President Donald Trump.
The appearance "to address issues of national and international importance" is scheduled for 7:30 a.m. EDT (1130 GMT), Cuba's government announced on Thursday ŌĆŗnight. Though it is rare for the Cuban president to face ŌĆŗthe media, foreign press was not invited, and if questions ŌüĀare allowed, they will come from carefully screened national reporters.
The address was ŌĆŗbilled as a continuation of a February 5 event when Diaz-Canel warned that ŌĆŗCuba was approaching a situation that would require "extreme measures" given the economic crisis, frequent power blackouts and fuel shortages exacerbated by Trump's imposition of an oil blockade on ŌĆŗthe Caribbean island.
But Diaz-Canel also reaffirmed Cuba's willingness to engage in talks ŌĆŗwith the United States, as long as Cuba was treated with respect and not subject ŌĆīto ŌüĀthreats or preconditions.
Diaz-Canel could address repeated statements by Trump that the United States is already in high-level talks with Cuban representatives. The Cuban government has denied that any official encounters are underway but has not explicitly denied media ŌĆŗreports of back-channel ŌĆŗdiscussions.
Since the U.S. ŌüĀcaptured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro and removed from power Cuba's most important foreign benefactor in January, Trump has cut ŌĆŗoff Venezuelan oil shipments to Cuba and threatened to ŌĆŗslap tariffs ŌüĀon any country that sells oil to Cuba.
Trump in recent weeks had made a series of statements, saying Cuba was on the verge of collapse or ŌüĀeager ŌĆŗto make a deal with the United ŌĆŗStates. On Monday he said Cuba may be subject to a "friendly takeover," then added, "it may not ŌĆŗbe a friendly takeover."

item.title: Explosion heard overnight near Turkish military base hosting US troops
item.description:
ANKARA, March 13 (Reuters) - An explosion was heard and windows were shaken overnight near the Incirlik military base in ŌĆŗsouthern Turkey where U.S. troops and others are ŌĆīstationed, according to a Reuters witness and social media footage.
NATO air defences stationed in the eastern Mediterranean Sea have in ŌĆŗthe last week intercepted two ballistic missiles fired from ŌĆŗIran towards Turkey amid the U.S. and Israeli ŌüĀwar on Iran.
NATO has since beefed up its ballistic ŌĆŗmissile defences in the region, deploying a U.S. Patriot air ŌĆŗdefence system to the southeastern Turkish province of Malatya to increase defence at a radar base there used by the alliance.
Social ŌĆŗmedia footage appeared to show a projectile flying ŌĆŗin the night sky near the Incirlik Air Base in Adana ŌĆīprovince.
Turkey's ŌüĀstate-owned Anadolu news agency said sirens were heard at the base early on Friday, without providing further details.
Ankara has not officially commented on the incident and it ŌĆŗwas not immediately ŌĆŗclear what ŌüĀhad caused the explosion.
Turkey has also not said where the previous missiles fired ŌĆŗfrom Iran were aimed at, but U.S. ŌĆŗair forces - ŌüĀalong with personnel from other countries - are stationed at the Incirlik base.
Ankara says that Washington has not used ŌüĀIncirlik ŌĆŗin its air assault with Israel ŌĆŗagainst Iran, which triggered Tehran's missile and drone attacks.

item.title: Japan to join Trump's 'Golden Dome' project, expects missile requests
item.description:
TOKYO, March 13 (Reuters) - Japan will inform the U.S. next week that it intends to join the "Golden Dome" missile defense initiative and expects that ŌĆŗWashington may seek its help with missile production due to the Middle ŌĆīEast war and other conflicts, two sources said.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi will announce the latest plan when she meets U.S. President Donald Trump in Washington D.C. at a leaders' summit on March 19, the Japanese government ŌĆŗsources said, requesting anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter.
Trump's Golden Dome ŌĆŗproject, announced last year with an ambitious 2028 timeline, envisions expanding existing ŌüĀgroundŌĆæbased defenses such as interceptor missiles with more experimental spaceŌĆæbased elements, meant to detect, ŌĆŗtrack and potentially counter incoming threats from orbit.
But the project has made little visible progress so ŌĆŗfar. Details of how Japan will participate also remain unclear.
The Yomiuri newspaper, which first reported Japan's plans on Friday, said Tokyo hoped the initiative could be used to defend the country against new ŌĆŗhypersonic glide weapons being developed by China and Russia.
Tokyo anticipates that Trump may request ŌĆŗJapan to produce or co-develop missiles that could help replace stocks of U.S. munitions depleted by the ŌĆīU.S.-Israeli ŌüĀwar on Iran, as well as its support for Ukraine, the sources said. It is still considering how to respond to any such request, they added.
Japan exported a batch of surface-to-air Patriot missiles built under license to the United States late last year, marking a ŌĆŗhistoric break from its ŌĆŗlong-standing ban on ŌüĀlethal weapons exports.
The Trump administration is pushing defense contractors to step up production of missiles and other munitions that have been drawn down ŌĆŗin recent years. Tokyo is seeking to bolster its own munitions reserves ŌĆŗto deter ŌüĀan increasingly assertive China and nuclear-armed North Korea.
Patriot interceptors have been critical in intercepting hundreds of Iranian ballistic missiles and drones fired at Gulf countries since the U.S. and Israel ŌüĀlaunched their ŌĆŗair war against Iran earlier this month.
Ukraine has also ŌĆŗrelied on Patriots to defend its energy and military infrastructure since Russia invaded it in 2022.

item.title: Russian seaborne oil product exports in February fall 3.3% from month earlier, data shows
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - Russia's seaborne oil product exports in February 2026 fell 3.3% from ŌĆŗJanuary to 7.97 million metric tons ŌĆīamid unplanned refinery outages and harsh weather conditions at key ports, data from industry sources ŌĆŗand Reuters calculations showed.
The oil product ŌĆŗexports from Russia's Baltic ports ŌĆö Primorsk, Vysotsk, ŌüĀSt. Petersburg and Ust-Luga - decreased 11.4% ŌĆŗmonth-on-month to 4.7 million tons, according to ŌĆŗthe data.
Unlike the two previous mild winters, non-ice-class tankers have been barred from entering Russian Baltic ŌĆŗports since mid-February, while Ice1 and ŌĆŗIce2 vessels have been allowed with icebreaker escorts.
In contrast ŌĆīto ŌüĀthe Baltic, oil product exports from Black Sea and Azov Sea ports rose in February by 13.7% from the previous ŌĆŗmonth to ŌĆŗ2.565 million ŌüĀtons, the data showed.
Oil product exports from the Arctic ports ŌĆŗof Murmansk and Arkhangelsk fell last ŌĆŗmonth ŌüĀto 30,400 tons, down from 61,200 tons in January.
Fuel export loadings at Russia's ŌüĀFar ŌĆŗEast ports increased in February ŌĆŗby 8% month-on-month to 665,000 tons, data from industry ŌĆŗsources showed.

item.title: Turkey says NATO defences intercepted third missile from Iran, asks Tehran to clarify
item.description:
ANKARA, March 13 (Reuters) - NATO air defences in the eastern Mediterranean have shot down a third ballistic ŌĆŗmissile that was fired from Iran towards Turkey, the ŌĆīTurkish Defence Ministry said on Friday, adding that it was asking Tehran for clarification.
NATO air defences shot down the first Iranian ballistic missile fired ŌĆŗat Turkey on March 4, followed by the shooting ŌĆŗdown of a second Iranian missile on March 9 . The ŌüĀministry has said the first incoming missile was downed en ŌĆŗroute to Turkish airspace, while the second entered it.
The missiles ŌĆŗincreasingly pose a test for NATO member Ankara and the alliance. Turkey, NATO's second-largest army and Iran's neighbour, has against any more attacks. ŌĆŗIt also protested to Tehran after every incident, but has ŌĆŗnot suggested it wants to formally call on alliance members for protection.
"All necessary ŌĆīmeasures ŌüĀare being taken decisively and without hesitation against any threat directed at our country's territory and airspace. Consultations are being conducted with the relevant country to clarify all aspects of the ŌĆŗincident," the ministry ŌĆŗsaid in ŌüĀa statement.
There was no immediate comment from Iran, but it has so far repeatedly rejected deliberately ŌĆŗtargeting Turkey amid its war with the United ŌĆŗStates ŌüĀand Israel.
Following the incidents, NATO has beefed up its ballistic missile defences in the region and deployed a U.S. Patriot air defence ŌüĀsystem to ŌĆŗthe southeastern Turkish province of Malatya ŌĆŗto increase defence at the Kurecik radar base there used by the alliance.

item.title: Four US service members killed in plane crash over Iraq
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Four of the six crew members aboard a U.S. military aircraft that ŌĆŗcrashed in western Iraq are confirmed to have been killed, the U.S. ŌĆīmilitary said on Friday, as rescue efforts continued for the remaining two.
A U.S. military refueling aircraft crashed in western Iraq on Thursday, in an incident the military said involved another aircraft but ŌĆŗwas not the result of hostile or friendly fire.
The deaths add to the ŌĆŗseven U.S. service members who have already been killed as part ŌüĀof U.S. operations against Iran which began on February 28.
"The circumstances of the incident are ŌĆŗunder investigation. However, the loss of the aircraft was not due to hostile fire ŌĆŗor friendly fire," a statement from U.S. Central Command said.
A U.S. official told Reuters that the second aircraft involved in the crash, which landed safely, was also a military refueling aircraft known as the KC-135.
The ŌĆŗUnited States has deployed a large number of aircraft into the Middle East to ŌĆŗtake part in operations against Iran and the incident highlights the risk of not just operations, ŌĆībut ŌüĀof refueling aircraft in the air.
The KC-135, built by Boeing (BA.N) , opens new tab in the 1950s and early 1960s, has served as the backbone of the U.S. military's air refueling fleet and is critical to allowing aircraft to carry out missions without having to land.
The Islamic ŌĆŗResistance in Iraq, an ŌĆŗumbrella group of ŌüĀIran-backed armed factions, claimed responsibility for downing the U.S. military refueling aircraft.
Reuters reported on Tuesday that as many as 150 U.S. ŌĆŗtroops have been wounded in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. News ŌĆŗof the ŌüĀcrash comes the same day two U.S. sailors were injured after the USS Gerald Ford suffered a non-combat-related fire on board.
The first seven U.S. troops were killed when a drone ŌüĀslammed into ŌĆŗa U.S. military facility in Port Shuaiba, Kuwait.
President ŌĆŗDonald Trump and other senior officials have warned the Iran conflict will result in more U.S. military deaths ŌĆŗas Tehran retaliates against U.S. and Israeli strikes.

item.title: How an obscure banker slipped into BrazilŌĆÖs political inner circle, triggering a scandal
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 13 (Reuters) - For years Daniel Vorcaro was best known in BrazilŌĆÖs financial world as the head of Banco Master, a bank whose breakneck expansion left some analysts puzzled.
Less noticed during Master's ascent was Vorcaro's ability to build a contact book that included Supreme Court justices, the heads ŌĆŗof Brazil's Senate and House, and top officials in the countryŌĆÖs central bank.
But after Vorcaro was arrested last week for the second time for his role in an alleged multibillion-dollar fraud scheme, the contents of his cellphone, ŌĆīleaked by lawmakers who obtained files from a Federal Police investigation, revealed a spider web of influence which could embarrass some of the nation's top power brokers in an election year.
The case is a ticking time bomb, said Senator Alessandro Vieira, who is pushing for a congressional inquiry into Vorcaro's relationship with Supreme Court justices.
ŌĆ£There are very powerful figures in the Republic with clear involvement,ŌĆØ said Vieira, whose party is part of the ruling coalition.
Some are already facing scrutiny. Two central bank officials were pushed out of their roles when regulators found they had been advising Vorcaro. Supreme Court Justice Dias Toffoli stepped down from overseeing a Federal Police investigation into ŌĆŗthe fraud case after the Brazilian press revealed his family's company had financial links to the banker. Toffoli said in a statement at the time that he never received any payments from Master or Vorcaro.
In July, as Vorcaro worked to save his bank from ŌĆŗliquidation, he vented to his girlfriend in a text message that ŌĆ£this bank business ... is just like the mafia," one of several occasions when he suggested that the country's dominant lenders were out to get ŌüĀMaster.
Federal Police investigators found text messages that suggest Vorcaro plotted to intimidate people he viewed as foes, including a journalist, with the help of an associate he called "Sicario," as hit men employed by Mexican drug cartels are known, according to the leaked files.
Vorcaro's lawyers denied, in ŌĆŗa statement, that Vorcaro had committed any irregularities or fraud, intimidated journalists, coopted public agents, or took any action to interfere with lawŌĆæenforcement work.
Vorcaro, 42, got his start in his family's real estate business. His rapid rise in finance began when he bought a troubled ŌĆŗbank, rebranded as Banco Master in 2021, initially used to finance property developments.
His lack of a track record in banking and preference for openŌĆæcollar shirts without a tie set him apart from the buttoned-up world of Brazil's financial sector.
But what Master was selling drew scrutiny.
The bank's debt securities offered above-average returns as the bank invested in extremely risky assets, such as payouts in lawsuits against the government, which can take years to materialize.
Master's biggest calling card was that its deposits were covered by the Credit Guarantee Fund, which is financed with mandatory contributions from banks.
But when regulators tightened rules in 2023 on the capital required ŌĆŗto support securities backed by legal payouts, the bank faced a liquidity crisis.
Vorcaro promised regulators he would raise $3 billion in the following year to address the problem.
In a 2025 deposition, Vorcaro blamed an ensuing "reputational campaign against the bank" as well as anticompetitive behavior by Brazil's top ŌĆŗbanks for his failure to stem the crisis.
Even as Master was teetering, documents obtained in the police investigation, seen by Reuters, show he also spent millions of dollars on events that seemed designed to cement his access to the country's political elite.
In April 2024, for example, he funded a $6 million "ideas forum" in ŌĆīLondon that attracted ŌüĀluminaries including Supreme Court justices and the head of the Federal Police. The event closed with a $640,000 tasting of Macallan whisky, the documents show.
Vorcaro separately hired the wife of Brazil's most powerful Supreme Court justice, Alexandre de Moraes, as the bank's lawyer. In a statement, the office of Viviane Barci de Moraes detailed several services it performed for the bank, but stressed that it never represented it in the Supreme Court.
Another adviser, former finance minister Guido Mantega, helped the banker arrange a December meeting with President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva and the head of Brazil's central bank to discuss what he described as his struggle against big banks. Mantega did not reply to a request for comment.
In an interview with news website UOL last month, Lula said he told Vorcaro that there would be no political interference in his bank's case, but "a ŌĆŗtechnical investigation conducted by the central bank."
Reuters confirmed the president's account ŌĆŗwith an official who was present at the meeting.
Vorcaro's underdog ŌüĀnarrative has at times clashed with descriptions of an extravagant lifestyle.
A party he threw for his girlfriend in Saint-Tropez, France in 2025, flying more than 30 guests in private jets, drew particular attention.
Documents reviewed by Reuters show Vorcaro spent at least $120 million on luxurious trips and parties between 2024 and April 2025. The provenance of those funds remains unclear.
For a medium-sized bank with 80 ŌĆŗbillion reais ($15 billion) in assets, regulators would expect Master to have liquidity of 3ŌĆ» billion or 4 ŌĆ»billion reais in unencumbered securities, Ailton de Aquino Santos, a central bank official, told the police ŌĆŗin a deposition, or about $530 million to $700 ŌüĀmillion.
But regulators examining its books in 2024 found that Master only had 4 ŌĆ»million reais in cash, he said.
Vorcaro and his allies tried many avenues to keep the bank alive after regulators flagged the liquidity problems, including raising hundreds of millions of dollars from public servants' pension funds, lobbying for congressional relief and finally trying to sell Master to a state-owned bank .
None of that seemed to work.
In November, investigation documents suggest Vorcaro was texting an unidentified number in what seemed like a final effort to stave off disaster: "Did you manage to block it?" one of the messages said.
Vorcaro was arrested that ŌüĀnight at an ŌĆŗairport in Sao Paulo. Police have said they believe he wanted to flee the country. The central bank liquidated Master the next day. A judge later freed him, ŌĆŗbut police arrested him again last week, arguing he was interfering with the investigation.
Weeks later, newspaper O Globo reported the texts Vorcaro exchanged on the day of his arrest had been sent to Justice de Moraes, which the judge denies.
Still, Vorcaro's political reach has many in Brasilia worried about whether others might be implicated in the investigations, Vieira ŌĆŗsaid.
"Facts influence politics," he said. And the facts, he added, "are very alarming, it's impossible to hide them."
($1 = 5.1792 reais)

item.title: ASEAN ministers urge halt to Middle East war as crisis rattles energy and trade
item.description:
MANILA, March 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign and economic ministers on Friday called for an immediate halt to the war in ŌĆŗthe Middle East, and said the effects of surging oil prices and ŌĆīdisrupted trade are already hitting Southeast Asia's economies.
Several members of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations have begun rolling out measures to counter the economic impact, with governments moving quickly to ŌĆŗconserve energy, stabilise domestic markets and protect vulnerable sectors such as tourism.
"We expressed ŌĆŗserious concern over the situation in the Middle East and its ŌüĀimpacts in the region, and emphasised the importance of the immediate cessation of ŌĆŗhostilities," Philippine Foreign Affairs Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro told a press conference after a ŌĆŗspecial meeting on the crisis, adding that ASEAN called on all parties to exercise the utmost self-restraint.
The Philippines, which is chairing ASEAN this year, convened the special meeting as concerns over the ŌĆŗIran conflict deepened. The ministers called for global energy supply chains to be kept ŌĆŗopen and to activate regional mechanisms to mitigate the economic fallout.
Crude oil is trading near $100 a ŌĆībarrel ŌüĀon worries about supplies, which have been heightened by the vow from IranŌĆÖs new supreme leader to keep the Strait of Hormuz, the route for a fifth of global oil supply, closed.
"The escalating conflict has generated broader economic repercussions beyond the region, particularly ŌĆŗthrough heightened volatility in ŌĆŗglobal energy markets ŌüĀand disruption of key maritime and supply chain routes," the economic ministers of the 11-member bloc said in a statement after ŌĆŗa separate meeting.
Lazaro said the Philippines, which is heavily dependent ŌĆŗon imported ŌüĀfuel from the Middle East, was considering buying oil from Russia, but she did not elaborate.
The economic ministers warned that the regionŌĆÖs exposure to global oil and LNG supply ŌüĀroutes ŌĆŗmade it especially vulnerable to further shocks, adding ŌĆŗthat reinforcing supply chain resilience, accelerating renewable energy transitions and deepening regional cooperation would be essential to preserving ŌĆŗeconomic stability.

item.title: Exclusive: Sinopec plans to cut crude runs by over 10% on Mideast supply squeeze, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 13 (Reuters) - China's Sinopec, the world's ŌĆŗbiggest refiner by capacity, aims to cut throughput this month by more than 10% from its original plan ŌĆīin response to a crude supply gap caused by the war in the Middle East, two sources familiar with its operations said.
The cuts by state-owned Sinopec, which accounts for a third of China's refinery output, are part of Beijing's widening measures to curb oil supply disruptions due to Iran's ŌĆŗblockage of the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit for 20% of the world's oil.
Throughput is likely to fall by ŌĆŗ600,000 to 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) on average in March, the two sources estimated, adding that ŌüĀthe cuts exclude losses from plant maintenance planned before the Israel-U.S. war on Iran began on February 28.
A Sinopec representative said the ŌĆŗcompany does not comment on operational matters.
Sinopec imports roughly 4 million bpd of crude oil, of which 2.4 million bpd come from ŌĆŗthe Middle East, including regular shipments under yearly contracts from Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq and Qatar.
China, the world's biggest oil importer, brought in 11.55 million bpd last year, roughly half from the Middle East.
This week, Beijing ordered an immediate ban on exports of diesel, gasoline ŌĆŗand aviation fuel to prioritise domestic supply. It also rejected Sinopec's request to tap a government-controlled oil reserve, Reuters reported.
The planned cuts ŌĆŗrepresent a decline of 11% to 13% from an initial plan to process 5.2 million bpd in March, one of the sources said.
"Sinopec has ŌĆīlittle option ŌüĀother than cutting runs, and immediately," said the second person.
In Asia, which buys 60% of its oil from the Middle East, refiners had already shut at least 1 million barrels of capacity since the start of the war, Reuters has reported.
Nearly 1.9 million bpd of Gulf refining capacity has been shut due to the war, consultancy IIR said on Tuesday.
In a further step to ŌĆŗavert a shortage of domestic ŌĆŗfuel, Sinopec will focus on ŌüĀmaximising fuel output at the expense of petrochemicals production, which garners weaker margins, four people familiar with the matter said.
Sinopec's run cuts include last week's shutdown of an 80,000-bpd crude unit at its ŌĆŗFujian Refining & Petrochemical Corp unit.
That facility also cut operations by 20% to 30% at its 1.1 ŌĆŗmillion ton-per-year steam ŌüĀcracker, two people said this week.
Separately, Sinopec's Zhenhai Refining and Chemical Corp also slashed operation rates at both of its steam crackers to around 70-80% of their combined capacity of 2.2 million tpy, one of the sources said.
On Monday, Sinopec-invested Fujian Gulei Petrochemical shut down ŌüĀits full ŌĆŗcomplex, comprising a 1.1-million-tpy steam cracker, for maintenance through April, the company ŌĆŗsaid in a notice.
Asia's naphtha market is grappling with a lack of supplies , as the region takes about 60% of the petrochemical feedstock from the Middle East, with ŌĆŗsteam crackers cutting runs and declaring force majeure since last week.

item.title: Kremlin says Russia and US share interest in stabilising energy markets
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - Russia sees a U.S. sanctions waiver on its oil as an attempt by Washington to stabilise global energy markets, ŌĆŗand the two countries have a shared interest ŌĆŗin this, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said ŌüĀon Friday.
The United States issued a 30-day waiver for ŌĆŗcountries to buy sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products ŌĆŗcurrently at sea, in what Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said was a step to stabilise global markets roiled by the Iran ŌĆŗwar .
Without significant volumes of Russian oil entering the ŌĆŗmarket, such stabilisation is impossible, Peskov said.
"We see actions by the ŌĆīUnited ŌüĀStates aimed at trying to stabilise energy markets. In this respect, our interests coincide," he said.
The move, the second significant rollback of Ukraine war-related U.S. sanctions in just ŌĆŗover one week, was ŌĆŗthe latest attempt by President ŌüĀDonald Trump's administration to tame energy prices after the U.S. and Israeli strikes ŌĆŗon Iran paralysed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
Peskov ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀthere was a risk the global energy crisis could escalate.
"Of course such actions will, to some extent, help ŌüĀstabilise ŌĆŗthis market, because without significant ŌĆŗvolumes of Russian oil, market stabilization is impossible," he said.

item.title: Zelenskiy heads to France as Iran war distracts from Ukraine
item.description:
PARIS, March 13 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy will seek reassurances of support from one of KyivŌĆÖs key Western allies on Friday, arriving ŌĆŗin Paris at a time when the war in the ŌĆīMiddle East is diverting attention from UkraineŌĆÖs fight against Russia.
The widening conflict has raised questions about the supply of weapons ŌĆō notably air defences ŌĆō from Western partners to ŌĆŗUkraine, as Gulf Arab states draw down their own air defence ŌĆŗstocks to repel daily attacks by Iran.
Zelenskiy has warned Ukraine's ŌüĀacute shortage of air defence missiles will be exacerbated. This week ŌĆŗhe said that Gulf states had used more PACŌĆæ3 air defence missiles ŌĆŗagainst Iranian strikes in a few days than Kyiv had received from Washington over four years.
Zelenskiy did not specify the source of his figures.
The war has also sent ŌĆŗthe price of oil, an important revenue source for Russia, surging while ŌĆŗKyiv struggles on financially.
The European Union has yet to agree on a proposed 90 ŌĆībillion ŌüĀeuro loan that would, in part, provide Kyiv with funding to purchase weapons. Ukraine hopes the loan will be in place by midŌĆæApril.
"The key message of this visit, and its primary purpose, is to show that ŌĆŗnothing ŌĆō no crisis, ŌĆŗno development ŌĆō will ŌüĀdivert our attention from Ukraine, which remains for us a major security issue," a French presidency official told ŌĆŗreporters ahead of the visit.
"Our support for Ukraine will ŌĆŗnot waver."
Zelenksiy ŌüĀhas sought to show Gulf Arab states ŌĆō many of which maintain close ties with Moscow ŌĆō that Ukraine can be a useful and reliable partner.
The Ukrainian ŌüĀleader ŌĆŗhas offered Ukrainian drone interception technology in ŌĆŗexchange for air defence missiles.
Ukraine sent experts and officials to four Middle Eastern countries this week ŌĆŗas part of those efforts.

item.title: Mexico's heartthrob security minister now available as miniature doll, shirtless or dressed as Batman
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 13 (Reuters) - Mexico's Security Minister Omar Garcia Harfuch ŌĆŗin real life wears a suit, but he can also now be found in Mexican markets ŌĆīand stores as a miniature doll, dressed as Batman, or sometimes even shirtless.
Cuddly blankets, soft toys and cardboard cutouts featuring the minister have gone viral in the Latin American country following the February 22 raid that killed infamous cartel leader Nemesio Oseguera, better known as El Mencho, head ŌĆŗof the brutal Jalisco New Generation Cartel.
Images of 44-year-old Garcia Harfuch, who has become a heartthrob in Mexico thanks ŌĆŗto his good looks, now adorn a long list of products: party decorations, cushions, towels and ŌüĀdolls known as "Harfuchitos" or little Harfuches.
Political and security analysts say Garcia Harfuch is a likely presidential contender in 2030, when ŌĆŗPresident Claudia Sheinbaum's term ends.
Early polling , opens new tab shows Garcia Harfuch ahead of other prominent political figures, including Mexico's Economy Minister Marcelo Ebrard, who sought ŌĆŗthe candidacy in 2023 and is considered likely to try to run again.
Being immortalized as a doll is an honor most often reserved for presidents in Mexico, as well as the beloved mascot of the country's omnipresent chain of pharmacies , Dr. Simi.
Garcia Harfuch's popularity has exploded following the raid ŌĆŗthat killed El Mencho.
The operation, which Garcia Harfuch helped lead, was personal for the security chief, who blamed El Mencho for a ŌĆŗ2020 assassination attempt that left him with three bullet wounds and killed two of his bodyguards.
Garcia Harfuch did not respond to Reuters' request ŌĆīfor ŌüĀcomment regarding the popularity of merchandise featuring his image.
"He is Mexico's crush," said Carolina Garcia, who sells Garcia Harfuch merchandise on demand from her home printing business in Tlaxcala state. "Of course, we get more orders from women, as gifts, but we've also gotten some orders from men."
Meanwhile, images that appear AI-generated showing the politician promoting products such as hair oil have proliferated ŌĆŗonline, as have apps and ŌĆŗtutorials that allow users ŌüĀto generate photos of themselves hugging Garcia Harfuch or lying together on the grass.
On MercadoLibre (MELI.O) , opens new tab , Latin America's top online marketplace, a Harfuch blanket was listed among the platform's bestsellers. Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , meanwhile, offered a ŌĆŗbed-sized flannel throw emblazoned with the minister's face.
Ingrid Rebeca Sanchez, 22, who designs Garcia Harfuch merchandise ŌĆŗat a factory ŌüĀin Mexico City, told Reuters the products were initially slow to take off, but since El Mencho's killing, business has boomed, with as many as 150 blankets selling per day and orders even coming from the United States.
Now, the factory is ŌüĀalmost exclusively ŌĆŗproducing Garcia Harfuch products and Sanchez is working on a new line of ŌĆŗbobbleheads and life-size pillows.
"All the ladies love him, they want to sleep with him, they want to dry themselves off with him," she said. "He's Mexico's ŌĆŗnew daddy."

item.title: Explainer: Why havenŌĆÖt the Houthis, IranŌĆÖs allies in Yemen, stepped into the war?
item.description:
RIYADH, March 13 (Reuters) - Iran's Shi'ite allies in Lebanon and Iraq have joined the war in the region unleashed by U.S. and Israeli strikes on Tehran. But Yemen's Houthi rebels, heavily armed and capable of striking Gulf neighbours and causing major disruption to maritime navigation around ŌĆŗthe Arabian Peninsula, have not yet entered the fray.
Here is a look at why that might be:
The Houthis are a military, political and religious movement led by the Houthi family and based in northern Yemen. They adhere to the Zaydi sect of Shi'ite Islam.
The Houthis have a history of fighting guerrilla wars with the Yemeni army but expanded their power and built closer ties with Iran after the 2011 "Arab ŌĆŗSpring" protests.
Seizing on instability in the country, the group captured the Yemeni capital Sanaa in 2014.
The following year, Saudi Arabia led ŌĆŗa coalition of Arab states in a military intervention to attempt to dislodge the group.
The Houthis demonstrated significant ŌüĀmissile and drone capabilities, attacking oil installations and vital infrastructure in Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.
After years of fighting that led ŌĆŗto one of the world's worst humanitarian crises, the U.N. brokered a 2022 truce between the warring sides in Yemen that has since held.
After the October 7, 2023, attack on Israel led by Palestinian militant group Hamas, which triggered a devastating Israeli military campaign in Gaza, the Houthis began firing on international shipping in the Red Sea, saying it was doing so in support of Palestinians.
They also fired drones and missiles at Israel, which responded with air ŌĆŗstrikes on Houthi targets. The U.S. also launched strikes against the Houthis.
The Houthis ceased their attacks following a U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Hamas ŌĆŗin October 2025.
On March 5, Houthi leader Abdul Malik Al-Houthi said his group was ready to strike at any ŌĆīmoment.
ŌĆ£Regarding military ŌüĀescalation and action, our fingers are on the trigger at any moment should developments warrant it," he said in a televised speech.
But unlike Lebanon's Hezbollah and Iraqi armed groups , they have not made any formal announcement of joining the war.
Houthi religious doctrine does not adhere to Iran's supreme leader in the same way Hezbollah and the Iraqi groups do.
While Iran champions the Houthis as part of its regional "Axis of Resistance", Yemen experts ŌĆŗsay the movement is motivated primarily ŌĆŗby a domestic agenda though ŌüĀthey share a political affinity with Iran and Hezbollah.
The U.S. says Iran has armed, funded and trained the Houthis with help from Hezbollah.
The Houthis deny being an Iranian proxy and say they develop their own ŌĆŗweapons.
Observers have been split on what course of action the Houthis, a notoriously ŌĆŗmercurial group, may ŌüĀtake.
Some diplomats and analysts believe they may have already undertaken individual attacks on targets in neighbouring states. Reuters could not substantiate those claims.
Others say the Houthis are keeping their powder dry for an opportune moment to enter the conflict, in coordination with Iran, in order to exert maximum pressure.
The effective closure ŌüĀof the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz to Gulf Arab hydrocarbon exports and a shift to heavy reliance ŌĆŗon the Red Sea might provide such an opportunity.
Lastly, given growing economic pressure at home and the likelihood of intense U.S., Israeli and even Saudi attacks should they join ŌĆŗthe war, some analysts say the Houthis may decide to sit out the conflict altogether.

item.title: Brazil's jailed ex-president Bolsonaro taken to hospital after feeling ill
item.description: SAO PAULO, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazil's imprisoned ŌĆīformer President Jair ŌĆŗBolsonaro ŌĆŗwas taken to ŌüĀhospital early ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗFriday after waking up with ŌĆŗchills ŌĆŗand vomiting, his ŌĆīson, ŌüĀSenator Flavio Bolsonaro, said ŌĆŗin ŌĆŗa ŌüĀpost on ŌĆŗX.

item.title: Rosatom boss warns of 'increased military risks' around Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - The head of Russia's state ŌĆŗnuclear corporation Rosatom warned on Friday ŌĆīof "increased military risks" around Ukraine's Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant due to intensifying combat in the ŌĆŗarea.
"The number of artillery ŌĆŗstrikes, drone attacks and mortar fire ŌüĀhas been increasing in recent ŌĆŗmonths," Alexei Likhachev said following ŌĆŗa meeting with International Atomic Energy Agency Director General Rafael Grossi in Moscow.
"The ŌĆŗindustrial zone is under constant ŌĆŗattack from both artillery and drones, which ŌĆīis ŌüĀcausing power cuts from time to time," he added.
Likhachev said Russia welcomed an initiative by Grossi ŌĆŗto establish ŌĆŗa ceasefire ŌüĀaround the nuclear plant, which has seen heavy ŌĆŗfighting between Russian and ŌĆŗUkrainian ŌüĀforces, in order to restore the power supply.
Such a ceasefire was ŌüĀdeclared ŌĆŗfor the fifth time ŌĆŗearlier this month.

item.title: Morning Bid: Markets over a barrel
item.description:
March 13 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
Wall Street just recorded its worst day since the Iran war began, as markets contemplated a shuttered Strait of Hormuz, an escalating tanker war and triple-digit oil.
Given that backdrop, however ŌĆō and indications that the conflict may be far from over despite President Donald TrumpŌĆÖs statements to the contrary ŌĆō the roughly 1.5% drops posted by the ŌĆŗS&P 500, Dow and Nasdaq on Thursday seem like barely a flesh wound.
Indeed, U.S. stocks, unlike their Asian and European counterparts, have been remarkably calm during the past two weeks as energy markets have been roiled.
Investors may be betting ŌĆīthat Trump will eventually pull back to avoid more market pain, the so-called ŌĆśTACOŌĆÖ trade (ŌĆ£Trump always chickens outŌĆØ). But the problem is that even if the president does wrap up U.S. involvement in Iran quickly, it may not matter. Given the damage that has already occurred in the Middle East, this may be one TACO too many .
The week in oil has been truly historic. It kicked off on Monday with a record-breaking $35 intraday move in Brent crude, as prices neared $120 per barrel only to fall below $90 at one point. Since then, prices have remained volatile, spiking 9% on Thursday to back above $100/bbl amid TehranŌĆÖs bombastic threats that crude could ŌĆŗreach $200.
This occurred even after the International Energy Agency said on Wednesday that its 32 member countries were moving forward with a 400-million-barrel reserve release ŌĆō the biggest collective drawdown ever. That market response is understandable, though, as this emergency move appears to be ŌĆŗlittle more than a very large Band-Aid .
One of the more disturbing elements of this crisis is that no one seems to have any clue how to price it , with the physical market often ŌüĀexhibiting far more pain than paper trading has reflected. ItŌĆÖs telling that an errant social media post from U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright was enough to send prices lower on Tuesday.
(For an in-depth discussion on the energy market confusion, watch ROI Asia Commodities Columnist Clyde Russell ŌĆŗon the Gulf Intelligence podcast , opens new tab .)
The region most at risk remains Asia, which imports the vast majority of its energy from the Middle East. The biggest squeezes are in refined fuel products, like gasoline, diesel and jet fuel. In response, the U.S. is now temporarily lifting some restrictions on ŌĆŗbuying Russian oil and petroleum products.
While we donŌĆÖt know when or how this conflict will end, a few things are clear. One, you can rip up all of those oil supply glut forecasts , and two, you might want to question previous arguments about fossil fuels being inherently more reliable than renewables .
U.S. consumers are already feeling the pinch from the energy crisis, with average gasoline prices soaring 20% since the war began to $3.58 per gallon , as of Wednesday.
On the topic of price rises, U.S. inflation figures were released on Wednesday, with the consumer price index rising 2.4% in the 12 months through February, unchanged from the prior ŌĆŗmonth, despite a modest uptick in the month-over-month gain. Given that this data predates the outbreak of the war in Iran, markets paid the announcement little heed.
TodayŌĆÖs release of personal consumption expenditures inflation data may be watched a bit more closely, as itŌĆÖs the ŌĆŗFederal ReserveŌĆÖs preferred inflation gauge and is likely to remain well above the 2% target.
Speaking of central banks, almost all of the big ones have meetings next week, including the Fed, Bank of England, European Central Bank, and the Reserve Bank of Australia, among others. Only the RBA ŌĆīis expected to move ŌĆō a ŌüĀhike is expected ŌĆō but the real news will be the communications. Markets will be keen to hear how policymakers are approaching what is shaping up to be the biggest crisis since the pandemic.
Finally, away from the Middle East, investors are increasingly worrying about risks hiding in the opaque private credit markets. JPMorgan this week said it was marking down the value of some loans to private credit funds . Worryingly, parallels are beginning to emerge between todayŌĆÖs private credit tremors and those in U.S. subprime housing that led to the 2007-09 global financial crisis .
That certainly doesnŌĆÖt mean another financial meltdown is around the corner, but itŌĆÖs a reminder that when exogenous shocks occur, pockets of risk in financial markets are often revealed .
For more data-driven insights on markets and commodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
IŌĆÖd love to hear from you, so please reach out to me at anna.szymanski@thomsonreuters.com , opens new tab .
This weekend, we're reading...
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: Amid Reuters' strong coverage of the war in the Middle East, this recent piece by fellow ROI columnist Ron ŌĆŗBousso stands out. It cuts to the heart of why this crisis's economic and market impact will endure.
GAVIN MAGUIRE, ROI Global Energy Transition Columnist: This recent report from JPMorgan , opens new tab , appropriately titled "Fighting WordsŌĆØ, covers ŌĆŗall things energy.
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI Editor-in-Charge: This ŌüĀsharp analysis by Reuters Breakingviews' Hugo Dixon outlines what the Iran conflict means for Europe. Short answer - nothing good. But he argues Europe can still emerge with a stronger hand if it treats this as a wake-up call to strengthen and unite.
We're listening to...
MIKE DOLAN, ROI Finance & Markets Columnist: On this Bruegel think tank podcast , opens new tab , former Dutch central bank chief Klaas Knot discusses the ECB's best response to today's oil shock, explains how it compares with 2022, and explores Europe's emerging "Industrial Accelerator" policies.
RON BOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This episode of the Foreign Affairs podcast , opens new tab with American foreign ŌüĀpolicy experts Nate Swanson ŌĆŗand Richard Haass clearly explains how U.S. relations with Iran have evolved over the years and how the current war fits into the countries' decades-long tensions.
And ŌĆŗwe're watching...
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: In this wideŌĆæranging conversation , opens new tab with economist Joe Stiglitz on Jack Farley's Monetary Matters podcast, the Nobel laureate shares his views on the economic impact of the Middle East conflict, artificial intelligence, tariffs, and the Trump agenda.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the ŌĆŗnewsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: France and Italy open talks with Iran on securing safe Hormuz passage, FT reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - France and Italy ŌĆŗhave opened ŌĆītalks with Iran seeking ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗnegotiate a deal to ŌüĀguarantee ŌĆŗsafe passage ŌĆŗfor their ships through the ŌĆŗStrait of ŌĆŗHormuz, the Financial ŌĆīTimes ŌüĀreported on Friday, citing people ŌĆŗbriefed ŌĆŗon ŌüĀthe efforts.
Reuters could ŌĆŗnot immediately ŌĆŗverify ŌüĀthe report.

item.title: Paris mayoral race tests support for green transformation
item.description:
PARIS, March 13 (Reuters) - Marion Soulet rides to Paris' City Hall along a once car-choked road that is now a cycleway and a symbol of the French capital's urban green transformation that faces a test in a mayoral election ŌĆŗon Sunday.
Soulet welcomes the construction of about 1,000 km (620 miles) of cycle lanes by leftist Mayor Anne Hidalgo over the last decade, which she ŌĆīsays means nearly half of Parisians now ride a bicycle at least once a week.
"The more the city is redesigned to accommodate it, the more cycling increases," Soulet, head of the Paris en Selle cyclists' group, told Reuters after rolling to a halt following her ride along the Rue de Rivoli. "People like it because it's easy, inexpensive, and fast."
The push to transform Paris from a polluted metropolis into a "15-minute-city" ŌĆŗof bike lanes with more trees is the result of efforts by Hidalgo and her leftist predecessors, who have run City Hall for a generation.
That ecological legacy ŌĆŗfaces a reckoning in Sunday's election, with Hidalgo not running and right-wing rivals hoping to profit from voter fatigue over the increasingly car-free ŌüĀcity, roadwork disruptions and mounting debt.
Opinion polls suggest the winner will either be Socialist Emmanuel Gr├®goire, who wants to double down on the green agenda, or conservative former minister ŌĆŗRachida Dati, who says the classical allure of Paris is being destroyed.
Sarah Knafo , a far-right nationalist aged 32, is rising in the polls and could complicate matters for Dati if she ŌĆŗreaches the second round on March 22. Knafo polls above the 10% threshold needed to reach a run-off.
Gr├®goire, 48, is polling at around 33%, with Dati, 60, on about 30%.
"We're not fighting an ideological battle on mobility issues," Dati told Reuters while greeting shoppers in northern Paris. "We just want things to be organised."
Under Hidalgo, city authorities have sought to adapt Paris ŌĆŗto climate change and make it more liveable for its 2 million residents, within a wider metropolitan population of 10 million.
City Hall has planted 130,000 trees and removed tens of ŌĆŗthousands of on-street parking spaces. Highways along the River Seine have been pedestrianised.
Car traffic has fallen by over 60% since 2002 and bike use has more than tripled, according to city hall data. ŌĆīAir pollution ŌüĀhas improved.
"There aren't many major cities in the world that have known such a spectacular transformation," said Patrick Le Gales, an urbanist at Paris' Sciences-Po University.
"But there's been strong criticism over cleanliness and the debt," he said, referring to a municipal debt of around 10 billion euros ($11 billion), up 42% since 2020.
Pierre Chasseray, head of motorists' lobby group 40 Million Motorists, said Hidalgo had built a "Berlin Wall" between wealthy inhabitants in the centre of Paris and car-reliant residents from poorer suburbs with no say over city decisions.
"We've ended up with a ŌĆŗcaricatured image of the capital: motorists on ŌĆŗone side, cyclists on the other ŌĆö the ŌüĀgood guys versus the bad guys," he said.
Hidalgo has also faced viral social media posts using the #saccageParis hashtag that highlight social blight - everything from chronic roadworks to trash-strewn sidewalks.
Gr├®goire put this down to Hidalgo being too ambitious.
"We did too many things at the same ŌĆŗtime," he said. "I would have chosen a different timetable, above all for reasons of implementation quality."
Dati, a lawyer ŌĆŗof North African descent, ŌüĀhas toned down criticism of popular cycle lanes to focus on condemning dirty streets, and released a video of herself in a fluorescent jacket, joining garbage crews on their rounds.
"The city is increasingly dirty ŌĆö it hasn't escaped anybody," she said.
Dati's increasingly moderate stance on transportation issues - and the fact she faces a September trial on corruption charges she denies - has provided an opening for ŌüĀKnafo.
Knafo has ŌĆŗunveiled a plan generated by Artificial Intelligence to return cars to the banks of the Seine and staged ŌĆŗinterviews from the passenger seat while cruising through Paris.
Soulet believes Knafo's appeal is limited to "a very small group of Parisians who...want to turn the clock back."

item.title: Incumbent Sassou favoured in Congo Republic ballot amid rights concerns
item.description:
DAKAR/BRAZZAVILLE, March 13 (Reuters) - The Republic of Congo heads to the polls on Sunday in a presidential election ŌĆŗexpected to keep longtime ruler Denis Sassou Nguesso in power, with his challengers hampered by voter apathy ŌĆīand what rights groups describe as an atmosphere of repression.
Sassou , 82, has ruled the oil-producing Central African nation for all but five years since 1979. He faces six little-known challengers after the main opposition parties opted not to field candidates.
Two of the best-known opposition figures, General Jean-Marie ŌĆŗMichel Mokoko and Andr├® Okombi Salissa, have been in jail for nearly a decade after being convicted of ŌĆŗundermining state security.
Much of the pre-election unease stems from what rights groups describe ŌüĀas a clampdown on dissent.
Activists have been arrested, several opposition parties suspended, and public gatherings closely monitored, said Joe ŌĆŗWashington Ebina, a prominent human rights activist.
He added that a recent violent crackdown by the state on youth gangs, known locally ŌĆŗas "b├®b├®s noirs", had further heightened tensions and "created a climate where people no longer feel safe leaving their homes and expressing themselves".
Security forces cast their ballots on Thursday.
"Intimidation and repression by state security forces will once again play a key role in sustaining Sassou Nguesso's rule. The ŌĆŗtactic of having security forces vote before election day - freeing them to patrol polling stations - proved effective in 2021," ŌĆŗsaid Maja Bovcon, an independent consultant focused on West and Central Africa.
In cities across Congo this week, Sassou's posters ŌĆīlined ŌüĀmajor roundabouts, while small groups of opposition campaigners handed out leaflets in markets with little visibility.
ŌĆ£Everywhere I look, I see only the president's face, as if the others werenŌĆÖt even in the race,ŌĆØ said Rosalie Tsianko, a communications professional in Brazzaville.
Prime Minister Anatole Collinet Makosso rejected accusations of repression and political bias, telling Reuters that ŌĆ£no election is won in advanceŌĆØ. He ŌĆŗsaid Congo was equipped with ŌĆŗoversight bodies ŌĆ£to ensure transparency, ŌüĀfairness and the vitality of our democracyŌĆØ.
Some voters backed Sassou's emphasis on stability. ŌĆ£I support him because he is a man of peace,ŌĆØ said businessman Roger Ennel Ahoue.
Meanwhile, opposition candidate Uphrem ŌĆŗDave Mafoula urged a small crowd of supporters at a rally in Brazzaville this week ŌĆŗto consider an ŌüĀalternative. "Forty years of suffering is enough,ŌĆØ he said, calling Sassou's economic and social record negative.
Over 3.2 million Congolese are eligible to vote, but many say they won't, expecting little suspense.
ŌĆ£I prefer to stay at home rather than go and vote,ŌĆØ ŌüĀsaid Boudibangoyi ŌĆŗBrunel, a student in Brazzaville.
Official turnout was nearly 68% in the 2021 ŌĆŗpresidential election, but civil society groups predict it will now be lower.
"The actual abstention rate will be very high. Congolese people are tired," Ebina said.

item.title: Britain says allies should keep pressure on Russia after US eases oil sanctions

"It's obviously a ŌĆŗdecision for the U.S., but our position ŌĆŗis clear. All partners should maintain pressure ŌüĀon Russia and its war chest," the spokesperson ŌĆŗfor Prime Minister Keir Starmer told reporters.
The United States has issued ŌĆŗa 30-day waiver for countries to buy sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products stranded at sea, in order to ŌĆŗstabilise global energy markets roiled by the Iran ŌĆŗwar.

item.title: In modern Netherlands, one local keeps traditional Dutch dress alive
item.description:
VOLENDAM, Netherlands, March 13 (Reuters) - The Volendam women's costume, with its high pointed bonnet (hul), is one of ŌĆŗthe most recognisable forms of traditional Dutch dress, appearing often on ŌĆīpostcards from the Netherlands.
But outside the tourist industry, no one publicly wears it in daily life anymore - except Annie In de Betouw-Kwakman, 85.
In the Netherlands, clothing used to ŌĆŗbe linked to a person's place of origin.
"Almost every Dutch village ŌĆŗused to have their own look," said fashion historian Birthe ŌüĀWeijkamp. "You could recognise where someone was coming from, what village they belonged ŌĆŗto. So it was very much about identity, about belonging somewhere."
In Volendam, a ŌĆŗvillage 22 kilometres north of Amsterdam, nearly everyone dressed like Annie until the mid-twentieth century, said Simone Kwakman-Brinkkemper, a specialist in Volendam's traditional dress.
The daily outfit includes a black ŌĆŗjacket (jak), a short scarf (dasje), an apron (bontje), a long skirt, a tight red ŌĆŗcoral beaded necklace and black slipŌĆæon shoes (muilen).
But as people began working outside the village, ŌĆīthe clothes, ŌüĀwhich are handmade and require great skill to create, became impractical. Annie quickly watched her friends and sisters switch to modern dress.
In Volendam, the only other person who wears the daily attire is another older woman who ŌĆŗno longer goes ŌĆŗoutdoors.
When Annie had ŌüĀschool-aged children, she briefly tried to modernise for them, as they said classmates were making fun of her attire. ŌĆŗShe bought a dress, but quickly abandoned it.
"The neighbour said, 'What ŌĆŗare ŌüĀyou wearing? You look like a candy cane,'" she said. "I'm colourŌĆæblind ... I looked like a fool."
Annie said she is proud of her traditional clothes and it ŌüĀpains her ŌĆŗthat the tradition will disappear when she ŌĆŗdoes.
"Everyone is different, and everyone finds something else beautiful or comfortable. For me, this is freedom," ŌĆŗshe said.
(This story has been refiled to update the byline)

item.title: Explosion heard in Homs caused by old missile, state media and sources say
