item.title: Italy weighs options as damaged Russian LNG tanker drifts in the Mediterranean
item.description:
ROME, March 13 (Reuters) - Italian authorities are weighing ŌĆŗhow to deal with a Russian liquefied natural gas tanker left adrift in the Mediterranean ŌĆīafter what Moscow described as a Ukrainian drone attack , sources said on Friday.
Russia's transport ministry said the Arctic Metagaz, carrying LNG from the Arctic port of Murmansk, was attacked last week by Ukrainian naval drones launched from the Libyan coast. Kyiv ŌĆŗhas not claimed responsibility.
Libya's maritime rescue agency initially said the tanker had sunk, but the vessel ŌĆŗhas remained afloat and is now drifting between Italy and Malta, around 30 ŌüĀnautical miles off the small Italian island of Linosa, the Italian navy said.
"The situation is under control. ŌĆŗThe ship is in international waters and the navy, a tugboat and an environmental response vessel are escorting ŌĆŗit," said Filippo Mannino, mayor of Lampedusa, which includes Linosa.
Two Italian sources said it was still unclear how much LNG and fuel remained aboard the tanker. The 30 crew members were evacuated after the attack, which the ŌĆŗsources said occurred south of Malta.
One source said all options were being considered, but the preferred solution ŌĆŗwould be for the vessel's manager, Russia-based LLC SMP Techmanagement, to hire a specialised company to tow it to safety.
Italy ŌĆīdoes ŌüĀnot want the tanker to dock at one of its ports, the source said, describing it as a "ticking time bomb filled with gas".
It added that the drone appeared to have struck the ship above the waterline. The hull was still holding, although photos showed a gaping hole on the port side, with the ŌĆŗstern sitting lower in the ŌĆŗwater than the rest ŌüĀof the ship.
A spokesperson for Italy's Civil Protection agency said the vessel was being monitored and was not heading towards Italian waters.
The boat got as close ŌĆŗas 22 nautical miles to Malta's coast earlier this week, but now appeared ŌĆŗto be ŌüĀdrifting away, and was reported about 61 nautical miles offshore by Thursday.
Malta's transport authority warned other ships to keep at least five nautical miles away from the Arctic Metagaz at all times. Prime Minister Robert Abela said ŌüĀMalta's government ŌĆŗwas ready to act if necessary, without giving details.
Since Russia's full-scale ŌĆŗinvasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Kyiv has repeatedly targeted Russian oil refineries and other energy infrastructure in an effort to undermine ŌĆŗfunding for Moscow's war machine.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader wounded, likely disfigured, Hegseth says

item.title: Uruguayan alleged cartel leader Marset arrested in Bolivia

item.title: Israeli evacuation orders affect 14% of Lebanon, NGO says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 13 (Reuters) - Over an eighth of Lebanon's ŌĆŗterritory is under Israeli orders for people to leave their homes, an aid ŌĆīgroup said on Friday, while the United Nations peacekeeping mission said Israeli ground troops were making incursions and erecting roadblocks.
Israel has been carrying out daily strikes on Lebanon since March 2 when the Iran-backed group Hezbollah ŌĆŗlaunched rockets at Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader in Tehran ŌĆŗon the first day of the U.S.-Israeli .
Almost 700 people in ŌüĀLebanon have died in Israeli attacks and over 800,000 have been displaced. Israel's military says ŌĆŗit has targeted Hezbollah militants and Iranian forces.
The Norwegian Refugee Council said Israel's evacuation orders for ŌĆŗsouthern Lebanon and parts of Beirut now covered about 1,470 square kilometres or about 14% of the country.
"IsraelŌĆÖs mass evacuation orders have expanded to broad geographic directives, often demanding immediate movement, creating panic and fear across ŌĆŗcommunities that strikes are imminent ŌĆō even when they are not," said Maureen Philippon, NRC Country ŌĆŗDirector in Lebanon.
U.N. human rights chief Volker Turk has said the blanket Israeli evacuation orders raise serious international ŌĆīlaw concerns .
NRC's ŌüĀoffice in Tyre, south Lebanon, was badly damaged, it said, with no injuries. The Israeli military has carried out several strikes on Tyre since March 2, including a Tuesday strike on what it described as a Hezbollah command centre in the area.
The International Organization for Migration's Mathieu ŌĆŗLuciano told a Geneva ŌĆŗpress briefing that ŌüĀaround 600 shelters had been set up across the country, with many of them almost full. Hospitals are increasingly overstretched due to surging trauma ŌĆŗcases, a World Health Organization official added.
The U.N. Interim Force in ŌĆŗLebanon told ŌüĀthe same briefing its operations had been limited by the ongoing hostilities which injured two soldiers a week ago. Still, its troops had observed Israeli troop incursions, saying they had travelled up to ŌüĀ7 ŌĆŗkilometres inside Lebanon and erected roadblocks restricting access.
ŌĆ£We are ŌĆŗdeeply concerned that the situation will deteriorate further," UNIFIL spokesperson Kandice Ardiel said by video link from Lebanon.

item.title: Trump says US would escort ships in Strait of Hormuz 'if needed' - Fox News interview

WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - President ŌĆŗDonald Trump said the United States ŌĆīwould escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz if needed, adding in a Fox ŌĆŗNews interview that aired on ŌĆŗFriday that the U.S. would strike ŌüĀIran "very hard over the next week."

item.title: Gulf states can make a financial point to Trump
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ŌĆśThe defense of Saudi Arabia is vital to the defense of the United StatesŌĆÖ. President Franklin RooseveltŌĆÖs 1943 assertion , opens new tab , which other Gulf states like Qatar and the United Arab Emirates came to see as equally relevant to them, now looks different given all three states have endured nearly two weeks ŌĆŗof Iranian drone and missile attacks in retaliation for U.S. and Israeli strikes. Traditionally warm relations between Washington and Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, UAE President Sheikh Mohamed ŌĆībin Zayed Al Nahyan and Qatari Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad al-Thani make it unlikely these Gulf leaders will publicly register any displeasure they might feel. But there are still various financial ways to send a message.
The Gulf disquiet is real. Dubai billionaire businessman Khalaf al-Habtoor questioned whether the costs of dragging the Gulf into the conflict were ignored. Sources close to Gulf Arab government circles told Reuters that many believe U.S. President Donald Trump brought the Gulf into a war shaped heavily ŌĆŗby Israel, and agree the political and economic fallout for allies was not fully weighted.
Such displeasure is unsurprising. Saudi, Qatar and the UAE have invested a lot ŌĆō in hard cash ŌĆō in ŌĆŗpublic displays of support for Trump that have gone way beyond the norm. Ahead of the presidentŌĆÖs visit to the region last May, Qatar gave Trump ŌüĀa luxury Boeing 747 airliner, while entities linked to a senior Abu Dhabi royal took a stake in World Liberty Financial, the Trump familyŌĆÖs stablecoin business. Meanwhile the three Gulf states collectively contributed $24 billion to the ultimately ŌĆŗsuccessful bid for Warner Bros Discovery (WBD.O) , opens new tab by Paramount Skydance, backed by the Trump-aligned Ellison family.
As such, when some of the Gulf states counselled the U.S. president against striking Iran, they had grounds to at least ŌĆŗhope heŌĆÖd listen. Instead, they now have to contend with a war of uncertain duration, and a blocked Strait of Hormuz that means they canŌĆÖt ship their main fossil fuel exports. They also, according to Adnan Mazarei, senior fellow at the Peterson International Institute for Economics, face rising defence bills, reconstruction costs and shrinking FDI inflows ŌĆō all while the cost of borrowing climbs.
ThatŌĆÖs probably why regional governments are examining their options. High-level representatives of three of the four biggest Gulf states ŌĆŗare reviewing how they globally deploy trillions of dollars invested by their sovereign wealth funds, Reuters reported on Wednesday, citing a Gulf official. The main reason for the move is to offset any losses triggered ŌĆŗby the war. But the rethink could also be used as a way to register irritation at Washington.
On the face of it, a high-profile way to do that would be to pull out of either the Paramount acquisition, or ŌĆīeven for ŌüĀSaudiŌĆÖs PIF to reverse course on the $30 billion of equity itŌĆÖs sinking into the $55 billion Electronic Arts buyout (EA.O) , opens new tab . Neither promises a good return, and it would represent a pointed snub. Unfortunately, as Elon Musk found buying Twitter, itŌĆÖs extremely difficult legally to wriggle out of a nearly done deal.
That still leaves scope for a major rethink on investment pledges made during TrumpŌĆÖs 2025 Gulf visit, which eventually led to Qatar pledging $1.2 trillion in the U.S., Saudi $1 trillion and the UAE $1.4 trillion. These are long-term commitments over a decade or so, and only a minority came with specific contracts at the time. Scope for a potential AI bubble to pop ŌĆŗmight suggest that Abu DhabiŌĆÖs MGX investment vehicle ŌĆŗshould dump its stakes in OpenAI and the ŌüĀTrump-endorsed Stargate venture to build out AI infrastructure. Yet with the emirate laser-focused on pushing its status as an AI hub ŌĆō and still reliant on Nvidia chips ŌĆō that might hurt the UAE more than the U.S.
There are other less high-profile ways to send a message to Washington, though. Among last MayŌĆÖs pledges, bets ŌĆŗthat look less strategically important include Saudi entity SURJ Sports InvestmentŌĆÖs $4 bln U.S. tie-up, and backing for U.S. LNG. Saudi ArabiaŌĆÖs drive to localise ŌĆŗ50% of defence spending by 2030 ŌüĀis gathering pace, including through non-U.S. partnerships with Chinese , opens new tab and Turkish drone manufacturers. Riyadh could emphasise the point by doing more defence deals in Europe.
Gulf leaders may feel itŌĆÖs better to avoid ditching even these commitments. But with Iranian regime change looking unlikely, they may find themselves locked into a riskier situation in which they canŌĆÖt count on Washington. It would be odd if that didnŌĆÖt come with financial as well as political consequences.

item.title: Swiss sticking to mandate for trade talks with US, minister says
item.description:
ZURICH, March 13 (Reuters) - Switzerland is sticking to its existing mandate for trade negotiations with the ŌĆŗUnited States that aim to finalise ŌĆīa deal over tariffs struck last year, Economy Minister Guy Parmelin said on Friday.
Parmelin was speaking ŌĆŗafter the United States said on ŌĆŗWednesday it was launching trade investigations into excess ŌüĀindustrial capacity in 16 major trading ŌĆŗpartners, including Switzerland, and into allegations of ŌĆŗforced labour.
"And we will most certainly also consult with these countries to see how to respond to ŌĆŗthe United States," Parmelin told reporters at ŌĆŗa press conference.
Switzerland was subject to the highest U.S. ŌĆītariffs ŌüĀin Europe when U.S. President Donald Trump imposed in August a 39% duty on products imported from the country.
In November, Bern ŌĆŗsealed an ŌĆŗinitial deal ŌüĀthat cut the tariffs to 15%, mirroring the rate for ŌĆŗthe European Union.
Switzerland has since begun ŌĆŗtalks ŌüĀto formalise that deal, which Washington has been pressing to conclude by the end of ŌüĀMarch.
"The ŌĆŗnegotiations and discussions are ŌĆŗcontinuing," said Parmelin, who is also Switzerland's president for ŌĆŗ2026.

item.title: Pope Leo says Christians who start wars should go to confession
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo suggested on Friday that Christian political leaders who start wars should go to ŌĆŗconfession and assess whether they are following the teachings ŌĆīof Jesus, without naming any specific leaders or conflicts.
"Do those Christians who bear grave responsibility in armed conflicts have the humility and courage ŌĆŗto make a serious examination of conscience and to ŌĆŗgo to confession?" the pope asked in a speech ŌüĀto priests.
While Leo did not name anyone on Friday, in ŌĆŗrecent days he has been ramping up calls for an end ŌĆŗto the ongoing Iran war , which began with joint U.S.-Israeli airstrikes on February 28.
U.S. President Donald Trump was raised in the Presbyterian Christian faith. Several ŌĆŗof his top deputies, including Vice President JD Vance and ŌĆŗSecretary of State Marco Rubio, are Catholic.
Jesus taught his followers to be non-violent. ŌĆīThe ŌüĀCatholic Church generally opposes war.
For centuries, the Church has evaluated conflicts according to the just war tradition, which uses a series of criteria to evaluate whether a conflict can be considered ŌĆŗmorally justifiable, for ŌĆŗexample repelling ŌüĀan unjust invasion.
Washington, D.C. Cardinal Robert McElroy said earlier this week that the U.S.-Israeli strikes against ŌĆŗIran were "not morally legitimate" because they did not ŌĆŗmeet ŌüĀthe Church's just war criteria.
Leo was speaking on Friday to a Vatican conference on the practice of confession, in which Catholics ŌüĀadmit their ŌĆŗsins to a priest and ask ŌĆŗGod's forgiveness.
The pope said that the ritual helps individual Catholics and promotes peace ŌĆŗand unity in society.

item.title: South Africa's president authorises deployment of 2,200 troops to help fight crime
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 13 (Reuters) - South African President Cyril Ramaphosa ŌĆŗon Friday authorised the ŌĆīdeployment of 2,200 members of the national defence force ŌĆŗto help combat ŌĆŗcrime.
($1 = 16.7304 rand)

item.title: Swedish Liberals and Sweden Democrats agree pact to boost right's September election chances
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 13 (Reuters) - Sweden's Liberal Party, the smallest of the ruling coalition, said on Friday it would back bringing the ŌĆŗanti-immigration Sweden Democrats into government after parliamentary elections ŌĆīin September, boosting the right's chance of victory.
The opposition left bloc currently leads in polls and the right needs to club together ŌĆŗto have a chance of forming a government ŌĆŗafter the vote on September 13.

item.title: UAE says Iran must halt attacks on neighbours to allow diplomacy
item.description:
DUBAI, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump will bring about a diplomatic conclusion to the war with Iran "in his time", and Tehran must halt attacks on its neighbours before they can mediate, ŌĆŗone of the UAE's top diplomats said in an interview on Friday.
Lana Nusseibeh, a former UAE ŌĆīambassador to the United Nations who now holds the rank of minister of state in the Gulf country's foreign ministry, expressed confidence that the war would end in a negotiated settlement. But she said neighbours were shocked that Iran had unleashed ŌĆŗattacks on them.
"Ultimately, it will be a diplomatic solution, but there needs to be that tipping ŌĆŗpoint moment, and I think that president Trump will lead us all to that ŌüĀmoment in his time," Nusseibeh said in an interview.
Asked about efforts to mediate, she said Iran must ŌĆŗhalt its attacks first: "It is difficult to talk about mediation when under attack... Mediation can only happen when the ŌĆŗguns go silent."
She said Iranian officials had given her no indication the UAE was a target when she visited Tehran for talks aimed at finding a diplomatic solution to the crisis two weeks before it erupted into conflict. That lack of ŌĆŗwarning made Iran's attacks on the UAE "so shocking and so egregious".
Iran has said its strikes are aimed at ŌĆŗthe U.S. presence in the region. The UAE, along with other Gulf countries, Iraq, Jordan and Turkey, hosts U.S. military ŌĆīfacilities.
Drones or ŌüĀmissiles have been fired at UAE civilian infrastructure including Dubai airport, landmark hotels and the financial hub.
Nusseibeh said the UAE was in constant touch with the Trump administration and praised it as a strong strategic partner.
She offered no criticism of the U.S. or Israel for launching the ŌĆŗattack on Iran, and said ŌĆŗtheir military campaign should ŌüĀbe viewed separately to what she called Iran's "egregious illegal and unlawful attacks on the Gulf countries and Jordan".
It would be hard to restore relations with Iran to ŌĆŗthe pre-war status quo "as you look around at the destruction and the chaos ŌĆŗthat Iran has ŌüĀcaused in the region," she said.
She said Iran was trying to attack the UAE's economic model, which had attracted 700,000 Iranians to live there, but that the Iranian strikes had shown that "our economy is strong and robust ŌüĀand resilient".
"People ŌĆŗare back at work, our airports are open, flights are taking ŌĆŗoff. It's the Iranian economy that was already strangled by sanctions and economic pressure that is going into freefall. It's their currency ŌĆŗthat's going into freefall," she said.

item.title: Putin questions crucial infrastructure protection amid intensified Ukrainian attacks
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - President Vladimir Putin discussed measures to protect Russia's critical infrastructure with his Security Council, the Kremlin said on Friday, after intensified Ukrainian attacks that ŌĆŗhit a major military plant among other targets.
Putin asked Deputy Prime ŌĆīMinister Alexander Novak, Transport Minister Vitaly Savelyev and Construction Minister Irek Faizullin to report on the proposed measures in his opening remarks, with the rest of the meeting not made public.
Ukraine said ŌĆŗon March 10 that it had used British Storm Shadow missiles to ŌĆŗhit a factory that produced semiconductor devices and integrated micro chips ŌüĀfor missiles in the city of Bryansk, just over 100 km (60 miles) from ŌĆŗUkraine's border.
After the attack, which killed six people, some Russian war bloggers expressed bewilderment ŌĆŗthat such a crucial site for meeting Russia's battlefield needs had not been evacuated during four years of war and was operating within reach of Ukrainian missiles.
On February 25, Ukrainian drones hit ŌĆŗa chemical plant owned by fertiliser producer Akron in the town of Dorogobuzh, ŌĆŗknocking off about 5% of Russia's fertiliser output just before the supply crunch caused by U.S.-Israeli ŌĆīattacks ŌüĀon Iran.
Russia's defence ministry said on Thursday that Ukraine had attempted to attack a pumping station operated by gas giant Gazprom that exports natural gas via the TurkStream subsea pipeline to European customers, but that the attack was foiled.
On March 2, ŌĆŗSheskharis, a major oil ŌĆŗterminal on Russia's ŌüĀBlack Sea coast, suspended loadings following a Ukrainian drone attack that injured five, damaged 20 buildings and set a fuel ŌĆŗterminal on fire.
Amid the stepped-up attacks, Russia has slowed down ŌĆŗor turned ŌüĀoff mobile internet in Moscow and some other major cities as part of what the Kremlin described as security measures.
Although such outages have become common across Russia during ŌüĀthe war ŌĆŗin Ukraine, Moscow has not previously experienced them ŌĆŗon such a scale since the war started, with millions of people losing access to popular services ŌĆŗsuch as maps or taxi hailing applications.

item.title: Africa-bound India-flagged fuel tanker sets sail from east of Hormuz, Indian official says
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 13 (Reuters) - The Indian-flagged oil tanker, Jag Prakash, which is carrying gasoline from Oman ŌĆŗto Africa, has set sail from east ŌĆīof the Strait of Hormuz, an Indian government official said on Friday.
Gulf shipping has been disrupted over the past ŌĆŗtwo weeks as Iran targets the region in ŌĆŗretaliation for the U.S.-Israeli attacks on it.
Rajesh Kumar ŌüĀSinha, India's special secretary at the ministry of ŌĆŗshipping, told reporters that there were four Indian-flagged ŌĆŗvessels stuck east of the Strait of Hormuz, in the Gulf of Oman, on Thursday, of which one, the Jag Prakash, ŌĆŗhad now set sail.
"Three vessels remain stuck at ŌĆŗthe east side, they have 76 Indian sailors onboard," he ŌĆīsaid.
Sinha ŌüĀadded that 24 Indian-flagged vessels were stuck west of the Strait, in the Persian Gulf, on Friday, the same number as on Thursday, with a total ŌĆŗof 677 Indian ŌĆŗsailors onboard.
Iran's ŌüĀnew supreme leader said on Thursday that the country will fight on and ŌĆŗkeep Hormuz shut as leverage against the ŌĆŗUnited States ŌüĀand Israel, in defiant first comments attributed to him since he succeeded his slain father.
Ship-tracking data from ŌüĀKpler ŌĆŗshowed the Jag Prakash will discharge ŌĆŗfuel loaded at Oman's Sohar Port at Tanzania's Tanga Port on ŌĆŗMarch 21.

item.title: Iran unleashes oil shock to blunt US firepower
item.description:
DUBAI, March 13 (Reuters) - Long before the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran , the Islamic Republic had devised its own weapon: holding the worldŌĆÖs main oil lifeline hostage to offset its foes' military superiority, three regional sources familiar with Iranian ŌĆŗplanning said.
For decades Iran has signalled that if pushed into a confrontation , it would restrict tanker traffic in the Strait of Hormuz , the chokepoint where its adversaries are most exposed because ŌĆīdisruptions there reverberate instantly through global energy markets.
With the GulfŌĆÖs main export artery in the crosshairs , Tehran has turned the regionŌĆÖs greatest economic asset into its most powerful deterrent , the sources said.
About a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes through the vital Strait, and Iran, which lies on its northern coast, has now effectively closed it. Traffic via the strait has dropped by 97% since the war against Iran began on February 28, according to United Nations data.
Iran has used similar tactics before. In ŌĆŗthe ŌĆ£Tanker WarŌĆØ of the 1980ŌĆō88 IranŌĆæIraq conflict, attacks on vessels turned the Gulf into one of the worldŌĆÖs most dangerous waterways, forcing Washington to escort tankers through the Strait.
But Iran now wields ŌĆŗfar more potent tools, including large arsenals of cheap missiles and drones capable of threatening shipping across a far wider area. Its attacks this month have shown how quickly ŌüĀTehran can disrupt traffic through the strait without heavily mining it.
"Iran is outgunned -- there is no way it can defeat them in a direct confrontation,ŌĆØ said ŌĆŗAli Vaez, the International Crisis GroupŌĆÖs Iran Project Director. Anticipating further U.S.-Israeli strikes after a 12-day war in June last year, Tehran examined how to extend any conflict ŌĆ£in time and spaceŌĆØ.
ŌĆ£If Iran takes the global economy hostage, ŌĆŗTrump would blink first,ŌĆØ added Vaez.
The regional sources, who declined to be identified as they were not authorised to speak publicly, said IranŌĆÖs powerful Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC) had long prepared for a showdown with Israel and Washington.
The Guards' plan, which seeks to protect Iran's 47-year-old system of rule by fiercely anti-Western Islamic clerics, was activated on February 28, after Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed on the first day of the conflict.
The core of the strategy is a recognition of IranŌĆÖs military ŌĆŗlimits against superior forces, the sources said. TehranŌĆÖs planners instead seek to pressure oil flows while inflicting asymmetric attacks on U.S. assets stationed across the region.
The strategy seeks to conjure ŌĆŗeconomic pressures -- both at home and overseas -- on President Donald Trump to halt the war.
"This is asymmetric warfare par excellence, in which Iran achieves outsized, even global effects through a small number of attacks that impose painful costs,ŌĆØ said ŌĆīMichael Eisenstadt of ŌüĀthe Washington Institute.
ŌĆ£The goal is to create economic pain, further undermining support for the war in the United States and increasing pressure on Washington to end it.ŌĆØ
Rather than concentrate forces on a single battlefield, Tehran is dispersing its campaign with waves of low-cost missile and drone strikes across the Gulf, of the kind once outsourced to Iran-allied forces in Iraq, Yemen, Syria and Lebanon.
The approach reflects a doctrine shaped over decades by the IRGC, built on the assumption that a stronger foe would try to decapitate IranŌĆÖs leadership and command structure at the outset of any war, the sources said.
The Guards are applying lessons from years of shadow conflict with the U.S., ŌĆŗthey said. But this time, instead of relying mostly ŌĆŗon regional proxies that once formed its forward line ŌüĀof defence, Tehran is now executing the playbook itself.
Ali Vaez said the U.S. had entered the war unprepared, driven by ŌĆ£a lot of wishful thinking and not a lot of well-thought-through strategies.ŌĆØ
Washington, he said, failed to anticipate drone attacks on Gulf states, disruptions to shipping lanes or the need to ŌĆŗevacuate citizens, shortcomings he said reflected a failure to absorb lessons from the risk of drones in modern warfare.
By contrast, IranŌĆÖs decentralised ŌĆ£MosaicŌĆØ doctrine --dispersing command and ŌĆŗcontrol to withstand decapitation -- remains ŌüĀin place, under one coordinating hub. Even after Khamenei's death, two sources said Parliament Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, a former Guards commander, and Ali Larijani , head of Iran's national security council, continued to direct the war effort from Tehran.
Vaez argued that while the U.S. can significantly weaken Iran, total defeat would need a land invasion involving up to a million troops operating in unforgiving terrain, a commitment Washington has shown ŌĆ£it doesnŌĆÖt have the stomach for.ŌĆØ
Trump, who once promised to ŌüĀkeep the U.S. ŌĆŗout of "stupidŌĆØ military interventions, is now pursuing what many experts see as an open-ended war of choice that could be the ŌĆŗbiggest military campaign since those in Iraq and Afghanistan.
IranŌĆÖs immediate objective is survival, Vaez said. Beyond that, its broader aim is to force Washington to accept that coercion, whether through military force, economic pressure or diplomatic isolation, does not work.
Whether such a lesson is learned remains ŌĆŗuncertain. But by weaponising the worldŌĆÖs most critical energy corridor and stretching the battlefield far beyond IranŌĆÖs borders, Tehran is betting it can endure longer than a far stronger enemy.

item.title: Saudi Arabia cuts oil output 20% to 8 million bpd amid Iran war, sources say
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Top oil exporter Saudi Arabia has cut oil production by some 2 million barrels per day to around 8 million bpd after ŌĆŗreducing output from two major offshore fields amid the Iran ŌĆīwar, two sources told Reuters.
Middle East Gulf oil producers have had to shut vast volumes of production due to the blocking of the Strait of Hormuz, a ŌĆŗnarrow waterway between Iran and Oman, since the U.S. and Israel ŌĆŗbegan airstrikes on Iran on February 28. Iran has said ŌüĀthe world should prepare for oil prices at $200 a barrel.
While Saudi ŌĆŗArabia is routing more oil to Yanbu on the Red Sea coast ŌĆŗto avoid the Strait, production is down to around 8 million bpd after the Safaniya and Zuluf offshore fields were shut, one source said, declining to be identified ŌĆŗby name. Another source said Saudi production was down to below ŌĆŗ8 million bpd.
The two offshore fields produce over 2 million bpd of mainly heavy ŌĆīand ŌüĀmedium heavy crude, while the pipeline to Yanbu mainly takes light crude.
State oil giant Saudi Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab declined to comment.
A cut in Saudi production to 8 million bpd is a sizeable drop from February, when Saudi Arabia ŌĆŗsupplied 10.111 million bpd ŌĆŗto the ŌüĀmarket and produced 10.882 million bpd. The February production boost was a contingency plan in case any U.S. ŌĆŗstrike on Iran disrupted Middle East supplies, sources said at ŌĆŗthe time.
The ŌüĀInternational Energy Agency in a report on Thursday said Middle East Gulf countries including Iraq, Qatar, Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia have cut ŌüĀtotal ŌĆŗoil production by at least 10 million ŌĆŗbpd, adding that without a rapid restart of shipping flows these losses were set to increase.

item.title: Ivory Coast welcomes 'talking drum', first artifact sent back from France
item.description:
ABIDJAN, March 13 (Reuters) - Traditional chiefs wearing crowns and gold chains gathered at Ivory Coast's main airport on Friday to welcome ŌĆŗthe return of a "talking drum" looted more than a ŌĆīcentury ago, the first artifact returned to the West African country by former colonial power France.
The drum - known as "Djidji Ayokw├©", meaning Panther-Lion in the Atchan ŌĆŗlanguage - is more than three metres long and weighs ŌĆŗnearly 400 kg, according to Ivory Coast's culture ministry.
It ŌüĀwas used by the Atchan people, native to southern Ivory Coast, ŌĆŗto alert locals to forced labour operations being carried out by ŌĆŗcolonizers and to mobilize fighters.
The Ivorian culture ministry is seeking the restitution of 148 artifacts from France, and the talking drum, previously displayed at the ŌĆŗQuai Branly Museum in Paris, is the first to make ŌĆŗthe return journey.
Confiscated in 1916, the talking drum was initially kept at the ŌĆīgovernor's ŌüĀpalace in Abidjan before being sent to France in 1930, according to the culture ministry.
"This is a historic day, and I am deeply moved," Fran├¦oise Remarck, Ivory Coast's minister of culture and Francophonie, ŌĆŗsaid during Friday's ŌĆŗceremony, punctuated ŌüĀby traditional songs and war dances.
Pressure has grown in recent years for former colonial powers such as ŌĆŗFrance and Britain to return artefacts taken from ŌĆŗAfrica ŌüĀand Asia.
Donning a traditional loincloth, Gervais Djoman, chief of an Atchan village, told Reuters on Friday that the return of the talking drum ŌüĀwas ŌĆŗa source of joy and pride for ŌĆŗthe Atchan people. "We are reclaiming our identity. Psychologically, something had been taken from us," ŌĆŗhe said.

item.title: All six US service members killed in plane crash over Iraq
item.description: March 13 (Reuters) - All six ŌĆŗcrew ŌĆīmembers aboard ŌĆŗa ŌĆŗU.S. military aircraft ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗcrashed ŌĆŗin western Iraq ŌĆŗare ŌĆŗconfirmed to have ŌĆībeen ŌüĀkilled, the U.S. ŌĆŗmilitary ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀon ŌĆŗFriday.

item.title: Poland presses on with EU defence plan despite president's veto
item.description:
WARSAW, March 13 (Reuters) - Poland's government on Friday pushed ahead with a European Union defence-funding programme despite the president vetoing the scheme, amid a row over debt and the role Brussels should play in the security of the deeply divided country.
Pro-European Prime Minister ŌĆŗDonald Tusk's government says the EU's Security Action for Europe (SAFE) scheme provides cheap financing and ŌĆŗis essential to Poland's security in the face of what it views as ŌüĀa rising threat from Russia.
However, opposition-backed President Karol Nawrocki on Thursday vetoed a bill intended to implement ŌĆŗSAFE in Poland, saying the scheme would leave future generations with large debts and allows the EU ŌĆŗto infringe on Poland's sovereignty.
The bill would have created a mechanism to spend the 43.7 billion euros ($50 billion) in EU loans Poland was entitled to under the programme.
"Given the unprecedented and rapid deterioration of security in Europe, Poland must urgently ŌĆŗand significantly increase its military investments," said a government resolution backing the scheme on Friday, which authorised ministers ŌĆŗto sign the loan agreements despite the veto.
The head of the president's chancellery Zbigniew Bogucki accused the government of ŌĆīcircumventing the ŌüĀlaw.
Government ministers, meanwhile, lashed out at Nawrocki's veto, saying it would delay vital defence investment.
Warsaw is the biggest beneficiary of SAFE but nationalist opposition party Law and Justice (PiS) has labelled it a German plot to meddle in Polish affairs, which would saddle the country with debt and limit its flexibility on arms ŌĆŗpurchases.
European Commission spokesperson Thomas ŌĆŗRegnier said Brussels would ŌüĀnot interfere in domestic debates, but that it remained committed to Poland's plan.
"We need Poland on board for European security," he said.
Due to the veto, the ŌĆŗgovernment will have to use an existing armed forces fund whose rules ŌĆŗmean it will ŌüĀbe unable to disburse some 7 billion zlotys ($1.87 billion) that had been earmarked for the border guard and the police.
Nawrocki has proposed an alternative bill that would involve using unrealised profits from the rising value of central ŌüĀbank gold ŌĆŗreserves to fund defence.
The government rejected this proposal, saying the ŌĆŗcentral bank has not been making a profit in recent years.
($1 = 0.8740 euros)
($1 = 3.7361 zlotys)

item.title: Greenland's Siumut party withdraws from governing coalition
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 13 (Reuters) - Greenland's Siumut party has withdrawn from the coalition government, the prime minister said on Friday, weakening ŌĆŗefforts to present a united front against U.S. President ŌĆīDonald Trump's campaign to take control of the Arctic island.
The departure follows Siumut chair Aleqa Hammond's warning that the party would leave after ŌĆŗtwo Greenlandic ministers announced candidacies for Denmark's March 24 ŌĆŗparliamentary election without prior leave.
Greenland's Prime Minister Jens-Frederik ŌüĀNielsen expressed disappointment but said his government would carry ŌĆŗon, emphasising the importance of governance during heightened global scrutiny.
"I ŌĆŗthink it's terribly bad timing and I'm very frustrated and disappointed that it's happening at a time when we should be standing ŌĆŗtogether," he told reporters.
"Anything that might look like division ŌĆŗin our country is grist to the mill for foreigners and we ŌĆīshould ŌüĀavoid that at all cost," he said.
The broad coalition had been a cornerstone of Nielsen's strategy to respond to what he has termed Greenland's most serious time in ŌĆŗrecent history.
Siumut's departure ŌĆŗmeans that ŌüĀGreenlandic Foreign Minister Vivian Motzfeldt , who has played a key role in diplomatic talks with the ŌĆŗUnited States, is leaving her post, according to ŌĆŗbroadcaster ŌüĀKNR.
The loss of Siumut's support, which holds four seats in Greenland's 31-seat Inatsisartut assembly, does not threaten the government's majority, ŌüĀas ŌĆŗNielsen's coalition retains control with its ŌĆŗremaining 19 seats.
Greenland will elect two members to Denmark's parliament later this month.

item.title: US veteran flees ahead of US trial over failed 2020 Maduro ouster plot
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 13 (Reuters) - A ŌĆŗU.S. military ŌĆŗveteran awaiting trial ŌüĀon U.S. ŌĆŗcharges concerning a ŌĆŗfailed May 2020 armed incursion to ŌĆŗoust now-jailed ŌĆŗVenezuelan President Nicolas Maduro ŌĆīhas ŌüĀabsconded and is a fugitive, federal prosecutors ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗon ŌüĀFriday.
Marissel Descalzo, a lawyer for ŌĆŗthe defendant ŌĆŗJordan ŌüĀGoudreau, said, "We do not know ŌüĀwhere ŌĆŗhe ŌĆŗis."

item.title: Lebanon's offer for direct talks with Israel falls on deaf ears, sources say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 13 (Reuters) - Israel has rebuffed a historic offer of direct talks from Lebanon, deeming it too little too late from a government that shares its goal of disarming Hezbollah but cannot act against the heavily armed Lebanese group ŌĆŗwithout risking a civil war.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun expressed the state's willingness to begin direct negotiations with Israel this week, seeking to secure an end to the ŌĆīconflict that erupted on March 2 when Hezbollah entered the regional war in support of its patron Iran.
Two sources familiar with Aoun's position said he has begun appointing a negotiating delegation and in some private meetings, he went as far as to say he was ready to move toward normalizing ties.
"Everything is on the table," a third source familiar with his position told Reuters, when asked about normalization.
The Lebanese state's stance reflects unprecedented levels of domestic opposition ŌĆŗto Hezbollah's status as an armed group: the government last week banned the group from military activities.
But with Hezbollah still wielding a powerful arsenal and backed by a significant portion ŌĆŗof Lebanon's Shi'ite Muslim community, carrying out the order is easier said than done for a fragile Lebanese state now facing one of its ŌüĀmost precarious moments since the 1975-90 civil war.
On Friday, Aoun told United Nations Secretary General Antonio Guterres that he had not received a response to his offer, according to a statement from the ŌĆŗpresidency.
Just a few years ago, such an offer from a Lebanese president would have been a major diplomatic overture - and a chance for the United States to claim success ŌĆŗin ending nearly 80 years of hostilities between the two countries.
But Aoun's proposal generated little interest from either Israeli or American officials, according to the two sources, a Lebanese official and two foreign officials.
The sources all said Lebanon's inability to rein in Hezbollah over the last year and prevent the group's March 2 attack left Beirut with little credibility and nothing tangible to offer at a negotiating table.
Israel's Foreign Minister Gideon Saar told the Times of ŌĆŗIsrael this week that his country was ready for dialogue with the Lebanese government to normalize ties.
"But the current problem is that dialogue with the Lebanese government cannot stop the fire from Lebanese ŌĆŗterritory," he said.
Israel's ambassador to the United Nations Danny Danon told the U.N. Security Council this week that Israel could not negotiate with Lebanon "while rockets are flying into our northern border."
"The time has come to decide: ŌĆīwill Lebanon stick ŌüĀto declarations or actually act?" he said.
Lebanon's presidency, the U.S. State Department, Israel's foreign ministry and the office of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Over the last year, Lebanese authorities have been treading carefully to confiscate the group's weapons in the country's south.
The moves would have been unimaginable just a few years ago, when Hezbollah was at the zenith of its power and exercised immense sway over Lebanon's multisectarian political system.
But the measures have had mixed results.
Hezbollah was still able to spend months re-arming , even stationing new rockets in ŌĆŗsouthern Lebanon as the Lebanese army said it ŌĆŗhad secured full operational control of the ŌüĀarea.
After the new war started, Lebanese authorities detained around 50 people for carrying arms without a license in southern Lebanon and near Beirut, Lebanese security sources told Reuters, saying the detained men were suspected of being Hezbollah members.
But several were swiftly released after paying a small fine, the sources said.
When ŌĆŗLebanon tried to reach out to U.S. officials this week to make the offer on negotiations, they were rebuffed, a Lebanese official said.
"They ŌĆŗsaid that 2025 was our ŌüĀwindow to confront Hezbollah and we didn't, so there's nothing they can do now," the official said.
Three people familiar with U.S. policymaking on the Middle East told Reuters that Washington also had little bandwidth to deal with Lebanon given its current war on Iran, and was allowing Israel to deal with Lebanon as it saw fit.
Israel still wants to see Lebanese troops dismantle Hezbollah's ŌüĀrocket and drone ŌĆŗlaunch sites and seize the group's weapons, Dannon told the Security Council.
The army has avoided directly confronting Hezbollah , worried about ŌĆŗinflaming tensions with the Shi'ite Muslim community and fracturing the army, which split during Lebanon's 15-year civil war.
"That is the problem: Lebanon cannot deliver. And I understand that. This is a multi-sectarian society and Lebanon cannot afford to declare war on ŌĆŗa community," the Carnegie Middle East Center's Michael Young told Reuters.

item.title: Cuba says FBI to help investigate speedboat incursion by Cuban exiles
item.description:
HAVANA, March 13 (Reuters) - The FBI ŌĆŗwill send a team to Cuba to investigate an incursion by 10 Cuban exiles aboard ŌĆīa speedboat accused of provoking a gun battle with Cuban forces that killed half the infiltrators, Cuba said on Friday.
Cuba announced bilateral cooperation over the incident the same day as it acknowledged it was talking with the United States to try to defuse tensions ŌĆŗbetween the two neighbors at a time when Washington's oil blockade on the Caribbean island has pushed the ŌĆŗnation deeper into economic crisis.
On Feb. 25, Cuban nationals tried to infiltrate the country ŌüĀby speedboat, armed with nearly 13,000 rounds of ammunition, 13 rifles and 11 pistols and other material, the ŌĆŗgovernment in Havana has said. Five were killed; the others, who were wounded, are in Cuban custody and receiving ŌĆŗmedical attention, Cuba said.
Cuba had previously said the U.S. had expressed willingness to cooperate in the investigation. U.S. officials have also expressed interest in gaining consular access as at least two of those aboard the speedboat were dual U.S.-Cuban citizens, and others were legal U.S. ŌĆŗresidents.
"There is cooperation with our American counterparts, and we are awaiting a group of FBI experts to continue ŌĆŗadvancing this investigation," Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel told a press conference on Friday, saying the exchange between the FBI and Cuba's ŌĆīInterior Ministry ŌüĀwas arranged through diplomatic and consular channels.
The FBI did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The incident took place at a moment of heightened tension in already frosty U.S.-Cuban relations, with U.S. President Donald Trump pressuring the Communist government by imposing a virtual oil blockade after capturing and ousting Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro, a crucial ŌĆŗCuban benefactor, on January 3.
Cuba ŌĆŗsaid the assailants were ŌüĀCuban exiles, some of whom had been previously identified as accused terrorists, who came from the United States with the intent to sow chaos and attack military units.
A ŌĆŗCuban patrol of five border guard members on a nine-meter boat spotted the incoming ŌĆŗvessel early that ŌüĀmorning about one nautical mile off a channel on the Caribbean island's northern coast, some 100 miles (160 km) from Marathon, Florida.
The infiltrators fired on the patrol from 185 meters away, striking the captain in the abdomen, Cuba said. Bleeding heavily, ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗwounded captain remained at the helm and steered toward the enemy vessel, ŌĆŗleading to a firefight at a distance of about 20 meters.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio said it was not a U.S. operation and ŌĆŗthat no U.S. government personnel were involved.

item.title: UN seeks $325 million for Lebanon as war displaces 800,000 people
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 13 (Reuters) - The ŌĆŗUnited Nations launched a $325 million flash appeal on Friday to help Lebanon cope with ŌĆīthe fallout of a war that has forced more than a seventh of its population from their homes.
"Solidarity in words must be matched by solidarity in action," U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said as he announced the campaign from Beirut.
Israel ŌĆŗlaunched an offensive against Hezbollah last week after the Iran-backed Lebanese militant group opened fire ŌĆŗon it on March 2, saying it was avenging the killing of Iran's ŌüĀsupreme leader.
Hezbollah has kept up daily rocket and drone attacks, while Israel has expanded its ground ŌĆŗoperations and air strikes, bombing the capital on Thursday along with other parts of the country on Friday.
Nearly ŌĆŗ700 people have been killed in the attacks and more than 800,000 displaced by Israel's orders for people to leave ever larger swathes of Lebanon.
But aid organisations say funding constraints have already forced them to ration supplies and that substantial ŌĆŗnew contributions are essential.
"We're only targeting those who are really on the verge of starvation or ŌĆŗin starvation," said Carl Skau, the World Food Programme's deputy executive director.
"There isn't any more margin, so with needs ŌĆīgoing up, ŌüĀresources will have to come up - and they can certainly not drop," Skau told Reuters.
Humanitarian organizations say global crises have restricted their response in Lebanon, a country already hit hard by a 2019 economic collapse, the 2020 Beirut port explosion and the 2024 war between Hezbollah and Israel.
Skau said ŌĆŗthe WFP fears donor ŌĆŗgovernments will face new ŌüĀbudget constraints following the spike in global energy prices triggered by the Iran war.
The U.N.'s refugee agency UNHCR last September said it had only received ŌĆŗ25% of the resources required for Lebanon in 2025, forcing it to ŌĆŗslash cash assistance ŌüĀprogrammes.
"The current spike or the current escalation of hostilities compounds an already tight funding landscape," said Kirollos Fares, Lebanon country director at humanitarian organisation Medair.
Aid group Solidarit├®s International had already seen a drop in both ŌüĀthe number ŌĆŗand size of grants, said Lebanon country director Daniele Regazzi.
ŌĆ£...Unless ŌĆŗfresh money comes out, what we are deploying now as emergency response...will be gone in the next roughly couple of weeks," ŌĆŗhe said.

item.title: Brazil top court panel keeps Banco Master owner Vorcaro in jail
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 13 (Reuters) - The majority ŌĆŗof a ŌĆīpanel of Brazil's Supreme Court ŌĆŗvoted on ŌĆŗFriday to keep ŌüĀthe owner ŌĆŗof liquidated lender ŌĆŗBanco Master, Daniel Vorcaro, in jail.
The ŌĆŗbusinessman ŌĆŗwas detained earlier this month ŌĆīafter ŌüĀa judge cited "strong indication" that Vorcaro attempted ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗbribe ŌüĀa former central ŌĆŗbank director ŌĆŗwith ŌüĀgifts in return for ŌüĀpreferential ŌĆŗtreatment.

item.title: Brazil to revoke Trump adviser Beattie's visa, source says
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazil's government will revoke the visa of Darren Beattie, who ŌĆŗwas recently tapped by U.S. President Donald Trump for a ŌĆīsenior advisory role monitoring the South American country, a source familiar with the matter told Reuters on Friday.
Earlier in the day, President Luiz Inacio Lula ŌĆŗda Silva suggested that Beattie would be barred from entering ŌĆŗBrazil until the U.S. reinstated a visa for his ŌüĀHealth Minister Alexandre Padilha, which was revoked last year .
Beattie, a critic of ŌĆŗBrazil's government, was appointed to the position shaping U.S. policy toward the ŌĆŗcountry last month, suggesting relations between the two nations remain delicate despite recent rapprochement.
Lawyers for imprisoned former President Jair Bolsonaro asked the Brazilian Supreme Court to ŌĆŗallow Beattie to visit him in jail next week, when he had ŌĆŗa trip scheduled to Brazil, but Justice Alexandre de Moraes denied it on Thursday.
Citing ŌĆīa ŌüĀdocument sent by Foreign Minister Mauro Vieira, Moraes noted the U.S. official only committed to attend a critical minerals forum and meetings with the government while in Brazil.
Bolsonaro, who served as president between ŌĆŗ2019 and 2022, was ŌĆŗa close ŌüĀTrump ally during the period when both were in office. He is currently serving a 27-year prison ŌĆŗsentence for plotting a coup against Lula, his ŌĆŗsuccessor.
"That American ŌüĀguy who said he was coming here to visit Jair Bolsonaro was prohibited from visiting, and I forbade him from coming to Brazil ŌüĀuntil ŌĆŗthey release the visa for my health ŌĆŗminister," Lula told an event on Friday.

item.title: Trump says Putin may be helping Iran a bit
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump said he ŌĆīthinks Russian President Vladimir Putin ŌĆŗmay be ŌĆŗhelping Iran a "little bit" ŌüĀin an ŌĆŗinterview with Fox ŌĆŗNews Radio that aired on Friday.
"I think ŌĆŗhe might ŌĆŗbe helping him (Iran) a ŌĆīlittle ŌüĀbit, yeah, I guess. And he probably ŌĆŗthinks ŌĆŗwe're ŌüĀhelping Ukraine, right?," Trump ŌĆŗtold "The Brian ŌĆŗKilmeade ŌüĀShow".

item.title: JP Morgan sees crude supply cuts nearing 12 million bpd as tanker halt tightens markets
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Crude oil supply cuts are on track to reach nearly 12 million barrels per day by the end of next week, intensifying deficits in physical ŌĆŗmarkets as tanker movements through a key Middle Eastern waterway face a ŌĆītwo-week disruption, JPMorgan said in a note on Friday.
"Commercial tanker traffic remains extremely limited, with most vessels now Iranian and likely headed to China," the bank said, adding that while cargoes ŌĆŗleaving the Gulf before the shutdown are still arriving, new shipments have ŌĆŗlargely stopped, while supplies to Asia could run out this week, ŌüĀwhile Europe-bound flows are likely to halt next week.
Production cutbacks by major Gulf ŌĆŗproducers began after the conflict nearly two weeks ago, disrupting the Strait of Hormuz, ŌĆŗthrough which one-fifth of global oil supply flows.
Meanwhile, an India-flagged oil tanker carrying gasoline to Africa exited the Strait of Hormuz, an Indian government official said on Friday.
Additionally, the U.S. issued a 30-day ŌĆŗlicense permitting countries to purchase stranded Russian oil and petroleum products.
"Production shut-ins have ŌĆŗalready reached about 6.5 million bpd, roughly 1 million bpd above our earlier estimates," JPMorgan said.
With ŌĆīglobal ŌüĀsupply approximately 7 million bpd below demand, the bank noted that markets are contending with a severe shortage of diesel, jet fuel, LPG, and naphtha.
JPMorgan highlighted that roughly 5 million bpd of refined products typically transit the disrupted waterway, a major ŌĆŗartery for middle distillates ŌĆŗand petrochemical feedstocks. ŌüĀIt added that Europe remains particularly exposed, as the region heavily depends on Middle Eastern diesel and jet fuel following its ŌĆŗban on Russian imports.
The bank pointed out that approximately 2 ŌĆŗmillion bpd ŌüĀof Middle Eastern refining capacity is effectively offline due to export constraints and infrastructure attacks, tightening global supply balances almost immediately.
While refiners in the U.S., Europe, India, and ŌüĀNortheast ŌĆŗAsia might increase operations to capture strong margins, JP ŌĆŗMorgan cautioned that limited spare capacity and reduced crude supply mean most adjustments will likely result in ŌĆŗhigher product prices and firmer margins.

item.title: Colombia ministers travel to Caracas after postponement of presidential meeting-sources
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 13 (Reuters) - Colombia's foreign and defense ministers were traveling to Caracas ŌĆŗon Friday, after the postponement of a meeting between Colombian President Gustavo Petro and his counterpart, acting President ŌĆīDelcy Rodriguez, two sources with knowledge of the matter told Reuters.
The meeting was to be Rodriguez's first presidential-level bilateral since she took power following her predecessor's ouster by the United States.
The officials are likely to discuss bilateral trade, which Petro, who leaves office in August, reopened at the start of his term, ŌĆŗand cooperation on energy, including an agreement this week to repair a damaged section of a binational pipeline that will allow ŌĆŗBogota to import natural gas from its neighbor.
The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has supported a ŌüĀseries of moves by Rodriguez, formerly vice president, to attract investors in oil and mining and stabilize the country since the January raid ŌĆŗthat captured President Nicolas Maduro, with Trump repeatedly praising Rodriguez for her cooperation with the U.S.
Rodriguez has repeatedly asserted that dialogue is the ŌĆŗway to resolve long-standing disagreements with the U.S., has welcomed the U.S. secretaries of energy and interior and potential investors in Caracas, and the two countries have formally re-established . Despite this outward cooperation, Reuters reporting has shown the Trump administration is also quietly building a legal case against her to ŌĆŗstrengthen its leverage with Caracas.
Petro, who had a cordial relationship with Maduro, has had repeated squabbles with Trump, though the two men ŌĆŗwere each positive after a face-to-face meeting in Washington last month and had a friendly call on Thursday, according to Petro's office, where they discussed the ŌĆīeconomy along ŌüĀthe Venezuela-Colombian border.
Trump has repeatedly demanded more cooperation from Colombia in the fight against drug trafficking and has accused Petro, without evidence, of being an "". Petro meanwhile, claims record drug seizures during his tenure and has said deadly strikes on alleged drug boats amount to war crimes.
Colombia, home to nearly 3 million Venezuelan migrants who fled economic collapse in their home country, and Venezuela have deep ŌĆŗhistorical and cultural ties, especially ŌĆŗin border areas where many ŌüĀfamilies are bi-national.
Colombia recorded a trade surplus with Venezuela of $973.4 million in 2025, after exporting some $1.07 billion in goods like food, tobacco, chemicals, plastics and machinery, according to Colombian statistics agency DANE. Imports from ŌĆŗVenezuela totaled $98.3 million and included iron and steel, fertilizer and paper.
The two leaders had been set ŌĆŗto meet near ŌüĀa border bridge between the Colombian town of Villa del Rosario and the Venezuelan village of Tienditas, but postponed the meeting late on Thursday for what the countries said in a joint statement were reasons of "force majeure," a term meaning extraordinary or unforeseeable circumstances. They gave no details, ŌüĀsaying they ŌĆŗintended to reschedule soon.
Venezuelan state oil company PDVSA will carry out the repairs ŌĆŗon the Antonio Ricaurte gas pipeline, which has been inactive for years, Colombia's energy ministry said in a Thursday statement.
The pipeline stretches 225 kilometers (140 mi) and has a ŌĆŗtransport capacity of 500 million cubic feet of gas.

item.title: Lured by profits, some shipowners brave mines and missiles to sneak oil past Iran
item.description:
ATHENS, March 13 (Reuters) - A handful of Greek shipowners have sent crude oil and dry bulk tankers through ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz during the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, risking destruction from mines, missiles and drones for a chance at millions of dollars ŌĆīin quick profits.
The voyages reflect the financial allure of soaring crude oil prices and surging tanker rates since the start of the war, which has effectively sealed off a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs oil and liquefied natural gas supply from global markets.
U.S. President Donald Trump has urged ships to "show some guts" and run the strait, although the U.S. military has declined requests from the shipping industry ŌĆŗfor escorts through the waterway because of the risk of attack.
"The risks are huge. But the sea has always been a risky business," said a ŌĆŗGreek shipowner involved in the voyages who declined to be identified because of the sensitivity of the trade.
At least 10 ŌüĀships operated by Greek companies, and at least two Chinese-operated vessels, have sailed through the strait between Iran and Oman since U.S. and Israeli strikes began on February ŌĆŗ28, according to data from maritime specialists Lloyd's List Intelligence and MarineTraffic.
Companies involved include shipping magnate George Prokopiou's Dynacom, and the Embiricos familyŌĆÖs Aeolos Management, according to six ŌĆŗindustry sources familiar with the matter.
Dynacom and Aeolos did not respond to requests for comment.
IranŌĆÖs military has struck several ships moving through the narrow channel, vowing to keep it closed and warning that oil will reach $200 per barrel . At least 16 ships have been attacked, including Greek-operated vessels hit by drones.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said on Friday there was no clear evidence ŌĆŗthat Iran has placed mines in the Strait of Hormuz, after news reports suggesting Tehran had deployed about a dozen mines there.
A second Greek shipping source involved ŌĆŗin the trade, who also asked not to be named, described the tense and fraught sailings in the narrow waterway as "like entering an enemy's bathtub".
The profits are good, though. Average daily ŌĆīearnings have ŌüĀsurged to the highest levels in six years, with tanker owners able to earn $500,000 a day for a charter, according to ship broking data.
Even with huge war insurance costs and higher salaries for crew members, companies can still make millions of dollars on each voyage, industry sources familiar with the matter said.
Trump, who is seeking to combat soaring prices caused by the war, has encouraged oil tankers to traverse the strait.
"These ships should go through the Strait of Hormuz and ŌĆŗshow some guts, thereŌĆÖs nothing to be ŌĆŗafraid of," Trump said, according to ŌüĀFox NewsŌĆÖ Brian Kilmeade, who recounted the presidentŌĆÖs remarks during a recent interview.
The White House did not immediately comment.
Trump has said the U.S. Navy will provide escorts through the strait when needed, but the Navy has told the shipping industry in ŌĆŗregular briefings that the risks are too high for escorts.
Stephen Cotton, General Secretary of leading seafarers' union the International Transport ŌĆŗWorkers' Federation (ITF), told Reuters ŌüĀthat navigating the strait now was a bad idea.
"Sending seafarers through the Strait of Hormuz right now is sending them into a live war zone," he said.
Tactics deployed by shipowners so far include switching off AIS ship-tracking transponders in the hope that vessels become less visible to IranŌĆÖs military, and travelling at night.
"Reports that some operators are switching ŌüĀoff AIS ŌĆŗto try and slip ships through and dodge attacks are extraordinarily alarming - itŌĆÖs gambling with seafarers' ŌĆŗlives," the ITF's Cotton said.
The voyages are among the most daring by shipowners since Norwegian-born billionaire John Fredriksen made his fortune in the "tanker wars" of the 1980s during the Iran-Iraq conflict, when his vessels ŌĆŗrisked missile fire to load and then transport crude oil cargoes from the conflict area.

item.title: Pentagon chief says he's eager for Trump ally to buy┬ĀCNN as he blasts war coverage
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth on Friday said he was ŌĆŗeager to see Trump ally and Paramount Skydance (PSKY.O) , opens new tab CEO David Ellison take over CNN, as he criticized ŌĆīthe U.S. news media's coverage of the Iran war.
"The sooner David Ellison takes over that network, the better," said Hegseth, a former Fox News show host and combat veteran, referring to Paramount's $110 billion deal to acquire CNN-parent Warner Bros (WBD.O) , opens new tab .
On the war's 14th day, Hegseth criticized CNN's reporting ŌĆŗon the impact of Iran's disruption of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping passage, which has ŌĆŗdriven oil prices sharply higher and shaken the stock market. Reuters/Ipsos polling has shown little public ŌüĀsupport for the war, which Americans fear will drive gasoline prices higher .
The Pentagon has restricted press access under Hegseth, imposing policies ŌĆŗthat have led about 30 major news organizations, including Fox, the Washington Post and Reuters, to give up their credentials, ŌĆŗwith defense officials inviting in new outlets.
On Thursday, CNN reported that the Pentagon and the White House's National Security Council significantly underestimated Iran's willingness to close the strait, citing multiple sources familiar with the matter.
"We stand by our reporting," a CNN spokesperson ŌĆŗsaid. Representatives for Paramount had no comment.
David Ellison, son of billionaire Larry Ellison, has deep ties to President Donald Trump's ŌĆŗadministration and is leading Paramount's Warner Bros takeover. Ellison took over Paramount after acquiring broadcaster CBS News in 2025 as part of ŌĆīits merger ŌüĀwith Skydance Media.
The Trump administration must approve the Paramount-Warner Bros deal, and the U.S. Federal Communications Commission chair this month signaled the agency would not block it.
Both Democratic and Republican lawmakers have raised concerns that the media merger could reduce choices and raise costs for consumers. Critics have also raised concerns about the impact on editorial independence ŌĆŗand the erosion of a ŌĆŗfree press despite protections under ŌüĀthe U.S. Constitution's First Amendment.
Hegseth has invited mainstream media outlets back for briefings in the past two weeks on Iran operations but has mostly taken questions from reporters with non-traditional ŌĆŗoutlets.
He spent much of his opening statement on Friday criticizing war coverage. He also ŌĆŗfaulted ABC News ŌüĀfor reporting on an FBI bulletin that warned that Tehran could try to retaliate for U.S. strikes by launching surprise drone attacks in California. ABC News did not immediately reply to a request for comment.
"We're used to bad reporting. We're used to ill-informed reporting, ŌüĀand so ŌĆŗit doesn't change how we operate, but we engage with it to ŌĆŗprove that it's not true," he said. He also called CNN's latest report on the strait "fake news," "patently ridiculous" and "a fundamentally unserious report."

item.title: White House official: Trump has said a US-Cuba deal could be done easily
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Donald Trump has said the U.S. is talking to Cuba, its leaders should ŌĆŗagree to a deal and that could easily be done, ŌĆīa White House official said on Friday, reiterating the U.S. president's previous remarks after Havana confirmed that negotiations were under way.
Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel ŌĆŗsaid earlier that his government had opened talks with ŌĆŗWashington, as an oil blockade imposed by Trump pushes ŌüĀthe Communist-run nation deeper into economic crisis.
"As the President stated, ŌĆŗwe are talking to Cuba, whose leaders should make a deal, ŌĆŗwhich he believes 'would be very easily made'," the White House official said, on condition of anonymity.
Diaz-Canel said in a video aired on state television ŌĆŗthat negotiations "have been aimed at finding solutions through dialogue to ŌĆŗthe bilateral differences we have between the two nations."
Since the U.S. captured ŌĆīVenezuelan ŌüĀPresident Nicolas Maduro in January, removing Cuba's most important foreign benefactor, Trump has cut off Venezuelan oil shipments to Cuba and threatened to slap tariffs on any country that sells oil to ŌĆŗCuba.
On Monday, ŌĆŗTrump said Cuba ŌüĀmay be subject to a "friendly takeover," then added, "it may not be a friendly takeover."
The Caribbean ŌĆŗnation's citizens, exhausted by years of economic crisis ŌĆŗand ŌüĀshortages, now live the majority of their days without electricity, strictly rationed fuel and shortages of medicine.
"Cuba is a failing nation whose ŌüĀrulers have ŌĆŗhad a major setback with the ŌĆŗloss of support from Venezuela and with Mexico ceasing to send them oil," the ŌĆŗWhite House official said.

item.title: Israel targets Iranian checkpoints using tip-offs from informants, source says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 13 (Reuters) - Israel has launched a new phase of its assault on Iran, targeting checkpoints manned by ŌĆŗthe Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) based on tip-offs from ŌĆīinformants on the ground, a source briefed on Israel's military strategy told Reuters.
The targeting of checkpoints secured by IRGC personnel suggests an intensification ŌĆŗof Israel's efforts to weaken Iran's elite forces as ŌĆŗit carries out joint aerial bombardment with the ŌüĀUnited States.
Israel says its war objectives include destroying Iran's military ŌĆŗand nuclear capabilities but also "creating the conditions" for Iranians to ŌĆŗoverthrow their government, though Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has said this is an uncertain prospect.
There has been no sign of organised dissent while the ŌĆŗcountry is under attack, and no sign of Iran's ŌĆŗrulers relinquishing power.
On Thursday, Israel's military said it had struck checkpoints in ŌĆīTehran ŌüĀoperated by the Basij, a part-time paramilitary force under IRGC control that is often used to quell protests inside Iran.
The source, who spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not ŌĆŗauthorised to ŌĆŗspeak publicly, said ŌüĀinformants on the ground in Tehran had passed intelligence to Israel on the locations of ŌĆŗthree checkpoints that were struck in the past ŌĆŗthree ŌüĀdays. The source was not able to confirm whether these included the Basij positions mentioned by the military.
The use of informants ŌüĀto ŌĆŗidentify checkpoint locations was first reported ŌĆŗby the Wall Street Journal.
Israel's military did not immediately respond to a request ŌĆŗfor comment.

item.title: Deputy head of Russian space centre detained on suspicion of bribery, TASS reports
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 13 (Reuters) - The deputy head of Russia's Star City, a settlement near ŌĆŗMoscow that hosts the country's main ŌĆīspace training facility, has been detained along with six associates on suspicion of ŌĆŗbribery, state news agency TASS reported ŌĆŗon Friday.
According to RIA news agency, ŌüĀin January the mayor of ŌĆŗStar City Yevgeny Barishevsky was detained on ŌĆŗsuspicion of accepting a bribe of 365,000 roubles ($4,557) and put under house arrest.
Investigators said on ŌĆŗFriday that the inquiry had ŌĆŗestablished that between 2023 and 2025 the mayor ŌĆīand ŌüĀhis deputy received several bribes from businessmen, both directly and through intermediaries, in exchange for favourable outcomes in the ŌĆŗawarding of ŌĆŗmunicipal ŌüĀcontracts.
The total amount involved exceeded 9.9 million roubles ($123,602).
Star City lies ŌĆŗabout an hour's drive northeast ŌĆŗof ŌüĀthe Kremlin. For decades, the town did not appear on maps, with ŌüĀits ŌĆŗlocation revealed only after ŌĆŗthe collapse of the Soviet Union.
($1 = 80.0955 roubles)

item.title: Drone strike in Sudan kills 11, injures over 20, MSF says
item.description: GENEVA, March 13 (Reuters) - French medical charity M├®decins Sans Fronti├©res said on Friday it had treated ŌĆŗover 20 people for injuries after a ŌĆīdrone strike set fuel reserves alight in western Sudan, killing 11 people.

item.title: Russia names great-granddaughter of Soviet leader Khrushchev as 'foreign agent'
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. academic Nina Khrushcheva, the great-granddaughter of a ŌĆŗformer Soviet leader, was designated by Russia on Friday as a "foreign agent" - a term ŌĆīwith connotations of spying that Moscow applies to people it views as engaged in anti-Russian activity.
Khrushcheva, 62, is a professor at The New School university in New York and has continued to make research trips to Russia since ŌĆŗits full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Her ancestor Nikita Khrushchev led the Soviet Union from ŌĆŗ1953 to 1964, when he was ousted by fellow members of the ruling ŌüĀPolitburo.
Contacted by Reuters, Khrushcheva said she was not surprised at being added to Russia's "foreign agent" list, ŌĆŗwhich as of Friday contains 1,164 names including politicians, journalists, artists, NGOs and media organisations.
"It would have ŌĆŗbeen sloppy on their part not to do this sooner or later," she said, adding that it was too early to say what the practical impact would be.
"There is certainly historical irony but not anything shocking. When ŌĆŗStalin is up, Khrushchev is down," she said.
She was alluding to a resurgence in Russia of ŌĆŗthe reputation of Soviet dictator Josef Stalin, whose reign of terror was denounced by Khrushchev in a famous ŌĆī1956 address ŌüĀto a Communist Party congress.
Russia marked the 70th anniversary of the speech last month, prompting renewed debate about the legacies of both men.
Khrushchev was the Soviet leader who transferred Crimea to Ukraine from Russia in 1954, an act reversed in 2014 when Russian forces invaded the peninsula and President Vladimir ŌĆŗPutin declared its annexation .
Khrushchev is also ŌĆŗremembered for facing ŌüĀoff against U.S. President John F. Kennedy in the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, when the world teetered on the brink of nuclear war.
Russian news agency ŌĆŗTASS cited the Justice Ministry as saying Nina Khrushcheva had disseminated false ŌĆŗinformation about Russian ŌüĀpolicies and opposed what Moscow calls its "special military operation" in Ukraine.
People listed as foreign agents are subjected to onerous bureaucratic requirements and restrictions on their income in Russia. They are obliged to place the foreign ŌüĀagent label ŌĆŗon social media posts or anything else they publish.
Some ŌĆŗKremlin critics have worn the label as a badge of honour, while others say it is a burden that hampers them ŌĆŗin their work because it causes other Russians to shun them.

item.title: Iran coach Jafari says television comments created difficult atmosphere for players during Asian Cup
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Comments from an Iranian state television presenter about the country's women's team during their Asian Cup campaign affected the players psychologically and drove some to ŌĆŗseek asylum in Australia, coach Marziyeh Jafari said on Friday.
The tournament, ŌĆīhosted by Australia, started just as the U.S. and Israel launched air strikes on Iran, killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
After the team opted to stand in silence during Iran's ŌĆŗanthem before their first match against South Korea, they were labelled as "wartime traitors" ŌĆŗby Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting presenter Mohammad Reza Shahbazi.
In a ŌüĀstatement posted by Iran's football federation (FFIRI) on its Telegram page on Friday, which ŌĆŗhas since been deleted, Jafari said: "Our girls were affected in the first match ŌĆŗby the heavy atmosphere that had been created...
"But the greater mistake was made by those who, at home, failed to understand that atmosphere and sounded the call to arms against the daughters ŌĆŗof this land," she added, referring to the presenter's comments.
"What I have asked ŌĆŗof the federation is to pursue the matter, because it affected our players psychologically, and ŌĆīwe suffered ŌüĀthe consequences. I am certain that if that atmosphere had not been created, not a single one of our players would have stayed in Australia."
Australia this week granted humanitarian visas to five Iranian players who sought asylum during the tournament.
Two more ŌĆŗsquad members were granted ŌĆŗasylum on Wednesday, ŌüĀand one decided to return to Iran .
"Australian police called the players in several stages and sat down with them one-on-one to ŌĆŗpersuade them to stay, influenced by the political atmosphere that ŌĆŗhad arisen," ŌüĀJafari said.
"Fortunately, the majority of the team members responded negatively. Even Mohaddeseh Zolfi, who had responded positively, soon changed her mind and, God willing, will come to Iran ŌüĀwith the ŌĆŗteam.
"The rumours about Golnoosh Khosravi and Afsaneh Chatrenoor ŌĆŗare also not true at all, and they are now with us in Malaysia and we will ŌĆŗbe leaving for Iran soon."

item.title: Pentagon elevates investigation into Iran school strike
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on Friday it has elevated the investigation into a devastating February 28 strike on an Iranian girls' school after media reports revealed ŌĆŗthe probe shows U.S. forces were likely responsible.
Iran has said the strike on the Shajareh Tayyebeh School killed 168 children. If U.S. fault is confirmed, ŌĆīit would rank among the worst incidents of civilian deaths in decades of U.S. military strikes in the Middle East.
Reuters first reported the investigation's initial findings on March 5.
Citing the seriousness of the matter, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to comment on the preliminary finding of the investigation, saying: "We're not going to let reporting lead us or force our hand into indicating what happened."
He said a higher-level investigation would be led by ŌĆŗan unnamed U.S. general officer from outside of U.S. Central Command, which is overseeing operations against Iran. Such a move is typically taken by the U.S. military to ŌĆŗensure greater independence for investigators.
"The command investigation will take as long as necessary to address all the matters surrounding this incident," Hegseth told ŌüĀa press conference at the Pentagon.
Iran's new supreme leader blamed the country's enemies for the school strike in his first message to the country on Thursday, without explicitly saying the ŌĆŗU.S. was responsible.
Three U.S. officials told Reuters the command investigation Hegseth referred to is an administrative probe known as a 15-6, which can become the ŌĆŗbasis for disciplinary action if warranted. It usually includes sworn statements or interviews with those involved. The officials spoke on condition of anonymity to describe internal military processes.
The head of U.S. Central Command, Admiral Brad Cooper, ordered the 15-6 probe last week after the initial review was completed. The outside officer was appointed on Thursday, one official said.
The development underscores the serious concern about how to respond to possible U.S. responsibility ŌĆŗfor the tragedy and the need to comprehensively examine the incident.
"It signals a recognition that something went wrong and to try to understand what happened and why," said Annie Shiel, ŌĆŗU.S. advocacy director with the Center for Civilians in Conflict.
It remains unclear how U.S. forces could have struck the school. Preliminary findings suggest U.S. forces may have relied upon outdated targeting data ŌĆīthat apparently did ŌüĀnot distinguish between the school and the adjoining Iranian military base in Minab, a town in southern Iran.
Video and other evidence suggest the school was hit by a Tomahawk cruise missile , a powerful, precision-guided munition that few nations other than the U.S. possess.
The investigation comes despite public comments by President Donald Trump initially doubting the possibility of U.S. involvement that stoked concern among some defense officials about whether he would be willing to accept blame.
The president initially said, without offering evidence, that Iran may have attacked the school and suggested Tehran may have Tomahawk missiles, ŌĆŗwhich military experts call extremely unlikely.
Still, Trump ŌĆŗon Monday said publicly he would "certainly" accept ŌüĀthe results of the U.S. investigation: "Whatever the report shows, I'm willing to live with that report."
A second U.S. official pointed to those remarks as evidence that Trump was moving toward accepting the preliminary investigation results. Further, the official said public discussion of the investigation had made it ŌĆŗpolitically difficult for Trump to reject the findings once they are finalized.
The official noted that a 15-6 investigation reflected the seriousness of ŌĆŗthe Trump administration's approach ŌüĀto the investigation because it would comprehensively examine what went wrong and how to correct those issues.
A Reuters visual investigation published on Thursday showed the Iranian school had a years-long, public online presence, including dozens of photos of the children and their activities.
The Shajareh Tayyebeh School was one of 59 schools within the Persian Gulf Martyrs' Cultural Educational Institute, a network affiliated with the ŌüĀIslamic Revolutionary ŌĆŗGuard Corps, the military force that reports to Iran's supreme leader, according to archived copies of the networkŌĆÖs ŌĆŗwebsite.
The schoolŌĆÖs website includes photos of students gathered in the yard, which matched verified videos outside the building after the strike.
Satellite imagery from mid-2015 shows the building was walled off from the Iranian military base and appears to have ŌĆŗoperated as a school since at least 2018, when the painted murals are first visible on its outer walls.

item.title: Israeli strike kills three in Gaza as regional offensives escalate
item.description:
CAIRO/JERUSALEM, March 13 (Reuters) - An ŌĆŗIsraeli airstrike in Gaza killed three Palestinians, including two 17-year-olds, Palestinian medics ŌĆīsaid on Friday, with violence continuing in the West Bank and Gaza even as Israel expands its offensive across the region.
Israel has used lethal force in Iran , Lebanon , Gaza and the occupied ŌĆŗWest Bank over the past 24 hours.
Israeli forces killed two in ŌĆŗthe West Bank city of Nablus on Thursday, according to Palestinian officials, ŌüĀwhile the death toll in Lebanon reached 773, its Health Ministry said on ŌĆŗFriday.
After nearly two weeks of war, 2,000 people have been killed, most of ŌĆŗthem in Iran, with several million people displaced from their homes.
The Israeli military told Reuters that they were not aware of the strike in Gaza.
The military said separately in a statement ŌĆŗon Thursday that they had killed two individuals after they attempted to carry ŌĆŗout a car-ramming attack on soldiers. It did not immediately respond to a request for ŌĆīevidence ŌüĀof the attempted ramming.
While Israeli attacks on Gaza declined at the beginning of the war with Iran, they have since begun to rise. Israel has killed 23 in Gaza since the U.S. and Israel jointly struck Iran on February 28.
While ŌĆŗa ceasefire went into ŌĆŗeffect in Gaza ŌüĀin October, there have been regular outbreaks of violence since then.
Reuters has reported that U.S. President Donald Trump's peace plan ŌĆŗfor Gaza has been on hold since the start of the ŌĆŗIran war.
Violence ŌüĀhas also persisted in the West Bank. Israeli settlers and military forces have killed at least eight Palestinians across the West Bank since the U.S.-Israeli attacks on ŌüĀIran began.
Reuters ŌĆŗhas reported that there has been a spike ŌĆŗin settler violence since then, as Israel has put much of the West Bank under lockdown.

item.title: Mother and three sons in custody over US embassy bomb in Norway
item.description:
OSLO, March 13 (Reuters) - Three brothers and their mother have been ŌĆŗremanded in police custody for ŌĆīup to four weeks on suspicion of taking part in ŌĆŗthe bombing of the ŌĆŗU.S. embassy in Oslo last ŌüĀweek, a Norwegian court ŌĆŗsaid in a ruling on ŌĆŗFriday.
The U.S. embassy was hit by an explosion on Sunday and police later ŌĆŗsaid they had apprehended the ŌĆŗsuspects, accusing them of a "terror bombing" ŌĆīintended ŌüĀto kill or cause significant damage.
The powerful early-morning blast from an improvised explosive device (IED) ŌĆŗdamaged the ŌĆŗentrance ŌüĀto the embassy's consular section but caused ŌĆŗno injuries, Norwegian authorities ŌĆŗhave ŌüĀsaid.
One of the men has admitted to placing a ŌüĀdevice, ŌĆŗwhile the three ŌĆŗother suspects have denied involvement, their lawyers ŌĆŗhave said.

item.title: Exclusive: Iran lets two gas tankers sail to India through Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 13 (Reuters) - Iran has allowed two Indian-flagged liquefied petroleum gas carriers to sail through the Strait of Hormuz, four sources with direct knowledge of the matter said, a ŌĆŗrare exception to the Iranian blockade that has disrupted global energy supplies.
The Indian-flagged LPG tanker Shivalik ŌĆīcrossed the Strait under escort from the Indian Navy, said two of the sources, and the second vessel, Nanda Devi, was expected to clear in the next few hours.
Since the United States and Israel launched a bombing campaign on Iran, Tehran has largely halted ŌĆŗtraffic through the strait, which runs past its coast and normally supplies around 20% of global oil ŌĆŗand seaborne liquefied natural gas.
Iran has said it will not permit any supplies for ŌüĀthe United States or its allies to leave the strait, but India has sought exemptions.
