Prime Minister Narendra Modi said ŌĆŗon Thursday he had spoken to Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian and discussed the transit of goods and energy from ŌĆŗthe Gulf.
India has also given safe harbour to 183 Iranian sailors from a vessel that docked after the war broke out. The vessel was one of three that had participated in exercises in India; one of the others was sunk by a U.S. torpedo off Sri ŌĆŗLanka.
India is reeling under its worst gas crisis in decades with the government cutting supplies for industries to shield ŌĆŗhouseholds from any shortage of cooking gas.
Shivalik and Nanda Devi are owned by state-run Shipping Corp of India. (SCI.NS) , opens new tab
Shivalik, which had arrived in ŌĆīQatari ŌüĀwaters on February 25, last reported its position on March 12 as within the exclusive economic zone waters up to 24 miles off the United Arab Emirates, according to tracking data on MarineTraffic.
Nanda Devi was last seen on Friday in Iranian waters close to the entrance of the Strait of Hormuz, according to MarineTraffic data. It had called ŌĆŗat QatarŌĆÖs Ras Laffan anchorage ŌĆŗon February 27 before ŌüĀsailing to UAE waters, the data showed.
Separately, a crude tanker is expected to arrive in India on Saturday carrying Saudi Arabian oil after sailing through the Strait around March ŌĆŗ1, according to two of the sources and data from Lloyd's List Intelligence.
The Liberia ŌĆŗflagged Smyrni crude ŌüĀoil suezmax tanker, which can carry a maximum capacity of 1 million barrels, is expected to arrive at an Indian port for state refiner Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab .
India's foreign ministry, navy, Shipping Corp and HPCL did not respond to Reuters emails ŌüĀsent out ŌĆŗafter business hours.
India consumed 33.15 million metric tons of cooking gas ŌĆŗlast year, with imports accounting for about 60% of demand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle East.
On Thursday India said 24 Indian-flagged ŌĆŗvessels were stuck in the Gulf area past the narrow strait.

item.title: US proposes easing limits on cancer-causing gas used to clean medical devices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - The Trump administration is proposing to lift some limits on the use of ethylene oxide, a carcinogenic gas used for sterilization, saying ŌĆŗprevious constraints imposed under former President Joe Biden may hamper medical device makers' ŌĆīability to clean their facilities.
In a statement on Friday, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency said its proposed rule would follow current law but make it easier for companies to ŌĆŗfollow commercial sterilization regulations while also saving costs and protecting the ŌĆŗnation's supply chain for devices such as heart stents and wound ŌüĀdressings.
The medical community, industry officials and the Food and Drug Administration all ŌĆŗexpressed concerns about the more stringent rule issued in 2024 under the Democratic ŌĆŗBiden administration, which included a second required risk review, as well as standards that required new monitoring systems, vents and enclosures.
The new proposal would allow medical device companies a choice ŌĆŗbetween installing new monitoring systems or making other adjustments to new aeration ŌĆŗroom vents in areas where ethylene oxide is greater than 10 tons per year.
"These changes ŌĆībetter ŌüĀreflect the complexity of facilities and give them the flexibility to work to use safe and effective equipment to sterilize medical tools and devices without compromising clean air for all Americans," the EPA said in a statement.
They would ŌĆŗalso save an estimated $43 ŌĆŗmillion annually, ŌüĀit added.
Ethylene oxide, or EtO, is a colorless gas used to sterilize equipment, but its toxic emissions pollute the air. ŌĆŗLong-term exposure has been linked to cancer, especially for ŌĆŗpeople living ŌüĀclose to manufacturing plants that make or use it.
The gas is used to sterilize about half of all U.S. medical devices made each year, according to the ŌüĀEPA.
The ŌĆŗproposed rule will face a public hearing in ŌĆŗabout 15 days and is open to public comment for 45 days before the administration makes ŌĆŗa final decision.

item.title: Britain's energy price-cap forecast to rise about 11%, Cornwall Insight says
item.description:
LONDON, March 13 (Reuters) - Britain's domestic energy price cap is forecast to rise by about 11% in July, analysts ŌĆŗat Cornwall Insight said on Friday, increasing ŌĆītheir forecast due to the ongoing impact of the Iran war on wholesale energy prices.
British finance minister Rachel Reeves said ŌĆŗon Wednesday she would consider action to shield households ŌĆŗfrom surging energy costs due to the ŌüĀconflict in the Middle East, but that it was ŌĆŗpremature at this stage.
Wholesale gas prices have risen more ŌĆŗthan 60% over the past two weeks. Those prices are the largest single factor contributing to British energy regulator Ofgem's ŌĆŗdomestic price cap, which is set quarterly using ŌĆŗa formula that also reflects suppliers' network costs and environmental and ŌĆīsocial ŌüĀlevies.
Disruptions to shipping in the Middle East and a halt to liquefied natural gas exports from Qatar, one of the world's largest gas exporters, has led ŌĆŗto a surge ŌĆŗin international ŌüĀgas prices which is showing no signs of abating.
Cornwall Insight forecasts Ofgem's price ŌĆŗcap will rise in July to 1,827 ŌĆŗpounds ($2,422) ŌüĀa year for typical use, from 1,641 pounds in April.
Last week they had forecast a rise of 10% ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe cap.
Ofgem is expected to ŌĆŗpublish the next price cap level by May 27.
($1 = 0.7545 pounds)

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras expects little impact on consumers from diesel price hike
item.description:
SAO PAULO/RIO DE JANEIRO, March 13 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run ŌĆīoil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab will hike the price of diesel sold to distributors by 0.38 reais ($0.0725) per liter, but expects next to no impact on consumers, CEO Magda Chambriard said on Friday.
The Petrobras hike starting on Saturday should lead to an added cost of ŌĆŗup to 0.06 reais per liter for consumers, Chambriard told journalists, calling it "residual" as federal tax cuts ŌĆŗease the impact.
"Petrobras is following its commercial strategy, avoiding passing on the volatility of ŌüĀinternational prices, and the government is doing its part by eliminating federal taxes," said Chambriard.
The move comes after ŌĆŗthe recent spike in oil prices due to the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran , which widened the gap between Petrobras' local diesel ŌĆŗprices and international benchmarks to a record level in recent days.
Diesel prices have become a major source of concern for Brazil's President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, who is running for reelection this year.
On Thursday, Lula's government scrapped taxes on diesel in a bid ŌĆŗto soften the impact of higher global prices on local consumers, while imposing a 12% levy on oil ŌĆŗexports to compensate for the lost revenue.
The Petrobras CEO said the export tax will be more than offset by higher oil ŌĆīprices. Exports ŌüĀhave become a major driver of the company's revenue growth, as it sold record volumes of oil abroad in the last quarter.
"I was exporting for $60," said Chambriard. "Now it's at $100. Can I complain about a temporary 12% tax?"
The CEO added that the higher oil prices are "good" for dividends to shareholders and said that shareholders - both the government ŌĆŗand private ones - are happy ŌĆŗwith the firm.
While most of the diesel Brazil consumes is produced locally by Petrobras, about 25% of the fuel is imported, leaving the country vulnerable to price hikes ŌĆŗabroad.
The imbalance between local and international prices was making distributors reluctant to sell at ŌĆŗPetrobras' prices, ŌüĀas they were worried they would have to resupply at higher cost.
To increase diesel production, Petrobras is operating its refineries at around 97% of capacity and postponed maintenance stoppages at two of them, Chambriard said.
Last year, the company was operating ŌüĀrefineries ŌĆŗat around 91% of capacity, and had previously announced it would raise ŌĆŗit to 95% over the first quarter.
"We are making an effort to deliver more product to the Brazilian market," she said.
($1 = 5.2441 reais)

item.title: Storms cut power to 396,000 customers in US Midwest, Mid-Atlantic; Ohio hardest hit
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 13 (Reuters) - Over 396,000 homes ŌĆŗand businesses were without ŌĆīpower on Friday as severe storms battered the U.S. ŌĆŗMidwest and Mid-Atlantic ŌĆŗregion, according to data from ŌüĀPowerOutage.us.
The hardest-hit state ŌĆŗwas Ohio, with around ŌĆŗ123,000 outages, representing about 2.3% of the state's roughly 5.4 ŌĆŗmillion customers, according to ŌĆŗthe data.
The hardest-hit utility in ŌĆīOhio ŌüĀwas a unit of American Electric Power (AEP.O) , opens new tab , with around 40,000 homes and ŌĆŗbusinesses in ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀstate without service. AEP provides service ŌĆŗto around 1.5 ŌĆŗmillion ŌüĀelectric customers in Ohio, the data showed.

item.title: US offers reward for information on Iran's supreme leader, senior officials
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The United States is offering ŌĆŗa reward of up to $10 million for information about senior Iranian military and intelligence ŌĆīofficials, including its new Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei.
The reward targets 10 officials associated with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), according to the State Department website. The military force, created after IranŌĆÖs 1979 Islamic Revolution, is ŌĆŗloyal to the supreme leader and tasked with protecting the ShiŌĆÖite clerical establishment.
Mojtaba Khamenei ŌĆŗrecently succeeded his father, Ali Khamenei, as Iran's supreme leader after the elder Khamenei ŌüĀwas killed along with several other top Iranian officials in joint U.S. and Israeli strikes ŌĆŗthat began on February 28. The younger Khamenei, believed to have been injured in the strikes , hasn't ŌĆŗbeen seen publicly since, although he released his first statement on Thursday.
In addition to the supreme leader, the U.S. is seeking information about Iran's security chief Ali Larijani, Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib, Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni and ŌĆŗtwo officials in Khamenei's office.
Larijani appeared Friday in videos verified by Reuters alongside President Masoud Pezeshkian ŌĆŗand Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi attending a rally in Tehran, despite an assertion by U.S. Defense Secretary Pete ŌĆīHegseth ŌüĀthat Iran's leadership was "cowering" underground.
The reward website also lists four other officials, including the IRGC commander and secretary of the defense council, but doesn't include their names or photos.
"These individuals command and direct various elements of the IRGC, which plans, organizes, and executes terrorism around the world," the ŌĆŗState Department said.
The Revolutionary Guards ŌĆŗcould not be ŌüĀimmediately reached for comment on Friday ŌĆö the weekly day of rest in Iran. Iran's mission to the United Nations in New York didn't immediately ŌĆŗrespond to a request for comment.
The U.S. has designated the IRGC as ŌĆŗa foreign ŌüĀterrorist organization, accusing it of being responsible for attacks that have killed U.S. citizens. Washington has also accused Iran of orchestrating assassination plots against President Donald Trump and other U.S. officials in retaliation for ŌüĀthe killing ŌĆŗof Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020.
Iran denies being ŌĆŗa sponsor of terrorism. Iranian officials routinely dismiss U.S. terrorism allegations as baseless political attacks, arguing Washington raises such claims ŌĆŗto justify pressure campaigns or sanctions.

item.title: Zelenskiy tells Ukrainian media US sought postponement of latest talks on war settlement
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy was quoted as saying ŌĆŗon Friday the U.S. ŌĆīhad sought a postponement of the latest round of ŌĆŗthree-sided talks on ŌĆŗa settlement to Ukraine's four-year-old ŌüĀconflict with Russia.
Zelenskiy, ŌĆŗwhose comments were quoted by ŌĆŗvarious Ukrainian media outlets at the end of a visit ŌĆŗto France, said ŌĆŗthe U.S. side said its negotiators ŌĆīwere ŌüĀnot permitted to leave the United States in view of circumstances in ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗMiddle ŌüĀEast.
He said Russia had not wanted to ŌĆŗhold the talks ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe United States and had proposed alternative sites ŌüĀin ŌĆŗSwitzerland or Turkey.

item.title: US expands Venezuela sanctions waivers amid rising prices
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 13 (Reuters) - The United States on Friday ŌĆŗupdated waivers to Venezuela sanctions ŌĆīaimed at easing the way for investment and activities related to the ŌĆŗcountry's oil and the export ŌĆŗof fertilizer, as prices rise ŌüĀamid U.S. strikes on Iran.
The ŌĆŗU.S. Treasury Department issued three updated ŌĆŗgeneral licenses on Friday as part of the move. In a post on ŌĆŗX, the department said it "updated ŌĆŗseveral Venezuela-related licenses to further support the revitalization ŌĆīof ŌüĀthe countryŌĆÖs energy sector and help ensure a well-supplied global commodity market."
"These authorizations expand permitted investment ŌĆŗand activities ŌĆŗin ŌüĀVenezuelaŌĆÖs energy industry and allow for the export ŌĆŗof fertilizer directly to the ŌĆŗU.S. ŌüĀto support our great American farmers," the Treasury Department said in ŌüĀa ŌĆŗpost on X.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 6.5 strikes near coast of central Chile, GFZ says
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - A magnitude ŌĆŗ6.5 ŌĆīearthquake struck near ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗcoast of ŌüĀcentral ŌĆŗChile ŌĆŗon Friday, the German ŌĆŗResearch ŌĆŗCentre for ŌĆīGeosciences (GFZ) ŌüĀsaid.
The quake was at ŌĆŗa ŌĆŗdepth ŌüĀof ŌĆŗ10 ŌĆŗkm (6.21 ŌüĀmiles), GFZ ŌüĀsaid.

item.title: US-owned tanker attacked near Iraq was hit by unmanned boats, early findings show
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 13 (Reuters) - Two explosive-laden, unmanned ŌĆīboats rammed the Safesea Vishnu tanker in an Iraqi seaport on Wednesday, sparking a fiery blast that engulfed the vessel's port side in flames and left crew only seconds to react, according to an early assessment conducted by the vessel's ŌĆŗU.S. owner and operator.
"After speaking to the surviving crew members, the attack appears to have ŌĆŗbeen deliberate and calculated," New Jersey-based Safesea Group said in a statement.
At least ŌüĀ16 tankers and other vessels have come under attack in the Gulf during the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran. ŌĆŗHundreds more have dropped anchor due to Tehran's threat to attack vessels in or near the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, which is used to transport roughly one-fifth of the world's oil.
The Marshall Islands-flagged Safesea Vishnu was anchored at the Iraqi port of Khor Al Zubair and engaged in ship-to-ship loading of 53,000 metric tons of naphtha at ŌĆŗthe time of the attack.
The vessel's 28 crew members, without time to deploy lifeboats, jumped into ŌĆŗthe water to escape the burning vessel. One died; the remaining 27 crew are safe and receiving assistance from ŌĆīthe Embassy ŌüĀof India in Iraq, Safesea said. The tanker is reported to be listing in the water, and a salvage team has been sent to stabilize the ship and make sure the surrounding marine environment is safe.
The attack must serve as a wake-up call for governments, maritime authorities, and the international community, ŌĆŗSafesea said.
"Commercial shipping lanes ŌĆŗcannot become battle zones," ŌüĀit said.
The Malta-flagged Zefyros was the other ship involved in the transfer. A projectile struck that vessel during the Wednesday night assault, its Greece-based manager ŌĆŗsaid on Thursday. All 23 crew on the Zefyros were safely evacuated.
Some 20,000 ŌĆŗseafarers aboard ŌüĀvessels operating in the region face "a dangerous and highly uncertain security situation," according to the World Shipping Council.
U.S. President Donald Trump has said the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when ŌüĀnecessary, ŌĆŗbut so far the U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests ŌĆŗfrom the shipping industry for military escorts since the start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too ŌĆŗhigh for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.

item.title: UN official calls for humanitarian cargo to be allowed through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A top United Nations humanitarian official on Friday called for humanitarian aid to be ŌĆŗallowed to pass safely through the Strait of ŌĆīHormuz amid the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
The comments made by Tom Fletcher, the U.N. under-secretary-general for humanitarian affairs and emergency relief coordinator, comes as ŌĆŗIran threatens to close the strait , which is considered a key ŌĆŗtrade route .
"When routes close and costs surge, the help ŌüĀwe can deliver shrinks ŌĆō and the people who need ŌĆŗit most are the ones who lose it first," Fletcher ŌĆŗsaid in a statement.
"So my message to the parties to the conflict and all those with influence over them is simple: humanitarian cargo ŌĆŗmust be allowed to pass safely through the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz."
The war started two weeks ago when the U.S. and Israel carried out ŌĆīstrikes ŌüĀon Iran, killing top officials, including its supreme leader. Hundreds others have also died since the Feb. 28 start of the war, which has spread across the Gulf region.
Iran retaliated ŌĆŗto the initial ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli attack ŌüĀby launching its own strikes and deploying about a dozen mines in the strait.
The ongoing conflict ŌĆŗhas already caused a near halt in traffic ŌĆŗthrough ŌüĀthe Strait of Hormuz, Fletcher said. This, he added, will make it harder and more expensive to deliver critical supplies, including ŌüĀfood ŌĆŗand medicine.
"I am speaking directly with ŌĆŗkey parties, pressing for humanitarian supplies to be allowed to keep moving unobstructed ŌĆŗthrough the Strait," he said.

item.title: Refiner MOL complains to EU over Croatian pipeline fees
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 13 (Reuters) - Hungarian refiner MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab said on Friday it and subsidiary Slovnaft had ŌĆŗfiled a complaint with the European Commission ŌĆīover Croatian pipeline operator JANAF's pricing policy.
It follows a complaint MOL filed last week with the EU's competition ŌĆŗwatchdog over JANAF's refusal to deliver ŌĆŗRussian crude to MOL and its Slovak ŌüĀsubsidiary.
An outage since January on the Druzhba pipeline delivering ŌĆŗRussian oil via Ukraine has left landlocked Hungary ŌĆŗand Slovakia reliant on oil supply via JANAF.
"JANAF has consistently applied abusive pricing practices," MOL said in its ŌĆŗlatest complaint, adding its orders by volume ŌĆŗhad increased by one and a half times while ŌĆīJANAF's ŌüĀfees had nearly doubled.
"The fee increases imposed by JANAF are excessive compared to its costs and cannot be objectively justified."
Janaf did not immediately ŌĆŗto a ŌĆŗrequest for ŌüĀcomment.
Hungary and Slovakia hold exemptions to EU restrictions on Russian oil imports.
MOL ŌĆŗis allowed to source Russian seaborne ŌĆŗcrude ŌüĀif the Druzhba pipeline is inoperable, the company has said.
Croatia has expressed its willingness to help ŌüĀsupply ŌĆŗcrude, but has baulked at ŌĆŗsending Russian crude via the JANAF's pipeline.

item.title: Iran war puts many in US on high alert, but synagogue attack shows limits
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. law enforcement officials are on heightened alert ŌĆŗas the Iran war enters its third week, but the limits of their vigilance were on display as seeming lone-wolf attacks unfolded more than 500 miles apart in Virginia and Michigan on ŌĆīThursday alone.
In Michigan, where a Lebanon-born man rammed an explosives-laden truck into the Temple Israel synagogue, it appeared to be one of the first known instances of a specific bombing in the Iran war leading to retaliatory violence on American soil since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28.
Although authorities have not announced a motive, the man, identified by U.S. officials as Ayman Ghazali, carried out his attack a week after Israel bombed his family's town in Lebanon on March 5, killing two of his brothers ŌĆŗand a niece and nephew, according to news reports.
Around the same time as the synagogue attack, a man who was previously convicted of supporting a designated terrorist group fatally shot one person and injured ŌĆŗtwo others, both U.S. Army personnel, at Old Dominion University in Norfolk, Virginia, which has close ties to the U.S. military. The attacker was killed.
These kinds of lone-wolf outbursts of retaliatory violence are the hardest to spot in advance and intercept, counter-terrorism experts say.
"Someone who is self-radicalized, a lone wolf, is the hardest to track," said Kenneth Gray, a former ŌĆŗFBI counter-terrorism agent who now teaches at the University of New Haven. "Normally there would be opportunities to track weapon transfers, foreign training, or transfers of funds. We apparently have none of that."
The latest incidents took place with the ŌĆŗU.S. engaged in what is already a deeply unpopular war among American voters, which was launched after President Donald Trump made drastic cuts to the Department of Homeland Security's intelligence unit.
Except for a security guard knocked down when the truck crashed through the entrance, everyone at the synagogue, including the children at its preschool, escaped unharmed, although dozens of law enforcement officers were treated for smoke inhalation. Ghazali, who became a U.S. citizen in 2016 and lived in nearby Dearborn Heights, was killed by synagogue security staff.
Shawn Brokos, ŌĆŗwho was an FBI official in Pittsburgh when 11 people were killed in the Tree of Life synagogue shooting in 2018, credited Temple Israel for a "textbook reaction" that protected those inside.
The attack also showed the need for ŌĆŗvigilance, she said, to be alert for someone who might be embracing "a grievance narrative."
"They're upset about current events," said Brokos, now the director of community security for the Jewish Federation of Greater Pittsburgh. "They're starting to access weapons. They're starting to make ŌĆīplans. These ŌüĀare all the things that are helpful for us to know so we can do these checks and share it with law enforcement."
Dearborn Heights Mayor Mo Baydoun said Ghazali's attack must be unequivocally condemned, and also noted Ghazali had been affected by the Iran war, in which more than 2,000 people have been killed , mostly in Iran and Lebanon.
"The tensions we see across the world too often find their way into our own neighborhoods, reminding us how deeply connected our shared safety is," Baydoun said in a statement.
Since the war began, security scares have affected airports in Kansas City and the Washington, D.C. suburbs; two men were charged with terrorism-related ŌĆŗcrimes after throwing home-made bombs at an anti-Muslim rally ŌĆŗin New York City; and a man who expressed ŌüĀpro-Iran views online killed four people after opening fire at a bar in Austin, Texas.
Trump has sought to defend his war to some lawmakers in Congress and, according to polls , many skeptical voters, including some of his "America First" base.
The Department of Homeland Security communicates significant threats to the American public through alerts under the National Terrorism Advisory ŌĆŗSystem. DHS issued such an alert in June 2025, saying brief hostilities between the U.S. and Israel and Iran had caused a "heightened threat environment" in the U.S.
That ŌĆŗalert, which cited the risk ŌüĀof cyber attacks or violence from extremists already in the U.S., expired in September 2025.
There are no current NTAS advisories. And, last week, the White House halted a bulletin from DHS, the FBI and the National Counterterrorism Center warning state and local law enforcement agencies of a heightened threat to the U.S. because of the Iran war.
Since Trump's return to the White House last year with vows to slash the federal workforce, the DHS Intelligence and Analysis unit went from about ŌüĀ1,000 employees to ŌĆŗsome 500-600, three former agency officials told Reuters. DHS did not respond to a request for comment.
Travis Nelson, director of Maryland's homeland ŌĆŗsecurity office, said DHS has been focused on Trump's mass deportation efforts in its public communications rather than the possible threat posed by the Iran conflict.
"It's all about immigration enforcement," Nelson said. "We haven't seen anything from Homeland Security about the potential risk to the homeland as a ŌĆŗresult of what's going on in the Middle East."

item.title: Five US Air Force refueling planes hit in Iranian strike on Saudi Arabia, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Five U.S. Air Force refueling planes ŌĆŗwere struck and ŌĆīdamaged on the ground at Prince Sultan ŌĆŗair base in ŌĆŗSaudi Arabia, the Wall ŌüĀStreet Journal reported ŌĆŗon Friday, citing two ŌĆŗU.S. officials.
The planes, which were hit during an ŌĆŗIranian missile strike ŌĆŗon the Saudi base in ŌĆīrecent ŌüĀdays, were damaged but not fully destroyed and are ŌĆŗbeing repaired, ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀJournal said, adding that no ŌĆŗone was killed ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe strikes.
Reuters could not immediately verify ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗreport.

item.title: US attacks Iran's Kharg Island, Trump says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗon Friday Said ŌĆīon Friday that the United States carried ŌĆŗout strikes against "every military ŌĆŗtarget" on Iran's Kharg Island ŌüĀexport hub.
"For ŌĆŗreasons of decency, I ŌĆŗhave chosen NOT to wipe out the Oil ŌĆŗInfrastructure on ŌĆŗthe Island," Trump wrote on ŌĆīTruth ŌüĀSocial.
"However, should Iran, or anyone else, do anything to ŌĆŗinterfere ŌĆŗwith ŌüĀthe Free and Safe Passage ŌĆŗof Ships through ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀStrait of Hormuz, I will immediately ŌüĀreconsider ŌĆŗthis decision."

item.title: Ecuador declares curfew in coastal provinces for security operations
item.description:
QUITO, March 13 (Reuters) - Ecuador's President Daniel Noboa on Friday decreed a nighttime ŌĆŗcurfew in four provinces of the Andean ŌĆīcountry, starting March 15, intended to facilitate military operations against criminal organizations with support from the ŌĆŗUnited States.
The measure covers the coastal ŌĆŗprovinces of Guayas, El Oro, Santo ŌüĀDomingo, and Los Rios - the country's main ŌĆŗdrug trafficking routes - and will run from ŌĆŗ11 p.m. to 5:00 a.m. local time.
Joint military operations between Ecuador and the U.S. started in ŌĆŗearly March. A recent mission near the ŌĆŗColombian border resulted in the destruction of a drug ŌĆītrafficking ŌüĀcamp, according to the Ecuadorean Ministry of Defense.
The site was operated by a group known as the Border Commandos, comprised ŌĆŗof FARC ŌĆŗdissidents, and ŌüĀhad the capacity to house 50 people, the ministry added.
Noboa ŌĆŗhas made military repression against organized ŌĆŗcrime ŌüĀa cornerstone of his administration. As part of his hardline stance he has criticized the ŌüĀgovernment ŌĆŗof neighboring Colombia for ŌĆŗfailing to combat drug trafficking, and implemented tariffs on Colombian imports.

item.title: China urges Afghanistan, Pakistan to resolve tensions via talks, not force
item.description:
SHANGHAI, March 14 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi told his Afghan counterpart Amir ŌĆŗKhan Muttaqi in a phone call ŌĆīthat disputes between Afghanistan and Pakistan should be resolved through dialogue and consultation, not ŌĆŗforce, the foreign ministry said in ŌĆŗa statement published on Friday.
Wang urged ŌüĀboth sides to remain calm and ŌĆŗexercise restraint, hold face-to-face talks as soon ŌĆŗas possible, seek an immediate ceasefire and address differences through dialogue, according to the statement.
The ŌĆŗfurther use of force would only ŌĆŗcomplicate the situation and intensify tensions, Wang said.
The ŌĆīministry's ŌüĀstatement came after Pakistan on Friday bombed the fuel depot of private airline Kam Air near Afghanistan's Kandahar airport, stepping ŌĆŗup the ŌĆŗneighbours' worst ŌüĀconflict in years, despite China's efforts to mediate.
Wang and ŌĆŗMuttaqi also exchanged views on the ŌĆŗsituation ŌüĀin Iran , the statement said.
Wang told Muttaqi, according to the statement, that Beijing is willing ŌüĀto ŌĆŗwork with the international ŌĆŗcommunity, including Afghanistan, to bring peace to Iran.

item.title: Palestinian actor says he can't attend Oscars because of US travel ban
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - Palestinian actor Motaz Malhees said on Friday that a travel ban imposed by the Trump ŌĆŗadministration is preventing him from attending this weekend's ŌĆīAcademy Awards, whose nominees include a movie in which he has a starring role.
"The Voice of Hind Rajab," a film about a ŌĆŗfive-year-old Palestinian girl killed by Israeli forces in Gaza ŌĆŗin 2024, has been Oscar-nominated for best international ŌüĀfeature film.
Malhees, who plays the role of a call ŌĆŗcenter operator attempting to help her, said he cannot ŌĆŗattend the Academy Awards show, also known as the Oscars, because he has been barred from entering the United States.
"I am not allowed ŌĆŗto enter the United States because of my Palestinian ŌĆŗcitizenship," Malhees wrote on Instagram, adding that "it hurts" that he would not attend ŌĆīthe ŌüĀOscars.
The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
In a December , opens new tab proclamation restricting entry of foreign nationals, U.S. President Donald Trump said he had "determined ŌĆŗto fully restrict ŌĆŗand limit ŌüĀthe entry of individuals using travel documents issued or endorsed by the Palestinian Authority."
The ŌĆŗproclamation restricting entry of people from some ŌĆŗcountries cited ŌüĀsecurity reasons.
The movie was spurred by an event in which five of Rajab's family members and two ambulance workers ŌüĀwho ŌĆŗwent to save her were also ŌĆŗkilled by Israeli fire. Israel says the incident is under review.

item.title: Eight convicted of terrorism-related charges for attack on Texas ICE facility
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 13 (Reuters) - A jury convicted nine ŌĆŗpeople on Friday for their involvement in a shooting at a U.S. ŌĆīImmigration and Customs Enforcement detention center in Texas last year, the Justice Department said.
Eight of the nine defendants were found guilty of providing material support to terrorists, rioting, conspiring to use and ŌĆŗcarry explosives, and using explosives during a riot, local media reported.
The conviction followed ŌĆŗa 12-day trial in Texas.
The defendants were charged for their roles ŌüĀin a non-fatal shooting of a police officer at the immigration facility on ŌĆŗJuly 4 in Alvarado, Texas.
The defendants, who authorities said were dressed in black "military-style" clothing with head and ŌĆŗface coverings when they carried out the attack on the facility, have been described the Trump administration as members of the antifa movement , which was designated a domestic terrorist organization , opens new tab by President Donald Trump ŌĆŗlast year.
The case was the first time federal prosecutors filed terrorism charges targeting antifa, a largely ŌĆŗunstructured, far-left movement whose followers broadly aim to confront those they view as authoritarian or racist.
Following ŌĆīthe ŌüĀconviction, U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi said this will not be the last case of its kind under the Trump administration.
ICE Director Todd M. Lyons welcomed the conviction, saying the summer attack by the defendants was violent in nature and "an abhorrent way ŌĆŗfor antifa terrorists to 'protest.'"
The ŌĆŗdefendants are Cameron ŌüĀArnold, Zachary Evetts, Benjamin Song, Savanna Batten, Bradford Morris, Maricela Rueda, Elizabeth Soto, Ines Soto and Daniel Rolando Sanchez-Estrada.
Lawyers for eight ŌĆŗof the nine defendants did not immediately respond to a ŌĆŗrequest for ŌüĀcomment
"We are thankful that the jury could see through the GovernmentŌĆÖs fear mongering antifa 'ambush' narrative," said Cody Cofer, a lawyer for Arnold, the lead defendant in the case.
"We are ŌüĀafraid ŌĆŗthat our clientŌĆÖs guilty verdicts for Riot and ŌĆŗrelated charges resulted from a compromise during deliberations or failure to consider our clientŌĆÖs individual conduct."
Cofer expressed an ŌĆŗintent to appeal the case.

item.title: US says oil from strategic reserve to start reaching market next week
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - The Trump administration said on ŌĆŗFriday it expects early deliveries of ŌĆīoil from the U.S. strategic petroleum reserve to reach the market by the end ŌĆŗof next week as part of ŌĆŗan effort to combat the energy price ŌüĀspike since the start of the Iran ŌĆŗwar.
The oil will be released in ŌĆŗthe form of loans that companies will return with extra barrels as a premium, the U.S. ŌĆŗEnergy Department said in a statement. ŌĆŗThis system was meant to stabilize markets "at no cost ŌĆīto ŌüĀAmerican taxpayers," it said.
The Energy Department said it had requested companies to bid for 86 million barrels of crude ŌĆŗoil, which ŌĆŗwill be ŌüĀthe first from the release of 172 million barrels that Washington ŌĆŗannounced this week.
The U.S. effort ŌĆŗis ŌüĀpart of a broader release of 400 million barrels of oil agreed to by the ŌüĀ32-nation ŌĆŗInternational Energy Agency on ŌĆŗWednesday.

item.title: Barclays raises 2026 Brent forecast to $85 a barrel on Strait of Hormuz disruption
item.description:
March 13 (Reuters) - Barclays on Friday raised its 2026 Brent crude forecast to $85 per barrel, ŌĆŗciting ongoing supply disruptions linked to the ŌĆīIran war that have sharply reduced oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
The bank said ŌĆŗoil flows through the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz route have fallen to a trickle ŌüĀand production shut-ins in Gulf countries ŌĆŗhave climbed to more than 10 million barrels ŌĆŗper day.
The revised forecast assumes the situation in the Strait of Hormuz normalizes within two to ŌĆŗthree weeks, Barclays said in a research ŌĆŗnote.
"If however, the market internalizes that it might take ŌĆīfour ŌüĀto six weeks for that, we could see 2026 Brent reprice to $100/b," the bank added.
Oil prices have risen despite efforts ŌĆŗby the International Energy ŌĆŗAgency ŌüĀto release strategic reserves, as uncertainty around the duration of the ŌĆŗconflict continues to support markets.
Brent futures ŌĆŗon ŌüĀFriday settled at $103.14 a barrel, up $2.68, or 2.67%.
Barclays said the path of least resistance ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗoil prices remains higher ŌĆŗuntil there is a inflection point in the conflict.

item.title: Cyclone Vaianu lashes New Zealand's North Island, hundreds evacuated
item.description:
April 12 (Reuters) - Cyclone Vaianu made landfall in New Zealand's ŌĆŗNorth Island on Sunday, triggering floods, power outages and forcing hundreds to evacuate.
The cyclone crossed the coast ŌĆīnear the Maketu Peninsula, packing destructive winds exceeding 130 kph (80 mph), heavy rain and large swells, national weather provider MetService said, describing Vaianu as a "life-threatening" system.
Authorities have placed several regions under emergency declarations and issued "red" level wind warnings, which are reserved for only the most extreme weather events.
The ŌĆŗcyclone was tracking towards the fringes of the North Island, sparing Auckland, the country's most populous city, from the ŌĆŗworst conditions, said Emergency Management Minister Mark Mitchell. But stronger winds and swells were still expected ŌüĀafter its afternoon landfall.
"ItŌĆÖs moved more to the fringes and more to the east, which means that we haven't quite ŌĆŗseen the intensity that we had prepared for or that we thought we were going to get hit with. So that ŌĆŗis good news," Mitchell said.
"In terms of what we're going to expect over the next 12 hours is we are going to see a bit more of intensification, as the cyclone starts to come down and get closer."
The combination of the afternoon's high tide and large swells from ŌĆŗthe storm could trigger coastal inundation, Mitchell warned.
"The concerning time is really from 2 p.m. this afternoon (0800 GMT) onwards when ŌĆŗwe've got high tides combined with those big swells," he said.
The cyclone has forced hundreds of residents to evacuate and knocked out electricity to ŌĆī5,000 ŌüĀhomes, with power restored to roughly 2,000, he said.
Authorities in the coastal Whakatane District reported a significant amount of damage as Vaianu passed through, with mandatory evacuations carried out at 270 properties.
New Zealand Defence Force members and heavy equipment have also been deployed to assist with evacuations.
MetService said it had recorded 130 kph wind gusts in some areas, 24-hour rainfall totals of more ŌĆŗthan 100 mm (4 inches) in the ŌĆŗcity of Whangarei and ŌüĀwave heights exceeding six metres (20 feet).
Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab said in a statement that it had cancelled more than 90 turboprop flights, primarily out of regional North Island airports.
"Domestic jet and international ŌĆŗservices are operating as scheduled, although there are some delays due to the weather conditions," the ŌĆŗairline said.
Fire and ŌüĀEmergency New Zealand said it responded to more than 100 calls for assistance relating to wind damage and surface flooding.
Conditions will ease as Vaianu works its way down the North Island before exiting on Sunday evening at Hawke's Bay, MetService said.
"Things do improve ŌüĀfrom tonight ŌĆŗand tomorrow, but at the moment this is still a life-threatening weather system," ŌĆŗsaid Heather Keats, MetService head of weather news.
Vaianu has conjured up memories of 2023's Cyclone Gabrielle, which killed 11 and displaced thousands in one of New Zealand's ŌĆŗbiggest natural disasters this century.

item.title: Vineyard Wind sues GE Vernova to block it from abandoning work on wind farm
item.description:
BOSTON, April 10 (Reuters) - Vineyard Wind has filed a lawsuit seeking to block a wind turbine-supplying unit of GE Vernova (GEV.N) , opens new tab from terminating agreements and abandoning work on a $4.5 ŌĆŗbillion offshore wind farm in Massachusetts on the eve of completion.
Vineyard Wind, a ŌĆījoint venture between Spain's Iberdrola (IBE.MC) , opens new tab and Denmark's Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners, filed a lawsuit , opens new tab this week in a state court in Boston accusing the GE Vernova subsidiary of wrongly trying to terminate their contract at the ŌĆŗwind project's "most vulnerable stage."
It is one of five major East Coast offshore wind ŌĆŗprojects that President Donald Trump's administration halted construction near the end of ŌüĀ2025, citing national security.
Federal judges following lawsuits allowed construction to resume on all five.
After resuming ŌĆŗconstruction, Vineyard Wind's 806-megawatt project off the coast of Martha's Vineyard in February became the ŌĆŗfirst large-scale offshore wind farm in the United States to begin initial operations.
But days later, GE Renewables US LLC, which designed, manufactured and installed Vineyard Wind's wind turbine generators, sent it a notice threatening to ŌĆŗend their turbine supply agreement, the lawsuit said.
Vineyard Wind, in a complaint dated Wednesday, ŌĆŗsaid GE did so citing a failure to pay amounts it was contractually due under their $1.3 billion ŌĆīcontract.
Vineyard Wind ŌüĀsaid it had exercised its right to withhold about $308 million as a result of GE's "admittedly poor performance," after one of its offshore blades in 2024 collapsed and fell into the waters off Nantucket, causing delays.
Vineyard Wind called GE's termination of the agreements improper and has asked ŌĆŗa judge to ŌĆŗblock GE from ceasing ŌüĀwork on the wind project, which "threatens to deprive hundreds of thousands of Massachusetts ratepayers of clean energy."
A judge has scheduled an April ŌĆŗ16 hearing to consider whether to issue a preliminary injunction.
GE Vernova ŌĆŗin a ŌüĀstatement said it had elected to terminate its agreements due to Vineyard Wind withholding the money. The company said it did so after completing the installation of all 62 wind turbines ŌüĀat the ŌĆŗwind farm.
"The company remains committed to the safety of ŌĆŗthe wind farm and stands by our performance and our contractual obligations," GE Vernova said. "We will vigorously defend our ŌĆŗposition through the appropriate legal process."

item.title: Iberdrola puts $1 billion solar stake sale on hold, sources say
item.description:
MADRID, April 10 (Reuters) - Iberdrola (IBE.MC) , opens new tab has put on hold for now the attempted sale of a minority stake in ŌĆŗa roughly 1-gigawatt Spanish solar energy portfolio, according ŌĆīto two people familiar with the matter.
The Spanish company decided to explore the sale last year, seeking a valuation of around $1 billion for ŌĆŗthe whole portfolio known as Project Julieta, one of ŌĆŗthe sources said.
Yet the size of the valuation ŌüĀcomplicated talks, one of the people said, while the other ŌĆŗpointed to waning interest from one unnamed bidder seen until ŌĆŗrecently as the frontrunner.
Iberdrola said it doesn't comment on what it called market speculation.
Spain's solar power capacity has increased rapidly in recent years, ŌĆŗwhile electricity demand hasn't kept pace, hitting the prices ŌĆŗthat producers get for their output and the value of their generating ŌĆīassets.
For ŌüĀIberdrola, asset rotation and partnerships - which include the disposal of non-core or mature assets and the sale of minority stakes - have been key elements of its strategy over recent ŌĆŗyears.
The company uses ŌĆŗproceeds from ŌüĀsuch deals to help fund new investments in core businesses, increasingly power grids. It set ŌĆŗa target to raise 13.2 billion euros ŌĆŗthrough 2028, 58% ŌüĀof which would come from partnerships and the rest from asset rotation. Three quarters of this has already been achieved.
It ŌüĀhas ŌĆŗpartnered with the likes of Japan's Kansai ŌĆŗElectric Power , Norway's sovereign wealth fund and Abu Dhabi's state-owned renewable energy company Masdar .

item.title: Dutch government to appeal ruling in Bonaire climate case
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 10 (Reuters) - The Dutch state is appealing a court ruling that it ŌĆŗmust set binding emissions targets ŌĆīto reach net zero by 2050 to protect the Caribbean island of ŌĆŗBonaire, a spokesperson for Climate ŌĆŗMinister Stientje van Veldhoven said ŌüĀon Friday.
Bonaire, a special municipality ŌĆŗof the Netherlands located in the ŌĆŗsouthern Caribbean and until 1954 a Dutch colony, is well-known as an eco-tourism ŌĆŗdestination.
In its ruling, the court ŌĆŗhad given the Netherlands 18 months to set ŌĆīup ŌüĀa legally binding plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to net zero by 2050 as agreed ŌĆŗin international ŌĆŗtreaties.
The ŌüĀcase had been brought by Greenpeace on behalf ŌĆŗof Bonaire residents and was ŌĆŗamong ŌüĀthe first to test national climate obligations set out in a ŌüĀlandmark ŌĆŗ2024 European climate ruling ŌĆŗand last year's influential World Court advisory opinion .

item.title: VietnamŌĆÖs Vingroup signs pact with India's Maharashtra to explore $6.5 bln investments
item.description:
April 10 (Reuters) - Vietnam's largest conglomerate Vingroup (VIC.HM) , opens new tab on Friday signed a memorandum of understanding with India's Maharashtra's ŌĆŗstate government to explore $6.5 billion in investments aimed at building a ŌĆīmulti-sector ecosystem in the financial capital.
The move will deepen Vingroup's footprint in India, where its electric vehicle unit VinFast operates a manufacturing facility in the ŌĆŗstate of Tamil Nadu, and where it said it ŌĆŗplans to build a $3 billion ecosystem in Telangana state.
As ŌüĀpart of the agreement, Vingroup will explore investments across ŌĆŗsectors including urban development, electric mobility, renewable energy and ŌĆŗpublic infrastructure in Maharashtra.
In urban development, the company is considering integrated townships spanning roughly 1,000 hectares near Mumbai at an estimated investment ŌĆŗof about $5 billion, while in electric mobility it ŌĆŗplans to deploy a fleet of 60,000 electric taxis at a $1.5 billion investment.
Vingroup did not ŌĆīprovide ŌüĀdetails on the investment disbursement timeline, but said that the plan is expected to create tens of thousands of jobs over the next three to five ŌĆŗyears.
Maharashtra is ŌĆŗIndia's largest ŌüĀstate economy, accounting for about 14% of the country's GDP. Major domestic EV players such ŌĆŗas Mahindra and Mahindra (MAHM.NS) , opens new tab and Tata Motors (TAMO.NS) , opens new tab ŌĆŗhave ŌüĀa strong manufacturing presence in the state.
Vingroup's operations span industrials, real estate and green energy. The company has been expanding ŌüĀinto ŌĆŗoverseas markets, with India identified as ŌĆŗa key strategic priority.

item.title: Trump says US will blockade Strait of Hormuz after Iran peace talks fail
item.description:
MIAMI/ISLAMABAD, April 12 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Sunday the U.S. Navy would start ŌĆŗblockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war , jeopardising a fragile two-week ŌĆīceasefire.
Trump also said in a post on Truth Social that the U.S. would take action against every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran, and begin destroying mines that he said the Iranians had dropped in the strait, a choke point for about 20% of global energy supplies that Iran has blocked.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," Trump wrote, adding: "Any Iranian ŌĆŗwho fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
Iran's Revolutionary Guards responded with a statement warning that military vessels approaching the strait will ŌĆŗbe considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
Iran's Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, ŌüĀwho led his country's delegation to the talks along with Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi, said Trump's new threats would have no effect on Iran.
"If you fight, we will fight, and if ŌĆŗyou come forward with logic, we will deal with logic," he said, in comments carried by state media.
Six weeks of fighting has killed thousands , roiled the global economy and sent oil prices soaring as ŌĆŗIran prevented traffic through the strait.
In an interview with Fox News after his post about the strait, Trump said that he believed Iran would continue to negotiate and called the weekend discussions "very friendly".
"I do believe they're going to come to the table on this, because nobody can be so stupid as to say, ŌĆśWe want nuclear weapons,ŌĆÖ and they have no cards," Trump told Fox News from his golf course near Miami, ŌĆŗFlorida.
Trump also said that NATO allies, whom he has criticised for failing to back the war he launched along with Israel on February 28, wanted to help with the operation in ŌĆŗthe strait.
There was no immediate comment from Washington's allies.
The weekend talks in Islamabad, which followed the announcement of a ceasefire last Tuesday, were the first direct U.S.-Iranian meeting in more than a decade and the ŌĆīhighest-level discussions ŌüĀsince the 1979 Islamic Revolution.
"The bad news is that we have not reached an agreement, and I think that's bad news for Iran much more than it's bad news for the United States of America," said Vice President JD Vance, who headed the U.S. delegation.
A U.S. official said Iran had rejected Washington's call for an end to all uranium enrichment, the dismantling of all major enrichment facilities and the transfer of highly enriched uranium. The two sides also failed to reach agreement on the U.S. demand that Iran cease funding for Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis as ŌĆŗwell as fully open the strait, the official ŌĆŗadded.
Qalibaf blamed the U.S. for not winning Tehran's ŌüĀtrust, despite his team offering "forward-looking initiatives". Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, who discussed the talks in a call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, said Tehran wanted "a balanced and fair agreement".
"If the United States returns to the framework of international law, reaching an agreement is not far off," he ŌĆŗtold Putin, Iranian state media reported.
Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency said "excessive" U.S. demands had hindered reaching a deal. Other Iranian ŌĆŗmedia said there was ŌüĀagreement on a number of issues, but the strait and Iran's nuclear programme were the main sticking points.
Despite the stalemate, three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed, in what appeared to be the first vessels to exit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal.
Israel has continued bombing Tehran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon, insisting - along with Washington - that that conflict ŌüĀwas not part ŌĆŗof the Iran-U.S. ceasefire. Iran says the fighting in Lebanon must stop.
The Israeli military said it struck Hezbollah rocket ŌĆŗlaunchers overnight into Sunday and black smoke could be seen rising in the southern suburbs of the Lebanese capital Beirut.
And in Israeli villages near the border, air raid sirens sounded, warning of incoming rocket fire from Lebanon.

item.title: Gaza aid flotilla aims to break Israeli blockade
item.description:
MADRID, April 12 (Reuters) - A second flotilla carrying humanitarian aid to Palestinians ŌĆīin Gaza was due to set sail on Sunday from the Spanish port of Barcelona, aiming to try to break the Israeli blockade.
Thirty-nine boats were due to leave the Mediterranean port city, a spokesperson for the flotilla ŌĆŗsaid, and more vessels also laden with medical aid and other supplies are expected ŌĆŗto join along the route towards Palestine .
Rough seas mean the flotilla will sail to ŌüĀanother port then head out to international waters later in the week, Thiago Avila, a ŌĆŗmember of the flotilla's organising committee, told a press conference on Sunday.
The Israeli military halted a previous flotilla ŌĆŗassembled by the same organisation last October as the boats attempted to reach blockaded Gaza, arresting Swedish activist Greta Thunberg and more than 450 other participants.
Israel, which controls all access to the Gaza Strip, ŌĆŗdenies withholding supplies for its more than 2 million residents. Yet Palestinians and international aid ŌĆŗbodies say supplies reaching the territory are still insufficient, despite a ceasefire reached in October which included guarantees of ŌĆīincreased ŌüĀaid.
Liam Cunningham, an actor who starred in the Game of Thrones television series who is supporting the flotilla but not taking part, told Reuters: "Every kilogram of aid that is on these ships is a failure because all these people on these ships giving up their time to help ŌĆŗtheir fellow human beings are ŌĆŗdoing what their ŌüĀgovernments are legally obliged to do."
The World Health Organization has said that even during armed conflicts, states are obligated under international humanitarian law to ŌĆŗensure that people are able to reach medical care in safety.
ŌĆ£This is ŌĆŗa mission ŌüĀthat aims to open a humanitarian corridor so the aid delivery organisations can arrive,ŌĆØ Saif Abukeshak, a Palestinian activist and member of the flotillaŌĆÖs organising committee, told Reuters.
Swiss and Spanish activists on last year's ŌüĀflotilla said ŌĆŗthey were subjected to inhumane conditions during their detention ŌĆŗby Israeli forces - an allegation that was rejected by an Israeli foreign ministry spokesperson.

item.title: Hungary's Orban concedes landmark defeat to centre-right opposition
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's veteran nationalist leader Viktor Orban conceded defeat on Sunday after a ŌĆŗlandslide election victory by the upstart opposition Tisza party, in a setback for his allies in Russia and U.S. ŌĆīPresident Donald Trump's White House.
Results based on 46% of votes counted showed the centre-right, pro-EU Tisza party of Peter Magyar , opens new tab winning 135 seats - or a crucial two-thirds majority - in the 199-member parliament, ahead of Orban's Fidesz party.
"The election results are not final yet, but the situation is understandable and clear," Orban said at the Fidesz campaign offices. "The election result is painful ŌĆŗfor us, but clear."
Pollsters predicted a record voter turnout, with Hungarian television showing long queues outside some voting stations in ŌĆŗBudapest. Data at 1630 GMT, half an hour before polls were due to close, showed 77.8% of voters ŌüĀcasting their ballots, up from 67.8% four years earlier.
If the final results confirm the early readings, an end to Orban's period in ŌĆŗgovernment after 16 years in power would have significant implications not only for Hungary, but for the European Union, Ukraine and beyond.
It would ŌĆŗlikely spell an end to Hungary's adversarial role inside the EU, possibly opening the way for a 90 billion euro ($105 billion) loan to war-battered Ukraine blocked by Orban.
Defeat for Orban could also mean the eventual release of EU funds to Hungary that the bloc had suspended due to what Brussels said was ŌĆŗOrban's erosion of democratic standards.
Orban's exit would also deprive Russian President Vladimir Putin of his main ally in the EU and send shockwaves ŌĆŗthrough Western right-wing circles, including the White House.
In Hungary, a Tisza victory could open the way for reforms that the party says would aim to ŌĆīcombat corruption ŌüĀand restore the independence of the judiciary and other institutions.
However, the extent of such reforms will depend on whether Tisza can secure the two-thirds constitutional majority it would need to reverse much of Orban's legacy.
Orban, a eurosceptic, carved out a model of an "illiberal democracy" seen as a blueprint by Trump's Make America Great Again (MAGA) movement and its admirers in Europe.
But many Hungarians have grown increasingly weary ŌĆŗof Orban, 62, after three years ŌĆŗof economic stagnation and soaring ŌüĀliving costs as well as reports of oligarchs close to the government amassing more wealth.
Tisza's leader Magyar appears to have successfully tapped into this frustration.
Casting his vote for Tisza in the Hungarian capital, Mihaly ŌĆŗBacsi, 27, said the country needed change.
"We need an improvement in public mood, there is too ŌĆŗmuch tension in many ŌüĀareas and the current government only fuels these sentiments," he said.
Another voter, who gave her name as Zsuzsa, said she wanted continuity.
"I would really like if all the results that have been achieved in recent years remain - and I am terribly afraid of the war," she said, referring ŌüĀto the ŌĆŗconflict raging in Ukraine, Hungary's eastern neighbour.
Orban sought to cast Sunday's election as ŌĆŗa choice between "war and peace" . During campaigning, the government blanketed the country with signs warning that Magyar would drag Hungary into Russia's war with Ukraine, something he strongly denies.

item.title: Ben-Gvir visits Al-Aqsa Mosque compound
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 12 (Reuters) - Israel's far-right police minister Itamar Ben-Gvir visited the flashpoint Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in Jerusalem on Sunday, saying ŌĆŗhe was seeking greater access for Jewish worshippers and drawing condemnation ŌĆīfrom Jordan and the Palestinians.
The compound in Jerusalem's walled Old City is one of the most sensitive sites in the Middle East . Known to Jews as Temple Mount, it is ŌĆŗthe most sacred site in Judaism and is Islam's third-holiest site.
Under ŌĆŗa delicate, decades-old arrangement with Muslim authorities, it is administered by ŌüĀa Jordanian religious foundation and Jews can visit but may not pray ŌĆŗthere.
Suggestions that Israel would alter the rules have sparked outrage among Muslims and ŌĆŗignited violence in the past.
"Today, I feel like the owner here," National Security Minister Ben-Gvir said in a video filmed at the site and distributed by his office. "There is still more to ŌĆŗdo, more to improve. I keep pushing the Prime Minister (Benjamin Netanyahu) to do ŌĆŗmore and more ŌĆö we must keep rising higher and higher."
A statement from the Jordanian foreign ŌĆīministry ŌüĀsaid it considered Ben-Gvir's visit to be a violation of the status quo agreement at the site and "a desecration of its sanctity, a condemnable escalation and an unacceptable provocation".
The office of Palestinian president, Mahmoud Abbas, said such actions could ŌĆŗfurther destabilize the ŌĆŗregion.
Ben-Gvir's spokesman said ŌüĀthe minister was seeking greater access and prayer permits for Jewish visitors. He also said that Ben-Gvir had prayed at ŌĆŗthe site.
There was no immediate comment from Netanyahu's office. ŌĆŗPrevious such ŌüĀvisits and statements by Ben-Gvir have prompted Netanyahu announcements saying that there is no change in Israel's policy of keeping the status quo.
Muslim, Christian and Jewish sites, ŌüĀincluding ŌĆŗAl-Aqsa had been largely closed , opens new tab to the public during the ŌĆŗIran war . There was no immediate sign of unrest on Sunday after Ben-Gvir's visit.

item.title: Nigerian airstrike hits market, 200 feared dead in northeast Yobe state
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, April 12 (Reuters) - At least 200 people are feared dead after Nigerian military jets struck a village market while pursuing Islamist militants in the northeast of the country on Saturday night, a councillor for the area and residents said on Sunday.
The Nigerian ŌĆŗAir Force, responding to reports of civilian casualties, said in a statement it had activated ŌĆŗits Civilian Harm Accident and Investigation Cell "to immediately proceed to the location on a ŌüĀfact-finding mission on the allegation".
Earlier, the Air Force said it had killed Boko Haram militants in ŌĆŗthe Jilli axis in Borno state.
The government of neighbouring Yobe state later said in a statement that an ŌĆŗair strike on the area had been conducted near a market where shoppers and vendors had gathered.
"Some people from Geidam LGA (local government area) bordering Gubio LGA in Borno state who went to the Jilli weekly market were affected," said ŌĆŗBrigadier General Dahiru Abdulsalam, military adviser to the Yobe state government.
He gave no further details.
The strike occurred in a village in Yobe on the border with Borno, the heartland of a long-running insurgency ŌĆīthat has ŌüĀkilled thousands of people and displaced millions more.
Lawan Zanna Nur Geidam, the councillor and traditional head of Fuchimeram ward in Yobe's Geidam district, told Reuters that those injured were being taken to hospitals in Yobe and Borno.
"It's a very devastating incident at Jilli Market. As I'm speaking to you, over 200 ŌĆŗpeople have lost their lives ŌĆŗfrom the air strike ŌüĀat the market," he said in a telephone interview.
Three other residents and an official from an international humanitarian agency confirmed the strike and the estimated ŌĆŗdeath toll.
The Yobe State Emergency Management Agency said it had received preliminary reports ŌĆŗof an ŌüĀincident at Jilli Market "which reportedly resulted in casualties affecting some marketers" and activated emergency response.
Ahmed Ali, a 43-year-old resident who sells medical consumables at the market, said he had been injured in a blast.
"I became so scared ŌüĀand attempted ŌĆŗto run away, but a friend dragged me and we ŌĆŗall lay on the ground," he said from hospital.

item.title: South Africa coalition party elects Cape Town mayor as leader
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, April 12 (Reuters) - South Africa's Democratic Alliance (DA) ŌĆīelected Cape Town Mayor Geordin Hill-Lewis as its leader on Sunday, as the ruling coalition's second-biggest party sought to capitalise on discontent to expand its power base in local polls this year.
The 39-year-old was widely considered the favourite ŌĆŗto succeed Agriculture Minister John Steenhuisen, who has led the pro-business party since 2019 and ŌĆŗis stepping down.
"If we work hard, we can win more towns and cities ŌüĀthan we've ever won before," Hill-Lewis said in his acceptance speech at a party conference near Johannesburg, while laying ŌĆŗout broad ambitions for the next national election in 2029.
"I am not satisfied being a junior partner ŌĆŗin a coalition government. Our ambition must be to lead the national government," he said.
Hill-Lewis has given few details about his plans but is not expected to depart significantly from the policies of his predecessor, who took the ŌĆŗDA into a coalition with the African National Congress (ANC) in 2024 while continuing to fight it ŌĆŗon issues such as national health insurance and affirmative action, which the DA opposes.
Africa's most industrialised country must hold ŌĆīlocal elections ŌüĀbefore November, and President Cyril Ramaphosa's ANC is expected to see its share of the vote slip again.
Local polls have traditionally gone worse for the ANC than the national vote, as voters furious at failures to deliver basic services like water and road repairs punish the party that has been ŌĆŗin power since apartheid ŌĆŗended in 1994.
The DA holds 22% of seats in the lower house of parliament, second to the ANC with 41%, and ŌĆŗhas kept roughly the same vote share for the last decade.
The DA ŌĆŗhas a reputation ŌüĀfor protecting the interests of South Africa's white minority - which it denies - and has not had a non-white leader since Mmusi Maimane resigned in 2019. Some analysts see that as a limiting factor for growth ŌüĀin a country ŌĆŗthat is more than 90% non-white.
"It would've been so much ŌĆŗeasier for them to appeal to a broader constituency if there was a dynamic person ... of another colour," author and political ŌĆŗscientist Susan Booysen said.

item.title: Three killed as armed men attack Pakistan Coast Guards in Arabian Sea
item.description:
QUETTA, Pakistan, April 12 (Reuters) - Separatist insurgents killed three Pakistan Coast Guard personnel on Sunday in the first-ever attack on one of the maritime authority's patrol boats operating ŌĆŗin the Arabian Sea, security officials said.
Intelligence and police officials ŌĆŗsaid the boat was on a routine patrol in ŌüĀa coastal area close to the Pakistan-Iran border when the militants ŌĆŗopened fire and killed three on board.
The incident adds to security ŌĆŗchallenges in Balochistan, a flashpoint of insurgency where armed groups have frequently targeted security forces and infrastructure.
The banned separatist group Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) claimed responsibility for ŌĆŗthe attack. "Following the operations on land, the action in maritime ŌĆŗboundaries marks a new development in the BLAŌĆÖs military strategy," the BLA said ŌĆīin ŌüĀa statement.
Authorities have launched an investigation and security has been heightened in the area, said the intelligence and police sources.
Pakistan carried out airstrikes in February that sparked a week-long conflict ŌĆŗwith Afghanistan. Islamabad blames ŌĆŗKabul for ŌüĀproviding safe haven to militant groups including the BLA to plan and coordinate their attacks. The Taliban ŌĆŗgovernment has denied the accusation.
Gwadar, an important district ŌĆŗwith a ŌüĀstrategic deep-sea port that is a key node in China's flagship "Belt and Road" infrastructure initiative, has seen repeated attacks on security forces ŌüĀand installations ŌĆŗby Baloch separatist militants.
Authorities did not ŌĆŗrespond to requests for comment about the incident.

item.title: Saudi Arabia summons Iraq envoy over drone threats from Iraqi territory
item.description:
April 12 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia said on ŌĆŗSunday it ŌĆīhad summoned Iraq's ambassador over ŌĆŗwhat ŌĆŗit described as threats ŌüĀtargeting the ŌĆŗkingdom and ŌĆŗother Gulf states from drones launched ŌĆŗfrom Iraqi ŌĆŗterritory.
The Saudi foreign ministry ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀin a statement that it had "emphasised ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗimportance ŌüĀof Iraq dealing ŌĆŗresponsibly with ŌĆŗthese ŌüĀthreats and attacks".

item.title: Australia and US boost support for critical minerals with $3.5 billion
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 12 (Reuters) - Australia and the U.S. have committed more than A$5 billion ($3.5 billion) to back a range of critical mineral projects, nearly double the amount pledged when the two countries struck ŌĆŗa co-operation agreement six months ago, Canberra said on Sunday.
The funding seeks to ŌĆīsupport Australian ventures to develop and refine metals vital to industries including defence, advanced manufacturing and the energy transition, a market that has long been dominated by China.
By doing so, they would support the re-industrialisation of America's ŌĆŗhigh-tech manufacturing base, while helping to "counter China's export dominance and ensure Western supply-chain resilience," ŌĆŗthe two nations said when they reached a framework deal last October .
In that ŌüĀdeal, Australia and the U.S. agreed to each provide at least $1 billion in investments towards ŌĆŗan $8.5 billion pipeline of priority critical mineral projects across the two countries over the subsequent six months.
Australia ŌĆŗhas a vast supply of critical minerals such as rare earths, but China has mastered the technically difficult and environmentally fraught refining process.
"Australia and the U.S. are delivering on the commitments made in the White House, with priority ŌĆŗprojects in Australia that support production of rare earths and critical minerals," Resources Minister Madeleine ŌĆŗKing said in a statement.
"Australia is taking a global lead to diversify crucial supply chains for critical minerals and ŌĆīrare ŌüĀearths, which are vital to support economic and national security for Australia and our trading partners."
The A$5 billion in funding to Australian critical mineral projects will be carried out through the Export Finance Australia (EFA) and U.S. Export-Import Bank (EXIM) agencies.
Among the largest investments is for a rare ŌĆŗearths refinery project owned by Tronox ŌĆŗHoldings (TROX.N) , opens new tab where EFA ŌüĀand EXIM have issued letters of support and interest worth a combined A$849 million. Tronox, with operations in Western Australia and the U.S., would tap its ŌĆŗexisting mining and processing capabilities to produce mixed rare earth carbonate containing ŌĆŗboth light ŌüĀand heavy rare earth elements, King said.
The agencies also committed up to A$1 billion in combined backing for Ardea Resources' (ARL.AX) , opens new tab Kalgoorlie Nickel Project in Western Australia.
Other projects supported under the framework include Alcoa's (AA.N) , opens new tab Gallium Recovery ŌüĀProject and ŌĆŗArafura's (ARU.AX) , opens new tab Nolans Rare Earths Project along with graphite, magnesium ŌĆŗand tungsten ventures.
Additional projects for minerals including vanadium and scandium have also received indications of support, King said.
($1 = 1.4164 Australian dollars)
(1 ŌĆŗAustralian dollar = $0.7060)

item.title: Israel approves appointment of new intelligence agency chief
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 12 (Reuters) - Israel approved the appointment of Roman ŌĆŗGofman as the new Mossad ŌĆīintelligence agency head, a statement from the Israeli prime ŌĆŗminister's office said on ŌĆŗSunday.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin ŌüĀNetanyahu announced his intention ŌĆŗto nominate Gofman in December ŌĆŗ2025 and a committee for senior appointments approved the decision on ŌĆŗSunday. Gofman will ŌĆŗstart his role in June 2026, ŌĆīreplacing ŌüĀthe agency's current director David Barnea.
Gofman, 49, is a seasoned military general who ŌĆŗrushed ŌĆŗto Israel's ŌüĀsouthern border to rescue Israeli communities ŌĆŗunder fire from a ŌĆŗHamas-led ŌüĀinfiltration on October 7, 2023. The incoming director ŌüĀcurrently ŌĆŗserves as the ŌĆŗprime minister's military secretary.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader has severe and disfiguring wounds, sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, April 11 (Reuters) - Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei is still recovering from severe facial and leg injuries suffered in the airstrike that killed his father at the beginning of the war, three people close to his inner circle told Reuters.
Khamenei's face was disfigured in the attack on the supreme leader's compound in central Tehran and he suffered a significant injury to one or both legs, all three sources said.
The 56-year-old is nonetheless recovering from his wounds and remains mentally ŌĆŗsharp, according to the people, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive matters. He is taking part in meetings with senior officials via audio conferencing and is engaged in decision-making on major issues including the war and negotiations ŌĆīwith Washington, two of them said.
The question of whether Khamenei's health allows him to run state affairs comes during Iran's moment of gravest peril for decades, with high-stakes peace talks with the United States opening in the Pakistani capital Islamabad on Saturday.
The accounts of the people close to Khamenei's inner circle provide the most detailed description of the leader's condition for weeks. Reuters couldn't independently verify their descriptions.
Khamenei's whereabouts, condition and ability to rule still largely remain a mystery to the public, with no photo, video or audio recording of him published since the air attack and his subsequent appointment as his ŌĆŗfather's replacement on March 8.
Iran's United Nations mission did not respond to Reuters questions about the extent of Khamenei's injuries or the reason he has not yet appeared in any images or recordings.
Khamenei was wounded on February 28, the first ŌĆŗday of the war launched by the U.S. and Israel, in the attack that killed his father and predecessor Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who had ruled since 1989. Mojtaba Khamenei's wife, brother-in-law ŌüĀand sister-in-law were among other members of his family killed in the strike.
There has been no official Iranian statement on the extent of Khamenei's injuries. However, a newsreader on state television described him as a "janbaz", a term used for those badly wounded in war, ŌĆŗafter he was named supreme leader.
The accounts of Khamenei's injuries tally with a statement made by U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth on March 13 when he said that Khamenei was "wounded and likely disfigured".
A source familiar with U.S. intelligence assessments told Reuters that Khamenei was ŌĆŗbelieved to have lost a leg.
The CIA declined to comment on Khamenei's condition. The Israeli prime minister's office didn't respond to questions.
Alex Vatanka, senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said that, regardless of the severity of his injuries, it was unlikely the new and inexperienced leader would be able to command the overarching power wielded by his father. While he is seen to represent continuity, it could take years for him to build up the same level of automatic authority, Vatanka added.
"Mojtaba will be one voice but it will not be the decisive one," he said. "He needs to prove himself ŌĆŗas the credible, powerful, overriding voice. The regime as a whole has to make a decision in terms of where they are going to go."
One of the people close to Khamenei's circle said images of the supreme leader could be expected to be ŌĆŗreleased within one or two months and that he might even appear in public then, although all three sources stressed he would only emerge when his health and the security situation allowed.
In Iran's theocratic system of rule, ultimate power is meant ŌĆīto be wielded ŌüĀby the supreme leader, a venerable Shi'ite Muslim cleric appointed by an assembly of 88 ayatollahs. The leader oversees the elected president while directly commanding parallel institutions including the Revolutionary Guards, a powerful political and military force.
Iran's first supreme leader, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, enjoyed unquestioned authority as the charismatic leader of the revolution and the most learned cleric of his day.
His successor, Ali Khamenei, was a less revered cleric but had served as Iran's president. He spent decades cementing his authority after his appointment in 1989, partly through promoting the power of the Revolutionary Guards.
His son Mojtaba does not command absolute power in the same way, senior Iranian sources have previously told Reuters. The Revolutionary Guards, who helped steer him into the top job after his father's assassination, have emerged as the dominant voice on strategic ŌĆŗdecisions during the war. Iran's U.N. mission didn't respond to questions ŌĆŗabout the power wielded by the Guards and the ŌüĀnew supreme leader.
As an influential figure in his father's office, Khamenei had previously spent years involved in exercising power at the top levels of the Islamic Republic, officials and insiders have said, building ties with senior Guards figures.
While he is widely seen as likely to continue his father's hardline approach due to his links to the Guards, we don't know much about his world view, said Vatanka ŌĆŗat the Middle East Institute.
Khamenei's first communication with Iranians as supreme leader came on March 12, saying in a written statement read out by a television news presenter that the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz should stay closed and ŌüĀwarning regional countries to shut U.S. bases.
His office has since issued a few other brief written statements from him, including on March 20 when he welcomed in the Persian new year, which he named the "year of resistance". Public statements of policy on Iran's war stance, its approach towards diplomacy, neighbours, ceasefire negotiations and domestic unrest, have been made by other senior officials.
Khamenei's absence is widely discussed on Iranian social media and in messaging app groups, when the country's patchy internet allows, with conspiracy theories widespread about his condition and ŌüĀwho is running the ŌĆŗcountry.
One popular meme circulating online is a picture of an empty chair under a spotlight with the slogan "Where is Mojtaba?"
However, some government supporters, including a ŌĆŗsenior member of the Basij militia, a volunteer paramilitary group run by the Revolutionary Guards, said that it was important for Khamenei to keep a low profile, given the threat posed by waves of U.S. and Israeli airstrikes that have already wiped out much of the country's leadership.
A lower-ranking Basij member agreed.
"Why should he ŌĆŗappear in public? To become a target for these criminals?" Mohammad Hosseini, from the city of Qom, said in a text message.

item.title: Some of Swalwell's fellow Democrats urge him to quit Congress amid sexual assault allegations
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Representative Eric Swalwell, a front-runner candidate for California governor, faced mounting calls to resign from Congress on Sunday as some of his fellow Democrats urged him to leave following accusations of ŌĆŗsexual assault.
In separate TV interviews on Sunday morning talk shows, Democratic Representatives Pramila Jayapal, Ro Khanna ŌĆīand Eugene Vindman each said Swalwell should quit Congress.
Their comments came two days after The San Francisco Chronicle and CNN reported that a woman who previously worked in Swalwell's district office accused him of two nonconsensual sexual encounters.
CNN also reported that three other women ŌĆŗmade sexual misconduct allegations against Swalwell, who represents a California district.
Swalwell has denied the accusations as "absolutely false" and ŌĆŗvowed to fight them.
"What he did is sick and disgusting," Khanna told "Fox News Sunday" while ŌüĀcalling for law enforcement and House ethics investigations of Swalwell.
The Manhattan district attorneyŌĆÖs office on Saturday confirmed that it is ŌĆŗinvestigating the sexual assault allegations.
Leading Democrats have previously called on Swalwell to drop out of the California governor's race. ŌĆŗAs pressure grows on him to also resign from Congress, some lawmakers have said they would back a motion to expel him from the House of Representatives if he does not leave on his own.
Representative Byron Donalds, a Republican from Florida appearing on NBC's "Meet ŌĆŗthe Press", and Jayapal, a representative from Washington also appearing on "Meet the Press", each said on Sunday they would ŌĆŗvote to expel Swalwell from Congress if such a vote came up.
In a social media post on Saturday, Representative Anna Paulina Luna, ŌĆīa ŌüĀFlorida Republican, wrote that she planned to file a motion to expel Swalwell from Congress. She told Fox News that she would file the disciplinary motion next week.
Another Democrat, Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick , a Florida representative, also faces a potential expulsion vote after a House panel recently concluded that she violated ethics rules. A grand jury indicted her in November after ŌĆŗshe was accused of stealing ŌĆŗpandemic relief funds and ŌüĀdirecting that money to her 2021 congressional campaign.
Cherfilus-McCormick's office did not immediately return a request for comment. She has previously denied wrongdoing.
While Luna and other Republicans have called for ŌĆŗexpelling Swalwell and Cherfilus-McCormick, Democrats have been calling for an expulsion of Representative Tony ŌĆŗGonzales, a Texas ŌüĀRepublican being investigated by the House over sexual misconduct allegations.
Gonzales' office did not immediately return a request for comment. Last month, he said he would not seek re-election later this year.
Donalds told "Meet the Press" that he wants to see Swalwell ŌüĀand Gonzales ŌĆŗleave. "As far as I'm concerned both gentlemen need to go home," ŌĆŗDonalds said.
While calling on Swalwell to resign, Khanna told Fox that he also believes "there are other members (of Congress) and Republican members who should resign ŌĆŗas well," such as Gonzales.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party leads last Median poll done before Sunday election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's centre-right Tisza party ŌĆŗwas projected to ŌĆīwin 135 mandates in Hungary's 199-seat ŌĆŗparliament, ahead ŌĆŗof Prime Minister Viktor ŌüĀOrban's Fidesz ŌĆŗparty, in a ŌĆŗpoll carried out in the days before Sunday's ŌĆŗelection by ŌĆŗpollster Median.
According to the ŌĆīsurvey ŌüĀannounced on RTL Klub television and conducted between ŌĆŗApril 7 ŌĆŗand ŌüĀ11, the far-right Mi ŌĆŗHazank (Our Homeland) ŌĆŗparty ŌüĀwould not pass the 5% threshold ŌüĀto ŌĆŗget ŌĆŗinto parliament.

item.title: Iran's Guards will view military vessels approaching strait as ceasefire breach
item.description:
CAIRO, April 12 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards said ŌĆŗon Sunday that ŌĆīany military vessels attempting to approach the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz ŌĆŗwill be considered a ŌüĀviolation of ŌĆŗthe two-week U.S. ceasefire ŌĆŗand be dealt with harshly and decisively.
The strait ŌĆŗis under ŌĆŗthe control and 'smart management' of ŌĆīIran's ŌüĀNavy, the Guards said in a statement reported by ŌĆŗIranian ŌĆŗstate ŌüĀmedia, adding it is "open for ŌĆŗthe safe passage ŌĆŗof ŌüĀnon-military vessels in accordance with specific ŌüĀregulations".

item.title: Trump says it will take a little while for US to blockade the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: MIAMI, Florida, April 12 (Reuters) - Implementing a ŌĆīnewly announced U.S. ŌĆŗblockade ŌĆŗof the Strait ŌüĀof ŌĆŗHormuz will ŌĆŗtake a little while, ŌĆŗPresident ŌĆŗDonald Trump said ŌĆīin ŌüĀan interview with Fox ŌĆŗNews' "Sunday ŌĆŗMorning ŌüĀFutures with ŌĆŗMaria ŌĆŗBartiromo".

item.title: Peruvians vote in crowded presidential race as runoff looms
item.description:
