LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Peruvians headed ŌĆŗto the polls on Sunday to vote for a new president and members of Congress, casting ballots in a first-round field of ŌĆīmore than 30 presidential candidates after years of political turmoil that have eroded confidence in institutions and left voters deeply disillusioned.
With no clear frontrunner and all major candidates polling well below the 50% needed to win outright, a June 7 runoff appears likely. That could prolong uncertainty in the world's third-largest copper producer at a time of rising crime ŌĆŗand intensifying competition for influence between the United States and China.
Voting stations opened at 7 a.m. local time (1200 GMT), with about 27 million people ŌĆŗeligible to vote.
Early in the day, voters in parts of Lima complained that several polling stations had yet to open.
Margarita ŌüĀSandoval, 35, said she had waited in line for two hours without being able to enter her ballot station in Chorrillos, a southern district of ŌĆŗthe capital.
ŌĆ£I have to work and I can't vote," Sandoval said. "These elections are a disaster."
Others said the crowded race had made the decision difficult. ŌĆ£I only decided ŌĆŗwho to vote for about a week ago,ŌĆØ said 33-year-old shop assistant Benjamin Alcantara.
Since 2018, Peru has cycled through eight presidents, fueling skepticism that any new administration will last a full five-year term following a dizzying turnover driven by impeachments, corruption scandals and weak governing coalitions that have paralyzed decision-making.
ŌĆ£People really despise the current Congress," said Martin ŌĆŗCassinelli at the Atlantic Council. "They recognize them as responsible for the political chaos we've had over the last ten years,ŌĆØ he added.
Political distrust has fueled ŌĆŗa crowded field spanning the ideological spectrum, including seasoned politicians, a far-right businessman and a television comedian.
Among the best known is conservative Keiko Fujimori , making her fourth presidential bid after reaching ŌĆīthe runoff ŌüĀin all three previous races.
Educated in the U.S. and leader of the powerful Popular Force party in Congress, Fujimori has framed herself as a guarantor of order and economic stability, appealing to voters alarmed by surging violent crime. Her candidacy remains polarizing, however, due to her family legacy and past legal troubles .
Ricardo Belmont, a former Lima mayor running for the center-left Civic Works Party, has shot up into second place after a late rise in support. He is followed by ŌĆŗpopular comedian Carlos Alvarez, who is ŌĆŗcampaigning on a tough-on-crime platform. Both ŌüĀare considered outsiders who have gained traction by tapping into a broader anti-establishment mood, according to analysts.
On the right, former Lima Mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga, a wealthy businessman with socially conservative views, has campaigned on an ultra-conservative platform ŌĆŗbut has seen support fluctuate.
Public insecurity has emerged as the dominant theme of the campaign. Homicides and extortion have ŌĆŗclimbed in recent years, ŌüĀdriven in part by drug trafficking and illegal mining. Most leading candidates have proposed expanding the role of the armed forces in internal security.
The election also carries geopolitical implications. Peru's deepening economic relationship with China -- now its largest trading partner and a major investor in mining and infrastructure -- has raised concerns in Washington , which has ŌüĀstepped up ŌĆŗdiplomatic and security engagement ahead of the vote.
Whoever advances to the runoff will face a fractured ŌĆŗCongress and newly reinstated Senate, which may complicate efforts to pass legislation and raise the risk of renewed impeachment battles.
Polls close at 5 p.m. (2200 GMT), with preliminary results from the country's election ŌĆŗmonitor expected soon after.

item.title: China offers incentives to Taiwan following opposition leader's visit
item.description:
BEIJING, April 12 (Reuters) - China on Sunday unveiled 10 new incentive measures for Taiwan, including easing tourist curbs, allowing in "healthy" television ŌĆŗdramas and facilitating food sales, following a visit by the island's opposition ŌĆīleader.
The move comes at the end of a trip to China by Cheng Li-wun, chairwoman of the Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan's largest opposition party. She met Chinese President Xi Jinping and talked about ŌĆŗthe need for peace and reconciliation.
The 10 measures, unveiled by the official ŌĆŗXinhua news agency, "explore" the establishment of a regular communication mechanism between ŌüĀthe KMT and China's Communist Party, the full resumption of flights between the ŌĆŗtwo sides and permission for individuals from Shanghai and Fujian province to visit Taiwan.
A ŌĆŗmechanism will be established to ease inspection standards for food and fishery products, but that has to be on the political foundation of "opposing Taiwan independence," Xinhua said.
Taiwanese TV dramas, documentaries and ŌĆŗanimation will be allowed to be shown as long as they have "correct orientation, ŌĆŗhealthy content, and high production quality," it added.
Taiwan's Mainland Affairs Council, which is in charge of ŌĆīthe ŌüĀisland's China policy, said in a statement that Beijing's so-called "unilateral concessions" are merely poisoned pills packaged as "generous gift packages."
The Taiwanese government supports healthy and orderly cross-strait exchanges, but they should not be subject to political preconditions or objectives, it said.
In a ŌĆŗstatement, the KMT welcomed ŌĆŗChina's announcement, saying ŌüĀit was a "gift" to the people of Taiwan.
China refuses to speak to Taiwan President Lai Ching-te, saying he is a "separatist." He ŌĆŗrejects Beijing's sovereignty claims over the democratically governed island.
China and ŌĆŗTaiwan have ŌüĀblamed each other for the lack of a resumption of large-scale Chinese tourism to the island since the end of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Taiwan has also previously complained about ŌüĀChinese ŌĆŗrestrictions on imports of certain agricultural and aquatic products, ŌĆŗsaying China has in some cases used unjustified excuses to stop the spread of pests and diseases.

item.title: Irish police clear blockades as Dublin acts to soften fuel price blow
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 12 (Reuters) - Irish police on Sunday cleared tractors and trucks that had ŌĆībeen blocking traffic and blockading oil infrastructure across the country during a wave of protests against surging fuel prices.
In a bid to ease the discontent, the government announced ŌĆŗ500 million euros ($586 million) worth of spending increases and tax ŌĆŗcuts to soften the impact on consumers and businesses.
Protesters, angered ŌüĀby a more than 20% rise in diesel prices since the outbreak ŌĆŗof the Iran war , had used vehicles to block an oil refinery, two ports, ŌĆŗa fuel terminal and Irish roads.
The protests caused major transport disruption in Dublin and left about a third of Ireland's petrol stations without fuel, creating what Finance Minister ŌĆŗSimon Harris described as a "very dangerous moment" for the country.
Police removed vehicles ŌĆŗblocking access to Ireland's only oil refinery on Saturday and cleared a blockade of ŌĆīGalway Port, ŌüĀa major fuel depot, and removed roadblocks in Dublin on Sunday.
The government has refused to negotiate with the protesters, who included farmers, drivers and contractors, who complained that an earlier 250 million euro package to temporarily cut ŌĆŗtaxes on petrol ŌĆŗand diesel did ŌüĀnot go far enough.
But after talks with agricultural and transport industry groups, Dublin announced measures including a 10 ŌĆŗcent per litre reduction for diesel and petrol and ŌĆŗa delay ŌüĀin an increase in a carbon tax. A fuel subsidy support scheme will also be introduced for farming and fisheries, the government said.
A poll in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗSunday Independent newspaper showed that 56% of ŌĆŗvoters surveyed supported the protesters, but that most supporters of the two governing parties opposed them.
($1 = ŌĆŗ0.8533 euros)

item.title: Gas prices might stay high through midterms, Trump says
item.description:
MIAMI, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday that the price of oil and gasoline ŌĆŗmay remain high through November's midterm elections, a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from his decision to attack Iran ŌĆīsix weeks ago.
"It could be, or the same, or maybe a little bit higher, but it should be around the same," Trump, who is in Miami for the weekend, told Fox News' "Sunday Morning Futures With Maria Bartiromo" when asked whether the cost of oil and gas would be lower by the fall.
The average price for regular gas at ŌĆŗU.S. service stations has exceeded $4 per gallon for most of April, according to data from GasBuddy. Trump's comments on Sunday came after ŌĆŗweeks of asserting that the spike in prices is a short-term phenomenon, though his top advisers are cognizant of ŌüĀthe war's economic impacts, officials have said.
Earlier on Sunday, Trump announced on social media that the U.S. Navy would blockade the Strait of Hormuz and intercept ŌĆŗany ship that paid a crossing fee to Iran, after marathon talks between the U.S. and Iran in Pakistan over the weekend did not yield ŌĆŗa peace deal.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," he wrote on Truth Social.
Any U.S. blockade is likely to add more uncertainty to the eventual resolution of the conflict, which is currently subject to a tenuous two-week ceasefire. The new tactic is in response to Iran's own closure of the ŌĆŗstrait's critical shipping lanes, which has caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50%.
The war began on February 28, when ŌĆŗthe U.S. launched a joint bombing campaign with Israel against Iran. The scope quickly expanded as Iran and its allies attacked nearby countries, while Israel targeted Hezbollah with ŌĆīmassive strikes ŌüĀin Lebanon.
The war has buffeted global financial markets and caused thousands of civilian deaths, mostly in Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's political standing at home has suffered, with polls showing the war is unpopular among most Americans, who are frustrated by rising gasoline prices.
The president's approval rating has hit the lowest levels of his second term in office, raising concern among Republicans that his party is poised to lose control of Congress in the midterm elections. A Democratic majority ŌĆŗin either chamber could launch investigations ŌĆŗinto the Trump administration while ŌüĀblocking much of his legislative agenda.
U.S. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the ranking Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, questioned the strategy behind Trump's planned blockade.
"I don't understand how blockading the strait is going to somehow push the ŌĆŗIranians into opening it," he told CNN's "State of the Union" on Sunday.
In a separate appearance on CBS's "Face the ŌĆŗNation," Warner said the ŌüĀblockade would not undermine Iranian control of the waterway.
"The Iranians have hundreds of speedboats where they can still mine the strait or put bombs against tankers in closing the strait," he said. "How is that going to ever bring down gas prices?"
Although Trump has repeatedly said that the war would be over soon, ŌüĀRepublican U.S. Senator ŌĆŗRon Johnson of Wisconsin told ABC News' "This Week" on Sunday that achieving U.S. aims ŌĆŗin Iran "could take a long time."
"It's going to be a long-term project," said Johnson, who was not asked about Trump's proposed blockade. "I never thought this would be easy."

item.title: Australia PM Albanese to visit Brunei, Malaysia to shore up fuel supply
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 12 (Reuters) - Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese will visit Brunei and Malaysia this week, continuing diplomatic efforts to secure fuel supplies ŌĆŗfrom Asian allies after disruptions caused by the Middle East ŌĆīconflict.
Albanese will travel to Bandar Seri Begawan and Kuala Lumpur from April 14 to 17, his office said in a statement on Sunday.
He is scheduled to ŌĆŗmeet Brunei's ruler Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah and Malaysian Prime ŌĆŗMinister Anwar Ibrahim.
Foreign Minister Penny Wong would also meet her ŌüĀcounterparts in Brunei and Malaysia, the statement said.
"Engaging with critical ŌĆŗregional partners such as Brunei Darussalam and Malaysia will help ensure Australia's ŌĆŗenergy supply remains secure during times of uncertainty," Albanese said.
"We are taking every step to reinforce relationships and engage with key partners to keep our fuel ŌĆŗsupply flowing."
The trip comes as Australia leans on its Asian allies ŌĆŗto maintain fuel supplies amid disruptions caused by Iran's blockade of the Strait ŌĆīof ŌüĀHormuz, through which about one-fifth of the world's oil and gas transited before the conflict.
Australia, which imports about 80% of its fuel, has experienced localised shortages and soaring prices since the strait's closure.
Malaysia is ŌĆŗAustralia's third-largest source ŌĆŗof refined fuel ŌüĀand supplies 10% of its fertiliser-grade urea imports, while Australia supplies 95% of Malaysia's imported natural ŌĆŗgas.
Brunei supplies 9% of Australia's diesel imports and 11% ŌĆŗof its ŌüĀfertiliser-grade urea imports.
The government has held talks on fuel with Brunei, China, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia and South Korea, among others.
Albanese also travelled to ŌüĀSingapore ŌĆŗlast week and met Prime Minister Lawrence Wong ŌĆŗon Friday, with the two leaders agreeing to closer cooperation to ensure energy security.

item.title: Morning Bid: Back from the brink?
item.description:
April 10 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
Markets returned from the long holiday weekend anything but rested, as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened civilizational destruction if Iran didnŌĆÖt reopen the Strait of Hormuz before his deadline on Tuesday. But instead of hellfire came news of a two-week ceasefire, which was initially cheered by markets. The question now is whether that optimism will last as investors consider the wide gap ŌĆŗbetween Washington and Tehran and the damage to energy supply chains caused by this conflict.
No one knows what will happen next, of course, but it seems likely that conditions will be less benign than many traders currently seem to believe .
The eleventh-hour ŌĆīceasefire announcement late on Tuesday sent both Brent and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude prices tumbling from upwards of $110/bbl to under $100 a barrel .
This, in turn, allowed global stocks and bonds to stage a relief rally on Wednesday , as U.S. equity indexes finished sharply higher , Asian markets surged , and EuropeŌĆÖs STOXX 600 logged its best day in over 4 years . Government bonds rallied as well, while the dollar weakened against developed-market peers on hopes of a lasting end to the conflict.
However, cracks quickly emerged in the Pakistan-brokered ceasefire, pushing crude prices back towards $100/bbl on Thursday and stalling the global equity relief rally .
The deal appeared increasingly fragile after Israel on Wednesday staged its largest attack on Hezbollah during this conflict, with Washington and Tehran ŌĆŗdisagreeing about whether strikes on the Lebanese militant group were meant to be included in the ceasefire agreement. Additionally, Iran attacked Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline ŌĆō its only crude oil export route since the war began ŌĆō shortly after the ceasefire was agreed .
For markets, the biggest ŌĆŗquestion is what is ŌĆō or is not ŌĆō happening in the Strait of Hormuz, the critical artery for the global energy system. Trump had said the ceasefire is contingent on the narrow waterwayŌĆÖs reopening, yet the ŌüĀstrait remains effectively closed .
For Tehran, reopening appears to involve charging tolls for transiting ships, a demand that would likely result in higher global energy prices for years to come . This has all led Trump to write on Truth Social: ŌĆ£That is not the agreement we have!ŌĆØ
Further peace talks are still ŌĆŗset to go ahead in Islamabad this weekend, despite Iran suggesting they would be ŌĆ£unreasonableŌĆØ in light of the Israeli strikes in Lebanon and Trump issuing further military threats . Israel has said it would open separate talks with the Lebanese government aimed at ending the war there and disarming Hezbollah.
Markets appear somewhat optimistic ŌĆŗabout the likelihood that fighting in Iran will not resume soon, with U.S. stock indexes advancing on Thursday and Asian equities rising on Friday , putting them on track for their best week in over three years. Wall Street was flat before the bell on Friday.
However, even if the parties agree on a lasting ceasefire, this will likely only bring partial relief to global energy markets. As a reminder, oil prices are still well above their levels before the conflict broke out, with Brent and WTI up around 34% and 48%, respectively, since the war began on February 28.
What's more, production capacity in the region is still depressed, with Saudi Arabia's oil output ŌĆŗdown by 600,000 barrels per day and throughput on its East-West Pipeline by about 700,000 bpd, according to government sources .
While exporters in the Middle East could soon begin shipping oil trapped in the Gulf if the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, hopes of a rapid return to normal ŌĆŗflows are almost certainly misplaced . This is particularly worrisome for Asia, where physical energy markets are likely to remain under stress for months even in the most optimistic scenarios.
All of this leaves the Federal Reserve in an increasingly unenviable position as higher global energy prices threaten to exacerbate already elevated inflation ŌĆō and potentially unanchor ŌĆīinflation expectations ŌĆō as ŌüĀAmericans eye higher prices at the pump .
On that front, this weekŌĆÖs macro releases included U.S. services PMIs that showed rising input costs ŌĆō an early signal of renewed inflationary pressure ŌĆō alongside PCE inflation figures that showed an expected uptick in U.S. price increases in February, before the war started.
At a global level, IMF chief Kristalina Georgieva warned again on Monday that ŌĆ£all roadsŌĆØ now point toward higher prices and slower growth , a message unlikely to comfort policymakers wary of the spectre of stagflation.
Federal Reserve minutes released on Wednesday suggested more officials are leaning toward a rate hike in response to inflation risks, though most continue to see rate cuts as the base case later this year, with scope for deeper easing should the conflict begin to weigh on U.S. employment.
Both policymakers and investors will thus be eagerly awaiting todayŌĆÖs release of consumer price index (CPI) data for March, as investors shift their attention away from the ŌĆŗbattlefield ŌĆō at least for now.
For more data-driven insights on markets and ŌĆŗcommodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
This weekend, we're reading...
RON ŌĆŗBOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This Foreign Affairs article , opens new tab offers a sharp analysis of ChinaŌĆÖs view on the Iran war and the growing risk that a volatile U.S. poses to BeijingŌĆÖs economic and diplomatic interests.
ANDY ŌĆŗHOME, ROI Metals Columnist: A new International Energy ŌüĀAgency report , opens new tab is essential reading on rare earths that goes beyond the basics to spotlight an often-overlooked driver: explosive demand for rare earth permanent magnets.
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: "Shattered Assumptions And The Energy Quandary" , opens new tab is a deep dive by the always interesting Louis-Vincent Gave of Gavekal Research into the place global energy holds in the minds of investors - the risks and rewards, the upsides and downsides.
We're listening to...
CLYDE RUSSELL, ROI Asia Commodities and Energy Columnist: I appeared on Gulf Intelligence's Daily Energy Markets Podcast , opens new tab for a great discussion on the Iran war ceasefire, with heaps of scepticism over whether it will hold and result ŌüĀin a lasting ŌĆŗdeal. We also explored why the real crisis lies in refined fuels, not crude prices.
And we're watching...
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI EditorŌĆæinŌĆæCharge: Why hasnŌĆÖt the Iran conflict triggered a classic flight ŌĆŗto safety? I explored this question on the Reuters Econ World Podcast , opens new tab with host Carmel Crimmins and my ROI colleague Mike Dolan, discussing the recent moves in gold, Treasuries and haven currencies. We ask whether it may be time to rethink the entire concept of a safe haven.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ŌĆŗSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: What the Chicago Bears relocation fight can teach us about economics
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 10 (Reuters) - The battle to house the Chicago BearsŌĆÖ new stadium is pitting Midwestern U.S. states against each other. Taxpayers are apt to be the losers - and not just because sports stadiums rarely live up to their economic billing.
After more than half a century, the National Football LeagueŌĆÖs Chicago Bears are looking for a site to build ŌĆŗa new stadium. The question of where has become a political football, with two states falling over themselves to pay for it.
The odds-on favorite destination was initially Arlington Heights, Illinois, a ŌĆīsuburb located northwest of Chicago. That cause was advanced last month by a committee of the Illinois General AssemblyŌĆÖs House of Representatives which approved a bill that would bestow tax breaks on qualified ŌĆ£megaprojects.ŌĆØ
Meanwhile, the other leading contender, Hammond, Indiana, won support from its own state legislature in the form of a $1 billion public funding package. Indiana Governor Mike Braun also signed a bill to create a Northwest Indiana stadium authority.
Players within the federal government have also gotten into the game. Democratic U.S. Representative Greg Casar of Texas and ŌĆŗIndependent Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont have introduced a Home Team Act that would bar any sports team from relocating without first offering itself for sale to local buyers.
Casar contended that such a ŌĆŗrestriction was imperative, saying that under current conditions, ŌĆ£Billionaire owners pit taxpayers against one another and then extort the government for billions of dollars.ŌĆØ
Leaving aside his demonization of the ŌüĀsuperrich, the congressman has a point about taxpayers often getting soaked to pay for athletic stadiums.
It is hardly news that tax-financed stadiums have a proven record of failing to deliver the economic benefits promised to voters.
My ŌĆŗbook ŌĆ£Unwarranted Intrusions: The Case Against Government Intervention in the Marketplace,ŌĆØ published 20 years ago, summarized research showing that the presence of a stadium merely diverts spending from other entertainment and creates mostly low-paying jobs.
Proponents of tax subsidies for ŌĆŗsports franchises often counter that having a major-league team enables a city to attract industrial facilities and corporate headquarters.
In reality, when considering a city as a potential site, corporations are primarily concerned with other factors important to their workforce, such as the quality of an areaŌĆÖs schools.
Another point to remember is that sports teams havenŌĆÖt always needed enticements to move to ŌĆō or stay in ŌĆō a city. Professional baseball's first enclosed ballpark opened in Brooklyn in 1862, yet it took another 91 years before a local government first ŌĆŗused taxpayer dollars to lure a team, when Milwaukee did so to attract the Boston Braves in 1953.
GovernmentsŌĆÖ rush to splash tax breaks on sports teams has only amplified the problem over time, a reminder of the ŌĆŗpower ŌĆō and danger ŌĆō of economic incentives.
If tax breaks for stadiums do not represent a worthwhile public investment in economic development, what kinds of government expenditures actually can move the needle?
The Information Technology and Innovation Foundation (ITIF), a well-respected nonpartisan ŌĆīU.S. nonprofit concerned ŌüĀwith public policy, publishes estimates of economic multipliers, measures of the proportional increase in gross domestic product (GDP) that results from private investment or government spending.
According to ITIF estimates, in an advanced economy like the U.S., an investment of 1% of GDP in infrastructure increases economic output by approximately 1.5%. A comparable investment in education or human capital produces an increase of about 3%.
These investments take time to mature, of course. Infrastructure spendingŌĆÖs peak impact is typically reached after five to ten years, with the full benefits of education and human capital investment taking 20 years or more to show up, the ITIF notes.
A third category of productive public investment is scientific research and development. ITIF ŌĆŗestimates that putting 1% of GDP into R&D raises ŌĆŗannual growth by more than 1%. Peak impact ŌüĀis reached in seven or more years.
The International Monetary Fund largely backs up these conclusions. Its October 2025 Fiscal Monitor found that in advanced economies, a reallocation of 1% of GDP from low-impact government consumption to infrastructure can raise output by approximately 1.5% over 25 years, increasing to 3%ŌĆō6% when redirected to human capital investments, namely, education ŌĆŗor health.
Unfortunately, economic growth payoffs that occur over years or decades are less visible to voters than the splashy arrival of an NFL ŌĆŗteam.
Legislators with reelection concerns are thus ŌüĀlikely to focus more on that type of short-term ŌĆ£winŌĆØ rather than investments with more potential to boost growth over the long haul. The future prosperity of constituents not yet old enough to vote is typically not a politicianŌĆÖs chief concern.
Given these dynamics, legislators are likely to continue chasing sports franchises, even if all this does is transfer money from taxpayers to wealthy team owners. Not only could that result in wasted tax dollars but also in wasted potential from the more economically ŌüĀviable investments that ŌĆŗarenŌĆÖt being made.
(The views expressed here are those of Marty Fridson , the publisher of Income Securities Investor. He is a past governor of the ŌĆŗCFA Institute, consultant to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, and Special Assistant to the Director for Deferred Compensation, Office of Management and the Budget, The City of New York.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab ŌĆŗand X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Trading Day: Ceasefire sends stocks higher
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, April 9 (Reuters) - U.S. stocks rallied on Thursday, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq clocking their seventh daily gain, as investors shrugged off a rise in oil prices and pinned their hopes on the fragile U.S.-Iran ceasefire extending to Israel and Lebanon too.
In my column today I look beyond the market euphoria uncorked ŌĆŗby Tuesday's announcement of the ceasefire, and outline why the economic, policy and geopolitical backdrop is still pretty sobering for investors.
If you ŌĆīhave more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Stagflation nation
Figures on Friday are expected to show annual headline U.S. CPI inflation last month was 3.3%, up sharply from 2.4% in February. That would be the highest in nearly two years. Core goods prices are running hot, and with oil 65% more expensive than it was a year ago, ŌĆŗinflation hitting 4% is more likely than the Fed's 2% target.
Some of the early March survey data suggest the economy remains pretty resilient. But incoming hard ŌĆŗdata isn't that robust, and the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow Q1 GDP estimate is now down to 1.3%. Plus, Q4 GDP was revised down on Thursday to 0.5%. Not great.
* AI disruption ŌĆīfears
U.S. software ŌüĀstocks tumbled on Thursday after Anthropic held back the wide release of a powerful AI model over concerns it could expose hidden cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Software stocks are down 25% this year, against a 4% decline for the wider tech sector.
Meanwhile, IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said Fund research shows AI could boost productivity by up to 0.8%, but also affect 60% of all jobs in developed economies. That is sobering, to put it mildly.
* Income incoming
The Q1 U.S. reporting season kicks into gear ŌĆŗnext week, and the outlook is pretty ŌĆŗrosy. The LSEG I/B/E/S consensus is ŌüĀfor a 14.4% rise in earnings, again led by tech - income is expected to rise 46%, and if consensus forecasts are met, that would mean tech accounting for 75% of the overall rise in dollar income.
A lot of optimism is ŌĆŗbuilt into these forecasts. The Nasdaq is back above its pre-war level, and on Thursday clocked its seventh straight ŌĆŗrise, a run not ŌüĀseen since August 2024. But remember, tech's valuation premium over the broader market has collapsed to a 7-year low. So maybe the bullishness is justified?
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want ŌĆŗto receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , ŌĆŗis committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Why investors can still believe in mean reversion
item.description:
LONDON, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Over the past century, U.S. small-cap stocks have outperformed the market by more than 2 percentage points a year. Companies that are cheap relative to book value have done slightly better since 1926. The latest Global Investment Returns Yearbook , opens new tab from UBS remains equivocal as to why these so-called investment factors have historically outperformed. Perhaps, suggest authors Elroy Dimson, Paul Marsh and Mike Staunton, it was due to ŌĆ£transient anomalies,ŌĆØ that ŌĆŗcease to work once theyŌĆÖve been spotted. These are not reassuring grounds for investing oneŌĆÖs wealth.
Veteran investor Jeremy Grantham, the co-founder of Boston asset manager GMO, has a more intellectually satisfying explanation for the value and ŌĆīsmall-cap ŌĆ£anomaliesŌĆØ. In his recently published memoir, ŌĆ£ The Making of a Permabear: The Perils of Long-term Investing in a Short-term World , opens new tab ,ŌĆØ which I helped compile, Grantham explains how in the 1970s he was perhaps the first investor to construct a data series for small-cap stocks that went back to 1926. In the era before computers were widely used in the investment business, he built his index painfully by hand. It was a labour of love.
Grantham made some interesting discoveries. First, small caps had beaten the market since 1926 ŌĆō a finding that finance academics only later latched onto. Second, ŌĆ£their relative performance [against the market] moved in cycles, ŌĆŗebbing and flowing.ŌĆØ After periods when small caps had badly lagged the market, they tended to catch up: ŌĆ£The pendulum swung back and forth. Bursts of superior performance werenŌĆÖt sustained indefinitely.ŌĆØ Third, small caps were riskier. Grantham noticed that they were ŌĆŗalmost wiped out during the Great Depression, but over the following decades slowly regained their lost ground. Given that smaller companies are more vulnerable to economic dislocations, it made sense for their ŌüĀstocks to trade at a discount to the broader market which is dominated by larger more resilient firms.
Grantham also found that most of the extra returns from small caps came from reconstituting the index. After a firm did well and its market capitalisation ŌĆŗgrew, it departed the series. And when a formerly large-cap firm fell on hard times, it joined the small-cap index. Rebalancing the portfolio worked thanks to mean reversion, he says. ThatŌĆÖs to say, on balance, the losersŌĆÖ fortunes improved over time while the winnersŌĆÖ advantage ŌĆŗeroded.
ŌĆ£In a moderately mean-reverting world, any strategy of selling winners and buying losers should win,ŌĆØ Grantham writes. ŌĆ£The fact that a companyŌĆÖs depressed profitability reverts to its average level seemed to me just the fortunes of war. Say a firm is operating in an industry that turns down. Its managers scramble around looking for ways to stop the rotŌĆ” they slash costs and eventually get something rightŌĆ” and as profits recover the stock price does too. Conversely, when a firm is superprofitable its management becomes overconfident, it invests too much, costs rise faster than sales and margins decline.ŌĆØ
There was no special ŌĆ£small-cap effectŌĆØ, ŌĆŗsays Grantham, just a process whereby the returns of winners and losers returned to the average, harvested through reshuffling the portfolio. Mean reversion also explains why value stocks have historically outperformed. Grantham realised that buying stocks that were cheap relative to their book value ŌĆŗonly worked because on average corporate profitability reverts to the mean. ŌĆ£I looked at the data in the Value Line hard copy and saw with my naked eye that in most cases when things get very very profitable, they mean revert downwards. And after profits get wiped out, the ŌĆīreverse happens. The ŌüĀreason this happened is straightforward. Capital moves towards profits: excess returns attract competition and bad returns drive capital away.ŌĆØ
Later in his career, Grantham implemented his belief in mean reversion at the asset-class level. It turned out to be a fraught experience. In the late 1990s, a great stock market bubble formed in the United States: growth stocks were extremely overvalued, while value stocks had never looked cheaper. Grantham bet his firm that bubble would break. Many clients lost patience and left.
At the time, he was told heŌĆÖd lost the plot. One client demanded he explain the firmŌĆÖs relative underperformance, ŌĆ£but for GodŌĆÖs sake donŌĆÖt give us any more of this nonsense about reversion to the mean.ŌĆØ Grantham held on and was vindicated. The bubble burst, growth companies collapsed and value stocks came roaring back. The experience imparted ŌĆŗa painful lesson: ŌĆ£Mean reversion is a useful and hitherto accurate ŌĆŗrule but still a painful oneŌĆ”,ŌĆØ he wrote in one ŌüĀof his quarterly letters, ŌĆ£you will arrive back on trend eventually, but not necessarily with the same clients you left with.ŌĆØ
After the financial crisis of 2008, the value factor went into one of its periodic doldrums. Over the past decade, the valuation of the US stock market has entered bubble territory - and stayed there. Corporate profits ŌĆō formerly the most reliably mean-reverting data in finance, according to Grantham ŌĆō ŌĆŗhave remained far above average. Mean reversion appears to have lost its gravitational powers.
Grantham frets that American corporations have become more less competitive. U.S. companies exert more political power, he says. ŌĆŗThey have used much of their profits ŌüĀto buy back shares rather than invest. A handful of large technology firms became super-profitable but did not attract competition because of their insuperable competitive strengths. ŌĆ£If exceptional profit margins do not attract assets and increased competition, then the capitalistic system is broken,ŌĆØ writes Grantham.
Elsewhere, mean reversion is alive and kicking. Value stocks have outperformed in most markets since late 2020, according to the UBS Yearbook. Last year was a banner period for the value factor almost everywhere, except in the United States. How long can this American exceptionalism last? The current artificial intelligence frenzy ŌüĀcould signal a change. ŌĆŗFormerly asset-light giants like Alphabet (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab are now investing heavily in data centres. AI breakthroughs could lead to more direct competition between large technology ŌĆŗfirms. ItŌĆÖs not clear that their competitive moats will remain intact.
ŌĆ£Higher capex and more competition could drive mean reversion. We could be entering a new phase,ŌĆØ says Grantham. Even if that doesnŌĆÖt happen, he believes that AI will accelerate the political pushback against large, powerful and obscenely profitable companies. One way or another, mean ŌĆŗreversion is coming for the U.S. stock market. As ever, though, the timing remains uncertain!
Follow @Breakingviews , opens new tab on X.

item.title: AI struggles to model for angry Main Street mobs
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Artificial intelligence is getting stumped by angry and anxious humans. Facing the fastest-rising electricity bills in ŌĆŗthe country, MaineŌĆÖs House of Representatives advanced , opens new tab a proposal to ban new data centers larger than ŌĆī20 megawatts until late 2027. Local governments and utility commissions have mustered even stronger resistance. The opposition leaves Silicon Valley and Wall Street to rethink their ambitious plans.
The backlash is broadening and accelerating. States introduced more than 300 bills spanning , opens new tab ŌĆŗmoratoriums, tax-incentive rollbacks and ratepayer protections in the first six weeks of 2026, according to lobbying ŌĆŗfirm MultiState. Community-based pushback blocked or stalled some $160 billion of projects last year, ŌüĀThe New York Times reported , opens new tab last month, citing Data Center Watch, a research outfit backed by AI safety ŌĆŗstartup 10a Labs.
When regulatory pressure squeezes developers, construction shifts to more receptive places like Texas, Indiana and Ohio. ŌĆŗThe flow creates new bottlenecks. The Lone Star StateŌĆÖs grid operator received 233 gigawatts of power requests , opens new tab as of December, nearly quadruple the previous year's amount.
The risk to ratepayers is quietly metastasizing. Utilities are booking guaranteed regulated returns on buildouts that ŌĆŗmay prove speculative. Georgia PowerŌĆÖs plan , opens new tab to add 9.9 GW carries an estimated $50 billion to $60 billion cost to ŌĆŗcustomers over decades. If demand disappoints , opens new tab , households face rate hikes well into the next decade.
Responses so far have failed to ŌĆīneutralize concerns. ŌüĀAmazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , Google, Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab and Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab signed the White House's ratepayer protection pledge , opens new tab in March, but the commitments are voluntary and carry no penalties. Utility AEP Ohio forced data centers to cover 85% of claimed capacity, weeding out speculative projects and shrinking , opens new tab its waitlist by 57% to 13 GW. Expanding this rule nationwide risks ŌĆŗconstraining investment and driving up ŌĆŗpower prices.
Individual policy spats ŌüĀmask the bigger issue: AI resistance is entrenching itself across every level of government. Only one in four Americans say they think data centers deliver meaningful , opens new tab local ŌĆŗjobs, while half are more concerned than excited , opens new tab about AI's increased use in ŌĆŗdaily life, according ŌüĀto Pew Research Center. Lawmakers at every level now have a convenient boogeyman. AI is meant to help solve diffuse and multi-faceted problems, but the technology isn't equipped to handle this emotionally charged one.
Follow Sebastian Pellejero ŌüĀon LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: 7-Eleven IPO delay plays into Couche-TardŌĆÖs hands
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Seven & i's (3382.T) , opens new tab decision to push back the listing of 7-Eleven was almost certainly the right call. But time is running out for the ŌĆŗJapanese corner store empire to improve operations at its North American gas-and-goods subsidiaryŌĆöa key plank in CEO Stephen ŌĆīDacus' bid to revive growth and improve shareholder returns. The case is building for spurned Canadian suitor Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD.TO) , opens new tab to take another run.
The planned spin-off of the $34 billion group's largest foreign business, spanning 13,000 shops across the U.S. and Canada, was whipped up last year to help fund 2 trillion yen ($12.6 ŌĆŗbillion) in share buybacks through 2030. That was part of a broader drive led by Dacus to reassure shareholders ŌĆŗdeprived of a juicy payout following the collapse of Couche-Tard's $46 billion buyout offer. After announcing the IPO ŌüĀdelay on Thursday, Seven & i insisted this programme will continue apace. That, however, didn't stop investors from dumping the stock, which ŌĆŗhas now fallen 6% since the Nikkei published a report on the delay just before Thursday's market close.
This reflects an uncomfortable reality ŌĆŗfor the Seven & i boss who, per the Financial Times , opens new tab , said that any listing would be ŌĆ£driven strictly by valueŌĆØ, adding the delay was necessitated by slow progress in turning the American business and market conditions. Dacus took the helm last year with a mandate to convince investors that their best bet ŌĆŗto secure sustained returns lay with management in Tokyo.
Progress has been dismal so far. 7-Eleven's same-store merchandise sales fell in three out ŌĆŗof the last four quarters, with those in the December quarter down 0.6% year-on-year. To further complicate matters, the American chain also relies on ŌĆīpetrol ŌüĀsales, leaving it exposed to oil price shocks like those now roiling global markets. Since Couche-Tard walked away in July, Seven & i has delivered measly shareholder returns of 4.5%, versus 34.6% for Japan's benchmark Topix (.TOPX) , opens new tab index.
Such struggles are music to the ears of Couche-Tard chair Alain Bouchard, who dropped his 2,600 yen per share takeover bid due to what the Circle K owner described as a ŌĆŗlack of engagement from Seven & i. ŌĆŗShares in the latter ŌüĀnow trade at a 20% discount to that offer price, leaving a wide gap for the Canadian rival to step through if it wants another crack at JapanŌĆÖs largest-ever foreign acquisition.
Follow Hudson Lockett ŌĆŗon Bluesky , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Discount Tesla could be a painful reality check
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ItŌĆÖs the irony of Elon MuskŌĆÖs life that his ambitions make battery-powered cars seem pedestrian. Whether itŌĆÖs rockets, robots, chips or chatbots, ŌĆŗthe Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab tycoon has had his hands full of late. The distraction is evident in the $1.3 trillion ŌĆīelectric-vehicle pioneer's results. Both sales and analystsŌĆÖ estimates of future earnings are sliding alarmingly. That wasnŌĆÖt supposed to matter, as MuskŌĆÖs driverless revolution would render anything with a steering wheel obsolete. A new effort to build a cheap sports-utility vehicle, reported by Reuters on Thursday, sits awkwardly with that narrative. ŌĆŗThe discount vehicle could be an expensive reality check for Tesla investors.
Musk has promised to build a super-cheap ŌĆŗcar in the past. After much talk of a brand-new manufacturing process that would slash costs in ŌüĀhalf, though, the plan seemed to disappear, replaced in part by cut-down versions of the best-selling Model 3 and Model ŌĆŗY. Instead, Tesla trumpeted the bizarre, no-steering-wheel Cybercab , with Musk decreeing that a ŌĆ£regular $25,000 modelŌĆØ would be not only ŌĆ£pointlessŌĆØ but ŌĆ£completely at odds with ŌĆŗwhat we believe.ŌĆØ The company's future, he implied, lay in building vehicles tuned to specifically serve as robotic chauffeurs.
This is a crucial thesis for TeslaŌĆÖs extraordinary valuation. Even as analysts' estimates of future profit have declined precipitously, according to Visible Alpha, the company's shares have traded at ŌĆŗan ever-higher multiple of that shrinking figure. First-quarter car sales of under 337,000 fell short of Wall Street expectations, and led ŌĆŗto the widest gap between vehicles manufactured and delivered since the pandemic-ravaged first quarter of 2020. ItŌĆÖs also a far cry from the ŌĆīnear-500,000 vehicles ŌüĀthat Tesla sold in the final three months of 2024.
Producing a cheaper conventional ride may shore up TeslaŌĆÖs slipping market share as battery-powered rivals from China and Europe gain ground. The company has held expenses admirably steady even as it ramped up production of the dud Cybertruck: the average car cost it about $34,000 after adjusting for leases last quarter. If old efficiency ŌĆŗtargets are still realistic, then ŌĆŗa cheap vehicle is within ŌüĀreach. Pricey gasoline due to conflict in the Middle East gives drivers an extra incentive to make the switch . The new car will surely incorporate existing self-driving software, allowing Tesla to cling ŌĆŗto the promise of future autonomy.
The problem is that the Middle East conflict is also ŌĆŗscrambling supply chains, ŌüĀjust as the pandemic aftermath pushed Tesla's costs above $41,000 per vehicle in 2022. Even worse, though, is the tacit admission that Musk cannot single-handedly redefine what a car is. Regulations and customer acceptance take time. The expansion of robotaxis like Waymo is halting. ŌüĀThe driverless ŌĆŗrevolution might be delayed. TeslaŌĆÖs valuation isnŌĆÖt yet pricing that in.
Follow Jonathan ŌĆŗGuilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Orban ousted after 16 years as Hungarians flock to pro-EU rival
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a key opponent of European Union efforts to help Ukraine fend off Russia's invasion, lost power after 16 years on Sunday as Hungarians voted in record numbers for ŌĆŗa pro-EU course spearheaded by centre-right rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, 62, had won endorsements from U.S. President Donald Trump and some top European conservatives, but early results showed ŌĆīhis nationalist Fidesz party losing to Peter Magyar 's pro-EU Tisza party due to Hungary's economic stagnation .
A fiery anti-Communist youth leader during the Cold War, Orban, the European Union's longest-serving leader, is a patriotic hero to supporters, but critics at home and abroad have accused him of taking Hungary on an authoritarian path.
Born in 1963 in a village west of Budapest, Orban trained as a lawyer, briefly studied political philosophy at Oxford, and even played semi-professional soccer before becoming ŌĆŗprime minister for the first time in 1998 aged just 35.
Hungary joined NATO on Orban's watch but he lost power in 2002. After eight years in opposition, he won ŌĆŗa landslide victory in 2010, enabling him to rewrite Hungary's constitution and pass major laws aimed at creating an "illiberal democracy".
His consolidation of executive power, new ŌüĀcurbs on NGO activities and media freedoms, and a weakening of judicial independence led to clashes with the European Union over democratic standards, culminating in a decision to suspend billions of euros in ŌĆŗfunding for Hungary.
But all that came crashing down for Orban on Sunday night as preliminary results put Magyar on course for a parliamentary supermajority, enabling his centre-right party to unwind all of Orban's ŌĆŗcontroversial reforms.
"What tonight's election result means for the fate of our country and nation and what the deeper or higher meaning of all this is, remains unclear. We do not know it yet. Time will tell," Orban told supporters, conceding defeat.
"But however it has turned out, we will keep serving our country and the Hungarian nation from opposition."
During Europe's 2015 migrant crisis, Orban cast himself as the guardian of Hungary's national identity and ŌĆŗChristian heritage, refusing to accept EU quotas for taking in asylum seekers, mostly Muslims from the Middle East and beyond. His government has gradually taken steps to erode LGBTQ+ rights .
His hard line on ŌĆŗimmigration and his efforts to revive Hungary's declining birth rate earned him praise from other conservative leaders, including Trump.
Orban - who also racked up sweeping election wins in 2014, 2018 and 2022 - had secured endorsements this time ŌĆīfrom Italy's ŌüĀPrime Minister Giorgia Meloni, Marine Le Pen of France's National Rally, and Alice Weidel of the Alternative for Germany.
Trump has also endorsed Orban, saying U.S.-Hungary relations have reached "new heights" owing to their leadership after years of conflict under Democratic administrations in Washington.
Orban has maintained close ties with Russia, a key energy supplier , and China, whose firms are building major EV and battery plants in the landlocked central European country.
He has sought to frame the election as a choice between "war or peace" , suggesting Tisza wants to drag Hungary into the war raging in neighbouring Ukraine , which it strongly denies.
"For peace, Fidesz is the ŌĆŗsafe choice," Orban said on the campaign trail in ŌĆŗFebruary. He has clashed frequently with Ukraine's ŌüĀPresident Volodymyr Zelenskiy and infuriated Hungary's EU partners by blocking a 90 billion euro aid package for Kyiv.
But surveys showed Hungarian voters were more concerned with domestic issues such as healthcare and the economy, which has stagnated for the past three years.
Hungary experienced the EU's worst inflationary surge following Russia's ŌĆŗFebruary 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which lifted food prices close to EU average levels, while Hungarian wages are still third-lowest in the 27-member ŌĆŗbloc.
Despite generous pro-family policies, including ŌüĀcheap loans and tax benefits, Orban appears to have lost the support of younger voters as he has lurched to the right.
Polls before the election showed young voters particularly eager for change, with Orban shifting back and forth between trying to woo the key demographic and deriding their opposition to his leadership as a 'phony rebellion'.
"I know young people like to turn against their parents and this can cause political problems," ŌüĀsaid Orban, a ŌĆŗfather of five and a grandfather.
Despite attending countless election rallies and keeping up a barrage of interviews and social ŌĆŗmedia posts, he gave a rare glimpse late last year into the toll that the campaign may have taken after so many years at the helm.
"When I was a soldier (doing military service), they told us a soldier cannot be cold, he can only ŌĆŗperceive the cold," he said. "I am the same. I am not tired. It is just that my strength is running out."

item.title: Once inspired by Orban, Hungary's Peter Magyar unseats him in landmark election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - When Peter Magyar was a child, he taped a photo of Viktor Orban , then an anti-Communist firebrand, on his bedroom wall, thrilled by Hungary's first democratic elections in 1990.
Decades later, he ended Orban's 16-year rule as prime minister in an election that brought a record-high turnout and was expected to rattle Russia and send shockwaves through right-wing circles across the ŌĆŗWest, including U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Magyar's centre-right, pro-European Union Tisza party beat Orban's nationalist Fidesz party in Sunday's parliamentary election. Partial results showed Tisza ŌĆīwould win 137 seats, or a two-thirds majority, in the 199-seat parliament.
Only nine years old when communism collapsed, Magyar said he had decorated his walls with photos of leading political figures in his Budapest family home.
Orban, then a young lawyer, had become a hero of Hungary's pro-democracy movement when he publicly demanded in 1989 that Soviet troops leave the country.
"There was a surge of energy around the regime change that swept me up as ŌĆŗa child," Magyar told the Fokuszcsoport podcast last year.
Magyar, whose family name literally means "Hungarian", burst into the limelight two years ago after his ex-wife, Orban's former justice minister ŌĆŗJudit Varga, resigned from all political roles after a sex-abuse case pardon that caused public uproar.
Magyar quickly distanced himself from the governing party and ŌüĀaccused it of corruption and spreading propaganda, saying he had become disillusioned with Fidesz.
Just four months after emerging from near-total obscurity with an interview at YouTube channel Partizan, MagyarŌĆÖs new party ŌĆŗwon 30% in the June 2024 European elections, finishing second to Fidesz and crushing the rest of the opposition.
Orban's defeat has significant implications not only for Hungary but for Europe and ŌĆŗits populist far right.
Orban has sought to create what he calls an "illiberal democracy" since 2010, curbing media freedoms and NGO activities, and weakening the independence of the judiciary.
He has forged good relations with Russian President Vladimir Putin and also with U.S. President Donald Trump but has clashed repeatedly with the EU, which suspended billions of euros in funding due to concerns over Hungary's democratic standards.
By contrast, Magyar has pledged to rebuild Hungary's Western ŌĆŗorientation and end its dependence on Russian energy by 2035 while striving for "pragmatic relations" with Moscow. He has also promised to unlock the frozen EU funds, which would help revive Hungary's ŌĆŗstagnant economy.
"On the first day we need to pass anti-corruption measures and we need to submit our application to join the European Prosecutor's Office," Magyar said on Sunday morning after casting his vote.
But he has ŌĆītrodden carefully ŌüĀduring the election campaign, keen not to scare away more conservative voters.
Unlike Orban, he does not reject in principle Ukraine's right to join the EU one day but Tisza's programme does not support fast-track entry for Kyiv. Like Fidesz, Tisza opposes EU quotas for taking in migrants and it would also keep in place a border fence built under Orban to keep out illegal migrants.
But analysts say tensions between Budapest and the EU - further aggravated by Orban's veto of a 90 billion euro aid package for Kyiv - could ease under Tisza.
"Orban has lost faith in the current form ŌĆŗand direction of European integration, and is pursuing ŌĆŗa policy of vetoes and obstruction," said ŌüĀBotond Feledy, a geopolitical analyst at Red Snow Consulting.
"Tisza has no objection in principle to integration and would pitch its battles at a practical level."
Magyar drew from Orban's playbook in this election, waging a grassroots campaign that took him into Fidesz's rural heartlands.
His rallies ŌĆŗalways featured lots of national flags, in an Orban-style appeal to Hungarian voters' patriotism.
His consistent and clear messages, and skilful use of social ŌĆŗmedia have all contributed ŌüĀto his rapid rise, said Gabor Toka, senior research fellow at the Vera and Donald Blinken Open Society Archives.
"Many people are also reassured by the story of someone who has irrevocably come into conflict with the system, and has no way back," he said, referring to Magyar's break with Orban.
Born in 1981 into a family of lawyers, Magyar also studied law. He married Varga in 2006 and ŌüĀwhen her career ŌĆŗtook her to Brussels, Magyar joined Hungary's diplomatic corps and worked on EU legislation. After returning to Hungary, he ŌĆŗjoined a state bank and then headed a student-loan agency.
Magyar and Varga, who divorced in 2023, have three sons.
Magyar describes himself as religious and says he enjoys cooking and playing soccer with his friends and sons.
Asked in December how ŌĆŗhe had changed since going into politics, Magyar alluded to media reports that describe him as short-tempered, saying: "Now I count to 10."

item.title: Reactions to PM Orban's defeat in Hungary's election
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 12 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition centre-right Tisza party appeared on track to win Sunday's national election , partial results showed, ending 16 years of rule by Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a nationalist backed by U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia.
Here are some quotes ŌĆŗfrom Hungarian politicians and foreign leaders on the election results.
"As ŌĆīwe promised, as we hoped, today, on April 12, 2026, Hungary and several million people made history again, exactly 23 years to the day after there was a referendum on joining the European Union in Hungary."
"The election results are not final yet but the ŌĆŗsituation is understandable and clear. The election result is painful for us, but clear. The responsibility and possibility ŌĆŗof governing was not given to us. I have congratulated the winner."
"Far-right authoritarian Viktor Orb├Īn has lost the election. Trump ŌĆŗsycophants and MAGA extremists in Congress are up next in November. Winter is coming."
"Hungary ŌĆŗhas chosen Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary. A country reclaims its European path. The Union grows stronger. Hungary has chosen Europe. Europe has always chosen Hungary. Together we are stronger. A country returns to its European path. The Union becomes stronger... EuropeŌĆÖs heart is beating stronger ŌĆŗin Hungary tonight."
"IŌĆÖve just spoken with Peter Magyar to congratulate him on his victory in Hungary! France welcomes ŌĆŗwhat has been a victory in terms of people taking part in the democratic process, and a victory which shows the attachment of ŌĆīthe Hungarian ŌüĀpeople to the values of the European Union and for Hungary's role in Europe."
"The Hungarian people have decided. My heartfelt congratulations on your electoral success, dear @magyarpeterMP
"I am looking forward to working with you. LetŌĆÖs join forces for a strong, secure and, above all, united Europe. Gratul├Īlok, kedves Magyar P├®ter!"
"I congratulate Peter Magyar and Tisza on ŌĆŗtheir victory in the Hungarian ŌĆŗparliamentary elections - a result ŌüĀwith great importance to all of Europe. I look forward to close and constructive cooperation in pursuing peace and stability, democracy and the rule of law on our continent."
"In democratic elections, the Hungarian people have demonstrated their strong will to be an active ŌĆŗmember of European ŌüĀUnion and NATO. The election result gives Hungary the opportunity to return to our community of values and security as a constructive actor."
"Hungarians have made a historic choice for a free and strong Hungary in a united Europe, rejecting forces that ŌüĀignore ŌĆŗtheir interests. Congratulations to Peter Magyar on winning the election! I look forward ŌĆŗto working together on what unites Estonia, Hungary and Europe."
"Big win for Hungary! Big win for Europe! Congratulations to @magyarpeterMP. There are a lot ŌĆŗof things we could and should do for the sake of democracy, justice and peace."

item.title: US military says it will start blockade of all ships going to and from Iran on Monday
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Central Command said it will begin implementing ŌĆŗa blockade of all maritime ŌĆītraffic entering and exiting Iranian ports on April 13 at 10 ŌĆŗa.m. ET (1400 GMT), after ŌĆŗPresident Donald Trump said the U.S. ŌüĀNavy would start blockading ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz.
"The blockade ŌĆŗwill be enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ŌĆŗports and coastal areas, ŌĆŗincluding all Iranian ports on the Arabian ŌĆīGulf ŌüĀand Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM forces will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting ŌĆŗthe Strait ŌĆŗof ŌüĀHormuz to and from non-Iranian ports," CENTCOM wrote ŌĆŗin a statement on ŌĆŗsocial ŌüĀmedia.
Additional information will be provided to commercial mariners through a ŌüĀformal ŌĆŗnotice before the ŌĆŗblockade starts, CENTCOM added.

item.title: Oil bounces back above $100 after US, Iran talks end in stalemate
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices jumped above $100 ŌĆŗa barrel on ŌĆīMonday as the U.S. Navy prepared ŌĆŗto blockade the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz after ŌüĀtalks between ŌĆŗthe U.S. and Iran ŌĆŗfailed to reach a deal to end ŌĆŗthe war.
Brent crude ŌĆŗfutures rose $7.11, or 7.47%, ŌĆīto $102.31 ŌüĀa barrel by 2204 GMT after settling 0.75% lower ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗFriday.
U.S. ŌüĀWest Texas Intermediate was at $104.43 ŌĆŗa barrel, ŌĆŗup $7.86, ŌüĀor 8.14%, following a 1.33% loss ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe previous ŌĆŗsession.

item.title: Spanish premier Sanchez heads for fourth China visit, risks annoying Trump
item.description:
MADRID, April 13 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez begins his fourth visit to China in as many years on Monday, underlining his ŌĆŗdetermination to steer an independent course that has riled U.S. President Donald Trump .
Spain has been one of Europe's loudest proponents of ŌĆīexpanding trade and treating China as a strategic ally rather than the economic and geopolitical rival that Trump sees it as.
The repeat visit highlights Sanchez's determination not to align himself with Trump ŌĆö who has heavily criticised Spain for denying landing rights to U.S. forces in the war with Iran and for its relatively low defence spending - ŌĆŗand threatened to punish uncooperative NATO military allies.
Sanchez's approach is popular with voters, but has unnerved firms and opposition politicians who fear that ŌĆŗfriction with the U.S. administration could be costly for Spain.
A government source ŌüĀwho was not authorised to speak to the media said Tuesday's meeting with President Xi Jinping would focus on geopolitics, and that Spain ŌĆŗsees China as a stabilising force.
Yet Ramon Gascon Alonso, Asia-Pacific coordinator of Spain's Exporters' and Investors' Club, said this focus could further harm trade burdened ŌĆŗby Trump's tariffs on imports from the European Union, and "make our already precarious situation much worse".
"The United States is the leading foreign investor in Spain," he said. "We have a significant volume of trade with the United States in sectors that are central to our economy ŌĆō absolutely vital."
Sanchez stands out among European allies as an outspoken ŌĆŗcritic of the U.S. and Israeli military campaigns.
A senior opposition source advised against any further criticism while Sanchez is in China, accusing Sanchez of ŌĆŗtangling with Trump for domestic reasons and putting at risk NATO unity and the U.S. military presence in Spain.
Chinese ambassador to Spain Yao Jing said warm ŌĆīrelations had ŌüĀencouraged Chinese companies to invest.
"Spain is more reasonable in dealing with China," he said. "It has its own judgment, its own interests, it wants access to Chinese markets. So we do business."
Chinese firms invested 643 million euros in Spain in 2025, up from 149 million a year earlier, data from the Economy Ministry shows, bringing the total to 9.7 billion euros between 2010 and 2025, primarily in extractive industry and ŌĆŗthe energy sector.
Xi will this time ŌĆŗfete Sanchez and his wife Begona ŌüĀwith a formal banquet, while he will also meet with China's premier, Li Qiang, and top legislator Zhao Leji during the three-day visit.
On day one, Sanchez will visit the headquarters of tech giant Xiaomi and host ŌĆŗa forum for Spanish and Chinese energy, infrastructure, bioscience and technology firms. He will also meet with ŌĆŗinternational relations specialists ŌüĀand make a speech at Beijing's Tsinghua University.
Madrid hopes the visit will help narrow a trade deficit that has more than doubled in four years to nearly $50 billion in 2025, looking to boost agricultural and manufacturing exports to offset high volumes of Chinese imports.
Still, Sanchez is not taking a business delegation ŌüĀwith him ŌĆŗthis time, and no major deals are planned.
Officials do, however, hope to sign a regionalisation ŌĆŗagreement for Spanish poultry exports affected by bird flu outbreaks. A similar deal for the pork industry is credited with staving off a major blow to one of Spain's most ŌĆŗlucrative exports during a recent African swine fever outbreak.

item.title: Keiko Fujimori leads Peru's presidential vote with 16.6%, shows early exit poll from Ipsos Peru
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Right-leaning candidate Keiko Fujimori ŌĆŗis leading Peru's ŌĆīpresidential vote with 16.6%, an early ŌĆŗexit poll by ŌĆŗIpsos Peru showed on ŌüĀSunday.
Leftist candidate ŌĆŗRoberto Sanchez trailed with ŌĆŗ12.1%, and a third candidate, center-leftist Ricardo ŌĆŗBelmont, had 11.8% ŌĆŗof the vote.
If no ŌĆīcandidate ŌüĀclears the 50% threshold needed to win outright, the ŌĆŗtwo ŌĆŗmost-voted ŌüĀcandidates would advance to a ŌĆŗsecond round, ŌĆŗscheduled ŌüĀfor June 7.

item.title: Cyclone Vaianu leaves North Island cleanup underway in New Zealand
item.description:
WELLINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - New Zealanders who evacuated ahead of a cyclone that battered the North Island on Sunday ŌĆŗwere gradually being allowed to return home as ŌĆīthe storm moved away from the country.
Local media reported cleanup crews were clearing roads, removing fallen trees and checking ŌĆŗfor possible landslides. Power companies continued to report ŌĆŗunplanned outages, while some roads remained closed.
Julie Jukes, ŌüĀacting mayor of Whakatane, one of the areas ŌĆŗhit by the cyclone, told Radio New Zealand it ŌĆŗhad brought the worst weather she had ever seen, but "the main thing is that everybody was safe and it's only damage ŌĆŗto property and trees, power and things like ŌĆŗthat."
Prime Minister Christopher Luxon told Radio New Zealand the emergency system ŌĆīhad ŌüĀworked well, people had prepared for the storm and the response had been much better than in previous emergencies.
New Zealand forecaster MetService said in a post ŌĆŗon X ŌĆŗlate on ŌüĀSunday that Cyclone Vaianu had moved offshore and was east of the North Island.
By ŌĆŗMonday morning, the only warnings still in ŌĆŗplace ŌüĀwere for large ocean swells off the coast of lower North Island.
New Zealand has been hit by a ŌüĀseries ŌĆŗof severe weather events this ŌĆŗyear, including heavy rain in January that triggered a landslide and killed ŌĆŗsix people.

item.title: US to blockade Iran after talks fail to yield a┬Ādeal
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said it will begin a blockade of all maritime ŌĆŗtraffic entering and exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday after weekend talks failed to reach a deal to end the war with Iran , jeopardizing a ŌĆīfragile two-week ceasefire.
The talks in Islamabad, which ran from Saturday into early Sunday, were the first direct U.S.-Iranian meeting in more than a decade and the highest-level discussions since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution. The negotiations came days after a ceasefire began on Tuesday, aimed at ending six weeks of fighting that has killed thousands of people across the Gulf, throttled vital supplies of energy and sparked fears of a wider regional conflict.
The U.S. Central ŌĆŗCommand said on Sunday that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ŌĆŗareas, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ŌüĀnot be impeded, the U.S. military said. Additional information would be provided to commercial mariners through a formal notice prior to the start of the blockade, it said.
President Donald Trump on ŌĆŗSunday also said U.S. forces would intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high ŌĆŗseas," Trump wrote on social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
He added that the U.S. Navy will begin destroying mines that the Iranians had dropped in the Strait of Hormuz , a choke point for about 20% of global energy supplies.
While shipping data showed three supertankers fully laden with oil passed through the Strait on Saturday, tankers were steering clear of the waterway on Monday, ahead ŌĆŗof the U.S. blockade.
After Trump's initial remarks on Sunday, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards warned that military vessels approaching the strait will be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with ŌĆŗharshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
A U.S. official said Iran rejected Washington's call for an end to all uranium enrichment, the dismantling of all major enrichment facilities and the transfer of ŌĆīhighly enriched ŌüĀuranium.
Iran also refused U.S. demands that Iran cease funding for Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis, as well as fully open the Strait of Hormuz, the official added.
Iranian media said there was agreement on a number of issues, but the strait and Iran's nuclear program were the main sticking points.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said Iran had "encountered maximalism, shifting goalposts, and blockade" when just inches away from an "Islamabad MoU."
"Zero lessons learned," he added. "Good will begets good will. Enmity begets enmity."
Six weeks of fighting has killed thousands , roiled the global economy and sent oil prices soaring as Iran prevented traffic through ŌĆŗthe Strait.
The U.S. dollar and oil rose in ŌĆŗearly trading on Monday after the failure ŌüĀof the talks, while stocks in Asia eased.
Trump told Fox News' "Sunday Briefing" program that oil and gasoline prices may remain high through November's midterm elections , a rare acknowledgement of the potential political fallout from the war.
Iran's Qalibaf posted a map of Washington-area gasoline prices on social media with the ŌĆŗcomment: "Enjoy the current pump figures. With the so-called 'blockade'. Soon you'll be nostalgic for $4ŌĆō$5 gas."
Trump told Fox News on Sunday that he believed ŌĆŗIran would continue to negotiate ŌüĀand called the Islamabad discussions "very friendly."
"I do believe they're going to come to the table on this, because nobody can be so stupid as to say, ŌĆśWe want nuclear weapons,ŌĆÖ and they have no cards," he said.
But several hours later, the U.S. president said he did not care whether a "desperate" Iran returned to the negotiating table.
"If they don't come back, I'm fine," Trump told journalists on Sunday night after ŌüĀhe returned ŌĆŗto the Washington area from an overnight stay in Florida.
Qalibaf blamed the U.S. for not winning Tehran's trust, despite ŌĆŗhis team offering "forward-looking initiatives," Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian, who discussed the talks in a call with Russian President Vladimir Putin, said Tehran wanted "a balanced and fair agreement."
"If the United States returns to the framework of international law, reaching an ŌĆŗagreement is not far off," he told Putin, Iranian state media reported.

item.title: Hungary opposition's landslide win heralds reforms, thaw in EU ties
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Peter Magyar's landslide victory in Sunday's Hungarian election has handed his centre-right Tisza party a sweeping mandate that will give it a free ŌĆŗhand to enact reforms, bolster the rule of law and potentially unlock billions in European Union funding.
Economists and political analysts say the incoming government's expected two-thirds supermajority ŌĆīwas the most EU- and market-friendly scenario - and before Sunday, one of the most improbable - and would likely trigger a strong rally in Hungarian assets on Monday.
A number of uncertainties remain, and wary diplomats and analysts say the new government must deliver on its promises before reaping the full benefits, but the markets for now look willing to give Budapest's new masters the benefit of the doubt.
"The result is a game-changer and will allow Magyar ŌĆŗto govern with a free hand," said Mujtaba Rahman, a managing director at Eurasia Group. "Most importantly, he will be able to unwind Orban's autocracy and deliver on all of ŌĆŗthe reforms the EU is demanding.
"That means at least 6.4 billion euros ($7.46 billion) from the resilience and recovery facility should flow quickly, shoring up ŌüĀthe real economy and further consolidating Tisza's win."
The election had long been anticipated as the most market-sensitive in Europe this year given the eurosceptic Orban's frequent clashes with ŌĆŗBrussels during his 16-year rule, over issues ranging from immigration to his closeness to Russia.
Orban had expressed confidence throughout the election campaign despite lagging in opinion polls, saying his goal was to protect ŌĆŗHungary's national identity and traditional Christian values within the EU, while denying any wrongdoing.
But the markets had been signalling for weeks that investors expected change. The share prices of companies linked to Orban fell sharply, while market volatility gauges indicated that big currency moves were likely after the election.
Magyar, addressing jubilant supporters chanting "Europe, Europe" after Orban conceded defeat, pledged to make Hungary a strong EU and NATO ally and rebuild ties marred by years ŌĆŗof conflict.
"With the two-thirds majority allowing us to amend the constitution, we will restore the system of checks and balances," Magyar said.
"We will join the European Public Prosecutor's Office and guarantee the ŌĆŗdemocratic functioning of our country. We will never again allow anyone to hold free Hungary captive or to abandon it."
One fundamental plank of Magyar's plan to kick-start Hungary's economy, which has been mired in near-stagnation ŌĆīfor the past ŌüĀthree years, was to unlock EU funds frozen as democratic standards eroded under Orban.
"A constitutional majority is a different story entirely," said Ian Bremmer at GZERO Media.
"That would give Magyar the power to rewrite the constitution, clear out Fidesz loyalists from captured institutions, fully access EU funding, and even adopt the euro ŌĆō a core campaign pledge."
On Sunday, Magyar called on Hungary's chief prosecutor, the head of the top court, the head of media authority and other officials to resign, saying the country's public institutions had been captured by Orban loyalists over the past 16 years.
Magyar has pledged a sweeping anti-corruption ŌĆŗdrive as his party seeks to meet ŌüĀEU conditions, including stronger judicial independence and public procurement, to unlock the funds.
However, credit rating agencies such as S&P Global and Fitch Ratings, as well as some EU diplomats, are sceptical about whether any money still available under Hungary's pandemic recovery funding would be released.
Diplomats and analysts say comparisons ŌĆŗwith Poland's 2023 election, when Prime Minister Donald Tusk's pro-EU cabinet secured a quick release of EU funding on promises to roll back ŌĆŗhis nationalist predecessor's policies, ŌüĀmay be misguided.
"There is no willingness to give out the money only on a promise like the EU did to Tusk in Poland, who was not able to deliver on most promises," said an EU diplomat.
"Tisza would need to demonstrate that it can deliver. But if something is legally impossible, and that can be demonstrated, then the EU could figure out a way."
Analysts at Capital Economics say the release ŌüĀof EU funding ŌĆŗcould help cut Hungary's budget deficit towards 3.5% to 4% of national output by the end of the decade ŌĆŗand stabilise public debt - the EU's highest outside the euro zone.
"Overall, the election result marks a major turning point for Hungary's economy," Liam Peach said in a note.
"The durability of any positive market reaction will now depend on how quickly ŌĆŗTisza moves to rebuild relations with the EU, secure EU fund disbursements and signal a credible medium-term fiscal anchor."
($1 = 0.8573 euros)

item.title: US, Australia, Philippines hold second joint drills in South China Sea this year
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Australia joined the Philippines for their second joint maritime exercises in ŌĆŗthe South China Sea this year, amid tensions ŌĆīwith China in the disputed waterway.
The four-day drills from April 9 to 12 brought together warships, fighter jets ŌĆŗand surveillance aircraft in a series of coordinated ŌĆŗoperations to strengthen maritime defence capabilities, the ŌüĀPhilippine military said on Monday.
The exercises underscored the "deepening ŌĆŗdefence cooperation among the three nations and their shared ŌĆŗcommitment to regional security", it said in a statement.
The Philippines deployed its FAŌĆæ50 fighter jets, while Australia contributed PŌĆæ8A ŌĆŗPoseidon aircraft and the United States the USS ŌĆŗAshland, a dock landing ship.
The drills came ahead of the April ŌĆī20 ŌüĀopening of the annual large-scale war games called Balikatan - or "shoulder-to-shoulder" - between Manila and Washington, which for the first time will include Japan as a full ŌĆŗparticipant rather ŌĆŗthan an ŌüĀobserver, alongside Australia.
The Philippines and China have traded accusations over a series of ŌĆŗincidents in the South China Sea, ŌĆŗincluding a ŌüĀrecent encounter in which Beijing fired flares at a Philippine Coast Guard aircraft.
Beijing has routinely opposed Manila's joint ŌüĀmilitary ŌĆŗactivities with its allied partners ŌĆŗin the South China Sea, saying such drills heighten tensions in ŌĆŗthe region.

item.title: Russia, Ukraine trade accusations of Orthodox Easter ceasefire violations
item.description:
KYIV/MOSCOW, April 12 (Reuters) - Russia and Ukraine accused each other on Sunday of breaching the 32-hour ceasefire in their four-year war, reporting ŌĆīmore than a thousand drone and shelling attacks just hours after the truce began on Saturday to mark Orthodox Easter.
The Russian defence ministry said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations overnight into Sunday.
The General Staff of Ukraine's military, in a late evening report issued as the end of the truce ŌĆŗwas approaching, said it had compiled a total of 7,696 Russian violations over the course of the ŌĆŗtruce.
According to the calendar of the Orthodox faith dominant in both countries, Easter is celebrated ŌüĀthis year on Sunday.
A soldier from Ukraine's 65th brigade fighting in Zaporizhzhia said Russian reconnaissance drones were still circling ŌĆŗthe area despite the ceasefire, blocking efforts to recover the bodies of fallen troops.
"We wanted to evacuate our fallen comrades ŌĆŗtoday, but (the Russians) are not letting us do so just yet," the soldier, known by the call sign Spider, said at a candle-lit military Easter service.
Both sides continued to report what they described as violations of the ceasefire throughout the day on Sunday. The ŌĆŗtruce was due to end at midnight (2100 GMT).
The latest report by the Ukrainian General Staff said the 7,696 Russian ŌĆŗviolations noted over the course of the truce included 1,355 artillery shelling incidents, 115 assault operations and 6,226 strikes by attack drones. ŌĆīIt said ŌüĀno air strikes had been recorded.
Ukraine's State Emergencies Service said two civilians were wounded on Sunday by a Russian drone attack in northeastern Kharkiv region. Police in the region later said a Russian drone had struck a car, injuring one person.
Russia said a child was among civilians injured in a Ukrainian drone attack on the Kursk region. The ŌĆŗBelgorod region's governor, Vyacheslav Gladkov, ŌĆŗreported two people killed by ŌüĀUkrainian shelling and several injured in other strikes through the border region.
