The Russia-appointed head of the parts of southern Kherson region held by Russian forces, Vladimir Saldo, said Ukrainian troops ŌĆŗhad shelled an administrative building and a private home in two villages, without causing ŌĆŗinjuries.
Reuters could not ŌüĀindependently verify reports of military activity.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said on Sunday the truce was a "humanitarian gesture" from Russian President Vladimir Putin, but that Russian troops would resume fighting as soon as it ends.
"Putin has instructed the Russian military to ŌüĀremain ŌĆŗon high alert in case of provocations," Peskov said.
Putin announced the temporary ŌĆŗOrthodox Easter ceasefire on April 9. Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, whose previous ceasefire proposals were turned down by Moscow, agreed to the truce.

item.title: Businessman Rafael Lopez Aliaga leads Peru's presidential vote with 23.4%, official count shows
item.description:
LIMA, April 12 (Reuters) - Right-leaning candidate Rafael Lopez ŌĆŗAliaga is leading Peru's ŌĆīpresidential race with 23.4% of the votes, ŌĆŗearly results ŌĆŗof the official vote count ŌüĀshowed Sunday.
Center-left ŌĆŗcandidate Jorge Nieto trailed ŌĆŗwith 16.4% of votes, with 5% of ballots ŌĆŗcounted.
A third candidate, ŌĆŗright-wing Keiko Fujimori, had 16.1% ŌĆīof ŌüĀthe votes.
If no candidate clears the 50% threshold needed ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗwin ŌüĀoutright, the two most-voted candidates ŌĆŗwould advance to ŌĆŗa ŌüĀsecond round, scheduled for June 7.

item.title: Trump attacks Pope Leo, calling him 'weak' on crime and 'terrible'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald ŌĆīTrump issued a scathing attack on Pope Leo late on Sunday, calling him "weak" on ŌĆŗcrime and "terrible" for foreign policy after ŌĆŗthe religious leader criticized Trump's foreign ŌüĀand immigration policies.
"Pope Leo is WEAK ŌĆŗon Crime and terrible for Foreign Policy," ŌĆŗTrump wrote in a lengthy post on Truth Social.
Leo, who is known for choosing his ŌĆŗwords carefully, has emerged as an ŌĆŗoutspoken critic of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran that ŌĆībegan ŌüĀon February 28.
The pope said this month's threat from Trump to destroy Iranian civilization was " unacceptable ." He has also called ŌĆŗfor " deep reflection " ŌĆŗabout the ŌüĀway migrants are being treated in the U.S. under ŌĆŗthe Trump administration.
"Leo should get his ŌĆŗact ŌüĀtogether as Pope," Trump wrote in his post, later telling reporters he was ŌüĀnot ŌĆŗa "big fan" of the ŌĆŗpontiff.

item.title: Budapest turns into party zone after historic election that ends Orban era
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Budapest turned into a party zone well into the early hours on Monday with people dancing outside pubs and cars honking as supporters of the opposition Tisza party celebrated ŌĆŗtheir sweeping victory in a pivotal election on Sunday.
The election, which brought a record ŌĆīturnout of 80% based on preliminary data, ended the 16-year rule of right-wing Prime Minister Viktor Orban and for Hungarians toasting and waving flags in the capital city this meant voting for the country's European orientation and ŌĆŗa restoration of democracy, eroded by Orban and his government since 2010.
Budapest's famous Chain ŌĆŗBridge was illuminated in HungaryŌĆśs national colors and along the embankment by the ŌüĀDanube river tens of thousands partied, waving the national flag and European Union flags, and some ŌĆŗtoasting with champagne in paper cups.
"I feel amazing! Really amazing! Like, I never thought this would ŌĆŗactually happen. I've been praying for this for 16 years so I'm super happy," said Szilvia, a Tisza supporter, as people watched the victory speech from opposition leader Peter Magyar.
Chanting and singing broke out on the city's metro, ŌĆŗwith people chanting "it's over." Other Tisza supporters celebrated at a rave outside the country's iconic parliament ŌĆŗbuilding.
"It's a big celebration. I'm quite happy. I have been waiting for this for some time. And I ŌĆīhope that ŌüĀthe new leadership, new prime minister will be really a prime minister for all Hungarians, as he said," said Tisza supporter Sandor Zoletnik.
"Amazing!" "Can't describe it!" "We are so happy that Orban is finally gone." "We did not believe that it would change," a group of young people hugged and jumped, shouting.
"So, ŌĆŗfinally, after at least ŌĆŗ16 years, we feel ŌüĀlike there is a hope. We feel like there is a hope that Hungary can start walking into the right direction," one of them ŌĆŗsaid. Orban's Fidesz party had the lowest support in the young generation .
Fidesz, originally ŌĆŗlaunched as ŌüĀan opposition youth movement in the late 1980s, is backed by just 8% of voters aged between 18 and 29, based on a survey by pollster Median, or 22% in the wider 18-39 age group, ŌüĀaccording ŌĆŗto Zavecz Research.
Traffic stopped along some of the main boulevards ŌĆŗas crowds in front of pubs overflowed and people waved national flags, stopping cars. Hundreds strolled along the bridges spanning ŌĆŗthe Danube well after midnight.

item.title: Pope Leo to begin 10-day Africa tour on mission to spotlight continent's needs
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo begins an ŌĆŗambitious 10-day tour of four countries in Africa on Monday urging global leaders to address needs on the ŌĆīcontinent where more than a fifth of the world's Catholics live.
The first U.S. pope heads to Algeria for two days before continuing to Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea in a whirlwind tour to 11 cities and towns, traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights.
The ŌĆŗpope is making the visit with a mission "to help turn the world's attention to Africa", Cardinal Michael ŌĆŗCzerny, a senior Vatican official and close adviser to Leo, told Reuters.
The pope, who has emerged as ŌüĀan outspoken critic of the Iran war , has made only one big overseas trip since being elected last May, ŌĆŗvisiting Turkey and Lebanon in November and December. He visited Monaco in March.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young and in good health ŌĆŗfor a pope, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for a pontiff in decades.
More than 20% of the world's Catholics live in Africa, according to Vatican statistics. The three sub-Saharan nations the pope is visiting have populations where more than half identify as ŌĆŗCatholic.
Algeria, though, is an overwhelmingly Muslim country with under 10,000 Catholics among its population of some 48 million people. ŌĆŗThis is the first time it will host a Catholic pope.
Leo's tour is the ŌĆī24th ŌüĀby a pope to Africa since the late 1960s .
He is expected to touch on many topics in 25 planned speeches over 10 days, Vatican spokesperson Matteo Bruni told journalists on Friday, given that the four nations face diverse issues.
Likely topics include exploitation of natural resources, Catholic-Muslim dialogue, and dangers of political corruption, said Bruni.
Cameroon and Equatorial Guinea have presidents who have ŌĆŗbeen in power for decades ŌĆŗand have been accused ŌüĀof human rights abuses, which they deny .
The biggest event of the itinerary will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a Mass in ŌĆŗthe coastal city of Douala.
Comfortable in several languages, Leo is expected to speak Italian, ŌĆŗEnglish, French, Portuguese ŌüĀand Spanish during the trip.
Landing in Algeria in mid-morning on Monday, Leo will address the country's political leaders before visiting the Great Mosque of Algiers in only his second visit to a mosque as pope.
He will travel on Tuesday to Annaba ŌüĀon Algeria's ŌĆŗnortheast coast, for a visit to the ruins of the ancient ŌĆŗtown of Hippo.
The site has special meaning to Leo, who is a member of the Augustinian religious order. The order is inspired by the teachings ŌĆŗof fourth-century St. Augustine of Hippo, a major figure in the early Church.

item.title: Russia, Ukraine accuse each other of violating Orthodox Easter ceasefire
item.description:
MOSCOW/KYIV, April 11 (Reuters) - Russia and Ukraine on Saturday accused each other of violating ŌĆŗa brief ceasefire in their four-year-old war hours into the truce put in place to mark Orthodox Easter.
Governors of two Russian ŌĆīborder regions said Ukrainian drones had attacked targets in the Kursk and Belgorod regions, injuring five people.
The General Staff of Ukraine's military said Russian forces had violated the terms of the 32-hour truce 469 times, including assault actions, shelling and drone strikes.
The ceasefire, announced by Russian President Vladimir Putin on Thursday, went into effect at 4 ŌĆŗp.m. Moscow time (1300 GMT). His Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelenskiy, said he would abide by it.
Reuters could not independently verify reports of military ŌĆŗactivity.
The ceasefire was put in place as U.S.-led negotiations to reach a settlement have been halted amid the ŌüĀwar in the Middle East. U.S. and Iranian negotiators were meeting in Pakistan on Saturday to try to end their six-week-old war.
According to ŌĆŗthe calendar of the Orthodox faith, which is dominant in both Russia and Ukraine, Easter is celebrated this year on Sunday.
Russian news agencies said ŌĆŗPutin attended services after midnight at Moscow's vast Christ the Saviour Cathedral.
In his Easter greeting, quoted by agencies, Putin described Easter as the "triumph of love, good and justice". He also expressed thanks to Orthodox Patriarch Kirill for his support of those fighting in Russia's military campaign, described as a "special military operation".
"I would like especially ŌĆŗto note the effective assistance you provide to our heroes - the participants and veterans of the special military operation," he wrote.
Alexander Khinshtein, governor of ŌĆŗRussia's Kursk border region, wrote on the state-backed messenger service MAX that a Ukrainian drone had struck a petrol station in the town of Lgov, injuring ŌĆīthree people, ŌüĀincluding a child.
Khinshtein said the attack took place after the start of the truce.
In the adjacent Belgorod region, Governor Vyacheslav Gladkov said two people were injured in Ukrainian drone attacks.
Gladkov, writing on Telegram, said a man and woman were injured in attacks in Shebekino and Grayvoron, two small towns just inside the border. He also said Ukrainian forces had shelled Shebekino, damaging homes and other buildings.
A local Russia-installed official in a ŌĆŗpart of southern Kherson region held ŌĆŗby Moscow also reported on social ŌüĀmedia that a Ukrainian drone attack had injured one person.
The Ukrainian General Staff, in a late evening report on front-line activity, said it had counted 469 ceasefire infringements since 1300 GMT.
"Specifically, 22 enemy assault ŌĆŗactions, 153 shelling incidents, 19 strikes by attack drones ... and 275 strikes by (First-Person View) drones."
During a similar ŌĆŗ30-hour Easter ceasefire ŌüĀagreed last year, each side accused the other of infringements.
Zelenskiy, in his nightly video address, made no reference to alleged ceasefire violations. He repeated that Ukraine would stick to the truce and wished it could be in effect longer.
"It would be right for the ceasefire to continue beyond this," Zelenskiy ŌüĀsaid.
"We have ŌĆŗmade this proposal to Russia and if Russia once again chooses war instead of peace, ŌĆŗit will show the world, and particularly the United States, who truly wants what."
Zelenskiy has proposed a halt to fighting on a number of occasions, but has been turned ŌĆŗdown by Moscow, which says it is seeking an overall settlement.

item.title: Oil tankers steer clear of Hormuz ahead of US blockade
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil tankers are steering clear of the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz ahead of a U.S. blockade later on Monday following failed peace talks ŌĆībetween the U.S. and Iran over the weekend, shipping data showed.
President Donald Trump said on Sunday the U.S. Navy would start blockading the Strait of Hormuz, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to reach a deal to end the war, ŌĆŗjeopardising a fragile two-week ceasefire.
U.S. Central Command said U.S. forces would begin implementing the blockade of ŌĆŗall maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports at 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT) on ŌüĀMonday.
It would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas, ŌĆŗincluding all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman," it said in a statement on X.
U.S. ŌĆŗforces would not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports, and additional information would be provided to commercial mariners through a formal notice prior to the start of the blockade, it said.
Iran's ŌĆŗRevolutionary Guards said on Sunday that any military vessels attempting to approach the Strait of Hormuz would ŌĆŗbe considered a violation of the ceasefire and be dealt with harshly and decisively.
Pakistan-flagged tankers Shalamar and Khairpur entered the ŌĆīGulf on ŌüĀSunday, data from LSEG and Kpler showed.
The Aframax tanker Shalamar is heading to the United Arab Emirates on Monday to load Das crude while the Panamax-sized Khairpur is heading to Kuwait to load refined products, the data showed.
Pakistan National Shipping, which manages Shalamar, did not immediately respond to a request for comment outside of ŌĆŗoffice hours.
Liberia-flagged very large crude ŌĆŗcarrier (VLCC) Mombasa B, which ŌüĀalso transited the strait earlier on Sunday, is ballasting in the Gulf, the data showed.
Malta-flagged VLCC Agios Fanourios I, which tried to pass through the strait on ŌĆŗSunday to enter the Gulf to load Iraqi Basra crude for Vietnam, has ŌĆŗsince turned ŌüĀback and is now anchored near the Gulf of Oman, the data showed. The tanker plans to head to Iraq.
Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, which manages the Agios Fanourios I, and CMB.TECH NV, the manager for the Mombasa B, did ŌüĀnot respond ŌĆŗto requests for comment outside office hours.
Despite the stalemate, three supertankers ŌĆŗfully laden with oil passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Saturday, shipping data showed. They appeared to be the first vessels to ŌĆŗexit the Gulf since the ceasefire deal was struck last week.

item.title: US blockade of Iran will be major military endeavor, experts say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - A U.S. naval blockade of Iran is a major, open-ended military endeavor that could trigger fresh retaliation from Tehran and put tremendous strain on an already ŌĆŗfragile ceasefire, experts say.
President Donald Trump, in a social media post after no deal emerged from peace talks this weekend in Islamabad, said the U.S. Navy "will begin ŌĆīthe process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz."
The U.S. military's Central Command later said the blockade will only apply to ships going to or from Iran, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. It will take effect on Monday at 10 a.m. in Washington (1400 GMT), CENTCOM said.
Trump also said U.S. forces would interdict vessels that have paid tolls to Iran, ŌĆŗeven if those ships are now in international waters. "No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," Trump wrote on Truth Social.
The ŌĆŗultimate goal, Trump said, would be to pressure Iran to end its effective closure of the strait, a choke point for about 20% ŌüĀof the world's oil, to all but the countries that secure safe passage from Tehran.
If Trump's strategy succeeds, he would eliminate Iran's greatest point of leverage in negotiations with the ŌĆŗUnited States and clear the strait again for global trade, potentially lowering oil prices. But a blockade, experts say, is an act of war that requires an open-ended commitment of a significant number of ŌĆŗwarships.
"Trump wants a quick fix. The reality is, this mission is difficult to execute alone and likely unsustainable over the medium to long-term," said Dana Stroul, a former senior Pentagon official during the Biden administration now at The Washington Institute for Near East Policy.
The U.S. military has not offered basic details yet about the blockade, including how many U.S. warships will enforce it, whether warplanes will be used ŌĆŗand whether any Gulf allies will assist in the effort. Central Command declined to respond to requests for comment.
With enough warships, the U.S. Navy could set up a blockade that intimidates ŌĆŗmany commercial tankers from trying to power through with Iranian oil, experts say.
But would the United States be prepared to board and seize ŌĆö or even damage or sink ŌĆö ships that try to break the blockade? What ŌĆīif they carry ŌüĀoil for China, a major power, or U.S. partners such as India or South Korea?
And what would Iran do? Retired Admiral Gary Roughead, a former chief of U.S. naval operations, cautioned that Iran could fire on ships in the Gulf or attack infrastructure of the Gulf states that host U.S. forces.
"I honestly believe that if we begin to do it, that Iran will have some kind of a reaction," Roughead said.
Iran's threats to shipping have caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50% since the U.S. and Israel launched the war on February 28.
Trump said on ŌĆŗSunday that the price of oil and gasoline may ŌĆŗremain high in the United States ŌüĀthrough November's U.S. midterm elections, which could see Trump's Republicans lose control of the U.S. Congress if there is a public backlash. The war has already been unpopular.
Frustrated by Iran's refusal to end the war on his terms, Trump on Sunday also floated the possibility of a ŌĆŗresumption of U.S. strikes inside Iran, citing missile factories as one possibility.
U.S. Senator Mark Warner of Virginia, the ranking Democrat on the ŌĆŗSenate Intelligence Committee, questioned ŌüĀthe strategy, noting Iran could send speedboats to mine the strait or put bombs against tankers.
"How is that going to ever bring down gas prices?" Warner asked on CBS's "Face the Nation."
Thousands of U.S. military strikes have severely weakened Iran's military. But analysts say Tehran has emerged from the conflict as a vexing problem for Washington, with a more hardline leadership and a buried stockpile of highly enriched ŌüĀuranium.
Trump threatened ŌĆŗon Sunday that "any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be BLOWN TO HELL!"
Iran's Revolutionary ŌĆŗGuards responded with a statement warning that military vessels approaching the strait will be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively, underlining the risk of a dangerous escalation.
Stroul said the crisis will require a long-term, ŌĆŗinternational effort to resolve.
"Over the long run, this will need to be resolved through diplomacy and international political will," she said.

item.title: Trump returns to weary and failing playbook with Hormuz blockade threat
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 13 (Reuters) - Escalate to de-escalate is a well-worn playbook employed by U.S. President Donald Trump, and no doubt global crude oil markets are hoping his promise to blockade the Strait of Hormuz is just the latest example of this ploy.
The announcement that the U.S. ŌĆŗNavy will block vessels from Iranian ports using the narrow waterway raises the stakes massively in the six-week-old conflict.
Trump's threat was delivered with his usual bombast ŌĆīin a social media post on Sunday, and it effectively ended the surge in optimism that a ceasefire between the two parties would result in a re-opening of the vital waterway.
Trump's action follows the failure of weekend talks between the two parties in Pakistan, which ended with both sides seemingly far apart on key issues.
The latest escalation saw global benchmark Brent crude futures jump about 8% in early Asian trade on ŌĆŗMonday, climbing to $102.80 a barrel from a close of $95.20 on April 10.
Since the conflict started on February 28, the flow of crude oil, refined products and liquefied ŌĆŗnatural gas through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a trickle from pre-war levels of roughly 20% of global supply.
What little traffic ŌüĀthat has gone through the strait since the start of April has mainly been from Iran, with data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler showing 15 vessels that loaded at Iran's ŌĆŗKharg Island terminal leaving between April 1 and 12.
Over the same period 12 tankers from other countries have made it through, with four from the United Arab Emirates, six from Saudi ŌĆŗArabia and two from Iraq, according to Kpler.
There are several ways to look at the threat to blockade vessels from using the Strait of Hormuz.
The first is that it's surprising this hasn't happened sooner.
By halting Iran's crude shipments the United States would put serious pressure on Tehran's revenue and also likely force China, the major buyer of Iranian oil, to become more involved, with the hope that Beijing would encourage ŌĆŗits ally to make concessions in order to ensure vessels moved freely.
However, there are major challenges to stopping tankers from Iran.
It's unlikely the U.S. military would fire missiles or other ŌĆŗweapons at them, given the risk of an environmental disaster.
This leaves the most likely option as trying to force them to change course through threats, and if that doesn't work, launching armed boarding parties ŌĆīto take ŌüĀphysical control of the ships.
Another way to look at Trump's threat is that it marks a return to escalation in the conflict, which makes the Iranian reaction key.
So far the hard-line clerical leaders in Iran have shown little sign of bending to U.S. demands, which range from ending its nuclear programme to fully re-opening the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has largely done the opposite, responding with escalation of its own, such as attacking energy and military installations in its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. bases.
If the U.S. military does successfully interdict vessels from ŌĆŗIranian ports, it would be logical to expect ŌĆŗTehran would launch more attacks on ŌüĀits neighbours, and the most effective targets would be export facilities that bypass the Strait of Hormuz.
Already Iran has damaged Saudi Arabia's east-to-west pipeline to Yanbu on the Red Sea, as well as hitting facilities at the United Arab Emirates' Fujairah terminal on the Gulf of ŌĆŗOman.
Further attacks on these export pathways would inflict maximum damage on the already shaky crude oil markets, and likely turn what is ŌĆŗalready a crisis into ŌüĀa full-blown emergency.
The hope in the crude market is that Trump is once again escalating to de-escalate, but there are limits as to how many times this can happen before the damage to the world economy becomes severe and long-lasting.
It's a common refrain among U.S. right-wing media and commentators that Trump is playing some sort of four-dimensional chess and will eventually outwit Iran.
But the reality ŌüĀis that Trump ŌĆŗappears to be playing noughts and crosses, known as tic-tac-toe to Americans, and he hasn't yet worked out ŌĆŗthat you cannot win.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can ŌĆŗhelp you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: War-fuelled debt spiral will shape global power
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The United Kingdom cut debt after the Napoleonic wars so it had the fiscal firepower to fight subsequent battles. But todayŌĆÖs superpower struggle between the United States and China, and the conflicts in Iran and Ukraine, are occurring when government borrowing across the world is already high.
Governments are spending more on defence and cutting their reliance on rivals in response to global threats. That is pushing ŌĆŗup debt relative to GDP, limiting their ability to project power. Borrowing costs have also shot up. The yield on 10-year U.S. government bonds has almost trebled to 4.3% over the past five years.
Few countries ŌĆīare immune to this spiral. The United States, China, France, the United Kingdom and Japan already have gross government debt equivalent to more than a yearŌĆÖs worth of economic output. Russia has run down its National Wealth Fund to finance its war in Ukraine, while foreign governments have frozen around $300 billion of MoscowŌĆÖs sovereign assets. Gulf Arab countries will also now have to dig deeper to defend themselves against Iran.
There is an old joke that the IMF stands for ŌĆ£itŌĆÖs mostly fiscalŌĆØ. When the International Monetary Fund and World Bank hold their annual meetings this week in Washington, the interaction between ŌĆŗgovernment borrowing and geopolitical tension will be high on the agenda.
Part of the problem is rising defence costs. President Donald Trump wants to increase U.S. spending by $500 billion to $1.5 trillion next year. Meanwhile, European members of NATO last ŌĆŗyear committed to increasing their outlay to 5% of output in 2035 from the previous goal of 2%.
Governments are also increasingly alive to the risk of blackmail. IranŌĆÖs control of the Strait ŌüĀof Hormuz, through which a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs daily oil output previously passed, is the latest example. ChinaŌĆÖs domination of rare earths and EuropeŌĆÖs reliance on Russian gas before its invasion of Ukraine are others. Meanwhile, many countries are heavily dependent on ŌĆŗU.S. defence, technology and finance ŌĆö which gives Washington the ability to bully them.
The sensible response is for countries to cut their exposure to potential enemies. But that is costly as it interferes with the free movement of goods, services and capital. Governments either ŌĆŗhave to subsidise the process or put in controls that limit economic growth. Borrowing will be higher or output lower than it would have been ŌĆö boosting debt ratios.
All borrowing is not created equal. While debt-to-output ratios and annual borrowing levels are a good starting point to assess a stateŌĆÖs fiscal strength, other factors are also important.
How a government spends money is vital. For example, defence spending can boost productivity , opens new tab if it is used for research and development. The United States is a well-known example. Ukraine is now building a thriving military technology sector as a result of innovation forced ŌĆŗby the need to defend itself from Russia. By contrast, subsidising energy consumption ŌĆö as some governments are doing to offset the higher oil prices that the Iran war has unleashed ŌĆö does nothing to spur long-run growth.
Off-balance sheet liabilities also matter. For example, pension ŌĆŗcommitments will show up in future debt figures. The damage will be a function of how rapidly a population is ageing and the generosity of retirement payments. China and Japan are in a bad place in terms of ageing, while France is burdened by an expensive pension ŌĆīsystem it cannot ŌüĀreform. By contrast, the United States gives relatively little money to retired citizens and its population is ageing more slowly.
Private-sector bad loans can also flip back onto governmentsŌĆÖ debt ratios ŌĆö either because states have to bail out banks as they did during the 2008 global financial crisis, or because sweeping problems under the carpet depresses growth. Moscow may be at risk because it has encouraged , opens new tab lenders to support its war effort. So may Beijing from its past promotion of housing investments. Washington could eventually find that leverage in areas such as private credit causes broader trouble.
How governments fund themselves also matters. If they rely mainly on domestic investors, like China, Japan and the euro zone do, they are in a stronger position. In extremis, they can use financial repression ŌĆö policies that lower government ŌĆŗfinancing costs ŌĆö to force their people to fund them.
The United States ŌĆŗis not so lucky. After years of running current ŌüĀaccount deficits, the value of its investments in the rest of the world is $27 trillion , opens new tab less than the value of what other countries have invested in America. This means Washington relies on the ŌĆ£ kindness of strangers ŌĆØ, as Mark Carney, now CanadaŌĆÖs prime minister, put it when he was governor of the Bank of England.
Of course, the dollarŌĆÖs status as the worldŌĆÖs main reserve currency gives the ŌĆŗUnited States a funding advantage. But TrumpŌĆÖs wars and U.S. economic bullying are undermining that privilege, creating an opportunity for the euro, yuan and crypto currencies.
If an ŌĆŗeconomy is vigorous, a government can ŌüĀgrow out of high debt. On this yardstick, all the main economic blocs have their problems.
While the United States has a thriving technology sector, TrumpŌĆÖs erratic policies and immigration curbs are undermining its vitality. Although China has grown spectacularly, its export model is unsustainable, and political repression may eventually dim creativity and animal spirits. Meanwhile, the European Union has not invested enough in technology or integrated the markets of its 27 member states adequately.
Yet another factor which determines a governmentŌĆÖs ability to carry heavy fiscal burdens is whether it can impose sacrifices ŌüĀon its people. ŌĆŗWestern states have little capacity to cut public spending or push up taxes. By contrast, China and Russia have high pain thresholds, in part because ŌĆŗtheir autocratic governments find it easier to push through unpopular policies.
But even they have limits. For example, Russia is seemingly so keen to avoid conscription that it has used troops from North Korea and elsewhere to help fight its war in Ukraine.
Debt dynamics will be one of the factors that determine the superpower struggle ŌĆŗbetween Washington and Beijing. On this score, both have challenges ŌĆö but China seems to have the edge.
Follow @Hugodixon , opens new tab on X

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: Too big to fail again
item.description:
LONDON, April 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week that started with Donald Trump threatening to destroy a ŌĆ£ whole civilization ŌĆØ ended with peace talks in Pakistan and Iran charging tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. IŌĆÖm taking a break from the conflict to write about another looming fight: over shadow banks. Let me know , opens new tab what else we should be paying attention to. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in ŌĆŗyour inbox every weekend.
ŌĆ£Simply stating the facts is enough to make clear how absurd things have become at Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena: shareholders must pick a new CEO who has never run a ŌĆīmainstream bank before, or the lenderŌĆÖs fired ex-boss.ŌĆØ
Read more: Rebel CEO is a rational vote in ItalyŌĆÖs bank drama .
HereŌĆÖs a potted history of financial regulation over the past 15 years: After 2008, watchdogs tightened capital and liquidity rules to prevent big banks from ever threatening the global economy again. To the surprise and consternation of some ŌĆŗbank CEOs, this effort broadly succeeded. Entire chunks of financial activity, including proprietary trading in bonds and equities and lending to riskier borrowers, migrated from regulated institutions into shadow banks, or what ŌüĀpolicymakers now prefer to call non-bank financial intermediaries.
The shift was expected, and deliberate. Regulators reasoned that non-banks which got into trouble would pose less of a threat to the economy and therefore be less prone to seeking politically toxic bailouts. Over the last ŌĆŗdecade and a half, however, several things have changed. The first is that non-banks have grown much bigger: they now account for more than half of the roughly $500 trillion of financial assets in existence around the globe, according to the Financial Stability Board. The second ŌĆŗis that they have moved closer to the heart of the financial system. Companies like Citadel Securities, Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab and Stripe are not regulated banks, but still play key roles in making markets, allocating capital, and processing transactions.
Up to this point, bankers and regulators agree. Where they diverge is over what to do next. Many bank executives argue that some of the regulations introduced since 2008 should be modified, enabling them to grab back lost business or take advantage of new opportunities. JPMorgan (JPM.N) , opens new tab boss Jamie Dimon this week singled out the ŌĆ£GSIB surchargeŌĆØ, which imposes an extra capital requirement on ŌĆŗthe worldŌĆÖs biggest banks. The proposed charge, he wrote in his annual letter , opens new tab to shareholders, seems to punish ŌĆ£our success, our strength, our consistency and our balanced business model. Frankly itŌĆÖs not right, and itŌĆÖs un-American.ŌĆØ ItŌĆÖs a rare post-2008 example of a top banker publicly ŌĆŗmaking the case that big is better .
Regulators take a different view. They point to the relative stability of big banks in the last six turbulent years as a sign of success. As Klaas Knot, the former head of the Dutch central bank, told me this week, 2008 showed ŌĆīthat bank exposures, ŌüĀwhich may seem diversified in calm times, tend to become highly correlated in a crisis.
Watchdogs would therefore prefer to tighten up oversight of non-banks, to avoid remaking the drama of 2008 with a different set of villains. A working group organised by the Group of Thirty former regulators and central bankers, chaired by Knot and Agustin Carstens, the former head of the Bank for International Settlements, this week warned of a brewing ŌĆ£ perfect storm , opens new tab ŌĆØ in the non-bank sector. Their report , opens new tab cites two specific concerns: the central role played by hedge funds and others in government debt markets, and the rapid growth of private credit providers in lending to companies. Both groups also tend to have extensive links to regulated banks.
The coming battle in financial regulation is therefore whether to raise the playing field for non-banks, or ŌĆŗto lower it for traditional lenders. The shape of the next ŌĆŗmeltdown will depend on who gets the upper hand.
The sums earmarked to build data centres around the world are so vast they can be hard to put into context. Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab boss Jensen Huang reckons it costs $60 billion to $80 billion to build a 1 gigawatt facility. With more than 100 GW of projects already in the planning stages, the implied outlay is at least $6.6 trillion. Is it possible to finance construction on such a ŌĆŗscale? Jeffrey Goldfarb added up the numbers and reckons thereŌĆÖs $7.5 trillion available. Spending it at high speed is a different challenge, though. The U.S. interstate highway system, which cost a mere $500 ŌĆŗbillion in todayŌĆÖs money, took three decades ŌüĀto complete.
Ransomware has become a large and lucrative global industry: half of all businesses with $10 million or more of turnover have suffered an attack on their computer systems in the past year. On The Big View , opens new tab this week I talked to Anja Shortland, professor of political economy at KingŌĆÖs College London and author of ŌĆ£We Know You Can Pay a MillionŌĆØ, about why the business has boomed, and the fresh threats posed by new artificial intelligence and geopolitical conflicts.
Over in the Viewsroom , opens new tab , Aimee Donnellan and Jonathan Guilford talked to Neil Unmack about the ŌüĀdramatic corporate moves by ŌĆŗglobal food groups, exemplified by Unilever (ULVR.L) , opens new tab and U.S. rival McCormick (MKC.N) , opens new tab creating a $65 billion mayonnaise-to-mustard giant.
The upsurge in Japanese takeovers has been one of the most ŌĆŗstriking recent developments in corporate finance. After decades in the doldrums, Japanese companies have started prioritising returns to shareholders, under pressure from the government, activist investors, and hostile suitors. Now a seemingly benign directive from JapanŌĆÖs Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry threatens to derail the M&A bonanza. Hudson Lockett explains why an instruction ŌĆŗfor companies to prioritise ŌĆ£corporate valueŌĆØ may be something of a poison pill.
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: US Democrats cheer defeat of Trump ally Orban in Hungary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Democrats celebrated the defeat of ŌĆŗHungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban on Sunday, while President Donald Trump's allies and Republican peers ŌĆīoffered a more mixed response to the loss of the leader that Trump had endorsed.
Trump had backed Orban leading up to the vote, even speaking briefly last week at a campaign rally in Hungary, when U.S. Vice President JD Vance telephoned ŌĆŗhis boss upon taking the stage.
But Orban lost power after 16 years as Hungarians voted ŌĆŗin record numbers for a pro-EU course spearheaded by center-right rival Peter Magyar.
U.S. ŌüĀlawmakers from both major parties congratulated Magyar on his victory.
Some Democrats framed Orban's loss as a ŌĆŗharbinger of things to come for the November midterm elections in the United States.
"Pay attention, Donald Trump. ŌĆŗWannabe dictators wear out their welcome," Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said.
"Far-right authoritarian Viktor Orban has lost the election. Trump sycophants and MAGA extremists in Congress are up next in November," said U.S. House of Representatives Minority Leader Hakeem ŌĆŗJeffries.
Republicans such as U.S. Senator Roger Wicker viewed the Hungarian election result as a repudiation of Russian ŌĆŗPresident Vladimir Putin, with whom Orban had cultivated ties over the years.
Wicker, a Mississippi Republican who chairs the Senate ŌĆīArmed ŌüĀServices Committee, said the results showed that Hungary's population rejected "the malign influence of Vladimir Putin" and "decided their own future."
Trump himself did not mention the Hungarian elections on Sunday, even as he opined on topics through social media posts, a television interview and a brief gathering with reporters.
But a few of his ŌĆŗpro-Orban allies, such as technology ŌĆŗtycoon Elon Musk, ŌüĀlamented the result.
"Soros Organization has taken over Hungary," Musk wrote on his X social media platform.
Billionaire financier and major Democratic donor George Soros, a Hungarian immigrant ŌĆŗto the United States, has long been vilified by many conservatives.
Orban has been ŌĆŗat loggerheads ŌüĀwith the European Union over a range of issues, including Russia's war in Ukraine .
His self-described "illiberal democracy" has echoed policies touted by Trump, including a hard line against immigration, hostility toward global institutions, and attacks on the media ŌüĀand ŌĆŗuniversities.
Orban was the first European leader to endorse Trump during his ŌĆŗ2016 presidential bid.
Trump said last week his administration stood ready "to use the full economic might of the United States to strengthen ŌĆŗHungary's economy" if Orban won.

item.title: Spanish premier urges China to take bigger role in multipolar order
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 13 (Reuters) - Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez on Monday said China should take on a more substantial role with issues including climate change, security, defense ŌĆŗand the fight against inequality, adding that Europe will also have to ŌĆīredouble its efforts as the U.S. withdraws from leadership roles on many fronts.
Sanchez, who is making his fourth visit to China in four years, spoke at Beijing's Tsinghua University.
His visit comes as ŌĆŗmany Western governments seek to maintain engagement with Beijing despite lingering security ŌĆŗand trade tensions. It follows visits to China earlier this year ŌüĀby the prime ministers of Britain, Ireland, Canada and Finland.
Spain has been one ŌĆŗof Europe's loudest proponents of expanding trade and treating China as a strategic ally ŌĆŗrather than an economic and geopolitical rival.
Sanchez said China could do more in terms of fighting climate change, promoting global health, controlling the development of responsible artificial intelligence as well as nuclear ŌĆŗweapons.
"For example, by demanding as it is doing, that international law be respected ŌĆŗand that the conflicts in Lebanon, Iran, Gaza and the West Bank and Ukraine cease," he ŌĆīsaid.
"Europe ŌüĀwill also have to redouble its efforts, especially now that the United States has decided to withdraw from many of these fronts."
Sanchez is expected to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping on Tuesday where they are expected to focus on geopolitics.
China accounted ŌĆŗfor 74% of Spain's ŌĆŗtotal trade deficit, ŌüĀSanchez said, adding that cooperation was important to build a "balanced, globalised economy that generates shared prosperity."
Madrid hopes Sanchez's visit will ŌĆŗnarrow Spain's trade deficit, which more than doubled in four ŌĆŗyears to ŌüĀnearly $50 billion in 2025. It is looking to boost agricultural and manufacturing exports to offset high volumes of China's imports.
China's official news agency Xinhua on Monday said Sanchez's visit ŌüĀwas ŌĆŗset to further consolidate bilateral ties and pointed to ŌĆŗa broader pathway for steady engagement between China and Europe at a time of growing global uncertainty.

item.title: US, Indonesia discussing agreement allowing US military overflight in Indonesian airspace, Indonesian ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Indonesia and ŌĆīthe United States are discussing ŌĆŗan ŌĆŗagreement to give U.S. ŌüĀmilitary aircraft ŌĆŗaccess to ŌĆŗIndonesian airspace, Indonesia's defence ministry said ŌĆŗon Monday, ŌĆŗadding that no deal ŌĆīhas ŌüĀyet been reached.
U.S. Defence Secretary Pete ŌĆŗHegseth ŌĆŗis ŌüĀset to meet ŌĆŗwith his ŌĆŗIndonesian ŌüĀcounterpart Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin later ŌüĀon ŌĆŗMonday.

item.title: South Korea and Poland to upgrade ties as Tusk calls Seoul key ally after US
item.description:
SEOUL, April 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung and Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk agreed on Monday to upgrade ties to a comprehensive strategic partnership, with the leaders ŌĆŗplacing defence cooperation at the centre of the relationship.
In remarks made before ŌĆŗtalks between the leaders at the presidential Blue House, Lee said ŌüĀthe countries would further expand defence industry cooperation under a $44.2 billion framework pact signed ŌĆŗin 2022.
"K2 tanks, K9 selfŌĆæpropelled howitzers, FAŌĆæ50 light-attack aircraft, and Chunmoo multiple rocket ŌĆŗlaunchers ŌĆö bearing South Korea's technology and pride ŌĆö are now safeguarding PolandŌĆÖs territory and its people across its vast lands," Lee said.
The South Korean leader said that the partnership extended beyond arms ŌĆŗsales to include joint production, technology transfers and training.
Tusk described South Korea ŌĆŗas Poland's "most important ally after the United States, especially in the defence industry" and said he ŌĆīwould ŌüĀpersonally oversee expanded defence cooperation between the countries.
He said the upgraded partnership meant the two countries would take on a shared responsibility and should play a role in contributing to global peace and international stability.
The two leaders also affirmed ŌĆŗexpanded cooperation across a ŌĆŗwide range of ŌüĀfields, including energy supply chains, infrastructure, science and technology, advanced industries, space and peopleŌĆætoŌĆæpeople exchanges.
South Korea has become one ŌĆŗof Poland's leading arms suppliers in recent years as Warsaw ŌĆŗmoves to ŌüĀrapidly modernise its military following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
In 2022, South Korea and Poland signed a defence framework agreement for South Korean companies to supply Poland with ŌüĀarms as ŌĆŗwell as jointly produce military equipment on Polish ŌĆŗsoil.
Since then, defence companies including Hanwha Aerospace (012450.KS) , opens new tab and Hyundai Rotem (064350.KS) , opens new tab have signed follow-up, multi-billion-dollar contracts to ŌĆŗsupply equipment such as tanks and missile launchers.

item.title: Australia appoints woman to lead its army for the first time
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 13 (Reuters) - Australia on Monday said a woman would lead its army for the first time in history, as part of a reshuffle of ŌĆŗthe country's defence force leadership.
Lieutenant General Susan Coyle, the current chief ŌĆīof joint capabilities, will become chief of army in July, the government said in a statement. She will replace Lieutenant General Simon Stuart.
Coyle's appointment comes as Australia's military seeks to ŌĆŗboost the number of female officers in its ranks. It ŌĆŗfaces a wave of allegations of systematic sexual harassment and discrimination.
"From ŌüĀJuly, we will have the first ever female chief of army ŌĆŗin the Australian Army's 125-year history," Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a ŌĆŗstatement.
Defence Minister Richard Marles called Coyle's appointment a "deeply historic moment."
"As Susan said to me, you cannot be what you cannot see," he said.
"Susan's achievement will be deeply significant ŌĆŗto women who are serving in the Australian Defence Force today and ŌĆŗwomen who are thinking about serving in the Australian Defence Force in the future."
Coyle, ŌĆī55, ŌüĀenlisted in the military in 1987 and has held a number of senior command roles. She will be the first woman to lead any service branch of the military, Marles said.
Women currently make up around 21% of ŌĆŗthe Australian Defence ŌĆŗForce (ADF) and 18.5% ŌüĀof senior leadership roles. The ADF has set a target of 25% of overall participation for women by 2030.
Last October, ŌĆŗa class action lawsuit was filed against the ADF alleging ŌĆŗit failed ŌüĀto protect thousands of female officers from systematic sexual assault, harassment and discrimination.
The government on Monday also appointed Vice Admiral Mark Hammond, the current chief ŌüĀof ŌĆŗthe navy, as the head of the ADF, succeeding ŌĆŗAdmiral David Johnston.
The current deputy chief of navy, Rear Admiral Matthew Buckley, will replace Hammond as ŌĆŗhead of the branch.

item.title: Haiti declares three days of national mourning for 25 killed in stampede
item.description:
PORT-AU-PRINCE, April 12 (Reuters) - Haiti declared three ŌĆŗdays of national mourning ŌĆīon Sunday, a day after a deadly stampede killed 25 people ŌĆŗduring an annual celebration thronged ŌĆŗby students and visitors at ŌüĀits Laferriere Citadel tourist ŌĆŗattraction.
In a national address, Prime Minister ŌĆŗAlix Didier Fils-Aim├® said mourning would begin from Tuesday, and the government ŌĆŗwould cover the funeral ŌĆŗexpenses for disaster victims.
Earlier in the day, ŌĆīEmmanuel ŌüĀPierre, the national head of the civil protection authority, told Reuters authorities had revised down ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗdeath toll ŌüĀto 25 from an initial tally of ŌĆŗ30.
The early-19th-century fortress built ŌĆŗshortly ŌüĀafter Haiti's independence from France is a UNESCO World Heritage ŌüĀsite.

item.title: Slovakia's Fico ready to cooperate with Hungary's new prime minister
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico congratulated Hungary's incoming leader, Peter Magyar, on Monday, offering "intensive cooperation", while expressing gratitude ŌĆŗto outgoing Prime Minister Viktor Orban after a major election upset .
Fico, ŌĆīa key ally who had backed Orban before voting started, said Slovakia was ready for the close ties and joint action in protecting energy interests remained a goal.
Veteran nationalist ŌĆŗOrban lost power in Sunday's election after 16 years in ŌĆŗoffice, with Magyar and his upstart centre-right Tisza party winning ŌüĀa comfortable majority of seats in parliament.
"With full respect, I take note ŌĆŗof the decision of the citizens of Hungary ... and am ready for ŌĆŗintensive cooperation with the new Hungarian prime minister, whom I congratulate on the election result," Fico said in a statement.
Under Fico, who returned to office in 2023, Slovakia ŌĆŗhas been a close ally of Hungary, with both neighbours maintaining warm ŌĆŗties with Moscow, opposing European Union sanctions and keeping up purchases of Russian oil ŌĆīand ŌüĀgas.
Both have sought to restore Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline, which has been out of action since late January after damage in Ukraine that Kyiv said was caused by a Russian strike.
Fico said he ŌĆŗbelieved Slovakia, Hungary and ŌĆŗcentral Europe ŌüĀwere still interested in resuming its operation, adding that joint action with Hungary in protecting energy interests remained unchanged ŌĆŗas a government goal.
Last week, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ŌĆŗsaid pipeline ŌüĀrepairs would be completed in spring. Fico and Orban have had tense relations with Zelenskiy despite Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
In a separate statement ŌüĀon Facebook, ŌĆŗFico said Slovak-Hungarian relations have never been ŌĆŗat such a high level, and he praised Orban for exemplary cooperation and protection of ŌĆŗsovereignty and national interests.

item.title: Millions of people in Sudan surviving on one meal a day as food crisis deepens, NGOs say
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - Millions of people in Sudan are surviving on just one meal a day, as the country's food crisis deepens and threatens to spread, according to ŌĆŗa report published on Monday by a group of non-governmental organisations.
Sudan's war between ŌĆīthe Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces, which enters its third year on Wednesday, has caused widespread hunger and displaced millions of people amid one of the world's largest humanitarian crises.
"In the two areas ŌĆŗworst hit by the conflict - North Darfur and South Kordofan - millions of families can ŌĆŗonly access one meal a day," the report by Action Against Hunger, ŌüĀCARE International, International Rescue Committee, Mercy Corps, and the Norwegian Refugee Council found.
"Often, they miss ŌĆŗmeals for entire days," the report stated, adding that many people have resorted to eating leaves ŌĆŗand animal feed to survive.
The army-aligned Sudanese government denies the existence of famine, while the RSF denies responsibility for such conditions in areas under its control.
Some 61.7% of SudanŌĆÖs population ŌĆō 28.9 million people ŌĆō is acutely ŌĆŗfood-insecure, according to the 2026 Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan.
The United Nations has reported widespread ŌĆŗatrocities and waves of ethnically charged violence. In November, the global hunger monitor confirmed, for the first time, famine ŌĆīconditions ŌüĀin al-Fashir, as well as Kadugli.
In February, the U.N.-backed Integrated Food Security Phase Classification found that famine thresholds for acute malnutrition have been surpassed in Um Baru, where the rate of acutely malnourished children under 5 was nearly double the famine threshold, and Kernoi.
The report, based on interviews ŌĆŗwith farmers, traders, and ŌĆŗhumanitarian actors in Sudan, ŌüĀdetails how the war in Sudan is driving communities towards famine conditions - due to disruptions to farming as well as the use of starvation ŌĆŗas a weapon of war - including deliberate destruction of farms and ŌĆŗmarkets.
Communal kitchens ŌüĀare increasingly unable to meet rising needs, while major donor funding cuts are impeding aid agencies' abilities to respond, the report said.
Women and girls have been disproportionately affected, as they face a high risk ŌüĀof rape ŌĆŗand harassment when going to fields, visiting markets or ŌĆŗcollecting water, the report said. Female-headed households are three times more likely to experience food insecurity than male-headed households, it ŌĆŗadded.

item.title: Taiwan government should lead engagement with China on new measures, senior official says
item.description:
TAIPEI, April 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan's engagement with China on looser controls proposed by Beijing for trade ŌĆīand tourism should be led by the government rather than private party-to-party contacts, a senior Taiwanese security official said on Monday.
China on Sunday unveiled 10 new incentive measures for Taiwan, including easing tourist curbs, allowing in "healthy" television ŌĆŗdramas and facilitating food sales, following a visit by the island's opposition leader.
But China refuses ŌĆŗto speak with Taiwan President Lai Ching-te's administration, saying he is a "separatist", ŌüĀand has rebuffed multiple offers of talks.
Tsai Ming-yen, head of Taiwan's National Security Bureau, said only ŌĆŗthe government should be assessing, planning and driving relevant exchanges.
"Having the government take charge of the ŌĆŗrelevant planning can therefore also better help avoid unnecessary risks and long-term consequences," he told reporters at parliament.
China's Taiwan Affairs Office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Kuomintang (KMT), Taiwan's largest opposition party whose leader ŌĆŗCheng Li-wun met Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing last week, has welcomed the measures, saying ŌĆŗthey can help with peace and bring economic opportunities.
Hou Yu-ih, the KMT's 2024 presidential candidate and mayor of Taipei's ŌĆīsister ŌüĀcity New Taipei, told reporters on Monday that exchanges must be initiated on the basis of equality, dignity, and mutual respect, starting with less sensitive areas like culture.
"I believe that the leaders on both sides of the strait must take the responsibility of avoiding war. Peace is the ŌĆŗultimate goal," he said. "Engagement ŌĆŗis about communication and ŌüĀminimising conflict as much as possible."
Tsai said such "goodwill measures" from China were historically brought up ahead of elections, concentrated on certain specific ŌĆŗcounties and cities, or specific companies, industries, or individuals.
"However, this has already ŌĆŗbecome a ŌüĀtool the Chinese communists use to interfere in Taiwan's elections," he added.
Taiwan will hold key local elections in November.
Ahead of the last presidential elections in early 2024, Taiwan complained that China had used ŌüĀa series ŌĆŗof trade measures to try to sway electors, including ŌĆŗreviewing tariff concessions on agricultural and fishery products, machinery, auto parts and textiles.
Taiwan's government rejects Beijing's territorial claims, saying only the ŌĆŗisland's people can decide their future.

item.title: Taiwan president to visit Eswatini, last diplomatic ally in Africa
item.description:
TAIPEI, April 13 (Reuters) - Taiwan President Lai Ching-te will visit Eswatini next week, his office said on Monday, the ŌĆŗisland's last remaining diplomatic ally in Africa.
Taiwan, which ŌĆīChina claims as its own territory with no right to state-to-state relations, now has formal ties with only 12 ŌĆŗcountries, almost all small, less-developed nations in Latin ŌĆŗAmerica, the Caribbean and the Pacific, like Belize ŌüĀand Tuvalu.
Lai will be in Eswatini from April ŌĆŗ22-26, his spokesperson Karen Kuo told reporters, for the ŌĆŗ40th anniversary of King Mswati III's accession and his 58th birthday.
Lai is flying directly to Eswatini, which is almost entirely surrounded ŌĆŗby South Africa, and does not require a ŌĆŗlayover, unlike visits to Latin America, which require transits via the ŌĆīUnited ŌüĀStates that routinely anger China.
This will be Lai's first trip outside of Taiwan since November 2024, when he visited the Marshall Islands, Tuvalu, and Palau, and ŌĆŗtransited through Hawaii ŌĆŗand the ŌüĀU.S. territory of Guam.
The last time a Taiwanese president visited Eswatini, formerly known ŌĆŗas Swaziland and home to around 1.3 ŌĆŗmillion people, ŌüĀwas in 2023, when Tsai Ing-wen made the journey.
Taiwan has provided large amounts of aid to the small ŌüĀsouthern ŌĆŗAfrican nation, an absolute monarchy. ŌĆŗIn 2021, it sent antiviral medication to help King Mswati III recover ŌĆŗfrom COVID.

item.title: TotalEnergies discovers hydrocarbons in Congo near existing production facilities
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 - French oil major ŌĆŗTotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab has found ŌĆīhydrocarbons as part of a drilling campaign to ŌĆŗexpand its currently ŌĆŗoperational Moho field offshore ŌüĀRepublic of Congo, ŌĆŗit said on Monday.
The ŌĆŗwell encountered a column of 160 metres of oil ŌĆŗand gas. Total ŌĆŗsaid the Moho G discovery ŌĆīand ŌüĀits previous Moho H find represent recoverable resources of close ŌĆŗto ŌĆŗ100 ŌüĀmillion barrels.
Oil majors have expressed renewed ŌĆŗinterest in West ŌĆŗAfrica ŌüĀin a bid to replenish resources from ŌüĀdeclining ŌĆŗfields elsewhere.

item.title: Baker Hughes sells Waygate unit to Hexagon for about $1.45 billion
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Oilfield services provider Baker ŌĆŗHughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab sold its Waygate Technologies ŌĆīunit to Swedish industrial technology group Hexagon (HEXAb.ST) , opens new tab for about $1.45 billion in ŌĆŗcash, the two companies ŌĆŗsaid on Monday.
The deal is expected ŌüĀto close in the ŌĆŗsecond half of 2026, the ŌĆŗcompanies added.
Hexagon said it plans to finance the acquisition using cash and existing ŌĆŗdebt capacity.
Last year, Baker ŌĆŗHughes agreed to buy Chart Industries (GTLS.N) , opens new tab in ŌĆīa $13.6 ŌüĀbillion all-cash deal to leverage its industrial and energy technology portfolio to drive growth and ŌĆŗexpand its ŌĆŗpresence ŌüĀin the natural gas and LNG sectors.
Baker Hughes ŌĆŗCEO Lorenzo Simonelli has ŌĆŗbeen ŌüĀreshaping the company to adapt to the global energy transition , shedding ŌüĀnon-core ŌĆŗassets while investing ŌĆŗin cleaner energy solutions.

item.title: Malaysia detains two tankers suspected of illegally transferring┬Ādiesel
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 13 (Reuters) - Malaysia's Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) said it has detained two ŌĆŗtankers for allegedly conducting an illegal ship-to-ship transfer of ŌĆīabout 700,000 litres of diesel off the island of Penang over the weekend.
Malaysia has been cracking down on fuel smuggling amid mounting shortages and supply disruptions ŌĆŗthroughout the region caused by the war in the ŌĆŗMiddle East.
($1 = 3.9700 ringgit)
(This story has been corrected to change the number to 700,000, from 700,00, in paragraph 1)

item.title: China model gains appeal in Vietnam as police expand power
item.description:
HANOI, April 13 (Reuters) - Vietnam is increasingly edging closer to China's model of governance, tightening state control while embracing Chinese technology and regulation as its most powerful leader in decades heads to Beijing this week, ŌĆŗaccording to internal documents, public policy plans and sources.
The two Communist neighbours have swung between conflict and cooperation over centuries. Now, Vietnam is leaning more openly toward Beijing, ŌĆīas China-friendly security figures rise in Hanoi under party chief To Lam, a former public security boss.
Lam will meet China's leader Xi Jinping on Wednesday on his first overseas trip since becoming state president on April 7, a move that formally unites two of Vietnam's most powerful roles, echoing Xi's own concentration of authority and breaking from Vietnam's traditional emphasis on collective leadership.
"Vietnam-China relations have entered a new stage, marked by higher political trust, more substantive defence and security cooperation, deeper and ŌĆŗmore practical cooperation across sectors," Lam said in a joint statement with Xi after they last met in April 2025.
This week's visit is expected to yield dozens of cooperation agreements, people ŌĆŗbriefed on the plans said. While such documents are often non-binding, the relationship is becoming more tangible: China's exports to Vietnam are at record highs , and Chinese ŌüĀinvestment in manufacturing south of the border is booming.
The people spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitive nature of the topic.
Vietnam is still hedging geopolitically, analysts say, keeping doors open to Washington ŌĆŗand others. But at home, it is moving closer to China's governance model - particularly control-driven regulation despite Western misgivings, underscoring how China's influence is deepening as Lam reshapes the state.
Vietnam has "a dual approach of actively ŌĆŗlearning from the Chinese model while selectively resisting its influence," said Nguyen Khac Giang, a visiting fellow at Singapore's ISEAS Yusof Ishak Institute.
Alexander Vuving of the Asia-Pacific Center for Security Studies in the U.S., said that closer ties with China without adequate guardrails "will have a negative impact not only on Vietnam's security, prosperity, and autonomy, but also on its relations with the U.S. and the West."
Vietnam's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Technology has emerged ŌĆŗas one of the clearest markers of warming ties.
Vietnam has dropped earlier concerns about the use of Chinese equipment in its 5G network , while the country's largest internet provider FPT (FPT.HM) , opens new tab announced investments in an undersea cable ŌĆŗto be built by a Chinese vendor that the U.S. considers linked to sanctioned telecom giant Huawei. A telecom company under Vietnam's public security ministry is in talks with Chinese companies for additional 5G deals.
Chinese firms are simultaneously exploring ŌĆīinvestments in Vietnamese ŌüĀdata centres, a strategic asset, according to people familiar with the discussions.
"Chinese interest in Vietnam's data-centre market has increased noticeably over the past 18-24 months," said Mickael Driol, head of investment advisory firm Mekong Partners. He said much of that is driven by manufacturers who moved operations to Vietnam from China.
Hanoi is prioritising state control in data regulation, similar to China. Western tech companies and the U.S. government have repeatedly raised concerns over data protection rules drafted by Vietnam's security ministry that limit cross-border data transfers.
Draft documents seen by Reuters show Vietnam plans to establish state-run data-trading exchanges overseen by the public security ministry - mirroring China's centralised ŌĆŗdata model and expanding the state's ability to deploy ŌĆŗinformation for surveillance and strategic goals. In ŌüĀWestern markets, such platforms are typically privately run.
Vietnam is also expanding a national electronic identification system, enabling authorities to identify individuals through AI camera networks that are being rolled out nationwide - another parallel with China's surveillance architecture.
"The police's rising power (in Vietnam) may partly explain a growing interest in Chinese-style social control tactics," said ŌĆŗGiang.
Unencumbered by public opinion that has grown less critical of China, the Vietnamese Communist Party is also advancing a more China-style economic model centred ŌĆŗon subsidies , public investment and large ŌüĀinfrastructure projects, sometimes in direct cooperation with Beijing on sensitive projects including high-speed rail links .
The shift has been reinforced by TikTok's popularity in Vietnam, where positive narratives about China often dominate, and by Hanoi's increasingly muted criticism of Beijing's actions in the disputed South China Sea.
Vietnam remains more open than China to foreign investors and still depends heavily on external capital. But China's share of total investments is rising, and Chinese brands are gaining popularity at home.
China's influence ŌüĀis also visible ŌĆŗin finance. Vietnam relies on unconventional monetary policy tools such as lending mandates to banks reminiscent of China's policy, maintains ŌĆŗtight foreign ownership caps in key sectors, and is grappling with a property bubble that echoes China's experience.
Now Hanoi is considering deeper intervention in equity markets. Proposed measures include a government-backed stabilisation fund to buy stocks during downturns - an idea explicitly modelled on China.
"China ŌĆŗcreated one and succeeded in reassuring investors," said an internal security ministry document reviewed by Reuters.

item.title: Two killed in Ukraine despite Easter ceasefire, governor says
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Two people were killed and one injured by Russian troops in Ukraine's frontline eastern Donetsk region ŌĆŗbefore the expiry of a ceasefire to mark Orthodox ŌĆīEaster , a local official said on Monday,
One person was killed in the city of Kramatorsk and one in Druzhkivka ŌĆŗon Sunday, regional governor Vadym Filashkin said on ŌĆŗthe Telegram messaging app, without giving more details.
Both ŌüĀRussia and Ukraine have accused each other of breaching ŌĆŗthe 32-hour ceasefire from Saturday afternoon to midnight (2100 GMT) ŌĆŗon Sunday.
Ukraine's Coordination Headquarters for the Treatment of Prisoners of War, citing a spokesperson of the 14th Army Corps, said on ŌĆŗMonday that during "the so-called 'Easter ceasefire'" Russian servicemen also ŌĆŗshot dead four Ukrainian prisoners of war in the Kharkiv region.
It ŌĆīdid ŌüĀnot give more details, adding that Russia was preparing a campaign to deny it.
There was no immediate comment from Russia and Reuters could not verify the ŌĆŗinformation independently.
The General ŌĆŗStaff of ŌüĀUkraine's military said on Monday it had compiled a total of 10,721 Russian ŌĆŗviolations over the course of the truce.
It ŌĆŗsaid the ŌüĀviolations included 1,567 artillery shelling incidents, 119 assault operations and 9,035 strikes by attack drones. It said no ŌüĀair ŌĆŗstrikes had been recorded.
The Russian ŌĆŗdefence ministry has said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations by Ukraine overnight ŌĆŗinto Sunday.

item.title: Kuwait raises May crude prices for Asia, pricing document shows
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Kuwait has raised ŌĆŗthe official selling ŌĆīprice for Kuwait Export ŌĆŗCrude to ŌĆŗAsia in May ŌüĀto $17 a ŌĆŗbarrel above ŌĆŗthe average of Oman/Dubai quotes, a ŌĆŗprice document ŌĆŗreviewed by Reuters ŌĆīshowed ŌüĀon Monday.
The producer increased the May Kuwait ŌĆŗSuper ŌĆŗLight ŌüĀcrude OSP to $17 ŌĆŗa barrel ŌĆŗabove ŌüĀOman/Dubai quotes.

item.title: What the Trump tax breaks giveth, the gasoline pump taketh away
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 10 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump in February touted this year's tax refunds as "substantially greater than ever before," thanks to new individual tax breaks approved in ŌĆŗ2025, telling taxpayers that they should think of him when the funds hit their bank accounts.
"Don't spend all of this money in one place!" he wrote on the Truth Social media platform.
But that's what's happening, as ŌĆīthe gasoline pump eats most of the larger tax refunds that people are seeing from new tax breaks on tips, Social Security retirement payments, overtime pay, car loan interest and state and local tax bills that were part of last year's Republican-backed tax-cut legislation.
Despite a tenuous ceasefire in the U.S. war with Iran, oil prices held close to $100 a barrel on Friday ahead of talks between U.S. and Iranian officials in Islamabad as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed . But even if the waterway, which handles about 20% of the world's oil supply, reopens soon, fuel prices could keep rising for months , according to ŌĆŗthe U.S. Energy Information Administration, which predicts global benchmark Brent crude prices will average $96 a barrel this year.
Destruction of energy infrastructure across the Gulf region will limit future output, according to Rystad Energy , opens new tab , which estimates rebuilding costs of at least $25 billion. ŌĆŗThat figure could rise further as attacks continue.
The drop in energy supply potentially means months more of pain at the pump, and economists say Americans with lower incomes, who spend more of ŌüĀtheir pay on gasoline, will see the smallest gains from the tax breaks.
U.S. consumer prices jumped by the most in nearly four years in March, rising 0.9%, thanks mostly to a record one-month increase in gasoline prices on the back of the spike in ŌĆŗglobal oil prices and the persistent pass-through of higher tariffs, Bureau of Labor Statistics data showed on Friday. Higher costs for diesel, fertilizer, aluminum and other inputs affected by the Middle East conflict, food inflation and other price hikes could eat what's left of those ŌĆŗrefunds.
As of April 3, the average individual refund for the 2025 tax year was up $346, or 11.1%, from the prior-year average to $3,462, according to the latest Internal Revenue Service filing data , opens new tab .
These figures could shift higher ahead of the April 15 tax filing deadline on Wednesday, with estimates clocking in at $560 from Morgan Stanley, $611 from the conservative-leaning Tax Foundation and $1,000 from the U.S. Treasury. But some of that relief may come in the form of reduced income withholding or lower quarterly tax payments by individuals.
Economists at the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research estimate that war-driven price spikes have ŌĆŗpushed up Americans' average annual gasoline costs for this year by $857. The March 23 estimate was made when Brent futures were trading at $99 a barrel - about $2 higher than on Friday - and assumed that the Strait of Hormuz, which is still largely shut, would reopen around ŌĆŗApril 10.
Democrats on the Joint Economic Committee in Congress estimate that Americans paid an additional $8.4 billion for gasoline during the first month of the Iran war, based on price data from motorist advocacy group AAA, vehicle fuel economy data from Edmunds.com and federal gasoline consumption data. ŌĆīThat figure is more ŌüĀthan a quarter of the $30.7 billion increase in total IRS individual refunds through April 3.
"Gas prices are probably the most salient price in the economy," said Neale Mahoney, a professor of economics at Stanford University. "The impacts may be modest from a macro perspective, but for the sort of kitchen-table economics of a family, and things that they're paying attention to, they can be big."
A family that was expecting a bigger refund and planning a bigger summer vacation or kitchen remodel this year may pull back, as they dig deeper into their wallets to fill up their tanks. Grocery bills also are expected to rise as higher diesel, fertilizer, jet fuel and aluminum prices work their way through the economy.
What was meant to be a mild boost to the economy is now turning into more of a cushion against ŌĆŗa deeper slowdown, but analysts are starting to mark down ŌĆŗU.S. consumption and GDP forecasts by a few tenths of ŌüĀa percentage point. Morgan Stanley now expects 2026 consumption growth to slow to 1.7% from 2.1% in 2025, with durable goods taking most of the hit, while Oxford Economics has slashed its global GDP growth forecast for 2026 to 2.6% from 3.0%, well below the pace of recent years.
Most of the tax breaks in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed by the ŌĆŗRepublican-controlled Congress last year were retroactive to the start of 2025, so most of the first-year benefits will come through claimed income deductions.
Several of these breaks, including those on tips, overtime, ŌĆŗSocial Security and auto loan interest, ŌüĀare available regardless of whether taxpayers itemize their returns or take the standard deduction, which was also increased by $1,150 for individual filers for the 2025 tax year. This change would yield a refund increase of $138 for those in the 12% tax bracket with incomes of $11,926 to $48,475.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent last week called the overtime deduction "a home run," with 25% of filers claiming it.
But one of the biggest breaks, a $30,000 increase in the deduction for state and local taxes paid, still requires itemization, largely putting it out of reach for non-homeowners who don't have mortgages ŌüĀor pay property ŌĆŗtaxes.
It's not until tax returns reach the market-income range of $71,659 to $126,348 - higher than that of 60% of earners - that taxpayers will still have money left over ŌĆŗfrom the new tax breaks after their higher fuel bills, according to data from the conservative-leaning Tax Foundation. Market income includes adjusted gross income, tax-exempt interest, employer-sponsored health and pension benefits and other items.
But Tax Foundation data shows that the cuts for the top 0.01% of returns above $2.24 million in income still yield a ŌĆŗbigger tax savings than for those earning up to $37,486.

item.title: PolandŌĆÖs premier hails Hungary vote as blow to authoritarian rule
item.description:
WARSAW, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's election shows that Europe is not on an inevitable path toward authoritarian rule, ŌĆŗPolish Prime Minister Donald Tusk said on Monday, ŌĆīafter the center-right Tisza party ended Viktor Orban's 16ŌĆæyear grip on power.
"Everyone feared there was a trend toward authoritarian, corrupt ŌĆŗregimes," Tusk said while on an official ŌĆŗvisit to South Korea , according to Polish state-owned ŌüĀnews agency PAP.
"That's not the case. First Warsaw, ŌĆŗthen Bucharest, Chisinau, now Budapest."
In 2025, centrist Nicusor Dan won ŌĆŗa presidential election in Romania and Moldova's pro-European ruling party won a resounding victory over its Russia-aligned rival.
Tusk had repeatedly criticized ŌĆŗOrban's government for its close ties with Moscow.
"I'm ŌĆŗglad that this part of Europe is showing that we ŌĆīare ŌüĀnot doomed to corrupt and authoritarian governments, because that's what Victor Orban's government unfortunately became after many years in power."
Neighbors Poland and Hungary are linked ŌĆŗby their long, ŌĆŗshared history, ŌüĀclose trade ties and cooperation within the European Union and NATO.
Tusk said he ŌĆŗhad spoken with Tisza leader Peter ŌĆŗMagyar ŌüĀto congratulate him on his victory.
"We briefly discussed his visit to Warsaw," Tusk said.
"As you know, he long ŌüĀago ŌĆŗchose Warsaw as his first ŌĆŗvisit for quite obvious reasons. I think our relationship will be ŌĆŗabsolutely exceptional."

item.title: Pope says he will continue to speak out against war after Trump attack
item.description:
ABOARD PAPAL PLANE, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo told Reuters on Monday that he plans ŌĆŗto continue speaking out against war after U.S. President Donald Trump's direct ŌĆīattack on the leader of the 1.4-billion-member Church.
In comments aboard the papal flight to Algiers, where the first U.S. pope is starting a 10-day tour to ŌĆŗfour African countries, the pontiff also said the Christian message ŌĆŗwas being "abused".
"I don't want to get into a debate with ŌüĀhim," Leo told Reuters as he greeted journalists on the plane. "I don't ŌĆŗthink that the message of the Gospel is meant to be abused ŌĆŗin the way that some people are doing."
"I will continue to speak out loudly against war, looking to promote peace, promoting dialogue and multilateral relationships among the ŌĆŗstates to look for just solutions to problems," he said, speaking ŌĆŗon English.
"Too many people are suffering in the world today," said Leo. "Too many innocent ŌĆīpeople ŌüĀare being killed. And I think someone has to stand up and say there's a better way."
"The message of the church, my message, the message of the Gospel: Blessed are the Peacemakers. I do ŌĆŗnot look at ŌĆŗmy role as ŌüĀbeing political, a politician," he said.
Leo, originally from Chicago, has emerged as an outspoken critic of the ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli war on Iran in recent weeks and decried ŌĆŗthe "madness of ŌüĀwar" on in a peace appeal on Saturday.
Trump, in an apparent response to the pope's criticisms of both the conflict and the White House's ŌüĀhard-line ŌĆŗimmigration policies, said late on Sunday that ŌĆŗLeo was "terrible".
"Pope Leo is WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy," Trump wrote in ŌĆŗa post on Truth Social.

item.title: Philippines warns of 'sabotage' after cyanide seizure in disputed South China Sea atoll
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines discovered cyanide on Chinese boats operating around a ŌĆīdisputed atoll in the South China Sea, security officials from the country said on Monday.
Authorities said laboratory tests confirmed the presence of the highly toxic substance in bottles seized by the Philippine navy in operations at Second ŌĆŗThomas Shoal last year.
Officials warned the cyanide could have had serious consequences for marine life ŌĆŗand weakened the reef supporting a warship that Manila grounded on the ŌüĀatoll to reinforce its maritime claim.
"We wish to underscore that the use of cyanide in ŌĆŗAyungin Shoal is a form of sabotage that seeks to kill local fish populations, depriving ŌĆŗNavy personnel of a vital food source," Cornelio Valencia, National Security Council spokesperson, told a press conference using the Philippines' name for the atoll. Valencia added that cyanide could damage the reef and "ultimately compromise" the warship's stability.
The ŌĆŗChinese Embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Philippines has ŌĆŗaccused China of disrupting resupply missions to troops on the vessel, including a June 17, 2024, incident that ŌĆīturned ŌüĀviolent and resulted in a Filipino sailor losing a finger. China has denied allegations of aggressive conduct during such encounters and accused the Philippines of trespassing in its waters.
The June confrontation later led to a provisional understanding for resupply missions to the grounded ship.
China and the ŌĆŗPhilippines held high-level talks ŌĆŗlast month over the ŌüĀSouth China Sea, exploring preliminary steps towards oil and gas cooperation , and confidence-building measures at sea, including communication between their coast guards.
The Philippine foreign ministry ŌĆŗsaid in a statement on Monday the scope of the coast ŌĆŗguard cooperation ŌüĀwould be limited, and "does not contemplate cooperation in sensitive operational areas," adding that there had been no discussions on joint patrols.
China claims nearly all the South China Sea, including areas claimed by ŌüĀBrunei, Malaysia, ŌĆŗthe Philippines and Vietnam.
A 2016 ruling by an international ŌĆŗarbitral tribunal found Beijing's sweeping claims had no basis under international law, a decision China rejects. More than $3 trillion ŌĆŗin annual ship-borne commerce travels through the key waterway.

item.title: Israelis oppose Iran ceasefire, divided over whether to respect it, poll says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 13 (Reuters) - Nearly two-thirds of Israelis oppose the Iran ceasefire but the public is divided over whether Israel should respect the two-week truce or resume attacks ŌĆŗon Iran, according to a poll from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.
The ŌĆīpoll was the first national survey of Israelis conducted after the U.S. and Iran agreed last week to a ceasefire brokered by Pakistan, the survey's authors said. The two sides failed to ŌĆŗreach a broader deal to end the war in weekend talks in Islamabad.
The ŌĆŗceasefire has halted U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran. But it ŌüĀhas not ended a parallel war between Israel and the Iranian-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon, ŌĆŗwhere the Israeli military has continued deadly bombardment that has killed many civilians. Hezbollah ŌĆŗhas continued to fire rockets at Israeli towns in the country's north.
On Lebanon, more than 61 percent of Israelis believe the truce should not extend to the fighting with Hezbollah, a core demand ŌĆŗby Iran in talks with the U.S., according to the poll, conducted by researchers ŌĆŗat Hebrew University's Agam Labs.
