Asked what Israel should do about Iran, 39 percent said Israel should ŌĆīcontinue ŌüĀattacks, 41 percent said their country should respect the ceasefire, and 19 percent said they weren't sure, the poll said.
The poll was based on a sample of 1,312 Israelis interviewed from April 9-10, with a margin of error of 3.2 percent.
With the fate ŌĆŗof the Iran ceasefire ŌĆŗunclear, Israel is digging ŌüĀin for a long, drawn-out conflict across the Middle East, with Israeli officials concluding that their enemies in Iran, Lebanon, Gaza ŌĆŗand beyond cannot be eliminated outright.
The public's perception of Israel's military ŌĆŗsuccess in ŌüĀIran holds high stakes for Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who faces an election due by October that most public opinion polls show he will lose.
According to the Hebrew University ŌüĀpoll, Netanyahu's ŌĆŗstanding among Israelis has decreased since the start ŌĆŗof the Iran war, with 34 percent of Israelis preferring him as premier now versus 40 percent at ŌĆŗthe start of the conflict.

item.title: Exclusive: Chilean copper miner Codelco, contractors fined after deadly mine collapse
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Chilean state copper miner Codelco was fined by labor authorities after last year's deadly collapse at its El Teniente mine, while three contractors whose workers were killed or injured were sanctioned more heavily, according to inspection records obtained by Reuters through public-records requests.
The previously unreported sanctions were imposed in the months ŌĆŗfollowing the July 31 underground seismic event that triggered a rock burst at El Teniente , the world's largest underground copper mine, killing six contract workers and injuring others.
Reuters obtained the files from ŌĆīChile's labor ministry through open-records requests. In Chile, such fines are notified directly to employers and can be challenged or reduced administratively, but are not typically disclosed in public announcements.
At the time of the accident, then-Labor Minister Giorgio Boccardo said that his office and the mining regulator, Sernageomin, would investigate the causes of the incident and whether any labor-safety rules had been breached.
The quake, measured at about magnitude 4.3, led to a halt in all underground operations at the sprawling mine complex amid rescue efforts and safety inspections.
The collapse ŌĆŗcarried a significant production cost for Codelco. The company has said the shutdown of underground operations at El Teniente and a slow restart cut copper output by tens of thousands of metric tons, disrupting shipments at ŌĆŗa time of tight global supply.
The disaster also underscored the geotechnical risks facing Chile's aging underground mines .
The records show the three contractors were fined the ŌüĀequivalent of about $87,000 combined, versus roughly $20,000 for Codelco, a gap that reflects Chile's split-liability framework for subcontracted work.
While the principal company - Codelco - can be penalized for overarching safety failures, contractors remain directly responsible as employers for accident reporting, ŌĆŗrisk assessment, worker assignment and other compliance duties, under Chile's labor code.
In one sanction, labor inspectors said Codelco lacked a complete, written procedure showing how seismic warnings were used to decide whether work should stop or be restricted.
After the ŌĆŗaccident, regulators also found that Codelco violated labor rules when workers were found entering, or preparing to enter, underground areas while the mine-wide suspension remained in force, according to a separate sanction record.
Under Chilean labor rules, some serious or fatal-accident violations can be fined at up to 150 UTM, an inflation-linked Chilean tax unit, or about $11,000 at current values, per infraction. The regulator levied a 340 UTM penalty, roughly $26,000 in today's terms, on a company after a fatal construction-site collapse in a 2007 case.
Labor advocates and worker-safety specialists ŌĆŗhave questioned whether such small penalties carry enough deterrent force for major employers.
"It is essential to raise the size of fines in order to effectively deter companies from violating mining safety regulations," a Chilean House of Representatives investigative ŌĆŗcommission report said in 2011 after a mining disaster.
Proposals since then to hike up fines for serious or fatal workplace accidents have not gotten off the ground.
Since the collapse, Codelco has said it tightened safety procedures for restarting work at ŌĆīEl Teniente, ŌüĀadding safety briefings at the start of shifts, improving communications underground, stepping up checks on workers' locations and reviewing protective equipment.
It later said an independent panel led by a former Anglo American CEO was examining what caused the accident and whether broader management or workplace problems played a role.
In a statement to Reuters, Codelco said that its seismic alert response system was active the day of the accident, and that it had appealed the fine by the labor ministry.
It added that there was an "ongoing legal proceeding related to the oversight of worker entry during the work stoppage" and that it was awaiting a decision from authorities.
Codelco said in August that El Teniente mine manager Andres Music would leave ŌĆŗhis role, and in February announced the departure of three ŌĆŗsenior executives after an internal audit found inconsistencies and ŌüĀconcealment following a separate rock burst at the mine several years earlier.
Among the three contracting firms fined, Strabag (STRV.VI) , opens new tab subsidiary Zublin was fined for failing to report a worker's death within 24 hours. Inspectors found that although the company knew about the fatality within two hours, it didn't notify labor authorities until the following evening.
Immediate reporting ŌĆŗis critical to implement emergency safety measures to protect remaining workers, the report said.
The Austrian company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A unit of ŌĆŗChilean construction firm SalfaCorp (SALFACORP.SN) , opens new tab was also ŌüĀsanctioned after one of its workers died in the Andesita sector of the mine. Inspectors said the company failed to immediately report the fatal accident to authorities, in addition to other offenses.
The records also said Salfa's risk assessment did not adequately account for seismic hazards and that the company failed to do enough to protect workers' lives.
SalfaCorp did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Chile's labor regulator also fined Constructora Gardilcic, the unlisted contractor whose workers were killed and injured in ŌüĀthe Recursos Norte ŌĆŗarea of the mine.
Inspectors said the company reported the accident late, delayed filing injury reports and showed weak safety planning.
Authorities also concluded Gardilcic ŌĆŗdid not adequately account for the risk of violent rock bursts outside designated danger zones and put some workers in jobs they were not cleared to do.
Gardilcic did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Codelco has said the areas hardest hit by the accident ŌĆŗwill remain under tight restrictions while criminal , regulatory and technical investigations continue.
The company has pledged a gradual, regulator-approved restart, leaving uncertainty over when the entire mine can resume normal operations.

item.title: Argentina credit stress deepens as inflation and utility costs hit households
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, April 13 (Reuters) - Gonzalo Martinez, a 37-year-old teacher, hopes that one day when he opens his bank ŌĆīaccount, he'll have no more debt looming over him.
Martinez is one of a growing number of Argentines falling behind on bank loans as shrinking purchasing power - the result of rising inflation and lower government subsidies - pushes household finances to the brink.
Data from Argentina's central bank (BCRA) ŌĆŗshowed that household loan delinquencies rose to 10.6% in January 2026, up from 9.3% in December, and ŌĆŗjust 2.8% in December 2023, when President Javier Milei took office.
Milei has overseen deep "chainsaw" ŌüĀcuts to public spending in order to rein in inflation, achieving Argentina's first budget surplus in more than ŌĆŗa decade. The austerity measures have been met with street protests, including from public university students and retirees who have seen ŌĆŗschool funding and pensions shrink.
Analysts told Reuters the rise in loan defaults reflects shrinking real incomes, as wage negotiations have lagged behind inflation and the government has slashed subsidies for public services such as electricity, gas and transportation.
ŌĆ£Increases in public service tariffs have ŌĆŗbeen compressing disposable income and, as a result, familiesŌĆÖ ability to settle their debts,ŌĆØ said Pablo Besmedrisnik, economist and ŌĆŗdirector of consultancy VDC.
Martinez, who lives in Buenos Aires, said he has accumulated credit card debt as his salary hasn't adjusted to inflation.
"I ŌĆīexpected ŌüĀwhat IŌĆÖd be paying on the card to represent a smaller share of my income, but it hasnŌĆÖt," he said.
Although annual inflation has dropped significantly during MileiŌĆÖs presidency - from 211.4% in 2023 to 117.8% in 2024 and ending 2025 at 31.5% - analysts said the improvement has failed to restore purchasing power.
Monthly inflation has edged up , accelerating from 1.5% in ŌĆŗMay 2025 to 3% ŌĆŗin March 2026, according to a ŌüĀcentral bank market expectations survey released on Wednesday.
ŌĆ£Credit delinquency reflects the income crisis facing families,ŌĆØ said Pablo Moldovan, economist and director at consultancy C-P Consultora.
Nahuel, a 37-year-old public ŌĆŗsector employee who preferred not to disclose his last name, told Reuters he took ŌĆŗa loan during ŌüĀhis vacation and then needed another loan to pay it off.
He currently has five overdue loans.
Analysts expect delinquencies to rise further as higher global energy prices boost inflation.
ŌĆ£There is no sign of a change in trend,ŌĆØ said Moldovan.
Several consultancies and economists ŌüĀwarned that ŌĆŗdelinquency levels on loans issued outside the formal banking system - such ŌĆŗas from companies that offer private loans - could be two to three times higher than the figures reported by the central bank.

item.title: UK will not back blockade of Strait of Hormuz, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on ŌĆŗMonday that whatever the pressure, Britain would not be dragged into ŌĆīthe Iran war nor be involved in a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
"We're not supporting the blockade," he told BBC Radio 5 Live, adding that it was ŌĆŗvital to get the Strait reopened.
"It is, in my view, vital ŌĆŗthat we get the Strait open and fully open, and ŌüĀthat's where we've put all of our efforts in the last ŌĆŗfew and we'll continue to do so," Starmer said.
Britain had minesweepers in the ŌĆŗregion, he said, and while he could not discuss operational matters, the military capability was "focused from our point of view on getting the Strait full open".
The U.S. military said it ŌĆŗwould begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting ŌĆŗIranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after weekend talks failed to reach a deal ŌĆīto ŌüĀend the war with Iran.
The U.S. Central Command said that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET on Monday (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ŌĆŗareas, including all ŌĆŗIranian ports on ŌüĀthe Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ŌĆŗnot be impeded, the U.S. military said.
President Donald Trump ŌĆŗsaid on ŌüĀSunday U.S. forces would also intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will ŌüĀhave safe ŌĆŗpassage on the high seas," Trump wrote ŌĆŗon social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be ŌĆŗBLOWN TO HELL!"

item.title: Iran's Hormuz gamble ushers in a tense new normal for Gulf energy
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have shattered a status quo that prevailed among Middle East oil and gas producers for decades. Even if the current, shaky ceasefire holds, the uneasy ŌĆ£new normalŌĆØ has likely set the stage for yet another round of conflict.
Tehran has now demonstrated both its ability and willingness to seal off the critical waterway and strike vital ŌĆŗinfrastructure across the region, fundamentally altering the risk calculus for its neighbours and jeopardizing Gulf countriesŌĆÖ longŌĆæterm oil and gas strategies.
The sixŌĆæweek war laid bare deep tensions between the Islamic Republic and its ŌĆīkey regional neighbours Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Bahrain and Iraq - all close U.S. allies. They had long avoided direct confrontation with Tehran under a tacit understanding that war would devastate their shared economic interests. But that entente cordiale has now been blown apart.
At the heart of the crisis lies the Strait of Hormuz . Iran closed the waterway for the first time in history, trapping nearly a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs oil and gas supplies inside the Gulf, delivering a devastating shock to the region and the global economy. Even if shipping ultimately resumes, ŌĆŗthe unprecedented act marks a historic rupture.
Iran has signalled that it wants to retain leverage over the strait under any future peace deal, floating the idea of controlling traffic and charging vessels a transit fee. U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump has urged Tehran to fully reopen the waterway, and on Sunday said the U.S. Navy would immediately start blockading the strait, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to ŌüĀreach a deal to end the war .
Yet even a formal reopening would do little to erase the lesson the war has taught IranŌĆÖs neighbours. The threat of Iran closing the strait - now proven feasible with limited military effort - is a genie ŌĆŗthat cannot be put back into the bottle.
The war has sharply exposed how vulnerable the regionŌĆÖs energy infrastructure is.
Fighting between the U.S., Israel and Iran quickly spilled across borders as Iranian missiles and drones struck dozens of targets in neighbouring ŌĆŗcountries, including major oil and gas facilities across the Gulf.
Around 11 million barrels per day of oil production and QatarŌĆÖs entire LNG output were forced offline, while refineries, export terminals and other critical installations , opens new tab suffered extensive damage.
For Saudi Arabia, the worldŌĆÖs largest crude exporter and de facto leader of OPEC, the implications are especially troubling. Even the alternative export route it painstakingly developed in recent decades to bypass Hormuz proved vulnerable.
The kingdomŌĆÖs EastŌĆæWest Pipeline, designed to divert roughly 7 million barrels per day from the eastern oil heartland to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ŌĆŗwas hit within hours of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announcement.
Saudi Arabia, which exported around 8 million bpd of oil before the war, said the attacks had cut its oil production capacity by around 600,000 bpd and reduced throughput on the EastŌĆæWest Pipeline by about ŌĆŗ700,000 bpd.
At the same time, the UAEŌĆÖs oil export pipeline to the port of Fujairah , which sits outside the strait, was also struck repeatedly. And for Qatar and Kuwait, the strait remains the sole export route.
While this situation might look like a checkmate for Tehran, ŌĆīthe Gulf is ŌüĀmore apt to see it as an intolerable status quo that will need to be remedied.
This new reality strikes at the core of the GulfŌĆÖs economic model ŌĆō both now and in the future.
For starters, the region faces years of reconstruction and instability that will undermine national development plans, even if the elevated geopolitical risk premium keeps energy prices higher.
The long-term threat is even greater.
The war has jolted many nations - particularly in Asia - into a painful reassessment of energy import dependence. For the Gulf, that comes at the worst possible moment: producers were already facing the need to maximise exports in the face of potentially diminishing demand as major importers shift away from fossil fuels. The conflict will only accelerate it.
For Gulf producers who ŌĆŗexpect uninterrupted exports of oil, liquefied natural gas, refined ŌĆŗproducts, chemicals and fertilizer to fund their economies for ŌüĀdecades to come, uncertainty over Hormuz is simply unsustainable, not only geopolitically but economically.
Consequently, the key regional powers, Saudi Arabia and the UAE - both of which harbour international ambitions economically and geopolitically - are unlikely to accept any strategic reality in which Iran can constrain their goals over the long term. That, in turn, points to a heightened risk of future confrontation.
"The strait ŌĆŗmust be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand," ŌĆŗSultan Al Jaber, the CEO of ŌüĀUAE state oil giant ADNOC, said last week .
The terms of whatever "permanent" peace deal the parties eventually agree to will matter less than the fact that this war has broken a longŌĆæstanding taboo: targeting vital economic infrastructure in the Gulf is no longer off limits.
That shift alone makes the regional balance of power more volatile, casting a long shadow over the Middle East for years to come.
The rally in global equities and the decline in oil prices following last week's ceasefire announcement suggest investors believe the Middle ŌüĀEast will soon ŌĆŗreturn to business as usual.
They are almost certainly wrong. The most likely outcome is not a restoration of the old order but a new ŌĆŗnormal - one in which Gulf powers seek to ensure Iran can never again wield the Hormuz trump card.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)

And listen to the Morning Bid ŌĆŗdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Hungarian voters strengthen EuropeŌĆÖs liberal case
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The defeat of Viktor Orban in the Hungarian parliamentary election didnŌĆÖt come as a total shock ŌĆō weeks of polls had showed his Fidesz party trailing by more than 10 percentage points ŌĆŗbehind opponent Peter MagyarŌĆÖs Tisza. The surprise came from the magnitude , opens new tab of the latterŌĆÖs victory, and the determined rebuke given by Hungarian ŌĆīvoters not only to the man who had dominated HungaryŌĆÖs political life for nearly 30 years, but to his allies, U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In the short term - outside Hungary, that is - the loudest sighs of relief will be heard in European capitals, and in Ukraine. In Brussels, there is now the clear prospect ŌĆŗof a more functional European Union. The type of ŌĆ£illiberal democracyŌĆØ that Orban had promoted put him in frontal opposition to the rest ŌĆŗof Europe, which had sanctioned what it considered breaches of the rule of law by cutting off funds to ŌüĀBudapest. Of these, 19 billion euros remain frozen pending the repeal of authoritarian legislation. And OrbanŌĆÖs Putin-friendly democracy, in its latest incarnation, had been ŌĆŗblocking a 90 billion euros loan to Ukraine that is vital to the countryŌĆÖs survival after the U.S. cut all support last year.
It was not ŌĆŗobvious before the vote that Magyar would obtain the two-thirds of parliamentary seats necessary to repeal the laws the EU most strongly objected to - on the judiciaryŌĆÖs independence, the media, public finances and even the Church. Hungarian voters proved the fears wrong - with 138 seats out of 199, Magyar will have all the space ne needs to de-Orbanise ŌĆŗHungary. He has a definite mandate to implement the pro-European, moderate program he advocated throughout his campaign.
Donald Trump, whose support for Orban has been ŌĆŗfrequent and vocal, to the point that he sent his Vice President JD Vance to Budapest a few days before the vote, has lost one his rare friends ŌĆīin Europe. ŌüĀAnd Vladimir Putin, with whom Orban had signed a 12-point cooperation agreement , opens new tab only five days ago, will not only lose an ally but see Europe more effective in its support of Ukraine. Hungarian voters also sent him an instructive message: in Europe, even autocrats leave the stage when voters show them the exit sign.
Magyar will not have an easy job - starting with finding the coffers near-empty, after the previous government reached half its deficit target , opens new tab in the ŌĆŗfirst two months of the year. But ŌĆŗhe will be able to ŌüĀcount on the benevolence of his EU counterparts, providing he takes action to undo OrbanŌĆÖs contentious reforms. At the very least international investors will start eyeing differently a country where corruption and nepotism kept growing in the ŌĆŗlast 15 years. And if the new prime minister-elect acts fast, 19 billion euros of frozen EU money ŌĆŗwill go a long ŌüĀway to jump-start a Hungarian recovery.
Follow Pierre Briancon on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: What does a US naval blockade of Iran mean for oil flows?
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said it would block shipping traffic in and out of Iran's ports starting ŌĆŗat 10 a.m. ET (1400 GMT) on Monday, a move that would prevent roughly two million barrels of Iranian oil a day ŌĆīfrom entering the world's markets, further tightening global supply.
Here are details on the planned blockade and its implications for oil markets.
After weekend peace talks in Islamabad between negotiators from the U.S. and Iran ended without a deal, President Donald Trump said the U.S. Navy "will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all ships trying to enter, ŌĆŗor leave, the Strait of Hormuz."
The U.S. military's Central Command later said the blockade would only apply to ships going to or from Iran, ŌĆŗincluding all Iranian ports on the Gulf and Gulf of Oman. U.S. forces would not impede freedom of navigation ŌüĀfor vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports and additional information would be provided, it said.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards responded to Trump ŌĆŗby warning that military vessels approaching the strait would be considered a ceasefire breach and dealt with harshly and decisively.
Retired Admiral Gary Roughead, a former chief ŌĆŗof U.S. naval operations, cautioned that Iran could fire on ships in the Gulf or attack the infrastructure of Gulf states that host U.S. forces.
Blocking Iranian shipments would disconnect a significant source of oil from the world's markets. Iran exported 1.84 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude in March and has shipped 1.71 ŌĆŗmillion bpd thus far in April, compared with a full-year average of 1.68 million bpd in 2025, according to Kpler data.
However, a surge in Iranian ŌĆŗoutput before the war started on February 28 has led to near-record levels of Iranian oil loaded on ships, with more than 180 million barrels floating as of ŌĆīearlier this ŌüĀmonth, according to Kpler data.
Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been severely curtailed by an Iranian blockade since the start of the war, remains nearly halted despite last week's two-week ceasefire agreement between Washington and Tehran.
Oil tankers were steering clear of the strait on Monday.
On Sunday, two Pakistan-flagged tankers, Shalamar and Khairpur, entered the Gulf to load cargoes from the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait; a third ship, ŌĆŗthe Liberia-flagged very large crude carrier (VLCC) Mombasa ŌĆŗB, also transited the strait ŌüĀearlier on Sunday and was ballasting in the Gulf.
Another VLCC, the Malta-flagged Agios Fanourios I, which tried to pass through the strait on Sunday to load Iraqi crude destined for Vietnam, turned back and was anchored near the Gulf ŌĆŗof Oman.
On Saturday, three fully loaded supertankers passed through the Strait of Hormuz in what appeared to be the ŌĆŗfirst vessels to ŌüĀexit the Gulf since the U.S.-Iran ceasefire deal.
Some 187 laden tankers carrying 172 million barrels of crude oil and refined products were inside the Gulf as of last Tuesday, according to Kpler.
Before the war, most Iranian oil exports were shipped to China, the top global crude importer. Last month, the ŌüĀU.S. unveiled ŌĆŗa sanctions waiver that has enabled other buyers, including India, to import Iranian oil.
India is set ŌĆŗto receive its first crude shipment from Iran in seven years this week, ship tracking data from LSEG and Kpler showed on Wednesday.
Before the war, roughly 20% of global oil and natural ŌĆŗgas exports were shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, with most cargoes headed to Asia, the largest importing region.

item.title: Trains through Angola's Lobito critical mineral corridor suspended by floods
item.description:
LUANDA, April 12 (Reuters) - A railway through Angola's Lobito corridor - a vital source of critical ŌĆŗminerals like copper and cobalt - was ŌĆīforced to suspend operations on Sunday after nearby rivers burst their banks, causing flooding.
The Lobito ŌĆŗAtlantic Railway said heavy rains flooded ŌĆŗbridges over the Halo River, between ŌüĀCubal and Caimbambo stations, as well as ŌĆŗa bridge over the Cavaco River, near ŌĆŗBenguela.
"As a result, rail traffic on the affected sections is suspended indefinitely," it said in a ŌĆŗstatement.
Angola in 2022 granted the Lobito ŌĆŗAtlantic Railway - a consortium of Trafigura, Mota-Engil and ŌĆīVecturis ŌüĀSA - a 30-year concession to operate the rail link and provide a quick route for copper and cobalt exports from ŌĆŗthe Democratic ŌĆŗRepublic of ŌüĀCongo to the Lobito port on the Atlantic coast.
The trains ŌĆŗalso move sulphur in the opposite ŌĆŗdirection ŌüĀto DRC mines as well as agricultural commodities and industrial products from the port.
Climate ŌüĀchange ŌĆŗis worsening floods across ŌĆŗthe southern Africa region, frequently disrupting transport.

item.title: Turkey concerned about any new Iran-US rules for Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey's foreign minister raised concerns on Monday about Iran or the United States proposing ŌĆŗany new regulations for transiting the Strait of Hormuz, and ŌĆīsaid he saw difficulties around proposals to re-open the waterway with an international force.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan - who is in close touch with the U.S., Iran and ŌĆŗmediator Pakistan - said in comments reported by state-owned Anadolu Agency that ŌĆŗHormuz should be opened through diplomacy, after weekend U.S.-Iran talks failed ŌüĀto reach a deal to end the war.
Negotiators should use "convincing methods" ŌĆŗin talks with Iran and Hormuz should be opened as soon as ŌĆŗpossible, he said, adding that "nobody wants" the strait to become part of the war.
"There are many difficulties to intervening here with an international armed force. Especially as the ŌĆŗwar continues, how much will it narrow, expand? We see many countries ŌĆŗare not keen on this," Fidan said.
"The problem is whether there will be proposals ŌĆīon ŌüĀnew regulations from now on, particularly from Iran," which could decide to be "more active" on the strait, he added.
In retaliation for U.S.-Israeli attacks, Iran effectively closed Hormuz, through which about a fifth of global energy supplies ŌĆŗtransit, sending energy ŌĆŗprices soaring.
Starting on ŌüĀMonday, the U.S. military said it would begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports ŌĆŗand coastal areas. Washington has sought help to re-open ŌĆŗthe strait ŌüĀfrom allies, who have not expressed interest .
Fidan said the U.S. and Iran were "sincere" in their ceasefire talks, but added that they could encounter problems if they ŌüĀapproached ŌĆŗthe issue of Iranian nuclear enrichment from ŌĆŗan "all-or-nothing" perspective.
A source said Fidan spoke on Sunday with U.S. and Pakistani officials involved in the ŌĆŗtalks.

item.title: Israeli fire kills three in Gaza following new ceasefire talks
item.description:
CAIRO/GAZA, April 13 (Reuters) - An Israeli airstrike killed at ŌĆŗleast three Palestinians in the Gaza Strip on Monday, health officials said, as mediators met leaders from ŌĆīHamas in an effort to shore up a U.S.-brokered ceasefire deal.
Medics said the strike had hit a group of men outside a school in Deir al-Balah in the central Gaza Strip. There was no immediate comment by the Israeli military.
At Al-Aqsa Hospital in Deir al-Balah, the bodies ŌĆŗof those killed lay on the ground in white shrouds outside the morgue as relatives and friends arrived ŌĆŗto bid them farewell. Some kissed the victims' foreheads before holding special prayers.
ŌĆ£This isnŌĆÖt a truce; ŌüĀitŌĆÖs a trap for our young men. Every day there are martyrs, every single day. How long can this continue?ŌĆØ ŌĆŗsaid Umm Hussam Abu El-Rous, a female relative of one of the victims.
ŌĆ£Isn't it unjust that a three-year-old child is afraid ŌĆŗof seeing his (dead) father? He says, 'My father went to bring me something from the shop,'" she added.
The ceasefire that began last October halted two years of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone demarcated by yellow-painted blocks that comprises well over half of ŌĆŗGaza, with Hamas in power in a narrow coastal strip and Israeli airstrikes continuing .
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since ŌĆŗthe deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli soldiers. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for ceasefire violations.
Palestinians say Israeli forces ŌĆīhave ŌüĀbeen moving some of the yellow concrete markers westward. Israel denies this.
The violence comes as leaders from Hamas and other Palestinian factions have been meeting since Saturday in Cairo with mediators from Egypt, Turkey, and Qatar to discuss implementing the second phase of the Gaza deal.
Under a plan put forward by U.S. President Donald Trump's Board of Peace , Hamas would be ŌĆŗrequired to lay down its arms ŌĆŗin stages over eight months ŌüĀafter a U.S.-backed committee of Palestinian technocrats takes control in Gaza.
However, Hamas' disarmament has been a major obstacle to progress on Trump's ceasefire deal and plan for Gaza , which have also ŌĆŗbeen put under strain by the war in Iran.
Two officials close to the latest talks ŌĆŗsaid Hamas told ŌüĀmediators that discussions on disarmament could only move forward after Israel fully implements the first phase of Trump's October deal, which includes a complete ceasefire in Gaza.
Israeli military officials have said they are preparing for a swift return to full-scale war if Hamas does ŌüĀnot ŌĆŗlay down its weapons.
The Gaza war began on October 7, 2023, with a ŌĆŗHamas-led attack on Israel that killed 1,200 people, according to Israeli tallies.
Israel's ensuing two-year campaign killed more than 72,000 Palestinians, according to Gazan health authorities, ŌĆŗand left the territory mostly in ruins.

item.title: Canada's Carney set to win majority government in special elections
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 13 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney is likely to secure a parliamentary majority for his Liberal government Monday, a win that would give him the ability to push ŌĆŗthrough the legislative agenda he says is needed in an increasingly uncertain and divided geopolitical world.
Three special elections are being held Monday in Ontario and Quebec, ŌĆīwith two in districts - known as ridings - that have long voted Liberal. Winning just one of those seats will give Carney a majority. Polls show they are expected to pick up at least two.
Carney has said that would help him deal more effectively with the trade war with the U.S. started by President Donald Trump. CarneyŌĆÖs Liberals currently have 171 seats in the 343-seat House of Commons.
With a parliamentary majority, Carney will have a ŌĆŗfreer hand to govern - and solidify his grip on leading Canada until at least 2029, when national elections are due to be held next.
ŌĆ£He will be able to pass ŌĆŗlegislation without having to go to the opposition to secure enough votes,ŌĆØ said Andrew McDougall, assistant professor in Canadian politics at the University ŌüĀof Toronto. The Liberals have relied on selective support from the Conservatives to pass economic and trade-related legislation in the last year.
McDougall said a majority government would also allow Carney ŌĆŗto decide the timing of the next election. Minority governments are at risk of a snap election if they lose a confidence motion and typically last less than two years.
After five opposition ŌĆŗlegislators in five months defected to CarneyŌĆÖs Liberals, he should also have a slightly more comfortable margin than a single seat. Only the governments led by John A. Macdonald, Canada's first prime minister, and Jean Chretien have seen more politicians defect to the ruling party.
On Wednesday, longtime Conservative politician Marilyn Gladu switched parties to join CarneyŌĆÖs government, saying Canada needs ŌĆ£a serious leader who can address the uncertainty that has arrived due ŌĆŗto the unjustified American tariffs.ŌĆØ
Gladu, a former chemical engineer who has previously drawn criticism for promoting unproven scientific treatments during the COVID-19 pandemic, opposing a ban on conversion therapy and ŌĆŗsuggesting the military be used to end Indigenous-led protests against oil pipelines, thanked Carney for inviting her into ŌĆ£the large Liberal tent.ŌĆØ
Carney said Thursday that the core values of the Liberal Party, like inclusivity, ŌĆīdiversity, and ŌüĀsafeguarding protections within Canada's Charter of Rights and Freedoms, have not changed and that being part of the government caucus means supporting those principles.
Yves-Francois Blanchet, leader of the separatist Bloc Quebecois party, however, said ŌĆ£the ideological differences between the Liberals and Conservatives are getting thinner and thinner by the minute.ŌĆØ
The Bloc Quebecois is in an extremely tight race with the Liberals in Terrebonne, Quebec. The Liberals won it by just one vote in the last federal election, but the result was overturned by Canada's Supreme Court because of a misprint on a voter's envelope.
The two other seats ŌĆŗare in Ontario, including one vacated by former ŌĆŗDeputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and ŌüĀanother previously held by former Liberal lawmaker Bill Blair, who resigned after he was appointed ambassador to the United Kingdom. The Liberals are expected to hold both seats.
ŌĆ£What weŌĆÖre seeing right now is a change of focus for the Liberal party,ŌĆØ said Laura Stephenson, chair of ŌĆŗthe political science department at the University of Western Ontario.
She said that while CarneyŌĆÖs predecessor, Justin Trudeau, shifted the party to ŌĆŗthe left and prioritized issues ŌüĀlike reconciliation with Indigenous peoples, rights for minority groups and immigration, there are more pressing matters for Carney, who has been a more centrist leader.
ŌĆ£He is focused on helping Canada survive the economic turmoil, not remaking society,ŌĆØ she said. ŌĆ£When weŌĆÖre in tough times like this, there are different calculations being made.ŌĆØ
Recent polling from Nanos shows more than half of Canadians prefer Carney as their ŌüĀprime minister, ŌĆŗwith just 23% picking Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. Before Carney became leader of the Liberal Party last year, ŌĆŗPoilievre had been projected to win the next election by more than 20 points.
ŌĆ£Carney has done a fairly good job showing Canadians he can handle Trump,ŌĆØ said McDougall of the University of Toronto. ŌĆ£HeŌĆÖs shown Canadians heŌĆÖs a ŌĆŗcompetent manager of the economy and the country,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£And so far Canadians have not been overly impressed by the alternatives.ŌĆØ

item.title: Keiko Fujimori holds lead in Peru's election with 50% of ballots counted
item.description:
LIMA, April 13 (Reuters) - Delays at polling stations ŌĆŗand lingering uncertainty over who will reach ŌĆīa runoff marked a second day of voting and vote counting in Peru on Monday, with conservative ŌĆŗKeiko Fujimori clinging to a narrow lead ŌĆŗin a crowded presidential race.
Logistical problems tied ŌüĀto the distribution of voting materials prompted ŌĆŗofficials to extend voting into Monday for tens ŌĆŗof thousands of voters who were unable to cast ballots on Sunday after the opening of some polling ŌĆŗstations was delayed.
The official count from Peru's ŌĆŗelectoral authority, ONPE, showed in early morning hours former congresswoman ŌĆīFujimori ŌüĀleading with about 17% of the vote, followed by rightŌĆæwing former Lima mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga on roughly 15%, and centerŌĆæleft candidate ŌĆŗJorge Nieto ŌĆŗin third ŌüĀplace with around 13%, with 50% of votes counted.
Exit polls on ŌĆŗSunday had placed Fujimori ŌĆō daughter of ŌĆŗformer ŌüĀPresident Alberto Fujimori, who was imprisoned for human rights abuses ŌĆō in the lead, although Lopez Aliaga ŌüĀbriefly ŌĆŗmoved ahead earlier in ŌĆŗthe official count, underscoring how tight and fluid the race ŌĆŗremains.

item.title: Cameroon separatists declare three-day ceasefire for papal visit
item.description:
DAKAR, April 13 (Reuters) - A separatist alliance in Cameroon's English-speaking regions said it would observe a three-day "safe travel passage" later this week to allow ŌĆŗcivilians and visitors to move freely during a visit by Pope ŌĆīLeo.
The pope left on Monday for a tour of four African countries - Algeria, Cameroon, Angola and Equatorial Guinea - aiming to urge global leaders to address the continent's challenges. Africa is home to ŌĆŗmore than a fifth of the world's Catholics.
A simmering conflict between secessionist ŌĆŗmilitias and government troops has killed thousands in the English-speaking west ŌüĀof Cameroon since 2017.
The alliance, which it said brings together the Ambazonia ŌĆŗGoverning Council (AGovC), Ambazonian Prisoners of Conscience and several separatist movements, said the arrangement ŌĆŗwould run from April 15 to April 17 across what it described as Ambazonian territory. The pope is expected in the Anglophone city of Bamenda on April 16 as part of ŌĆŗhis whirlwind 10-day tour.
In a statement sent to Reuters, the group said ŌĆŗits forces had been instructed to ensure a secure environment for civilians, pilgrims, visiting dignitaries, the ŌĆīHoly ŌüĀFather and his entourage.
The alliance described the move as a humanitarian and security measure aimed at safeguarding civilian life and allowing people to participate in the visit without fear, stressing that the papal trip was spiritual in nature.
"This decision ŌĆŗreflects a deliberate ŌĆŗcommitment to responsibility, ŌüĀrestraint, and respect for human dignity, even in the context of ongoing conflict," said Lucas Asu, spokesperson of AGovC, which ŌĆŗruns one of the largest militias in western Cameroon ŌĆö the ŌĆŗAmbazonia Defence ŌüĀForces.
Cameroon's government in the capital, Yaounde, did not immediately respond to the announcement. Calls to a government spokesperson were not answered.
Separatist leaders also urged all parties to ŌüĀrespect ŌĆŗthe safe passage period.
However, previous ceasefires and humanitarian ŌĆŗpauses announced by Anglophone separatists have not always been observed by all armed factions operating in the ŌĆŗCentral African nation's restive regions.

item.title: South Korean president's Holocaust remarks spark outcry from Israel and controversy at home
item.description:
SEOUL, April 13 (Reuters) - South Korean President ŌĆŗLee Jae Myung has sparked a diplomatic row with Israel and criticism at home after ŌĆīcomparing Israeli military actions against Palestinians to the Holocaust in a post on social media platform X.
The controversy began on Friday after Lee said "wartime killings" by the Israel Defense Forces were "no different from the Jewish massacre" by the Nazis in World War Two, ŌĆŗand reposted footage with a caption that said it showed Israeli troops had tortured and thrown a ŌĆŗPalestinian from the roof of a building.
Israel's Foreign Ministry said in a post on ŌüĀX on Saturday that Lee "for some strange reason, chose to dig up a story from 2024." It said ŌĆŗthe incident occurred during an IDF operation against what it called "terrorists" and had been thoroughly investigated.
The ministry accused ŌĆŗLee, who had said that he needed to verify the footage, of the "trivialization of the massacre of Jews on the eve of Holocaust Remembrance Day in Israel," saying his remarks were "unacceptable and warrant strong condemnation."
Israel marks Yom HaShoah on Monday remembering the ŌĆŗsix million Jews murdered by the Nazis.
The Israeli military said in 2024 it was investigating the incident in the ŌĆŗvideos and described the actions as serious and not in keeping with its values.
Friday's comments are a rare instance of Lee discussing ŌĆīinternational ŌüĀpolitics on social media and come as his government navigates a surge in energy prices following U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iran. Tehran has closed the Strait of Hormuz to tanker traffic and South Korea is one of the world's largest importers of oil and gas.
Lee did not mention the Iran war in his posts but ŌĆŗsaid that South Koreans were ŌĆŗtoday feeling "immense pain and ŌüĀnational hardship."
The president later on Saturday said it was "disappointing" that Israel criticised his comments and that it was natural to feel sorry if someone was suffering.
South Korea's foreign ministry ŌĆŗlater said it was regrettable Israel "misunderstood" Lee's remarks, which were about universal human ŌĆŗrights.
Lee's comments also ŌüĀproved controversial at home.
South Korea's conservative party hit out at Lee for failing to speak more prudently and said he was showing double standards for his silence on human rights abuses in North Korea, while Lee's Democratic Party praised him ŌüĀfor speaking ŌĆŗout on the universal value of human dignity.
The mainstream Joongang Ilbo ŌĆŗnewspaper said on Monday Lee would be well advised to recognise the weight of a president's remarks and the risk of misunderstanding from unfiltered ŌĆŗcomments on social media, especially in sensitive global disputes.

item.title: UK and France to co-host talks this week on possible defensive naval mission for Hormuz
item.description:
PARIS/LONDON April 13 (Reuters) - Britain and France will this week co-host ŌĆŗtalks aimed at restoring freedom of navigation in ŌĆīthe Strait of Hormuz, including discussions on what President Emmanuel Macron described as a possible strictly defensive naval mission.
The talks are intended ŌĆŗto bring together Britain, France and other countries ŌĆŗwilling to join a peaceful multinational mission aimed at ŌüĀrestoring freedom of navigation in the strait.
"This strictly defensive ŌĆŗmission, which will be separate from the warring parties, is ŌĆŗintended to be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said in a post on X.
Tensions in the Gulf have intensified after ŌĆŗthe United States said it would begin a blockade of ŌĆŗmaritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports, while Iran has effectively shut ŌĆīthe ŌüĀStrait, a route that carries about a fifth of the world's oil consumption.
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz was deeply damaging ŌĆŗglobal shipping ŌĆŗand was adding ŌüĀto costŌĆæofŌĆæliving pressures.
"This week the UK and France will co-host a summit to advance ŌĆŗwork on a coordinated, independent, multinational plan to ŌĆŗsafeguard ŌüĀinternational shipping when the conflict ends," Starmer posted on X.
Britain has previously convened representatives of more than 40 countries that share ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗaim of restoring freedom of navigation. ŌĆŗThe United States was not involved in those talks.

item.title: ASEAN foreign ministers urge US and Iran to push for permanent resolution
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign ministers called on Monday for the United States ŌĆŗand Iran to continue negotiating a permanent ŌĆīend to their conflict, and to implement the ceasefire fully and effectively.
The ministers, who met virtually to discuss the war ŌĆŗin the Middle East, also called for the restoration of ŌĆŗsafe, unimpeded and continuous passage of vessels and ŌüĀaircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Kremlin says it hopes for pragmatic ties with new Hungarian leadership after Orban defeat
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Monday it hoped to continue its "highly pragmatic ties" with ŌĆŗHungary's new political leadership after Prime Minister ŌĆīViktor Orban, a close Russian partner, was beaten at the ballot box by upstart rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, Hungary's veteran nationalist leader, lost power to Magyar ŌĆŗand his centre-right Tisza party in an ŌĆŗelection on Sunday after 16 years in office - ŌüĀa setback for his allies in Russia and ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
"Hungary has made its ŌĆŗchoice, and we respect that choice," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"We look forward to continuing our highly pragmatic engagement ŌĆŗwith HungaryŌĆÖs new leadership. We noted MagyarŌĆÖs statement ŌĆŗregarding his willingness to engage in dialogue. Naturally, this will be ŌĆībeneficial ŌüĀfor both Moscow and Budapest."
Orban, who last visited Moscow in November, blocked a 90-billion euro ($105 billion) loan to Ukraine after accusing it of sabotaging Russian oil ŌĆŗdeliveries to ŌĆŗhis country, ŌüĀsomething Kyiv denied, and delayed and sometimes opposed EU sanctions on Russia.
Hungary has ŌĆŗalso continued to buy oil and gas ŌĆŗfrom ŌüĀRussia despite Moscow's war in Ukraine - which Orban said he did not want his country to be dragged ŌüĀinto - ŌĆŗand Moscow is building a new ŌĆŗnuclear power plant for Hungary.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Hungarians look to changed future after pro-EU Magyar's election landslide
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungarians were waking up to a political earthquake on Monday, after a landslide victory for the centre-right ŌĆŗopposition reverberated everywhere from Washington to Kyiv, sending local markets surging and turning Budapest into a party zone .
The ballot's implications stretched ŌĆīfar beyond the borders of the landlocked country of under 10 million people, as outgoing nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban was a key ally of both U.S. President Donald Trump and Russia's Vladimir Putin, and had frustrated European efforts to aid war-torn Ukraine.
Supporters of election winner Peter Magyar's Tisza party hope he can unblock billions in European Union ŌĆŗfunds frozen over concerns about democratic standards, bolster the rule of law and hold those they accuse of wrongdoing under the ŌĆŗprevious government to account thanks to his two-thirds supermajority.
"It is an immense honour that you have empowered us ŌüĀto form a government with the most votes ever received, and to work for the next four years for a free, European, functioning, and ŌĆŗhumane Hungary," Magyar posted on X on Monday.
The Hungarian forint surged 2.5% to a more than four-year high against the euro on Monday, while ŌĆŗthe Budapest stock exchange gained almost 3% in anticipation of the EU funds starting to flow.
EU leaders, Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and Trump's Democrat opponents in the United States have all warmly welcomed Magyar's victory. Orban has blocked a 90-billion-euro ($105 billion) loan to war-battered Ukraine.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Monday that decision-making in the EU, ŌĆŗincluding with regard to Russia, would become easier after Magyar's win.
The Kremlin said on Monday Russia respected the choice of Hungarian voters and would continue ŌĆŗwhat it called "pragmatic ties" with the new government in Budapest.
In Budapest many residents were recovering from wild celebrations that saw ŌĆīthe streets ŌüĀof the capital thronged with revellers well into the early hours.
Among those letting themselves go was Zsolt Hegedus, a leading candidate for health minister, whose dance moves on stage following Magyar's speech went viral online.
Tisza supporters spoke on Monday morning of their optimism about the end of Orban's adversarial relations with Brussels.
"We are part of the European Union and we want to stay within the European Union," said network engineer Gyula Ferenc Teleki.
Magyar ŌĆŗhas pledged a sweeping anti-corruption drive, ŌĆŗincluding stronger judicial independence and ŌüĀtougher public procurement rules to help secure the release of the EU funds.
On Sunday, Magyar called on Hungary's chief prosecutor, the heads of the top court and the media authority, and other officials to resign, saying ŌĆŗthe country's public institutions had been captured by Orban loyalists over the past 16 years.
"I cannot believe...," said ŌĆŗPeter, who declined ŌüĀto give his surname, his voice cracking with emotion as he spoke about the election result.
"We have so many problems because the Hungarian state is not in good condition, but I think we have many funds from the EU, they will be coming, and maybe the new government has a better ŌüĀway to ŌĆŗrule this country."
Some conservatives, including a former Polish minister granted asylum in Hungary by Orban, ŌĆŗlamented the election result.
"Conservative Poland and Europe have lost a very important bastion," said Marcin Romanowski, who served in Poland's previous nationalist Law and Justice government. He is wanted by ŌĆŗPolish prosecutors on charges of abuse of power.

item.title: Ghana says France is open to engaging on slavery reparations
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Ghana said France was open to having discussions with a coalition of countries that are calling for reparations for transatlantic slavery, following a meeting ŌĆŗlast week with President Emmanuel Macron.
Ghana's President John Dramani Mahama, accompanied ŌĆīby foreign minister Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa and other officials, held talks with Macron in Paris on Wednesday last week.
Ablakwa said on X after the meeting that Macron has indicated ŌĆŗFrance was open to discussions on reparations, including the return of ŌĆŗlooted artefacts, addressing global economic inequities and dismantling structural racism.
An official ŌüĀfrom ├ēlys├®e Palace said on Sunday that both countries discussed France's efforts ŌĆŗto return culturally significant objects and human remains, as well as the legal ŌĆŗframeworks around these restitutions.
The official did not mention the additional measures cited by Ablakwa.
The meeting followed the United Nations' adoption last month of a Ghana-led resolution recognising slavery as the "gravest crime ŌĆŗagainst humanity" and calling for reparations. France, along with other European countries, abstained.
France's ŌĆŗrepresentative at the U.N. said the abstention was due to concerns that the resolution appeared ŌĆīto "establish ŌüĀa hierarchy among crimes against humanity".
Ablakwa said that despite that abstention, Macron had said France was willing to have an "open and honest dialogue" on the matter.
In 2001, France recognised transatlantic slavery as a crime against humanity. But, like most ŌĆŗEuropean nations, it ŌĆŗhas not formally ŌüĀapologised for its involvement or committed to reparations.
From the 15th to the 19th century, at least 12.5 million Africans were ŌĆŗkidnapped and forcibly transported by mostly European ships and ŌĆŗsold into ŌüĀslavery. France trafficked an estimated 1.3 million people, according to the Slave Voyages database.
Last year, Macron said he would set up a commission to examine France's past with ŌüĀHaiti.
Longstanding ŌĆŗcalls for reparations have gained momentum worldwide but so ŌĆŗhas a backlash , with critics arguing modern states should not be held accountable for historical wrongs.

item.title: Police fire tear gas as workers' protest in India's Noida turns violent
item.description:
NOIDA, India, April 13 (Reuters) - Police lobbed tear gas shells and used "minimum force" on Monday to quell a factory workers' protest in the ŌĆŗIndian national capital's suburb of Noida, which turned violent on its ŌĆīfourth day, with vehicles torched and stones pelted in parts of the satellite city.
Noida is among the largest planned industrial townships of Asia and houses thousands of ŌĆŗindustrial units.
Living costs across the world have risen as the ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli war in Iran curbed fuel supplies. Similar protests in ŌüĀthe auto-making state of Haryana last week led to the government ordering ŌĆŗa 35% hike in minimum wages.
Vinay Mahoti, 30, who hails from the ŌĆŗeastern state of Bihar and works at a hosiery company in Noida, said he initially protested inside his manufacturing unit but later joined workers from other companies who took ŌĆŗto the streets.
"Duty hours should be fixed, overtime hours should be ŌĆŗpaid, and companies...should adhere to the guidelines laid by the federal government," he said, ŌĆīlisting ŌüĀhis demands.
Visuals from news agency ANI, in which Reuters has a minority stake, showed dozens of protesters marching on the street and chanting slogans, while security personnel in anti-riot gear looked on.
Other visuals showed an ŌĆŗoverturned vehicle with ŌĆŗflames rising out ŌüĀof it, and protesters hurling stones and trying to break through barricades.
Local police said "minimum force" was being used ŌĆŗto maintain law and order, while Narendra Kashyap, ŌĆŗa lawmaker ŌüĀin Uttar Pradesh state, where Noida is located, called on protesters to discuss their demands with the government.
"Senior police and administrative officials are making ŌüĀpersistent efforts ŌĆŗto counsel the workers and urge them ŌĆŗto maintain peace and restraint," Gautam Budh Nagar police said in a statement.
($1 = 93.3200 Indian ŌĆŗrupees)

item.title: Venezuela hopes to lure back international miners, but it's a risky business
item.description:
PUERTO ORDAZ, Venezuela, April 13 (Reuters) - Venezuela's effort to attract foreign investors to develop its gold, iron and bauxite seams has enthusiastic backing from the Trump administration but faces big challenges, including armed groups with deep interests in the chaotic, largely illegal prospecting industry that has developed over the past 20 years, according to nine miners, residents and community activists who spoke ŌĆŗto Reuters.
The sources in the sprawling southeastern state of Bolivar said they were skeptical international companies will be able to meaningfully invest without major improvements to security in the state, where local criminals operate alongside Colombian rebels and state security forces ŌĆīhave been accused of colluding with criminals to prop up illegal gold operations.
"The (crime) syndicates control the mines. TheyŌĆÖre the ones who set the rules and enforce the law in many of the mines where we work. Depending on the situation, they impose punishments and can be very violent," said Ines Garcia, a 51ŌĆæyearŌĆæold informal miner in El Callao municipality. "You look after yourself, because even talking is a risk.ŌĆØ
The administration of U.S. President Donald Trump has supported moves by Venezuela's acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, to attract investors since she took power in January, including a mining law passed in April by the ruling party-controlled national assembly, headed by Rodriguez's brother Jorge, which allows foreign and private companies or ŌĆŗconsortiums to extract gold and strategic minerals.
Venezuela's oil-dependent economy has for years been battered by hyperinflation, a sustained brain drain, international sanctions, deteriorated oil infrastructure and deep corruption . It desperately needs income to fund Rodriguez's promises to improve public services and wages after Washington removed former president ŌĆŗNicolas Maduro in January. Mining investments would offer another source of taxes, royalties and employment.
U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said during a March visit the law will create opportunities for companies and that Rodriguez will ensure ŌüĀtheir security. The U.S. has already issued a license authorizing certain transactions involving Venezuelan-origin gold, including with state-owned mining company Minerven.
ŌĆ£You have guarantees, you have legal certainty, political security, stability and peace of mind so your investments can be developed fully ŌĆö not only in the hydrocarbons sector, where there ŌĆŗare many opportunities, but also in the mining sector,ŌĆØ Rodriguez told visiting investors at a March event. The government has provided no details about its security plans for Bolivar.
Canada-listed Gold Reserve, which has said it intends to resume mining in Venezuela and which was part of the Burgum delegation, was ŌĆŗafterward granted a U.S. license allowing it 30 days to negotiate with the government. Gold Reserve did not respond to Reuters questions about whether it was seeking the return of its Brisas mine, which was seized by the government in 2009.
Swiss commodities trader Trafigura is already working with Venezuela's state gold miner Minerven on a responsible sourcing program announced last month and said the work is in compliance with a license issued by the U.S. Treasury. Mining companies Hartree, Peabody Energy, Ivanhoe and TechMet, which were named in local press as having been part of the Burgum delegation, did not respond to questions from Reuters.
Though many international miners have wide experience grappling with security ŌĆŗissues, for people who live and work in the so-called Orinoco Mining Arc, a quartet of municipalities in Bolivar designated by Rodriguez's predecessor as strategic for mining development, corporate interest feels like the cart before the horse.
ŌĆ£For real investment to take place it is essential to resolve supplyŌĆæchain transparency, ŌĆŗsecurity ŌĆö because this is a hot zone with armed groups present ŌĆö and oversight of the socioŌĆæenvironmental impact of operations,ŌĆØ said an engineer who worked for Minerven for a decade and asked to remain anonymous for fear of reprisals.
Colombia's National Liberation Army (ELN) rebels and former members of the now-demobilized Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC) are ŌĆīactive in Bolivar, the ŌüĀsources said, as are local criminal organizations the Tren de Guayana and 3R, along with unnamed groups run by gang leaders who go by the aliases ŌĆśJuanchoŌĆÖ and ŌĆśFabio.ŌĆÖ
Criminals have supplied mining machinery, fuel and weapons in the region, as well as extracted gold, for years, said Pedro Yepez, 61, a two-decade veteran of mining in El Callao and Sifontes municipalities. "There is no way they can do that without the governmentŌĆÖs complicity and permission,ŌĆØ he said.
A community activist who has monitored human rights violations in the area for decades and asked not to be identified out of safety concerns also said armed groups control the mines and operate in complicity with the government. Other residents and community leaders echoed his concerns, with several telling Reuters that criminals regularly extort businesses as the government looks the other way.
The Venezuelan communications ministry, which handles all press queries for the government, did not respond to detailed ŌĆŗquestions about the allegations of complicity by the military, security plans for ŌĆŗthe region or contacts with potential investors.
The military's 'Operation Roraima,' meant to ŌüĀcombat illegal mining in Bolivar, began in 2023 and has continued in the years since, with top leaders publicly celebrating the destruction of illegal mining camps and equipment, including by posting photos of controlled explosions to social media.
The U.S. Department of the Interior did not respond to detailed questions about security concerns in the region.
Both the military and armed groups have been accused of involvement in killings and ŌĆŗdisappearances, along with other rights violations in the region, a UN Human Rights Council factŌĆæfinding mission said in a 2022 report, which also expressed deep concern over worker exploitation, child labor, human trafficking, violations of ŌĆŗIndigenous rights and environmental destruction.
There has been ŌüĀa sustained increase in gold flows from Venezuela since Maduro created the Arc in 2016, driven primarily by informal and illegal mining, the Organization for Economic CoŌĆæoperation and Development said in a separate 2021 report. The report highlighted human rights violations, environmental destruction and military collusion with armed groups related to the industry.
The new mining law would provide "a veneer of legality" to "severe environmental degradation and ongoing human rights violations," 16 local non-governmental groups said in an open letter published in late March, adding they were concerned about continued military control.
"Military presence in mines has historically been associated with human rights violations, corruption and the creation of hybrid ŌüĀgovernance structures,ŌĆØ the ŌĆŗletter said.
Even if foreign mining companies were able to establish themselves in Bolivar, some activists and residents said they still worry about the impact on communities and what foreign presence ŌĆŗwould mean for the livelihoods of small-scale and informal miners, including the region's Indigenous groups.
ŌĆ£You have to put the term ŌĆśbenefitŌĆÖ in quotation marks ŌĆö who benefits? Because the same state that negotiates and seeks investors forgets all the problems that exist," said Italo Pizarro, an activist from Sifontes municipality, who said Indigenous communities could face particular risks.
A Bolivar geologist with more than ŌĆŗ40 years of experience in the sector said it would be difficult to oust tens of thousands of informal miners who work in the region, as well as criminals who live off extortion: "This wonŌĆÖt be simple."

item.title: Morning Bid: Blockade takes its toll
item.description:
April 13 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Oil prices zoomed back above $100 a barrel and global stocks were shaky on Monday as U.S. President Donald Trump sought to blockade traffic to and from Iranian ŌĆŗports in the critical Strait of Hormuz after peace talks in Islamabad failed over the weekend.
ItŌĆÖs not clear whether the failure of ŌĆīthe talks jeopardises the two-week ceasefire announced last week , but TrumpŌĆÖs attempt to up the ante by sending the U.S. Navy into the Gulf is already starting to unwind some of last weekŌĆÖs relief rally.
IŌĆÖll get into that and more below.
But first, listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I break down the latest twist in the U.S.-Iran ŌĆŗwar - and why markets should care about Viktor Orban's election defeat in Hungary.
And finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when ŌĆŗI'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign ŌüĀup here , opens new tab .
Both Brent and WTI crude leapt back above $100 a barrel on Monday as the U.S. Navy prepared its Hormuz blockade, set to come ŌĆŗinto effect at 10 a.m. EDT, though the benchmarks remain below last weekŌĆÖs highs before the ceasefire announcement.
Wall Street futures were down almost 1% before the bell and ŌĆŗEuropean shares slipped , while major Asian indexes closed lower. The dollar advanced against major currencies in early trading but later pared some of those gains.
Brent crude is up some 40% since the conflict started. Perhaps just as worrying for American consumers was President TrumpŌĆÖs admission on Sunday that gas prices may stay elevated through the midterm elections in November - or rise even more. That ŌĆŗacknowledgement suggests the pressure of domestic politics alone is unlikely to secure an early end to the Middle East conflict.
The war's inflation implications became clear last ŌĆŗFriday as U.S. consumer prices increased by the most in nearly four years in March, leaving annual inflation at 3.3% , with gasoline prices accounting for most of the monthly rise.
Elsewhere, ŌĆīHungaryŌĆÖs nationalist ŌüĀleader Viktor Orban was voted out of office after 16 years in power in a landslide weekend election thatŌĆÖs set to give winner Peter MagyarŌĆÖs party a two-thirds majority in parliament. That will allow Magyar to seek warmer ties with the EU and enact constitutional reforms.
The Hungarian forint surged , as did the countryŌĆÖs bonds, with some 18 billion euros of frozen EU funds now potentially back on tap.
Finally, the first-quarter U.S. earnings season will kick off in earnest today with Goldman SachsŌĆÖ update, while ŌĆŗthe IMF and World BankŌĆÖs Spring Meetings ŌĆŗare set to begin in Washington.
Chart ŌüĀof the day
An estimated 10% of the S&P 500 will have reported first-quarter results by this Friday , with a flood of earnings due in the following weeks. Aside from banks, major company results this week include Netflix, Johnson & Johnson and PepsiCo.
Overall ŌĆŗS&P 500 company earnings are expected to rise by about 14% compared to the year-ago period, according to analyst ŌĆŗestimates compiled by LSEG ŌüĀIBES as of Friday. Instead of cowering at the prospect of a year-long oil shock, analysts have actually marked up estimates for full-year S&P 500 earnings growth. Whether that gets sideswiped by companies' own guidance now remains to be seen.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March existing home sales (10 a.m. EDT)
* Fed's Stephen Miran speaks
* World Bank ŌüĀand IMF ŌĆŗSpring Meetings begin in Washington
* OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report
* U.S. corporate earnings: Goldman Sachs
Want to ŌĆŗreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ŌĆŗthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Germany plans $1.9 billion of fuel price relief to tackle energy crisis
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters) - Germany's coalition government has agreed fuel price relief for consumers and businesses worth ŌĆī1.6 billion euros ($1.9 billion), ending a dispute over how to respond to an oil price surge triggered by the Iran war .
The energy tax on diesel and petrol will be cut by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, the conservative CDU party and its ŌĆŗcentre-left SPD coalition partners said on Monday.
The Iran war has caused the biggest disruption to global energy supplies ŌĆŗon record, with plans for a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas further driving ŌüĀup crude prices .
"This war is the real cause of the problems we are experiencing in our own ŌĆŗcountry as well," Chancellor Friedrich Merz said at a press conference.
He said the coalition was doing everything possible to ŌĆŗcushion the impact of the conflict, which has been put on hold by a fragile ceasefire, and urged oil companies to pass the tax cut on in full. "We expect the oil industry to pass along these relief measures directly and fully to consumers," Merz ŌĆŗsaid.
Economists and industry groups were sceptical.
Marcel Fratzscher at economic research institute DIW Berlin said a large share of the ŌĆŗtax break could "end up in oil companies' bank accounts" and criticised the measures for failing to encourage fuel-saving.
Germany's filling station operators ŌĆīechoed that ŌüĀconcern, calling on the government to impose price controls on oil majors or risk them marking up prices to pocket part of the relief.
"The government needs to be tough on oil majors," a spokesperson told the Rheinische Post newspaper.
The coalition also agreed to let companies pay a 1,000 euro relief bonus per employee, exempt from payroll taxes ŌĆŗand social security contributions.
Weekend talks ŌĆŗappeared to defuse a dispute ŌüĀthat flared on Friday when Economy Minister Katherina Reiche, a Merz ally, criticised proposals from Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil of the SPD to impose a windfall tax on oil companies.
A ŌĆŗsource close to Merz told Reuters that Reiche's remarks undermined a push by the ŌĆŗchancellor to resolve ŌüĀcoalition disagreements quietly.
The government is under pressure to act as Europe's largest economy struggles with weak growth and disruptions from global trade tensions.
Merz said on Monday that Germany would oppose a planned 2027 tightening of European Union CO2 levies on ŌüĀhybrid ŌĆŗvehicles and would argue in Brussels for a more "technology-open" approach, including recognition ŌĆŗof cars running on renewable fuels.
The coalition is also preparing broader income tax cuts for lower- and middle-income groups from January 2027.
($1 = 0.8556 ŌĆŗeuros)

item.title: Japan risks summer power crunch due to Middle East LNG disruptions -IEEJ analyst
item.description:
TOKYO, April 13 (Reuters) - Japan may face a power supply crunch if the Middle East crisis drags on and liquefied natural gas shipments remain disrupted, just as ŌĆŗair-conditioning demand rises during the Japanese summer, an analyst at a leading ŌĆīenergy think tank said on Monday.
Takafumi Yanagisawa, executive analyst at the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan (IEEJ), said that a prolonged conflict could also delay the start of additional supplies expected by Japan ŌĆŗfrom 2028 from new projects in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: Nigeria seeks IMF, World Bank support as Iran shock hits reforms
item.description:
ABUJA, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria will seek stronger international financial support at this week's IMF-World Bank ŌĆīSpring Meetings as the Iran war lifts fuel costs at home and complicates reforms, its finance minister said on Monday.
Surging crude prices had some clear benefits for Africa's ŌĆŗtop oil producer, boosting foreign exchange earnings, Wale Edun said in ŌĆŗa statement ahead of this week's meetings in Washington.
"But the ŌüĀshock comes at a critical transition point, intensifying inflationary pressures and ŌĆŗraising living costs for households," he added.
Petrol prices have surged more than ŌĆŗ50% to 1,330 naira ($0.9788) per litre - and diesel more than 70% to 1,550 naira a litre - since the start of the conflict, hurting people and businesses, Edun said.
The jolting ŌĆŗchange threatened to derail reforms launched in 2023 to stabilise the economy ŌĆŗand revive growth.
President Bola Tinubu has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in ŌĆīdecades ŌüĀby ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system.
At this week's meetings, Edun, who chairs the G24 group of developing countries, said he planned to push for lower borrowing costs, ŌĆŗfairer global financial conditions ŌĆŗand more ŌüĀsupport for countries pursuing reforms.
Nigeria's benchmark Bonny Light crude grade climbed from about $70ŌĆō$73 a barrel before the conflict ŌĆŗto current levels above $120, the government said.
Inflation eased sharply ŌĆŗto 15.06% ŌüĀin February from around 33% in December 2024, but remains high compared with regional peers and has come under renewed pressure since the conflict began, ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗWorld Bank said last week.
Edun said the ŌĆŗgovernment would focus on attracting private investment, creating jobs and sustaining growth, while shielding vulnerable households ŌĆŗfrom rising prices.
($1 = 1,358.8200 naira)

item.title: Philippines' Marcos suspends taxes on fuel products
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum ŌĆŗgas on Monday to help consumers cope with ŌĆīrising fuel costs.
Congress had granted Marcos emergency powers to adjust fuel taxes, one of several measures the Philippines has ŌĆŗundertaken to cope with a sharp surge ŌĆŗin oil prices driven by the Middle East ŌüĀconflict.
Marcos said the temporary tax reduction cuts LPG prices by 3.36 ŌĆŗPhilippine pesos ($0.0559) per kilogram and almost 37 Philippine pesos ($0.6154) per tank ŌĆŗand 5.60 Philippine pesos ($0.0931) per liter, lowering costs for households that rely on it for lighting and cooking.
Marcos did not ŌĆŗsay how long the suspension will last, but ŌĆŗan interagency committee tasked with leading the government's response to ŌĆīthe ŌüĀenergy crisis will meet on April 14 to discuss whether to suspend, reduce, or maintain excise taxes on gasoline and diesel.
Diesel and gasoline prices in the ŌĆŗPhilippines have ŌĆŗmore than ŌüĀdoubled since the start of the Middle East conflict on February 28, as ŌĆŗthe country, which relies heavily on imported ŌĆŗfuel, ŌüĀfaces supply pressures amid a global price surge.
The Philippines' central bank warned last week of "spillover effects" from the Middle ŌüĀEast ŌĆŗconflict after headline inflation in ŌĆŗMarch hit 4.1% on an annual basis.
($1 = 60.1270 Philippine pesos)

item.title: EU should reconsider its plans to ban imports of Russian gas, Eni CEO says
item.description:
MILAN, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union should reconsider its plans to ŌĆŗprogressively ban imports of Russian ŌĆīgas from the start of 2027, Claudio Descalzi, the CEO of Italian ŌĆŗenergy group Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said.
In ŌĆŗan address to an event organised ŌüĀby the co-ruling League ŌĆŗparty on Sunday, Descalzi was quoted ŌĆŗby Italian news agencies as saying it was unclear how the bloc could ŌĆŗreplace 20 billion cubic ŌĆŗmetres of gas from Russia, which provides ŌĆīvital ŌüĀflexibility to the output of power stations.
Russia is facing a European ban on Russian LNG imports ŌĆŗon short-term ŌĆŗcontracts ŌüĀfrom April 25 and on long-term contracts from ŌĆŗJanuary 1, 2027.
Descalzi said ŌĆŗthat ŌüĀin terms of the disruption of energy supplies, the conflict in ŌüĀIran ŌĆŗis "the most important ŌĆŗevent of the last 40 years."

item.title: India's HPCL issues rare tender seeking tanker to load Russian LPG, document shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's state-run Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab has issued a rare ŌĆŗtender seeking a liquefied petroleum gas tanker ŌĆīto immediately load propane and butane from Russia's Ust-Luga port, a tender document seen by Reuters ŌĆŗon Monday showed.
The vessel, to be loaded ŌĆŗwith 12,000 metric tons of butane and ŌüĀ8,000 tons of propane for discharge ŌĆŗon India's west coast, must not be under ŌĆŗsanctions and must have no links to Iran, the document said.
HPCL did not immediately respond to a ŌĆŗrequest for comment.
Indian state refiners are seeking ŌĆŗto buy LPG, used as cooking gas, from more diversified ŌĆīsources ŌüĀincluding Russia as India faces its worst LPG supply crisis in decades.
Supplies from the Middle East have been disrupted by the ŌĆŗclosure of the ŌĆŗStrait of ŌüĀHormuz following the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Indian authorities have previously said they ŌĆŗare purchasing LPG from countries including ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀU.S., Norway, Canada, and Russia.
India consumed 33.15 million tons of LPG last year, with imports ŌüĀaccounting ŌĆŗfor about 60% of ŌĆŗdemand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle ŌĆŗEast.

item.title: CPC raises oil exports in March by 48% from February, sources say
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), which supplies oil from Kazakhstan and Russia, increased crude exports by 48% in March, two industry ŌĆŗsources said on Monday, buoyed by a recovery ŌĆŗin output at the Tengiz oilfield.
Chevron-led (CVX.N) , opens new tab Tengiz is the ŌüĀlargest single field in Kazakhstan, a country that produces ŌĆŗ2% of the world's daily supply of crude oil, mainly ŌĆŗexported via CPC to the Russian port of Novorossiysk.
The field, operated by Tengizchevroil, went offline in January after power outages were reported ŌĆŗat the site near the Caspian Sea in western ŌĆŗKazakhstan.
Tengizchevroil's general director, William Lacobie, said last week that the field had returned ŌĆīto ŌüĀfull production.
The sources said that CPC, which pumps oil via the Tengiz-to-Novorossiysk pipeline, increased oil exports last month to 6.196 million metric tons, or 1.58 million barrels per day. ŌĆŗThat compared with ŌĆŗFebruary's 3.79 ŌüĀmillion tons, or 1.07 million bpd.
CPC declined to comment.
Challenges facing CPC have included Ukrainian ŌĆŗdrone attacks on the Russian terminal. It had ŌĆŗto ŌüĀhalt its operations for a few days after Ukraine attacked its facilities at the end of November, effectively destroying one of ŌüĀthree ŌĆŗfloating moorings.
Kazakhstan's energy ministry said on ŌĆŗTuesday that oil exports via CPC were stable after Russia's military had accused Ukraine ŌĆŗof damaging its loading facilities.

item.title: Germany details fuel relief, tax cuts, EU auto policy response
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 - Germany's coalition government on Monday announced a package of measures to cut fuel costs, allow a tax-free worker bonus, set a timeline for overhauling the statutory health insurance system and ŌĆŗpush changes to EU car emissions rules.
The following are key points from the coalition ŌĆīpaper:
- Cut Germany's energy tax on diesel and petrol by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, relief worth around 1.6 billion euros ($1.87 billion). Cost to be offset by competition- or tax-based measures targeting oil ŌĆŗcompanies.
- Tougher antitrust: expand powers of Germany's federal Bundeskartellamt competition watchdog to collect data ŌĆŗacross the motor-fuel supply chain and pursue remedies, including clawing back excess profits after sector ŌüĀprobes, to ensure wholesale price drops are passed on to consumers.
- Longer term: expand domestic energy ŌĆŗsupply, including by tapping selected German gas fields, accelerating renewables and strengthening cross-border power grid links.
- 2026: Employers may pay a one-off, tax- and social-contribution-free bonus of 1,000 euros. Lost tax revenue to be offset by a tobacco tax hike this year.
- 2027: Major overhaul of income tax to permanently reduce the burden ŌĆŗon lower- and middle-income households.
Germany's statutory health insurance system, covering most residents and backed ŌĆŗby payroll contributions from employers and employees, faces a funding gap that could reach 40 billion euros in 2030.
- ŌĆīGoal: curb ŌüĀspending growth, align outlay with revenue and stabilise contribution rates, with all stakeholders required to contribute.
- Cabinet to approve a draft law on April 29, with passage targeted before the summer parliamentary recess.
The European Commission has opened a new lawmaking process. Germany is responding ŌĆŗto the Commission's draft:
- ŌĆŗGermany supports a "technologyŌĆæneutral" approach, ŌüĀwhich would still allow new combustionŌĆæengine cars to be registered after 2035 if they meet EU rules.
- Germany will seek to pause the tougher ŌĆŗ2027 formula for plugŌĆæin hybrids ("Utility Factor"). It will push for vehicles that ŌĆŗrun exclusively ŌüĀon renewable fuels, including advanced biofuels, to be credited as zeroŌĆæemission without delay.
- Germany rejects the Commission's proposed bonus compliance credits for small electric vehicles and any size-based preferential treatment.
