It ŌĆŗplans to reduce services to Dubai, Doha and Tel Aviv to one daily flight from JulyŌĆ» 1, and to cut Riyadh services from two daily flights to one from midŌĆæMay. Changes apply through the summer ŌĆŗseason that ends on October 24, with one Dubai service restarting on October 16.
IAG's Spanish low-cost airline Iberia Express has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv through May 31.
JAPAN AIRLINES (9201.T) , opens new tab
Japan Airlines has suspended scheduled Tokyo-Doha flights until May 10 and ŌĆīDoha-Tokyo flights ŌüĀuntil May 11. The airline also announced extra flights between Tokyo and London on April 25.
The Polish airline suspended its flights to Tel Aviv until May 31. It also cancelled flights to Riyadh until June 30 and to Beirut from March 31 to May 30. The airline plans to operate its winter route to Dubai in October.
LUFTHANSA GROUP (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab
Lufthansa, Swiss, Austrian Airlines, Brussels Airlines, ITA Airways and Edelweiss suspended flights to Dubai and Tel Aviv until May 31, and to Abu Dhabi, Amman, Beirut, Dammam, Riyadh, Erbil, Muscat and Tehran until October 24. Lufthansa Cargo is the same, except for the Tel Aviv suspension, which will last through April ŌĆŗ30.
Low-cost carrier Eurowings plans to suspend flights to Tel ŌĆŗAviv, Beirut and Erbil through April 30 and ŌüĀto Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman through October 24.
The Malaysian carrier suspended flights to Doha until June 14.
NORWEGIAN AIR (NAS.OL) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline has pushed back planned launches of its Tel Aviv and Beirut services to June 15.
PEGASUS (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab
Turkey's Pegasus Airlines cancelled its Iran, Iraq, Amman, Beirut, Kuwait, Bahrain, Doha, Dammam, Riyadh, Dubai, Abu Dhabi ŌĆŗand Sharjah flights until May 1.
The Moroccan carrier says flights to Doha are cancelled until June 30 and those to Dubai until May ŌĆŗ31.
QANTAS (QAN.AX) , opens new tab
Australia's flag carrier is adding ŌüĀflights to Rome and Paris to meet an upswing in demand for European routes. Flights to Paris will increase to five return flights per week from three and the Perth-Singapore service will increase from daily to 10 flights per week. An updated schedule will come into effect progressively for flights from mid-April and run until late July.
The carrier said it is gradually increasing flights from Doha to more than 120 destinations by mid-May.
SINGAPORE AIRLINES (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab
The carrier ŌüĀextended its ŌĆŗSingapore-Dubai flights suspension until May 31, while adding services on the Singapore-London Gatwick and Singapore-Melbourne routes from late March until October ŌĆŗ24 to meet higher demand.
TURKISH AIRLINES (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab
SunExpress, Turkish Airlines' joint venture with Lufthansa, has cancelled flights to Dubai until April 30.
WIZZ AIR (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline suspended flights to Israel until April 25, and to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman from mainland European destinations until mid-September. All ŌĆŗflights to Medina have been suspended indefinitely.

item.title: Nigerian airstrike at village market is latest in which civilians have been killed
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 - A Nigerian airstrike at a village market in which more than 200 people are feared dead is the latest incident in ŌĆŗwhich the military has hit civilian targets while pursuing Islamist ŌĆīmilitants or armed groups.
Here are some of the recent incidents where airstrikes killed civilians.
April 2026 - At least 200 people were feared dead after Nigerian military jets struck a village ŌĆŗmarket while pursuing Islamist militants in the northeast of the ŌĆŗcountry on Saturday night, a councillor for the area and ŌüĀresidents said on Sunday.
January 2025 - Nigeria's Air Force said it was investigating ŌĆŗreports of civilian casualties during an airstrike that targeted armed gangs in the ŌĆŗnorthwest . Residents told Reuters that at least 15 civilians, including local security guards, were killed.
December 2024 - Nigeria's military acknowledged that an airstrike targeting a Lakurawa militant group hideout ŌĆŗin the northwestern Sokoto state killed 10 civilians because of secondary explosions.
September ŌĆŗ2024 - At least 24 people were killed in an airstrike on a village in ŌĆīKaduna state ŌüĀduring an Air Force operation targeting armed gangs and their hideout.
April 2024 - An airstrike on a village in Nigeria's northwestern Zamfara state killed at least 33 people during Eid after a military operation targeting armed kidnapping ŌĆŗgangs and their ŌĆŗhideouts.
December 2023 - At ŌüĀleast 85 people, including women and children, were killed following a military drone attack in northern Kaduna state. ŌĆŗTwo military officers faced court-martial for the incident after ŌĆŗPresident Bola ŌüĀTinubu ordered an investigation.
January 2023 - Dozens of ethnic Fulani herders were killed in an aerial bombing in the central Nigerian state of Nasarawa as they ŌüĀwere ŌĆŗunloading cattle retrieved from authorities in a ŌĆŗneighbouring state. The incident was the subject of a Reuters special report and the Nigerian Air ŌĆŗForce later acknowledged responsibility for it.

item.title: EU's von der Leyen compares Orban defeat to Hungary's anti-Soviet uprising
item.description:
April 13 - EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen hailed Hungary's election as "a victory for fundamental freedoms", and compared the ousting of ŌĆŗnationalist Viktor Orban to the country's 1956 anti-Soviet uprising ŌĆīand its 1989 break with communism.
Hungarians were waking up to a political earthquake after a landslide victory for the centre-right opposition reverberated everywhere from ŌĆŗWashington to Kyiv, sending local markets surging and turning ŌĆŗBudapest into a party zone.
"I really want to ŌüĀsay to the Hungarian people, you've done it again!" von ŌĆŗder Leyen told reporters in Brussels on Monday.
"Again against all odds, ŌĆŗlike you did in 1956 when you courageously stood up, like you did in 1989 when you were the first to cut the barbed ŌĆŗwire that was dividing our continent."
Orban was Russian President ŌĆŗVladimir Putin's main ally in the European Union, and regularly played an adversarial ŌĆīrole ŌüĀin the bloc, frustrating its efforts to aid war-torn Ukraine.
His exit after 16 years as prime minister could move Hungary more towards the mainstream in the EU, analysts have said.
Hungary's ŌĆŗ1956 uprising against ŌĆŗthe Soviet Union ŌüĀwas brutally crushed by the Red Army.
On the 50th anniversary of the revolt, Orban's Fidesz party ŌĆŗsought to align itself with the rebels' anti-communist ŌĆŗideals, ŌüĀto the outrage of the left, liberals and some on the right.
Orban, a fiery anti-communist youth leader during the Cold War, was ŌüĀa ŌĆŗpatriotic hero to supporters, but critics ŌĆŗat home and abroad accused him of taking Hungary on an authoritarian path.

item.title: 'We have been in exile': Norway government back to old quarters 15 years after Breivik bomb
item.description:
OSLO, April 13 (Reuters) - Fifteen years after far-right fanatic Anders Behring Breivik detonated a bomb damaging much of Norway's government quarters, the prime minister and his government on Monday celebrated the ŌĆŗreturn of several ministries to the redeveloped area.
On July 22, 2011, Breivik ŌĆŗdetonated a car bomb outside the PM's office, killing eight, before gunning ŌüĀdown 69 , mostly teenagers, at a Labour Party youth camp on Utoeya ŌĆŗisland.
The explosion in Oslo caused massive damage to the office tower then housing the ŌĆŗPM's office as well as adjacent government buildings, forcing ministries to move into temporary sites scattered across the city.
Monday marks the official reopening of the quarters following extensive renovations of existing ŌĆŗbuildings, as well as the construction of new ones.
"We have been in exile," ŌĆŗPrime Minister Jonas Gahr Stoere told reporters on Monday as he showcased his new office ŌĆīfor ŌüĀthe first time, adding that the return was a historic day.
"It is an important part of history for us: it was the Labour Party that was bombed out of these offices, and it is the Labour Party that is coming back," ŌĆŗhe said.
Today, Stoere ŌĆŗleads a minority ŌüĀLabour government. At the time of the bombing, he was foreign minister of a Labour-led government of then-prime minister Jens ŌĆŗStoltenberg.
In classic Scandinavian style, Stoere's office is clad in Norwegian ŌĆŗwood and ŌüĀis decorated with artwork representing various Norwegian landscapes.
Stoere brought his own personal touch to the office ŌĆö a 1995 picture of then South African President Nelson Mandela, looking through ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗbars of his former prison cell on Robben ŌĆŗIsland together with ex-Norwegian Prime Gro Harlem Brundtland, Stoere's former boss.
"This picture is a reminder that we ŌĆŗare part of something bigger," Stoere said.

item.title: Pope Leo criticizes 'neocolonial' powers hours after Trump attack
item.description:
ALGIERS, April 13 (Reuters) - Pope Leo criticized violations of ŌĆŗinternational law by 'neocolonial' world ŌĆīpowers in a forceful speech on Monday ŌĆŗduring an Africa ŌĆŗtour, hours after U.S. ŌüĀPresident Donald Trump's direct ŌĆŗattack on the ŌĆŗleader of the 1.4-billion-member Church.
The first U.S. pope urged ŌĆŗleaders in Algeria ŌĆŗon the first day of ŌĆīa ŌüĀfour-nation tour to build a society based on principles of justice ŌĆŗand ŌĆŗsolidarity.
"Today, ŌüĀthis is more urgent than ever ŌĆŗin the face ŌĆŗof ŌüĀcontinuous violations of international law and neocolonial ŌüĀtendencies," ŌĆŗLeo said.

item.title: France's Macron to hold talks with Venezuelan opposition leader Machado
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron will ŌĆŗhold talks on Monday ŌĆīwith exiled Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado , the ŌĆŗFrench presidency said.
It did ŌĆŗnot say what would ŌüĀbe discussed and ŌĆŗgave no further details of ŌĆŗthe planned discussion.
Machado has been in exile since leaving ŌĆŗthe country to ŌĆŗcollect the Nobel Peace Prize last ŌĆīDecember.
She ŌüĀis under investigation in Venezuela and the interim president, Delcy Rodriguez, ŌĆŗhas ŌĆŗsaid ŌüĀMachado should have to "answer to Venezuela" ŌĆŗfor her support ŌĆŗof ŌüĀU.S. military action against Caracas.
The United States captured ŌüĀVenezuela's ŌĆŗlongtime leader Nicolas ŌĆŗMaduro in January.

item.title: Wall Street's earnings fantasies may soon get harsh reality check
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, April 13 (Reuters) - Something doesn't quite add up. U.S. oil prices have risen over 70% in the last year, inflation remains above 3%, geopolitical risk is off the charts. But Wall Street's earnings outlook could not be rosier.
As the first quarter reporting season kicks into gear this week, the Nasdaq (.IXIC) , opens new tab is higher ŌĆŗthan it was before the Iran war started on February 28, and the broader S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab isn't far behind. It's almost as if the last six weeks didn't happen.
But ŌĆīeven if the shaky ceasefire in the Middle East turns into a permanent cessation in hostilities - a big "if" - the economic damage and price impact will still last for months, possibly years.
The magnitude of these shifts, their duration, and ultimate impact on the U.S. economy are all up for debate. But their existence isn't.
Bank of America economists sum it up neatly: "Even if the ceasefire persists, we are unlikely to move back to the pre-war scenario."
Yet that's exactly where Wall Street seems ŌĆŗto think we're heading.
In some ways, the stock market's nonchalance shouldn't come as a surprise, as Wall Street has proven surprisingly bulletproof amid the ructions of U.S. President Donald Trump's second term.
The ŌĆŗrecovery from his "Liberation Day" tariff turmoil a year ago was stunning. It underscored how resilient Wall Street has become to shocks and how confident investors are in ŌüĀthe money-spinning powers of artificial intelligence.
In that light, the LSEG consensus forecast for 13.9% year-on-year growth in first quarter earnings seems reasonable. That would be in line with the previous four quarters and may help ŌĆŗjustify the Nasdaq and S&P 500's return to pre-war levels.
The outlook for the rest of this year, however, is more puzzling.
Consensus forecasts for earnings growth in the remaining three quarters are 20%, 22%, and ŌĆŗ19.9%, respectively. That would represent the strongest growth in U.S. corporate earnings since 2018, excluding the pandemic-distorted year of 2021, according to Tajinder Dhillon, head of earnings research at LSEG.
But if corporate America is to rack up profits at such a breakneck speed, it will have to overcome a growing number of obstacles.
The whiff of stagflation is in the air.
The Consumer Price Index report on Friday showed that gasoline prices in March spiked 21% on the month, ŌĆŗwhile other motor fuels, including diesel, soared more than 30%. Both were record rises.
Meanwhile, consumers have never been more glum. The University of Michigan's survey also released on Friday showed that consumer sentiment had ŌĆŗslumped to its lowest on record.
It's easy to see why. Growth this year will likely be weaker than it would have been absent the war.
Bank of America economists lowered their 2026 U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast by 50 ŌĆībasis points ŌüĀto 2.3% ŌĆō a significant revision. They also raised their core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation outlook by 70 bps to 3.1%.
And that may be optimistic: with oil 70% more expensive than it was a year ago, headline inflation may creep up toward 4%, moving even farther from the Federal Reserve's 2% target.
This has altered the expected path of U.S. interest rates in the coming months. At the start of the year, futures markets implied 60 bps of easing by December. Those expectations have now evaporated, and the Fed's next move appears as likely to be a hike as a cut ŌĆō something unthinkable only six weeks ago.
Of course, none ŌĆŗof this is set in stone. The economy is ŌĆŗmuch less oil-intensive than it used to ŌüĀbe, consumer sentiment increasingly bears little relation to actual spending patterns, and companies could prove resilient if these shocks are short-lived.
Still, it would be remarkable if none of this dented profits.
For starters, much of this earnings boom is expected to be powered by the tech industry. The all-powerful sector's earnings are expected to ŌĆŗrise 46% in the first quarter, meaning tech would account for nearly 80% of the expected $74 billion total profit growth.
But "Big Tech", with its $800 billion ŌĆŗcapital expenditure plans on energy-intensive ŌüĀdata centers and other artificial intelligence infrastructure this year ŌĆō much of it debt-financed ŌĆō is now particularly sensitive to both soaring energy prices and the cost of money.
Equity investors are optimistic by nature, but the positivity permeating Wall Street is striking. The question now is whether corporate guidance in the coming weeks deflates those high hopes.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Save the date: On April 23 at 9 a.m. EDT /0100 GMT, ROI ŌüĀcolumnists Mike Dolan ŌĆŗand Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "ROI Markets Unpacked: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times". Click here to ŌĆŗsign up. , opens new tab
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and ŌĆŗfinance seven days a week.

item.title: Intergovernmental deal for $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline due this year, official
item.description:
RABAT, April 13 (Reuters) - An intergovernmental agreement (IGA) on a planned $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline will be signed this year, the head of Morocco's hydrocarbons and mining ŌĆŗagency (ONHYM) said.
Agreed a decade ago, the project - known as the African ŌĆīAtlantic Gas Pipeline - would run 6,900 km on a hybrid offshore-onshore route with a maximum capacity of 30 billion cubic metres (bcm), including 15 bcm to supply Morocco and support exports to ŌĆŗEurope, ONHYM's Amina Benkhadra told Reuters by email.
The pipeline, which has the ŌĆŗbacking of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), has completed ŌüĀits feasibility study and front-end engineering design (FEED) stages.
Following the intergovernmental agreement, a high ŌĆŗauthority for the pipeline will be established in Nigeria, bringing together ministerial representatives from ŌĆŗeach of the 13 participating countries to provide political and regulatory coordination, Benkhadra said.
A project company will also be created in Morocco as a joint venture between ONHYM and the Nigerian ŌĆŗNational Petroleum Company (NNPC) to lead the execution, financing and construction phase, she said.
The ŌĆŗpipeline would spur economic integration across West Africa by expanding electricity generation and facilitating industrial and ŌĆīmining ŌüĀdevelopment, while helping Morocco position itself as an energy bridge between Africa and Europe, she said.
Initial segments of the project would connect Morocco to gas fields in Mauritania and Senegal, and link Ghana to Cote d'Ivoire further south, before a final ŌĆŗsegment connects Ghana to ŌĆŗNigeria's gas fields, ŌüĀshe said.
First gas from the initial phases is expected in 2031, Benkhadra said.
"The project does not rely on a single ŌĆŗglobal final investment decision," she said, adding that each segment ŌĆŗis designed ŌüĀto be developed as "standalone system" to allow for early value build up, she said.
No final funding commitments have been secured yet, she said, adding that the financing structure ŌüĀwill be ŌĆŗled by the project company, which will mobilise ŌĆŗa mix of equity and debt.
"The project is attracting strong interest due to its scale, its phased ŌĆŗstructure, and its strategic positioning," Benkhadra said.

item.title: BP to buy interest in three offshore Namibian blocks as shift to oil and gas sharpens
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab on Monday agreed ŌĆŗto buy an operating interest in three ŌĆīoffshore exploration blocks in Namibia from Canada-based Eco Atlantic Oil & Gas (EOG.V) , opens new tab as it ramps up its upstream portfolio.
BP will pay ŌĆŗEco Atlantic $2.7 million in cash for the ŌĆŗ60% interest in the three petroleum exploration licences, ŌüĀthe Canadian firm said.
The oil major has turned ŌĆŗits focus back to oil and gas after an ŌĆŗill-fated foray into renewables, pledging to dispose $20 billion worth of assets and cut its debt to between $14 billion-$18 billion by ŌĆŗend-2027.
BP has been under pressure to publish more information ŌĆŗto prove its strategy of shifting spending from low-carbon to oil ŌĆīand ŌüĀgas projects will boost shareholder value.
The deal marks BP's entry as an operator in the southern African country, a global oil and gas exploration hot spot ŌĆŗwhich hopes ŌĆŗto produce ŌüĀits first oil by 2030.
The blocks are located in the Walvis Basin, a ŌĆŗvast area north of the prolific Orange ŌĆŗBasin ŌüĀwhere all of Namibia's offshore discoveries have been made by oil majors including Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab .
Eco Atlantic will ŌüĀremain ŌĆŗa partner in the blocks along ŌĆŗwith NamibiaŌĆÖs national oil company NAMCOR, BP said.

item.title: India gets first Iranian oil in 7 years, ship tracking data shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Two very large crude carriers loaded with Iranian oil have reached ŌĆŗIndian ports, ship tracking data from LSEG ŌĆīshows, as local refiners utilise a temporary waiver granted by the United States last month to resume purchases from ŌĆŗTehran for the first time in seven ŌĆŗyears.
The current waiver is due to expire on ŌüĀApril 19.
The Iran-flagged Felicity has reached Sikka ŌĆŗPort in western India, while the Curacao-flagged Jaya ŌĆŗis at the eastern port of Odisha, the data shows.
A VLCC carries 2 million barrels of oil.
India, the world's third-biggest ŌĆŗoil importer and consumer, has not received ŌĆŗa cargo from Iran since May 2019 after coming under ŌĆīU.S. ŌüĀpressure not to buy the country's crude.
Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , the country's top refiner, has bought Iranian oil loaded on the Jaya, a vessel under U.S. ŌĆŗsanctions, Reuters ŌĆŗreported last ŌüĀweek.
India has also allowed Reliance Industries Ltd (RELI.NS) , opens new tab , the operator of the world's ŌĆŗbiggest refining complex, to buy Iranian oil ŌĆŗloaded ŌüĀon the Comoros-flagged aframax Kaviz, Curacao-flagged VLCC Lenore and Iran-flagged VLCCs Felicity and Hedy, all of which are ŌüĀmore ŌĆŗthan 20 years old and ŌĆŗare also under U.S. sanctions.

item.title: UBS raises Brent oil price view as Strait┬Āof Hormuz disruptions persist
item.description:
April 13 - The path of least resistance for crude oil prices continues to remain upward as long as flows through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz stay restricted, UBS said in a note on Monday ŌĆīas it raised its Brent crude prices forecasts.
The bank increased its Brent price forecast to $100 a barrel by the end of June 2026, $95 by the end of September, and $90 ŌĆŗby the end of December. It also expects Brent to trade at $85 ŌĆŗa barrel by the end of March 2027.
"There is elevated uncertainty ŌüĀin respect to these forecasts depending on how the conflict evolves, what ŌĆŗenergy infrastructure is disrupted, and when flows through the strait resume," it noted.
A ŌĆŗprolonged disruption would raise the risk of a short-term price overshoot, with Brent potentially trading above $150 a barrel, while heightening the likelihood of demand destruction, UBS said.
It added that scarcity-driven ŌĆŗhoarding could further amplify price swings.

item.title: Saudi state-backed utility invests in UK energy tech firm Kraken, forms joint venture, Sky News reports
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - State-backed ŌĆŗSaudi Energy is acquiring a minority ŌĆīstake in Kraken, the software business spun off from UK's Octopus Energy Group, ŌĆŗand will form a joint ŌĆŗventure to deploy its energy management platform across ŌüĀthe Middle East and North ŌĆŗAfrica, Sky News reported on Monday.
Kraken's platform ŌĆŗwill be rolled out to Saudi Energy's 11 million customer accounts under the deal, with the ŌĆŗtransaction expected to be announced ŌĆŗin the coming weeks, the report said.
Saudi Energy, ŌĆīmajority-owned ŌüĀby Saudi sovereign wealth fund PIF, joins investors including D1 Capital Partners, Fidelity International and Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan, all of ŌĆŗwhich ŌĆŗbacked KrakenŌĆÖs ŌüĀspin-off at a valuation of nearly $9 billion last year.
Kraken licenses its AI-powered ŌĆŗoperating system to utilities worldwide ŌĆŗand ŌüĀis contracted to serve more than 70 million accounts.
Saudi Energy and Kraken ŌüĀdid ŌĆŗnot immediately respond to ŌĆŗReuters' requests for comment.

item.title: Italy rallies round pope as Trump attack tests ties with Meloni
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - Italian political and church figures rallied behind Pope Leo on Monday after Donald Trump attacked ŌĆŗthe pontiff, leaving Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni to balance Italy's close ties to the Vatican and her own alliance with the ŌĆīU.S. president.
Trump set off the furore by calling Leo "terrible", drawing a rare, direct response from the pope, who said he had "no fear" of the U.S. administration and would continue speaking out against the U.S.-led war on Iran and in defence of migrants.
Meloni, who has cultivated close relations with Trump in recent years, issued a statement backing Leo as he flew ŌĆŗoff on an ambitious four-nation visit to Africa, but made no direct mention of the U.S. president's broadside.
"May the Holy Father's ministry help foster ŌĆŗthe resolution of conflicts and the return of peace, both within nations and among them," she said, making clear ŌüĀher support for the pope without openly criticising the U.S. president.
The omission was leapt on by political opponents, who ŌĆŗsense that Meloni's closeness to Trump is becoming an electoral handicap in a country where 66% of people have a negative view of the U.S. leader, ŌĆŗtied to his aggressive foreign policy.
"As a Catholic, I am outraged by a prime minister who invokes Christian values but cannot find the strength and courage to condemn Trump's unacceptable blasphemy against the pope and the Catholic world," said Angelo Bonelli, a prominent figure in the left-wing AVS party, who referenced a later post by Trump showing an ŌĆŗAI-generated image of himself as Jesus.
However, Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini, who has also in the past associated himself with Trump, was more explicit ŌĆŗin his criticism of the U.S. leader, highlighting how Europe's far-right is trying to draw back from the MAGA orbit.
"Pope Leo is a spiritual leader for billions of Catholics, ŌĆībut ŌüĀbeyond that, if there is one person striving for peace, it is Pope Leo, and so attacking him does not seem either wise or helpful," he said in a statement.
The pope is the bishop of Rome and spiritual leader to millions of Italian Catholics, making politicians of all stripes extremely wary about taking him on.
"It has been centuries since such a blatant act of aggression against the Roman Pontiff was seen," said former centre-left Prime Minister Matteo ŌĆŗRenzi, adding that it was vital ŌĆŗfor Catholics and non-believers alike to ŌüĀdefend Leo.
"He is, after all, a 'builder of bridges,' unlike Trump, a destroyer of relationships and of civilisation. The only advantage is this: Trumps come and go, popes remain," he said.
The comment echoed an Italian saying "chi mangia papa crepa" ŌĆŗwhich roughly means, "whoever tries to devour the pope dies" -- a proverb born of centuries of tension between successive ŌĆŗpapacies and temporal rulers.
"Trump ŌüĀhas made the mistake of the century, because 'chi mangia papa crepa' has been borne out repeatedly," said church historian Alberto Melloni, pointing to Italy's royal family, the House of Savoy, which clashed repeatedly with the Vatican only to be swept away while the papacy lived on.
Antonio Spadaro, a Roman Catholic priest and undersecretary ŌüĀof the ŌĆŗVatican's Dicastery for Culture and Education, said Trump's attack revealed his own weakness.
"If Leo were ŌĆŗirrelevant, he would not merit any comment. Instead, he is invoked, named, opposed -- a sign that his words matter," Spadaro wrote on X. "This is where the Church's moral force emerges. Not ŌĆŗas a counter-power, but as a space in which power is judged by a standard it does not control."

item.title: Red Cross calls consecutive strikes in Lebanon 'gravely concerning'
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - The International Committee of the Red Cross said it was deeply concerned by ŌĆŗattacks on medical workers in Lebanon after a deadly strike on ŌĆīa Red Cross centre in the country on Monday and the death of a volunteer a day earlier.
Lebanon's state news agency reported that Monday's strike, which it said was carried ŌĆŗout by Israel, killed one person and damaged Lebanese Red Cross ŌĆŗvehicles.
The ICRC said the Lebanese Red Cross centre in the district ŌüĀof Tyre, a city on Lebanon's coast, was hit by the strike. ŌĆŗIt did not comment on who was responsible or give details of the ŌĆŗvictim.
The Israeli army did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Monday's incident.
On Sunday, the Lebanese Red Cross said one of its volunteers, Hassan Badawi, had died from his ŌĆŗinjuries after a strike by an Israeli drone in the district of ŌĆŗBint Jbeil in southern Lebanon.
Israel's military said it had struck a "Hezbollah terrorist" in the area and ŌĆīthat ŌüĀthe incident was under review after it received reports of injury to a Red Cross team.
Agnes Dhur, head of the ICRC delegation in Lebanon, said in a statement on Monday: "The loss of those who dedicate their lives to saving ŌĆŗothers is gravely ŌĆŗconcerning, given the ŌüĀimpact on the civilians who depend on their help."
"Humanitarian and medical personnel must be protected. They must be allowed to ŌĆŗreach and help the wounded, and return unharmed," she added.
The ŌĆŗlatest war ŌüĀin Lebanon began on March 2, when Lebanese armed group Hezbollah fired at Israeli positions in support of its patron Iran.
Israel has since escalated its air and ŌüĀground ŌĆŗcampaign in the country where its operations have killed ŌĆŗmore than 2,000 people, displaced more than one million, and triggered a warning that hospitals could run out ŌĆŗof life-saving supplies.

item.title: Trump says Iranian 'fast-attack' ships that come close to US blockade will be eliminated
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump warned on Monday that any Iranian "fast-attack" ŌĆŗships that go near a U.S. maritime blockade on ŌĆīIran would be eliminated.
Trump made the threat shortly after the U.S. blockade on vessels entering and departing Iran had been due to come ŌĆŗinto effect at 1400 GMT on Monday.
Describing Iran's navy ŌĆŗas "completely obliterated" during the six-week-long war between the ŌüĀU.S. and Iran, Trump posted on Truth Social: "What we have ŌĆŗnot hit are their small number of, what they call, 'fast attack ŌĆŗships,' because we did not consider them much of a threat."
"Warning: If any of these ships come anywhere close to our BLOCKADE, they will ŌĆŗbe immediately ELIMINATED, using the same system of kill ŌĆŗthat we use against the drug dealers on boats at Sea. It is ŌĆīquick ŌüĀand brutal," Trump wrote.
Trump was referring to the dozens of U.S. strikes carried out against suspected drug boats in the Caribbean and Pacific since September in a campaign that has ŌĆŗkilled at least ŌĆŗ110 people.
Iran has ŌüĀeffectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, cutting off a vital waterway that normally carries about ŌĆŗa fifth of global oil and liquefied natural ŌĆŗgas ŌüĀsupplies, in retaliation for U.S.-Israeli strikes.
Iran's conventional navy has largely been destroyed but Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still have plenty of ŌüĀoptions ŌĆŗincluding fast-attack craft, mini submarines, mines ŌĆŗand even jet skis packed with explosives, said Tom Sharpe, a retired Royal ŌĆŗNavy commander, last month.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar says to suspend state media news broadcast
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's ŌĆŗelection winner ŌĆīPeter Magyar ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗon Monday that ŌüĀhis ŌĆŗgovernment will ŌĆŗsuspend the news broadcast ŌĆŗof ŌĆŗpublic state media ŌĆīuntil ŌüĀunbiased news coverage ŌĆŗcan ŌĆŗbe ŌüĀensured.
Magyar's Tisza ŌĆŗparty ŌĆŗscored ŌüĀa landslide victory ŌüĀin ŌĆŗSunday's ŌĆŗelections.

item.title: China calls US-Iran ceasefire 'very fragile', urges unified opposition to escalation
item.description:
BEIJING, April 13 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Monday ŌĆŗsaid the current ceasefire ŌĆībetween the U.S. and Iran is "very fragile" and urged ŌĆŗthe global community to "unequivocally ŌĆŗoppose any actions that undermine ŌüĀthe ceasefire or escalate ŌĆŗthe confrontation."
Wang made the ŌĆŗremarks during a phone call with his Pakistani counterpart, Mohammad Ishaq ŌĆŗDar, Wang's ministry said. ŌĆŗThe priority is to prevent the ŌĆīresumption ŌüĀof hostilities and "to preserve the hard-won momentum of ceasefire," Wang said.
China would be ŌĆŗpleased ŌĆŗto ŌüĀsee Pakistan playing a greater role in ŌĆŗhelping resolve the conflict, ŌĆŗWang ŌüĀsaid, adding that Beijing too stands ready to ŌüĀmake ŌĆŗits contributions.

item.title: Hungary vote removes Ukraine's staunchest foe in EU
item.description:
KYIV/BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Ukraine welcomed with relief on Monday the defeat of its harshest EU foe, Hungary's ŌĆŗPrime Minister Viktor Orban , an outcome which paves the way for a 90 billion euro ($105 billion) loan that Kyiv urgently needs to fund the war with Russia .But the winner of Hungary's ŌĆīelection , Peter Magyar , is no outspoken ally of Ukraine and some other leaders in the European Union remain sceptical about stronger backing for Kyiv, particularly its efforts to join the EU, analysts said.
Magyar's centre-right Tisza party won a landslide victory on Sunday, ending Orban's 16-year rule and winning the two-thirds majority it needs to enact constitutional reforms.
Orban - a nationalist who maintained warm ties with Russian President Vladimir Putin - systematically opposed Brussels' efforts to support Ukraine, most recently obstructing the two-year EU loan over ŌĆŗaccusations that Kyiv had deliberately halted Russian oil flows across its territory via the Druzhba pipeline.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy says Druzhba was damaged by a Russian strike in January and that ŌĆŗrepairs should be completed in the spring - though he has urged Europe to stop buying Russian oil.
"One should not call Magyar pro-Ukrainian - he isn't - but he ŌüĀis not anti-Ukrainian, unlike Orban, and that's already great," said Oleksiy Honcharenko, a Ukrainian lawmaker with the European Solidarity Party.
"Putin has lost his main ally in Europe."
In his first news conference after the election, ŌĆŗMagyar said on Monday Ukraine was the victim in the war with Russia and he pledged to diversify Hungary's energy sources.
But he also said Budapest would maintain "pragmatic" ties with Moscow and that repairing relations ŌĆŗwith Kyiv depended on the restoration of the rights of the Hungarian ethnic minority in western Ukraine - a long-running source of tension.
EU officials and analysts expect Magyar to drop Hungary's veto on the loan and on a 20th round of sanctions against Russia after he takes office, probably in mid-May. Magyar is keen to unlock EU funds earmarked for Hungary that were suspended amid accusations of democratic backsliding by Orban's government.
But the timeframe is tight. Ukraine ŌĆŗis reliant on Western financial support, four years into the war. It needs $52 billion this year and economists say it could start to run out of money next month.
"Hungary's vetoes on EU financial support ŌĆŗfor Ukraine will likely cease, improving EU decision-making capacity," said Zsuzsanna Vegh, analyst at the German Marshall Fund think tank, adding Hungary itself would still not provide funding. "Tisza is unlikely to embrace expansive military support."
In Kyiv, residents ŌĆīgreeted Hungary's election ŌüĀoutcome with cautious optimism.
"This is very positive because it gives us a chance to improve relations with Hungary," said Yehor Vasylchencko, a 33-year-old IT specialist.
But, noting that Magyar is a former member of Orban's nationalist Fidesz party, he added: "I have some doubts about whether Magyar will be an improvement. Time will tell."
Ukraine's Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha said "meticulous, pragmatic, and calm work" was needed to find common ground with Hungary's new leadership.
Volodymyr Fesenko, director of the Penta think tank, said that, as Ukraine looks to reset relations with Hungary, restarting Druzhba could be an olive branch.
After years of anti-Ukraine rhetoric ŌĆŗin Hungarian media, however, much of the Hungarian ŌĆŗpopulation is wary of closer ties with Kyiv. ŌüĀIn Monday's news conference, Magyar restated his opposition to fast-track EU entry for Ukraine.
Elsewhere in central and eastern Europe, Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico, a critic of EU sanctions on Russia, and Czech Prime Minister Andrej Babis are Ukraine-sceptics. Both publicly backed Orban ahead of Sunday's vote.
In Bulgaria, another proponent ŌĆŗof improving relations with Moscow - former President Rumen Radev - leads in polls ahead of a parliamentary election next Sunday.
Oleksandr Merezhko, chairman of Ukraine's parliamentary ŌĆŗforeign affairs committee, said Slovakia's ŌüĀFico would likely be more cautious without Orban's political cover.
But Yehor Cherniev, a lawmaker for Ukraine's ruling Servant of the People party and deputy chair of the national security, defence and intelligence committee, said any dramatic shift in European support was unlikely.
"A miracle isn't going to happen," Cherniev said.
Analysts expect Orban's departure to end the shrill tone of EU discussion around Ukraine and stop Hungary's obstructionist position. Budapest will likely drop its ŌüĀveto of ŌĆŗdiscussions on technical issues of Ukraine's EU membership.
But broad concerns remain in several EU capitals - including Paris and Berlin - that ŌĆŗproposals to shorten Ukraine's path to membership might remove the incentive to enact promised reforms , such as tackling corruption.
"Magyar will be obviously less hostile than Orban was," said Orsolya Raczova, analyst at Eurasia Group. "But, with Magyar as prime minister, Hungary will basically join ŌĆŗthe mainstream opposition to speedy EU accession for Ukraine."
($1 = 0.8557 euros)

item.title: Strait of Hormuz situation is an argument for strong international maritime coalition, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - European Union's foreign policy chief ŌĆŗKaja Kallas said ŌĆīon Monday that the situation in the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz ŌĆŗis a clear point ŌüĀin favour of ŌĆŗa "strong international coalition on ŌĆŗmaritime security."
She did not specify what such a ŌĆŗcoalition would do ŌĆŗspecifically, but added the European ŌĆīUnion ŌüĀrejected any arrangement that would limit "the free and safe passage ŌĆŗthrough ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀstraits in accordance with the international ŌĆŗlaw."
She was ŌĆŗspeaking ŌüĀin a meeting of the United Nations' ŌüĀSecurity ŌĆŗCouncil in ŌĆŗNew York.

item.title: ECB's Vujcic says energy prices are still closest to ECB basic scenario
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Current energy prices are still closest ŌĆīto the ECB basic scenario and they should not influence inflation and growth, the ŌĆŗEuropean Central Bank's incoming vice ŌĆŗpresident Boris Vujcic said on Monday, ŌüĀN1 regional television reported.
Vujcic said the ŌĆŗEurope's energy system is now more ŌĆŗresistant to energy shocks caused by the clashes in the Middle East than it was ŌĆŗin 2022, when Russia invaded Ukraine, ŌĆŗthanks to the diversification of supplies.
"We are not ŌĆīin ŌüĀan unfavorable and even less so in a very unfavorable scenario, either with regards to oil or with ŌĆŗregards to ŌĆŗgas," Vujcic ŌüĀtold a financial forum in Croatia. "And that is good ŌĆŗnews."
Vujcic said that the energy ŌĆŗshock ŌüĀis still of weak intensity due to the two-week ceasefire in the U.S.- ŌüĀIran ŌĆŗwar but that the ŌĆŗmarket may be hit if the war escalates again.
(This story has been corrected to say that Boris Vujcic is ECB's incoming vice president in paragraph 1)

item.title: TotalEnergies plans to explore Black Sea with Turkey's TPAO
item.description:
PARIS, April 13 - TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab ŌĆŗhas signed ŌĆīa preliminary agreement with state-owned ŌĆŗTurkish company ŌĆŗTPAO to explore ŌüĀTurkey's Black ŌĆŗSea waters for ŌĆŗhydrocarbons, the French oil major said ŌĆŗon Monday.
The ŌĆŗagreement announced by Turkey's energy ŌĆīminister ŌüĀlast month provides a framework for ŌĆŗtechnical ŌĆŗcollaboration ŌüĀand possible joint future ŌĆŗexploration opportunities ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe Black Sea and ŌüĀinternationally.

item.title: Equinor aims to sell 8% stake in solar energy firm Scatec
item.description:
OSLO, April 13 (Reuters) - Norway's Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab said on Monday it is contemplating a ŌĆŗsale of some 12.9 million ŌĆŗshares in Scatec, representing around ŌüĀ8.1% of the ŌĆŗsolar energy firm's share ŌĆŗcapital.
Equinor said Brokers Morgan Stanley and Nordea are conducting an ŌĆŗaccelerated bookbuilding to ŌĆŗsell the stake, which represents around ŌĆīhalf ŌüĀof the oil and energy company's 16.1% holding in Scatec.
Equinor did ŌĆŗnot provide ŌĆŗa ŌüĀreason for its planned divestment.
Scatec's share ŌĆŗprice has risen ŌĆŗ26.6% ŌüĀyear-to-date and is up 87% in the last ŌüĀ12 ŌĆŗmonths, according ŌĆŗto LSEG data.

item.title: Trump posts AI image of himself as Jesus-like figure, drawing outrage
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump posted an AI-generated image of himself as a Jesus-like figure on Sunday, drawing widespread ŌĆŗcriticism even from some religious conservatives who typically support him, before deleting the post on Monday.
The post on TrumpŌĆÖs Truth Social platform came ŌĆīamid his escalating feud with Pope Leo, who has criticized the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran as inhumane. Shortly before publishing the image, the president posted a lengthy screed against Pope Leo, calling him ŌĆ£WEAK on crime and terrible for Foreign Policy.ŌĆØ
Leo, the first U.S.-born pope, said in response to TrumpŌĆÖs attacks that he had ŌĆ£no fearŌĆØ of the Trump administration and would continue to ŌĆŗspeak out. In a forceful speech on Monday in Algiers, he denounced ŌĆ£neocolonialŌĆØ world powers who are violating international law, without specifically referring to the United ŌĆŗStates.
SundayŌĆÖs post, depicting Trump in a white robe with an apparently healing hand on a prone man's head, could create a ŌüĀrift between Trump and the religious right, whose support was critical to his victory in the 2024 election.
In the painting-like image, Trump holds a glowing orb ŌĆŗin one hand and uses his other hand to touch a seemingly sick man on the forehead. The Statue of Liberty, fireworks, a fighter jet and eagles ŌĆŗcould be seen in the background.
The post was removed on Monday morning. The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Brilyn Hollyhand, who served as the co-chair of the Republican National Committee Youth Advisory Council, wrote on X: ŌĆ£This is gross blasphemy. Faith is not a prop. You donŌĆÖt need to portray yourself as a savior when your record ŌĆŗshould speak for itself.ŌĆØ
Riley Gaines, a former collegiate swimmer and outspoken critic of transgender athletes in womenŌĆÖs sports who has appeared with Trump at rallies, wrote ŌĆŗon X she could not understand why Trump posted the image.
ŌĆ£Does he actually think this?ŌĆØ she wrote. ŌĆ£Either way, two things are true. 1) a little humility would serve him well ŌĆī2) God ŌüĀshall not be mocked.ŌĆØ
Trump, who does not attend church regularly, won large majorities of Christian voters in the 2024 election. He made gains among Catholic voters, who backed him by a 56% to 42% margin after splitting more evenly in previous elections, according to an analysis by Ryan Burge, a political science professor at Washington University and a former pastor.
After Trump narrowly survived an assassination attempt in July 2024, some evangelical supporters said it was evidence he had been blessed by God.
Last ŌĆŗyear, after the death of Pope ŌĆŗFrancis, Trump posted an image showing himself ŌüĀas pope , prompting outrage from many Catholics.
In recent weeks, Leo has become one of the most prominent critics of the war in Iran, even making an unusual direct appeal to Trump and urging him to find an ŌĆ£off-ramp.ŌĆØ
Leo has also said that ŌĆŗJesus cannot be used to justify war and that God rejects the prayers of those who start conflicts. Those ŌĆŗremarks were widely seen ŌüĀas a rebuke to Trump officials like U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth, who has cited scripture to justify the use of "overwhelming violence" against enemies and likened the rescue of a U.S. airman inside Iran to the resurrection of Jesus Christ.
Trump also feuded at times with LeoŌĆÖs predecessor, Francis, who publicly opposed TrumpŌĆÖs deportation campaign as un-Christian.
At least eight ŌüĀmembers of ŌĆŗTrump's cabinet are Catholic, including Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio.
Bishop Robert ŌĆŗBarron, who serves on a Trump-created religious liberty commission, said on X that the president owed Leo an apology for his "inappropriate" statements on social media. But he also praised Trump in the same ŌĆŗpost for his outreach to Catholics.

item.title: Protracted Hormuz crisis could trigger agrifood catastrophe, UN food agency says
item.description:
ROME, April 13 (Reuters) - A prolonged crisis in ŌĆŗthe Strait of ŌĆīHormuz could trigger a global agrifood ŌĆŗcatastrophe by disrupting ŌĆŗfertiliser and energy exports, ŌüĀdriving up ŌĆŗfood prices and ŌĆŗsqueezing crop yields, the U.N. Food and Agriculture ŌĆŗOrganization said ŌĆŗon Monday.
FAO Chief Economist Maximo ŌĆīTorero ŌüĀsaid poorer countries were most exposed because planting ŌĆŗcalendars meant ŌĆŗdelays ŌüĀin access to key ŌĆŗinputs could quickly ŌĆŗtranslate ŌüĀinto lower output, higher inflation ŌüĀand ŌĆŗslower global ŌĆŗgrowth.

item.title: Emerging economies in focus at IMF World Bank meetings
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 - Global financial policy makers gather in Washington for the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Group Spring Meetings this week against the backdrop of yet another global economic shock - this time from the Iran war .
The latest economic strife - marked by soaring borrowing costs, energy prices and inflation prospects - will amplify ŌĆŗpressure on a number of emerging market nations , from Sri Lanka and Egypt to Pakistan, that had been just emerging from the ŌĆīother crises of recent years.
Here is a selection of those and other countries expected to be in focus:
IMF funding is key for Ukraine, which struck a two-year $8.1 billion programme with the Fund in February.
One major external obstacle to keeping IMF funding on track may have been removed: Sunday's election ouster of Hungarian Premier Viktor Orban is expected to pave the way for 90 ŌĆŗbillion euros of European Union funding to Kyiv, a precondition for the IMF programme.
However, Ukraine also still needs to pursue an ambitious domestic reform ŌĆŗprogramme ranging from raising revenue to tackling corruption and greater exchange rate flexibility.
"The key question domestically is if we see more, ŌüĀand we do need to see more, efforts by the Ukrainians to pass these pieces of reform, and they seem like they're going there," Roger Mark ŌĆŗat asset manager Ninety One said.
The West African nation's economic outlook is still murky due to the discovery of billions in undisclosed debt, which prompted the IMF to halt ŌĆŗa $1.8 billion loan programme in 2024.
Talks on a new programme are ongoing, but correcting the debt burden would likely require a painful and lengthy fiscal consolidation , which may not be easy for local authorities.
"Without the large fiscal effort, the Fund may ask Senegal for some sort of debt treatment, which has been firmly rejected by the authorities," JPMorgan analysts said in a ŌĆŗnote, adding they expected talks between Dakar and the IMF to continue. "Our base case of a new IMF arrangement remains tricky, with a muddle-through scenario as ŌĆŗanother option."
Mozambique has been in talks with the IMF since mid-2025 over a new lending programme. The country has said it wants to restructure its debt, though it has yet to ŌĆīoutline any ŌüĀdetails. In a surprise move, it also repaid dues to the IMF early in March - though that was interpreted as a sign it wanted to secure fresh funds down the line.
"Mozambique's recent payment is a bold attempt to acquire further credit from the IMF," Christian Franken at Oxford Economics said. "Indeed, we expect such a loan agreement to be finalised in Q2 2026."
Gabon's authorities formally requested a Fund programme in March to support their reform agenda. Years of political instability have left Gabon, the ŌĆŗsecond-largest CEMAC economy, in an economic crunch with dwindling reserves.
Talks on ŌĆŗthe contours of a programme are ŌüĀexpected to continue during the Spring meetings.
In the past two years, Egypt has unlocked billions in IMF funding and foreign direct investment from Gulf states. But heavy exposure to energy imports, GCC remittances and tourism makes Egypt vulnerable to the fallout ŌĆŗfrom the Iran war.
Some expect Egypt to ask for an augmentation of its IMF programme which includes an $8ŌĆ» billion ŌĆŗExtended Fund Facility and ŌüĀa $1.3 billion Resilience and Sustainability Facility. The Fund is due to review the programme for the next financing tranche in June, but analysts expect delays could push that to later in the year.
Venezuela's $5 billion in special drawing rights - the IMF's own currency - has been frozen since 2021 as its government was not recognised by a majority of IMF ŌüĀBoard Member countries - a ŌĆŗstatus that has yet to change under the leadership of President Delcy Rodriguez.
But with the ŌĆŗFund consulting on the matter, this could soon change. A data blackout, which has stymied engagement with multilateral lenders, is also being faded out, with Caracas releasing batches of macroeconomic data in recent weeks. Some analysts ŌĆŗsay this could be seen as a precursor to a formal IMF mission visiting Caracas in coming months.

item.title: Hungarian election winner Magyar outlines his party's plans, views
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 - Hungarian election winner Peter Magyar vowed on Monday to change the constitution in a drive to restore democratic standards, following a landslide victory he said showed the country wanted to be anchored in Europe after 16 years of nationalist rule.
Hungary's veteran Prime Minister Viktor Orban ŌĆŗand his nationalist Fidesz party lost power to the upstart centre-right Tisza party in Sunday's election, marking a setback ŌĆīfor Orban's allies in Russia and U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Following is a rundown of some key quotes from Magyar's news conference in Budapest.
"According to the rules, the result of the election becomes final by May 4 the latest, but this could happen earlier and the President... must convene the first session ŌĆŗof the new parliament 30 days after the election at the latest."
"I call on President Tamas Sulyok to convene the inaugural ŌĆŗsession as soon as possible, and not wait until May 12."
"The Hungarian people yesterday, exactly 23 years ŌüĀafter the referendum on our EU membership, confirmed Hungary's place in Europe."
"I would like to see a strong Central Europe within a strong ŌĆŗEU."
"We will have a lot of tasks... the first, to adopt the functional measures, to start Hungary's accession to the European Public Prosecutor's ŌĆŗOffice... to amend the fundamental law (constitution), and to write into it that in the future in Hungary anyone can only serve as prime minister for two terms, which is eight years."
"We will fundamentally restore, we will do everything to restore the rule of law, plural democracy, and the system of checks and balances."
"I ŌĆŗwould like to underline that we will not use anti-democratic measures to restore the rule of law, but luckily our two-thirds mandate allows ŌĆŗus to do a lot."
"A government's job is to ensure the independence of the judiciary, the independence of the investigative authorities, to provide all possible financial ŌĆīand other ŌüĀresources to carry out their tasks."
"We don't need this president," Magyar said, referring to Sulyok, saying he was only signing off on all papers that were proposed to him.
"I call on him to leave, if he doesn't, we will find a solution."
Sulyok became Hungary's ceremonial head of state with the backing of Orban's Fidesz party.
"Hungary is in a difficult financial and economic situation. The National Bank is primarily responsible for ŌĆŗensuring exchange rate stability. I myself ŌĆŗbelieve... Governor Mihaly Varga is ŌüĀdoing his job in line with the central bank law and I think that in a country which is in a difficult economic situation, cooperation with the national bank is important... of course while respecting the ŌĆŗindependence of the central bank.
"So I will try to work this way, and if we see that ŌĆŗthe central bank ŌüĀgovernor and the bank itself work in line with their legal mandate and are not, for example, trying to obstruct the financial policy of the new government... then we will be able to work together. We do not need yet more chaos... to harm investors' confidence in Hungary."
"I ŌüĀhave clearly ŌĆŗstated several times already last year that everyone has the right to assemble in ŌĆŗHungary.
"We have clearly stated that, according to Tisza and the many millions of Hungarians who support Tisza, everyone can live with, and love, whomever they want, as long as ŌĆŗthey do not violate the laws and do not harm others."

item.title: Nigeria plans July start for gas deliveries to Abuja with AKK pipeline
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria hopes to begin delivering natural gas to the capital Abuja by July through its long-delayed AjaokutaŌĆōKadunaŌĆōKano (AKK) gas ŌĆŗpipeline , the upstream oil regulator said, marking a key milestone ŌĆīfor the country's gas development plans.
"We're hoping that by July, gas will be delivered to Abuja through the AKK gas pipeline," a spokesperson for the ŌĆŗNigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission told the regulator's in-house magazine.
The ŌĆŗ614-kilometre (382-mile) pipeline is designed to deliver more than 2.2 billion ŌüĀcubic feet of gas per day and is a core ŌĆŗpart of Nigeria's strategy to shift its energy mix towards gas, supply ŌĆŗpower plants and industries in the north, and reduce reliance on diesel and fuel oil.
Nigeria holds Africa's largest gas reserves, estimated at over 210 trillion cubic ŌĆŗfeet, but much of the country's gas infrastructure remains underdeveloped, ŌĆŗmaking the AKK pipeline a critical test of its gas-led growth ambitions.
The $2.8 billion project, ŌĆīfirst ŌüĀconceived in 2008, has missed several delivery targets, including earlier deadlines of 2023 and the final quarter of 2025.
Construction began in 2020 but was slowed by funding pressures and engineering challenges, most notably the ŌĆŗcrossing of the ŌĆŗRiver Niger. That ŌüĀsection, widely regarded as the project's most technically demanding, required drilling beneath the riverbed using horizontal directional ŌĆŗdrilling, often compared to a scaled-down version of ŌĆŗthe Eurotunnel.
An ŌüĀenergy lawyer involved in the project told Reuters that work was now at an advanced stage, with the pipeline more than 90% complete.
Gas ŌüĀtransported ŌĆŗthrough the AKK pipeline will be sourced ŌĆŗfrom Nigeria's southern producing areas largely through its interconnection with the East-West Obiafu-Obrikom-Oben (OB3) gas ŌĆŗpipeline, according to industry officials.

item.title: Botswana signs energy and mineral exploration deals with Oman
item.description:
GABORONE, April 13 (Reuters) - Botswana's president has clinched multiple ŌĆŗagreements with Oman, including on mineral exploration, oil storage infrastructure and renewable ŌĆīpower, during a visit to the Gulf nation designed to strengthen economic ties, the presidency said on Monday.
Botswana has been seeking to engage Gulf states as part of its strategy to diversify beyond ŌĆŗdiamond mining and into critical minerals such as copper, gold, graphite, and ŌĆŗiron ore.
President Duma Boko, who is on a three-day visit, presided over ŌüĀthe signing of deals with the Sultan of Oman, Haitham bin Tariq, the ŌĆŗpresidency said.
They included a joint mineral exploration deal under which Botswana will seek to increase mining ŌĆŗexploration , targeting unexplored areas covering roughly 70% of its territory.
Revenue from diamond sales, which typically account for about a third of Botswana's national revenue, has fallen as the global diamond market has been ŌĆŗweakened by economic uncertainty and the rise in popularity of lab-grown stones.
Another deal was for the development of a 500-megawatt solar power plant that ŌĆīfollows ŌüĀon from an announcement on March 20 that NAQAA Sustainable Energy LLC, a subsidiary of Oman's state-owned O-Green, had been selected to design, finance, construct, and operate the plant in northwest Botswana.
It will have a minimum operational life of 25 years as Botswana ŌĆŗseeks to increase ŌĆŗthe contribution of renewable ŌüĀenergy in its power supply from 8% now to 50% by 2030.
The presidency's statement said the plant would position Botswana "to secure ŌĆŗits energy future and unlock long-term economic value".
It did not provide ŌĆŗany financial ŌüĀdetails.
State-owned Botswana Oil Limited also signed a joint development agreement with Oman's energy investment company, OQ S.A.O.C to build oil storage infrastructure in Walvis Bay, Namibia, and in Botswana.
Oman ŌüĀis also ŌĆŗtrying to diversify its economy, which is dependent ŌĆŗon oil, although its revenues have increased as a result of the oil price surge linked to the Middle Eastern ŌĆŗconflict that began at the end of February.

item.title: Colombia to control hippo population through euthanasia
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Colombia will launch a plan in the second half of 2026 to control its invasive hippopotamus ŌĆŗpopulation, including euthanizing an initial 80 animals, Environment Minister ŌĆīIrene Velez said on Monday.
The South American country is home to some 200 hippos in the central region near the Magdalena River. If ŌĆŗcontrol measures are not adopted, the population could increase ŌĆŗto as many as 1,000 by 2035, Velez said.
"We ŌüĀmust act to reduce the hippopotamus population. These actions ŌĆŗare essential to protect our ecosystems and our native species," Velez ŌĆŗtold journalists, noting that population growth threatens species like river turtles and manatees and causes water pollution. "We believe there may be approximately 80 individuals ŌĆŗthat could be subject to the measure (of euthanasia)."
Colombia's first ŌĆŗfour hippos were illegally imported in the 1980s by the late drug trafficker ŌĆīPablo ŌüĀEscobar, who established a zoo on one of his properties. The population has since grown largely unchecked, despite some efforts by provincial environmental authorities.
The program announced on Monday has a ŌĆŗbudget of 7.2 ŌĆŗbillion pesos ($1.98 ŌüĀmillion) and includes measures like confinement and relocation. Though Colombia began talks months ago with eight ŌĆŗgovernments including India, Mexico, Philippines, Ecuador, Peru ŌĆŗand South ŌüĀAfrica to possibly transfer some animals to zoos or sanctuaries in those countries, the necessary authorizations have not yet been obtained, ŌüĀVelez ŌĆŗsaid.
The Colombia hippos have genetic defects ŌĆŗdue to inbreeding, which has reduced some institutionsŌĆÖ interest in them.

item.title: Dominican Republic rains force 30,000 to evacuate, 3 reported dead
item.description:
SANTO DOMINGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Some 30,000 people have evacuated their homes in the Dominican Republic over recent days due to torrential rains ŌĆŗfrom weather systems that have hovered over the Caribbean ŌĆīnation hurling rain onto much of its northern region for more than a month.
Local media reported three people had died, including a seven-year-old girl who ŌĆŗtried to cross a river, a 19-year-old man who ŌĆŗtried to cross on horseback, and an infant girl ŌüĀin the capital, where the wall of her house collapsed due ŌĆŗto the rains.
"The government's priority at this time is to save ŌĆŗlives, protect property and preserve infrastructure," President Luis Abinader's office said in a statement on Monday, urging people to stay away from vulnerable areas.
Abinader declared ŌĆŗa national emergency in five provinces as well as in ŌĆŗthe capital.
A day earlier, the country's Emergency Operations Center (COE) Director Juan Manuel Mendez ŌĆītold ŌüĀa press conference 30,500 people had been evacuated, 14 communities were left isolated, 6,100 homes were flooded and highways and bridges were damaged.
"The rains will continue," meteorological institute chief Gloria Ceballos said on ŌĆŗMonday, noting these ŌĆŗshould ease ŌüĀslightly before intensifying again on the weekend.
"We have a frontal system that remains stationary in the northeast," ŌĆŗshe said. "The soil is already saturated. We have ŌĆŗhad ŌüĀpractically 45 days of it raining every day in some parts of the territory."
In the nearby U.S. territory of Puerto Rico, authorities warned ŌüĀthat ŌĆŗheavy rain and thunderstorms could trigger urban ŌĆŗflooding later on Monday.

item.title: Opposition candidate concedes defeat to Wadagni in Benin presidential race
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - The sole ŌĆŗopposition candidate in Benin's presidential election, Paul Hounkpe, has conceded defeat to Finance ŌĆīMinister Romuald Wadagni following the vote in the West African country, state television reported on Monday.
A 49-year-old former Deloitte executive, Wadagni is the chosen successor of outgoing President Patrice Talon and had the backing of the ruling ŌĆŗcoalition in Sunday's election, which took place four months after the government was nearly ŌĆŗtoppled in a coup attempt .
The opposition candidate, Paul Hounkpe of the Cowry Forces ŌüĀfor an Emerging Benin party, acknowledged his defeat in a statement on Monday before provisional ŌĆŗresults were announced, according to a social media post by the state television network.
Wadagni was ŌĆŗthe overwhelming favourite after the main opposition party, The Democrats, failed to get enough lawmakers to sponsor a candidate of its own and did not back Hounkpe.
Wadagni will come under ŌĆŗpressure to bolster the country's defences in the north, which faces a growing threat from jihadist ŌĆŗgroups based in the central Sahel.
The disgruntled soldiers who staged the December coup attempt said they were ŌĆīpartly ŌüĀmotivated by deteriorating security conditions there.
"A stable transition in Benin is important not only for the country itself but also for the broader West Africa region, given the political instability caused by successive coups in the Sahel," said Heni Nsaibia, senior West Africa analyst for Armed Conflict Location & ŌĆŗEvent Data (ACLED), a conflict ŌĆŗmonitoring group.
"It is ŌüĀtherefore especially necessary to focus efforts on stabilizing the north, preventing further upheaval, and demonstrating a model of stability for the broader region."
Wadagni ŌĆŗhas also vowed to deliver on bread-and-butter issues like expanding access to ŌĆŗclean water, ŌüĀexpanding social security programmes and guaranteeing emergency healthcare regardless of ability to pay.
On the campaign trail, Hounkpe said high-profile tourism projects and impressive GDP growth under Talon and Wadagni had not improved ŌüĀthe lives ŌĆŗof most people.
He has also decried what he described ŌĆŗas a climate of fear as political space for the opposition shrinks, with the ruling coalition holding every seat in ŌĆŗthe National Assembly.

item.title: Former Brazil intelligence chief arrested by ICE in the US, Brazilian media says
item.description: SAO PAULO, April 13 (Reuters) - Former ŌĆŗBrazilian intelligence ŌĆīchief Alexandre Ramagem has ŌĆŗbeen ŌĆŗarrested by ICE ŌüĀin the ŌĆŗUnited States, ŌĆŗthe Brazilian media reported on ŌĆŗMonday ŌĆŗciting Brazil's federal police, ŌĆīafter ŌüĀfleeing Brazil in September following ŌĆŗhis ŌĆŗconviction ŌüĀfor plotting ŌĆŗa coup ŌĆŗwith ŌüĀformer President Jair ŌüĀBolsonaro.

item.title: Goldman Sachs nears another precarious peak
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Climbing the mountain is one thing. For David Solomon, staying on top might be the real challenge. On Monday, the boss of ŌĆŗGoldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab announced that revenue , opens new tab for the first three months of 2026 had reached within 3% of ŌĆīa quarterly peak set five years ago. Back then, an ill-advised foray into retail banking and a drop-off in initial public offerings led to a quick descent. This time around, rising global conflict and uncertainty, paired with increasing risk in trading and lending, could arrest the Wall ŌĆŗStreet titanŌĆÖs rise.
For now, many of the firmŌĆÖs businesses are riding high. Revenue from dispensing deal advice rose ŌĆŗ89% year-over-year, as mergers and acquisitions stage a massive comeback . Equity traders turned in the best ŌüĀquarter in GoldmanŌĆÖs history. All told, the bankŌĆÖs revenue hit $17.2 billion, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion ŌĆŗin the first quarter of 2021. That was right before rising inflation halted the Federal ReserveŌĆÖs easy-money policy, and with ŌĆŗit, the frenetic pace of initial public offerings and buyouts. SolomonŌĆÖs push into retail banking also proved a misstep, from which he has since retreated.
Profitability still lags: earnings rose 19% from a year ago, to $5.6 billion, but still well below a $6.8 billion peak. That has to ŌĆŗdo with mix. Five years ago, investment banking, generally Goldman's most profitable business, generated $3.6 billion in sales versus $2.8 billion now.
A ŌĆŗlower reliance on investment banking ŌĆö notoriously vulnerable to market whipsaws ŌĆö could be a good thing. Indeed, Goldman's share price has more than doubled ŌĆīover ŌüĀthe past half-decade. Executives repeat the common Wall Street refrain that the deal pipeline remains strong. In a nod to variability, though, they added that raging Gulf conflict is weighing on corporate confidence, a necessary ingredient for massive transactions.
These days, Goldman generates more revenue from asset management, which rose to $3.3 billion from $735 million five years ago, and lending. The firm says ŌĆŗthese are more "durable" than investment banking. ŌĆŗThat could be the ŌüĀcase, but they are still far from risk-free.
GoldmanŌĆÖs reckoning of how much it could lose from its market activities on an average day rose 40% from a year ago. Provisions ŌĆŗfor loan losses remain small, at just 2% of overall revenue, but nearly tripled in ŌĆŗits corporate and ŌüĀinvestment bank. History suggests that lending is as subject to booms and busts as anything else. The view from the summit, of course, tends to make any problems look small.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: IEA is ready to further tap global oil reserves if needed, chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The head of the International Energy Agency, ŌĆīFatih Birol, said on Monday he hopes another oil stockpile release is not needed but "we stand ready to act" if ŌĆŗthe energy shock resulting from the war ŌĆŗwith Iran requires it.
The 32-member IEA agreed last ŌüĀmonth to release 400 million barrels of oil ŌĆŗfrom reserves, the largest coordinated release ever, in a ŌĆŗbid to calm oil markets. The U.S., the world's largest oil and gas producer, agreed to release 172 million barrels from ŌĆŗits Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"I hope, very much hope, ŌĆŗwe don't need to do it but if it is ŌĆīneeded ŌüĀwe are ready to act," Birol said.
Birol reiterated at an Atlantic Council event that the war has resulted in the worst global energy disruption ever and ŌĆŗsaid that more ŌĆŗthan 80 ŌüĀoil and gas facilities including production, terminals and refineries across the Middle East have been ŌĆŗdamaged by war with Iran. Benchmark oil ŌĆŗprices ŌüĀare trading near $100 a barrel.
Due to the vast extent of the production shut-ins and closure of the Strait ŌüĀof ŌĆŗHormuz, the oil releases are "not ŌĆŗa solution," Birol said, "it's just reducing the pain."

item.title: RussiaŌĆÖs Rosneft diverts oil to Tuapse refinery from drone-hit Novorossiysk port, sources say
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Rosneft is diverting oil supplies to the Tuapse refinery from the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk after the terminal, which handles roughly 14% of Russia's crude exports, was heavily ŌĆŗdamaged in a drone attack, three sources said on Monday.
A Ukrainian drone attack ŌĆīsparked a large fire at the Sheskharis oil terminal in the Novorossiysk port last week, damaging two of its main berths and forcing oil exports from the port to halt, according to the sources, who ŌĆŗwere familiar with the matter but were not authorised to speak to the ŌĆŗmedia.
The development underscores Russia's challenges in exporting oil as the rising number of ŌüĀUkrainian drone attacks deals damage to its energy infrastructure, putting additional pressure on the ŌĆŗRussian pipeline system and increasing the risk of disruptions to crude oil output.
Russian oil producer Rosneft, ŌĆŗone of the country's largest exporters shipping crude via Novorossiysk, diverted a significant volume of oil to its Tuapse refinery, two of the sources said.
Runs at the refinery, which is located close to the Novorossiysk ŌĆŗport, are expected to increase to nearly 800,000 metric tons this month, they added, from ŌĆŗusual 500,000-600,000 tons per month.
Tuapse processed about 500,000 tons in March, the sources said. The refinery is focused ŌĆīon fuel ŌüĀexports and ramping up its capacity makes sense amid higher margins, they added.
Rosneft did not respond to a request for comment.
Other exporters transporting oil via the port have begun diverting crude volumes to alternative destinations, including other export routes and domestic refineries, the sources added, saying ŌĆŗthey do not expect ŌĆŗa quick resolution.
They ŌüĀare likely to redirect oil to Baltic ports that have resumed loadings after the attacks or also to the domestic market, the sources ŌĆŗsaid.
Novorossiysk, which can export about 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, resumed ŌĆŗlimited oil ŌüĀloadings last week, but traders said the flows were minimal and the port was not operating anywhere near full capacity.
The loadings resumed for berth number two, which accommodates smaller tankers, while the ŌüĀtwo main ŌĆŗoil export berths remained shut.
Moscow ramped up oil loadings in ŌĆŗearly April and exported more crude on average than in March, Reuters reported last week, helping to offset part ŌĆŗof the losses caused by disruptions in the Black Sea.

item.title: EU launches operations of critical minerals procurement platform
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union on Monday launched the critical minerals section of its energy and materials procurement platform, which aims ŌĆŗto give more power to regional buyers and cut dependence on ŌĆīdominant producer China by aggregating their purchases.
The platform is part of the bloc's RESourceEU strategy , announced in December to develop its supply chains for rare earths and other ŌĆŗstrategic minerals needed for the energy transition and defence applications. China ŌĆŗcontrols up to 90% of output in that sector.
The EU's Energy and Raw Materials Platform , opens new tab opened to submissions from buyers on ŌĆŗMonday in the first phase of a multi-month process, an EU spokesperson ŌĆŗtold Reuters.
The platform connects buyers with suppliers, but leaves the two sides to finalise trades.
The first round of connections between buyers and sellers will start on April 13, ŌĆŗthe spokesperson said, and will focus on "immediately and soon-to-be-available rare earths, battery ŌĆŗand defence raw materials". The results will be announced in September.
Last year, the EU chose ŌĆīPriceWaterhouseCoopers ŌüĀand a Slovak software company to develop its 9 million euro platform, which has separate mechanisms for strategic raw materials, hydrogen, and energy products such as natural gas and biomethane.
The platform kicked off with hydrogen, which started submissions ŌĆŗin November and announced ŌĆŗresults , opens new tab on April ŌüĀ1, with 273 match-ups.
The REsourceEU plan aims to accelerate the bloc's Critical Raw Materials Act, passed in 2023. ŌĆŗThe EU wants to speed up the development of ŌĆŗits own ŌüĀsupply chains so as not to rely on any single country for more than 65% of its demand.
Under the CRMA, the bloc has set ambitious ŌüĀ2030 targets ŌĆŗfor domestic production of critical minerals required ŌĆŗfor its green transition - 10% of annual needs mined, 25% recycled and 40% processed domestically ŌĆŗby the end of the decade.

item.title: South Africa agrees new climate loan, critical minerals cooperation on Germany visit
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Germany has agreed to give South Africa ŌĆīa new 200 million euro ($234 million) concessional climate loan, and the two countries will deepen cooperation in ŌĆŗother areas like critical minerals, South Africa's ŌĆŗforeign minister said on Monday.
The loan will ŌüĀsupport investment in South Africa's power grid ŌĆŗand renewable energy capacity, Minister Ronald Lamola said ŌĆŗafter talks with German counterpart Johann Wadephul in Berlin.
He said German and European Union funding for green hydrogen and ŌĆŗbattery value chain cooperation had also been ŌĆŗextended by more than 270 million euros.
Lamola thanked Wadephul ŌĆīfor ŌüĀGerman support despite South Africa's strained relationship with the United States during President Donald Trump's second term in office.
Trump has excluded South Africa from meetings ŌĆŗof the ŌĆŗGroup of ŌüĀ20 nations this year and criticised its foreign policy and domestic ŌĆŗrace laws. He boycotted a G20 summit in ŌĆŗJohannesburg ŌüĀin November.
Lamola said: "We feel we are part of it (the G20) because of the support that we ŌüĀhave ŌĆŗreceived from Germany and from ŌĆŗother G20 members."
($1 = 0.8547 euros)

item.title: Hezbollah chief urges Lebanese government to cancel Washington talks with Israel
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 13 (Reuters) - Hezbollah chief Naim ŌĆŗQassem on Monday ŌĆīcalled on the Lebanese government to ŌĆŗcancel a ŌĆŗTuesday meeting between ŌüĀthe Lebanese and ŌĆŗIsraeli ambassadors ŌĆŗin Washington, describing the talks as pointless.
In ŌĆŗa televised ŌĆŗspeech, Qassem said the ŌĆīarmed ŌüĀgroup will continue to confront Israeli attacks on ŌĆŗLebanon.
Lebanon's ŌĆŗforeign ŌüĀminister said Beirut would ŌĆŗuse Tuesday's face-to-face ŌĆŗnegotiations ŌüĀto press for a ceasefire ŌüĀin ŌĆŗthe war.

item.title: US government agrees to return rainbow pride flag to New York's Stonewall Monument
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's administration has agreed ŌĆŗto restore the rainbow pride flag ŌĆīto the Stonewall National Monument, which marks the birthplace of the modern gay rights ŌĆŗmovement in New York City, ŌĆŗaccording to a proposal settlement filed in ŌüĀcourt on Monday.
The National Park ŌĆŗService, the federal agency overseeing U.S. national ŌĆŗmonuments, had removed the flag without warning in February, saying the flag was not an expression ŌĆŗof the Trump administration's "official sentiments."
Some New ŌĆŗYorkers sued the Trump administration, which has sought ŌĆīto ŌüĀlimit the rights of some LGBT people and vilified transgender people in particular, in order to restore the flag.
Under ŌĆŗthe proposed ŌĆŗjoint ŌüĀsettlement filed for U.S. District Judge Jennifer Rochon's approval in ŌĆŗthe Manhattan federal court, the ŌĆŗNational ŌüĀPark Service agreed to hang the pride flag alongside the U.S. flag within ŌüĀseven ŌĆŗdays and to not ŌĆŗremove it "save for maintenance or other practical purposes."

item.title: Turkey's TPAO to begin acquiring shares in foreign companies, minister says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey's state-owned upstream oil and gas company TPAO will start acquiring ŌĆŗshares in foreign companies as part ŌĆīof Ankara's plans to develop international partnerships to boost its energy exploration, Energy Minister Alparslan ŌĆŗBayraktar said on Monday.
Earlier, French oil ŌĆŗmajor TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab said it signed a preliminary ŌüĀagreement with TPAO to explore Turkey's Black ŌĆŗSea waters for hydrocarbons, marking the fifth ŌĆŗsimilar accord signed by the Turkish firm with oil majors this year.
"Soon, we will turn a ŌĆŗnew page too. And that is ŌĆŗthis: as TPAO, we will enter yet another ŌĆīnew ŌüĀarea by buying shares of companies abroad," Bayraktar said without elaborating.
He said the agreement with TotalEnergies would allow the two ŌĆŗcompanies to ŌĆŗcarry out ŌüĀexploration in Angola, adding Ankara also aimed to increase its ŌĆŗoperations in Libya and Pakistan in ŌĆŗcoming ŌüĀmonths.
Turkey has been pushing to develop its domestic resources via international partnerships, including with U.S. major ŌüĀExxon , ŌĆŗto boost security of ŌĆŗsupply and become a regional exporter.

item.title: EU plans more fuel subsidies to tackle Iran war price spikes
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Commission wants to let countries unleash more public money to help businesses with fuel and fertiliser bills, ŌĆŗit said on Monday, as governments race to offset the ŌĆīeconomic shock from soaring prices triggered by the Iran war.
Oil prices jumped about 6% to more than $100 a barrel on Monday after the U.S. military ŌĆŗsaid it will blockade ships leaving Iran's ports, raising fears ŌĆŗof prolonged disruptions to oil and gas shipments.
As part ŌüĀof a broader package of measures Brussels is preparing to respond ŌĆŗto the energy price spike, the Commission on Monday proposed changing ŌĆŗEU state aid rules to allow more public spending for industries hit acutely by fuel price increases, including agriculture, road transport and shipping within Europe.
The changes ŌĆŗwould let governments cover part of the price increase companies have ŌĆŗpaid for their fuel or fertilisers, versus prices before the U.S.-Israeli war with ŌĆīIran ŌüĀbegan on February 28.
The draft EU plan would also increase, to above 50%, the maximum share of aid that energy-intensive industries can receive to help pay their power bills.
European governments including Germany , Italy, Poland ŌĆŗand Hungary have already ŌĆŗintroduced a ŌüĀraft of funding measures including fuel price caps and tax cuts, to try to contain the Iran ŌĆŗwar's economic fallout.
Governments will give feedback on the ŌĆŗEU proposals, ŌüĀbefore the Commission plans to adopt a final version by the end of the month.
The proposed changes would be temporary, introduced specifically to ŌüĀaddress ŌĆŗthe energy fallout of the Iran war.
The ŌĆŗCommission vets national governments' state aid to check that it does not distort competition ŌĆŗin the EU single market.

item.title: Morgan Stanley maintains oil price forecasts and predicts slow recovery in supply
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Morgan Stanley left its Brent crude oil price forecasts unchanged on Monday, at $110 a barrel for ŌĆŗthe second quarter of 2026 and $100 a ŌĆībarrel in the third quarter, falling to $80 a barrel in 2027.
The bank said it expected oil supply chains to take ŌĆŗmonths to normalise even if a reopening of the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz can be achieved.
Under its ŌüĀbase-case scenario, exports through the Strait stay at low ŌĆŗlevels in April, recover about 70% of lost volumes ŌĆŗbetween May and July, and return to steady-state levels by October.
Oil prices climbed back above $100 per barrel on Monday, as ŌĆŗthe U.S. Navy prepared to block ships to ŌĆŗand from Iran via the Strait of Hormuz, potentially restricting Iranian ŌĆīoil ŌüĀexports, after Washington and Tehran failed to reach a deal to end the war .
Brent crude futures were at $102.23 a barrel by 0810 GMT and U.S. West Texas ŌĆŗIntermediate traded at $103.88.
Meanwhile, ŌĆŗMiddle Eastern ŌüĀproducers including Kuwait and Iraq have lifted Asia-bound official prices sharply for May.
Saudi Arabia ŌĆŗset the price of its Arab Light ŌĆŗcrude ŌüĀto Asia at a record premium of $19.50 a barrel to the Oman/Dubai average.
Analysts expect the hit to global oil ŌüĀproduction ŌĆŗto flip the oil market into ŌĆŗa supply deficit this year, versus pre-conflict estimates of a comfortable oversupply.

item.title: Exclusive: US, Iran leave door open to dialogue after tense Islamabad talks
item.description:
