ISLAMABAD/DUBAI/WASHINGTON April 13 (Reuters) - After a sleepless and at times tense night in Islamabad, Iranian and U.S. officials ended their highest-level talks in decades without a breakthrough, but 11 sources familiar with the negotiations said dialogue was still alive.
The weekend meeting to resolve the conflict between the U.S. and Iran , held four days after last Tuesday's ceasefire announcement, was the first direct encounter between U.S. and Iranian officials ŌĆŗin more than a decade and the most senior engagement since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution.
Inside Islamabad's luxury Serena Hotel, the talks unfolded across two separate wings and one common area ŌĆö one for the U.S. side, one for the Iranians and one for trilateral meetings ŌĆīinvolving Pakistani mediators, operational staff told Reuters.
Among the slew of issues at stake was the Strait of Hormuz, a major transit point for global energy supplies that Iran has effectively blocked but the U.S. has vowed to reopen, as well as Iran's nuclear programme and international sanctions on Tehran.
Phones were not allowed in the main room, forcing delegates, including U.S. Vice President JD Vance and Iranian Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, to step out during breaks to relay messages back home, two of the sources said.
"There was a strong hope in the middle of the talks that there would be a breakthrough and the two sides would reach an agreement. However, things changed within no time," a Pakistani government source said.
Another source involved in the talks said ŌĆŗthe parties came "very close" to an agreement and were "80% there", before running into decisions that could not be settled on the spot.
Two senior Iranian sources described the atmosphere as heavy and unfriendly, adding that while Pakistan tried to soften the mood, neither side showed any willingness to ease tensions.
Nevertheless the two Iranian sources said that by early Sunday morning the atmosphere had shown some improvement, and the possibility of a one-day extension began to take shape.
However, differences persisted. A U.S. ŌüĀsource said the Iranians did not properly understand that the core U.S. aim was to have a deal that ensured Iran would never obtain a nuclear weapon. Among Iran's concerns was a distrust of U.S. intentions.
This account, based on sources who spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter, offers ŌĆŗa first account of the internal dynamics of the meeting, how the mood in the room shifted, how talks ended after signs the meeting might be extended, and how further dialogue remains on the cards.
There was no immediate response from the Iranian government to a request for comment on the issues reported in this ŌĆŗstory.
On Monday U.S. President Donald Trump said Iran had "called this morning" and that "they'd like to work a deal." Reuters could not immediately verify the assertion.
A U.S. official, referring to Trump's comment, said there was continued engagement between the U.S. and Iran and forward motion on trying to get to an agreement.
Asked for comment, White House spokeswoman Olivia Wales said the U.S. position had never shifted in the Islamabad meeting.
"Iran can never have a nuclear weapon, and President TrumpŌĆÖs negotiating team stuck to this red line and many others. Engagement continues toward an agreement,ŌĆØ she said.
A Middle East-based diplomat said conversations between mediators and the Americans have continued since Vance left Islamabad, while the source involved in the talks said Pakistan was ŌĆŗstill passing messages between Tehran and Washington.
"I want to tell you that a full effort is still on to resolve the issues," Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said on Monday.
Despite numerous obstacles to peace, both sides appear to have strong reasons to consider de-escalation.
The U.S. strikes appear unpopular at home and look unlikely ŌĆŗto topple Iran's theocratic ruling system, while Tehran's strangling of energy supplies is hurting the global economy and pushing up inflation months before U.S. midterm elections.
Also, war damage to Iran's ailing economy risks leaving the authorities there weaker internally just weeks after protests they were able to put down only with mass killings.
In Islamabad, the longtime foes had gathered ŌĆīto try to chart ŌüĀa path to a long-term settlement, after a Pakistan-brokered ceasefire paused six weeks of war that has killed thousands of people and disrupted the world's energy supplies.
Central to the dispute is a belief among Western countries and Israel that Iran wants a nuclear bomb. Iran denies seeking nuclear weapons.
A White House official said the U.S. wanted Iran to end all uranium enrichment, dismantle all major nuclear enrichment facilities, turn over its highly enriched uranium, accept a broader peace, agree a security framework that includes regional allies, end funding for regional proxies and fully open Hormuz, charging no tolls.
IranŌĆÖs demands included a guaranteed permanent ceasefire, assurances of no future strikes on Iran and its allies in the region, lifting of primary and secondary sanctions, unfreezing of all assets, recognition of its right to enrichment and continued control of Hormuz, Iranian sources have said.
Four of the 11 sources said that at times the dialogue appeared close to producing at least a framework understanding, but unraveled over Iran's ŌĆŗnuclear programme, the Hormuz Strait and the amount of frozen assets Tehran wants access ŌĆŗto.
The Iranian sources said most of the substantive exchanges in Islamabad ŌüĀwere between Vance, Qalibaf and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi.
"There were ups and downs. There were tense moments. People left the room, and then came back," the security source said.
Pakistani representatives, including Army Chief Asim Munir, and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, moved between the sides through the night to keep things on track, five Pakistani sources said.
The talks stretched for more than 20 hours with on-duty hotel staff eating, sleeping and working on site after undergoing expedited ŌĆŗbackground checks, they said.
When discussions turned to guarantees, both non-aggression assurances and sanctions relief, the tone of the normally mild-mannered Araqchi grew sharper, the two Iranian sources said.
The sources quoted him as saying: ŌĆ£How can we trust you when, ŌĆŗin the last Geneva meeting, you said the ŌüĀU.S. would not attack while diplomacy was under way?ŌĆØ
The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran began two days after the two sides held a previous round of talks in Geneva.
In addition to differences over Hormuz, sanctions and other topics, the two sides also disagreed over the scope of any deal. While Washington focused on the nuclear file and Hormuz, Tehran wanted a broader understanding, according to two of the sources.
During one tense moment, raised voices could be heard outside the negotiating room before Munir and Dar called a tea break and moved the two sides back into separate rooms, the government source said.
Toward the final stages of the discussions, which spilled into Sunday ŌüĀmorning, the U.S. delegates ŌĆŗwere moving between the negotiating room and their private floor far more often than the Iranians, the senior Pakistani official said.
A U.S. source said the vice president came to the talks ŌĆŗwith the aim of making a deal and reaching a mutual understanding. The U.S. side has been wary of protracted negotiations with Iran, believing the Iranians are adept at delaying tactics and refusing to make concessions, the source said.
Despite the deadlock, when Vance appeared in front of reporters later to announce the talks had ended, his remarks suggested more exchanges of some kind might be in ŌĆŗprospect.
"We leave here with a very simple proposal, a method of understanding that is our final and best offer," he said. "We'll see if the Iranians accept it."

item.title: Minnesota county probes federal arrest as possible kidnapping
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - A Minnesota county is investigating an arrest by federal agents who allegedly broke into a St. Paul home with guns drawn, handcuffed ŌĆŗa man and forced him outside in the snow in his underpants ŌĆīand sandals, only to discover they had the wrong man.
Ramsey County officials said in a statement on Monday that federal agents "may have committed the crimes of kidnapping, burglary, and false imprisonment" ŌĆŗin the January incident.
The man mistakenly detained, ChongLy ŌĆ£ScottŌĆØ Thao, has said the ŌĆŗofficers had no warrant.
The incident has drawn further scrutiny to how federal ŌüĀagents have operated in Minneapolis, a city at the center of debates over ŌĆŗlaw enforcement tactics in President Donald Trump's immigration enforcement actions.
Minneapolis and St. Paul, situated ŌĆŗside by side, are known as the Twin Cities. Ramsey County officials said they were seeking information from the Department of Homeland Security.
County Attorney John Choi and Sheriff Bob Fletcher said in ŌĆŗthe statement that Thao was handcuffed, removed from his home without consent and ŌĆŗdriven around and questioned while in custody for about an hour before being returned home.
The two "criminal ŌĆīillegal ŌüĀaliens" who DHS said it was seeking in that raid turned out to be elsewhere, one of them behind bars in a Minneapolis-area prison serving a four-year sentence.
Asked about the investigation, DHS said in a statement that U.S. Immigration and Customs ŌĆŗEnforcement does not kidnap ŌĆŗpeople.
It said its ŌüĀlaw enforcement officers were executing a warrant and concluded through surveillance and intelligence that suspected sexual predators had ties to the ŌĆŗproperty, adding that a U.S. citizen at the house refused ŌĆŗto be ŌüĀfingerprinted or identified facially.
DHS added: "As with any law enforcement agency, it is standard protocol to hold all individuals in a house of an operation for safety of the public ŌüĀand ŌĆŗlaw enforcement.ŌĆØ
Trump earlier this year deployed thousands of ŌĆŗarmed agents in and around Minneapolis to detain and deport migrants in an operation in which agents fatally ŌĆŗshot two American citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti .

item.title: TRADING DAY US stocks gain, dollar dips on hopes for Iran war negotiations
item.description: NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Wall Street's main indexes rallied to close sharply higher on Monday as hopes for de-escalation in the Middle East helped investors look past the failed U.S.-Iran talks and focus on the start of first-quarter earnings season.

item.title: North Korea tests cruise and anti-ship missiles from naval destroyer
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - North Korea conducted another test-fire of strategic cruise missiles ŌĆŗand anti-warship missiles on Sunday as part ŌĆīof operational efficiency trials of its destroyer Choe Hyon, state media KCNA said on Tuesday.
Leader Kim Jong Un ŌĆŗobserved the test alongside senior defence officials ŌĆŗand naval commanders, the report said.
Two strategic cruise ŌüĀmissiles and three anti-warship missiles were fired ŌĆŗto check the warship's integrated weapons command system, train ŌĆŗcrews in missile-launch procedures and verify the accuracy and anti-jamming performance of upgraded navigation systems, KCNA said.
The cruise missiles ŌĆŗflew for about 7,869 to 7,920 seconds and ŌĆŗthe anti-warship missiles for about 1,960 to 1,973 seconds over ŌĆīwaters ŌüĀoff the country's western coast, striking their targets with what the report described as ultra-precision accuracy.
KCNA said Kim was briefed the same day on ŌĆŗweapons system plans ŌĆŗfor two ŌüĀadditional destroyers under construction.
Kim said strengthening what he called the country's nuclear ŌĆŗwar deterrent remained a top priority, and ŌĆŗcalled ŌüĀfor improving strategic and tactical strike capabilities and rapid-response readiness, the report said.
Pyongyang first test-fired weapons on ŌüĀthe 5,000-ton ŌĆŗChoe Hyon-class warship in April ŌĆŗ2025, and Kim personally oversaw a missile test from the ship ŌĆŗlast March.

item.title: Bloom Energy to supply up to 2.8 GW of fuel cells under expanded Oracle deal
item.description: April 13 (Reuters) - Bloom Energy (BE.N) , opens new tab said on Monday it would supply Oracle (ORCL.N) , opens new tab with up to 2.8 ŌĆŗgigawatts of fuel cell capacity under ŌĆīan expanded deal as rising artificial intelligence use drives up power consumption.

item.title: Peak oil price likely to come 'in next few weeks,' US Energy secretary says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices are likely to ŌĆŗhit their peak "in the next few ŌĆīweeks" once ship traffic resumes through the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Department of Energy ŌĆŗSecretary Chris Wright said on ŌĆŗMonday.
Prices are expected to continue rising until "meaningful" ŌüĀship traffic resumes through the ŌĆŗstrait, Wright told the Semafor World Economy ŌĆŗForum in Washington, despite previous comments he made that oil prices would likely come down ŌĆŗsoon.
"We're going to see energy prices ŌĆŗhigh - and maybe even rising - until we get ŌĆīmeaningful ŌüĀship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz," Wright said. "That'll probably hit the peak oil price at that time. That's ŌĆŗprobably sometime ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe next few weeks."
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday ŌĆŗthat the price of oil ŌĆŗand ŌüĀgasoline may remain high until November's midterm elections, a rare admission of the ŌüĀpotential ŌĆŗpolitical fallout from his ŌĆŗdecision to attack Iran six weeks ago.

item.title: IMF, World Bank, IEA urge countries to stop hoarding energy supplies, imposing export controls
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The International Monetary Fund, World Bank and International Energy Agency on ŌĆŗMonday urged countries to avoid hoarding energy supplies and imposing export controls that could worsen what they called the biggest ŌĆīshock ever to the global energy market.
IEA chief Fatih Birol told reporters after a meeting with IMF and World Bank leaders that several countries were holding onto stocks and imposing export restrictions, and appealed to all countries to let energy stocks flow to the markets. He did not name the countries.
"Do no harm," said IMF Managing Director ŌĆŗKristalina Georgieva, noting that she was meeting with countries that were being hit hard in Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa and some South ŌĆŗPacific islands that were worried about supplies.
"The first principle should be: don't impose export restrictions that are only making ŌüĀthe disequilibrium worse," she said, adding that the war would have a more severe impact on growth and inflation if it continued for ŌĆŗa prolonged period.
The U.S. military on Monday began a blockade of ships leaving Iran's ports and Tehran threatened to retaliate against its Gulf neighbors' ports after ŌĆŗweekend talks in Islamabad on ending the war broke down. Oil prices jumped back over $100 per barrel, with no sign of a swift reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, which carries 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas.
Birol told an Atlantic Council event earlier that the conflict had triggered the worst global energy disruption ever, ŌĆŗwith more than 80 oil and gas facilities across the Middle East damaged to date. He said the situation was bad in March, when ŌĆŗsome cargoes had been loaded, but could worsen this month.
"The scale of the problem is huge, and countries will suffer under this, some more than others, but ŌĆīI can ŌüĀtell you... no country is immune," Birol said.
The leaders of the three institutions vowed to keep coordinating their responses to the conflict in the Middle East , which has sent oil prices up by 50% since it began on February 28. The shock has also driven gas and fertilizer prices higher, triggering concerns about food security and job losses.
"We recognize that when we act together, the impact of our action is higher. We are more efficient, ŌĆŗwe help the membership the most," ŌĆŗGeorgieva said.
The statement ŌüĀnoted that the situation remained very uncertain, and even after a resumption of regular shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz, it would take time for global supplies of key commodities to move back toward pre-conflict ŌĆŗlevels.
The IMF and World Bank have said they expect to downgrade their growth forecasts and lift their inflation numbers ŌĆŗas a result ŌüĀof the war. The IMF will release new forecasts on Tuesday, and the IEA is due to release a new monthly oil market report. The war has cast a deep shadow over the spring meetings of the IMF and World Bank, being held in Washington this week.
Birol said the IEA had already ŌüĀreleased some ŌĆŗ400 million barrels of oil from its reserves and was prepared to take further action ŌĆŗif additional releases were deemed necessary.
"The 400 million is only 20% of our reserves. We have still 80% in our pocket," he said. "We are assessing the situation, and if and ŌĆŗwhen we decided it is the time, we are ready to act and act immediately."

item.title: Vance says US made a lot of progress in talks with Iran
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States made a lot of progress ŌĆŗin talks ŌĆŗwith Iran, U.S. Vice President JD ŌüĀVance said in ŌĆŗan interview on ŌĆŗFox News' Special Report with Bret Baier on Monday.
Vance, ŌĆŗasked whether more ŌĆŗtalks were coming, said the ŌĆīball ŌüĀwas in Iran's court.
He added that the U.S. expects Iran ŌĆŗwill ŌĆŗmake ŌüĀprogress on opening the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, warning ŌĆŗthat ŌüĀthe negotiation would change if Tehran does ŌüĀnot.

item.title: US crude futures fall over $1 as traders weigh supply risks
item.description:
BENGALURU, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. crude futures fell more ŌĆŗthan $1 in early ŌĆŗAsian trade on Tuesday ŌüĀas traders balanced ŌĆŗheightened supply risks linked ŌĆŗto a U.S. blockade of Iranian shipping in the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz ŌĆŗwith reports that Washington and Tehran ŌĆīwere ŌüĀstill open to talks after a tense meeting in Islamabad.
U.S. ŌĆŗWest ŌĆŗTexas ŌüĀIntermediate was at $97.76 a barrel, down $1.32, ŌĆŗor 1.3% ŌĆŗby ŌüĀ2205 GMT, following a 1.5% gain in ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗprevious session.
(This story has been corrected to say U.S. West Texas Intermediate was down $1.32 a barrel, not up, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Chevron agrees to asset swap in Venezuela to focus on heavy oil projects
item.description:
CARACAS/HOUSTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab signed two key agreements on Monday to expand ŌĆīoperations at Venezuela's vast Orinoco Belt, including an asset swap adding an extra heavy crude area to its main project while returning an offshore gas field and a small crude area, executives and officials said at an event.
The agreements are among the first big expansion deals since the U.S. launched ŌĆŗa $100 billion reconstruction plan for Venezuela's energy sector after capturing President Nicolas Maduro , and a sweeping reform of ŌĆŗthe country's main oil law was approved in January, encouraging foreign investment.
The pacts, expected to allow ŌüĀthe U.S. major to boost crude output and participation at the OPEC country's main oil region, were signed ŌĆŗby company executives led by Javier La Rosa, head of Chevron's Base Assets and Emerging Countries, and officials from state-owned ŌĆŗcompany PDVSA in the presence of acting President Delcy Rodriguez.
The deals include the increase of Chevron's stake at one of its joint ventures with PDVSA in the Orinoco, Petroindependencia, to 49% from a previous 35.8%.
The company also agreed to relinquish two gas blocks that include the ŌĆŗcoveted Loran offshore field and its stake at a small oil project in western Venezuela, while receiving a new ŌĆŗoil area, Ayacucho 8, as part of its existing Petropiar project also in the Orinoco, the company's largest.
The deals give Chevron, PDVSA's main ŌĆījoint ŌüĀventure partner, a strong foot to expand heavy oil projects in the country amid expected increased competition with foreign companies.
Chevron's asset swap with PDVSA and its subsidiaries is "a mutually beneficial agreement, which will consolidate all parties' focus on strategic assets in the country," the company said in a release after the event.
Chevron executives said in January the firm could increase ŌĆŗoutput in Venezuela by about ŌĆŗ50% in the next ŌüĀtwo years within its existing footprint. The company's joint ventures with PDVSA are producing 260,000 barrels per day of crude, about a fourth of the country's total output.
The agreements will ŌĆŗallow Venezuela and Chevron "to progress to increase output and secure revenue for the benefit ŌĆŗof the people," ŌüĀRodriguez said during the broadcast event.
Another energy major progressing to rapidly expand in Venezuela, Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , is expected to sign a separate deal later this week to develop the Loran gas field, which extends into Trinidad and Tobago, as a single project ŌüĀwith its ŌĆŗManatee field, sources said.
The possible reassignment of the area would provide ŌĆŗthe fastest alternative to start gas output at the 7.3 trillion-cubic-foot field, whose gas Trinidad needs to produce and export liquefied natural gas.

item.title: Petrobras in initial talks to buy back Brazil refinery from Mubadala, sources say
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, April 13 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil firm Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab ŌĆŗis in initial direct negotiations with Abu Dhabi's ŌĆīMubadala sovereign wealth fund to repurchase the Mataripe refinery in Brazil, two sources with knowledge of the matter told Reuters on ŌĆŗMonday.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva said last ŌĆŗmonth that Petrobras would repurchase the refinery, which had ŌüĀbeen sold during the administration of his predecessor ŌĆŗJair Bolsonaro. After Lula's remarks, Petrobras had said it would ŌĆŗanalyze a potential deal.
A deal could be inked by the end of this year, the sources said. Mataripe is Brazil's second largest ŌĆŗrefinery, but is operating at only around 60% ŌĆŗof capacity, while Petrobras' plants are operating at their limit in ŌĆīorder ŌüĀto boost local production, one source said.
Petrobras' plans to increase refining capacity have gained fresh urgency after the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran caused global diesel prices to ŌĆŗsurge, impacting Brazilian ŌĆŗconsumers due ŌüĀto the country's dependency on foreign diesel. Brazil imports about a quarter of its ŌĆŗtotal diesel needs.
The rise in fuel prices ŌĆŗhas ŌüĀbecome a major concern for Lula ahead of a presidential election in October, where he will seek a fourth ŌüĀnon-consecutive ŌĆŗterm.
Petrobras did not immediately comment. Mubadala ŌĆŗdeclined to comment.

item.title: Benin's Wadagni wins presidency in landslide with over 94% of votes
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - Benin's ŌĆŗFinance Minister Romuald ŌĆīWadagni secured a landslide victory in the West ŌĆŗAfrican nation's ŌĆŗApril 12 presidential election, garnering ŌüĀover 94% of ŌĆŗvotes, provisional results ŌĆŗfrom the electoral commission showed on Monday.
Sacca Lafia, head ŌĆŗof the country's ŌĆŗindependent electoral commission, announced on ŌĆīnational ŌüĀtelevision that the result was based on more than 90% ŌĆŗof ŌĆŗvotes ŌüĀcounted, highlighting Wadagni's insurmountable lead.
Voter ŌĆŗturnout for the ŌĆŗelection ŌüĀstood at 58.78%, Lafia added.
Opposition candidate Paul ŌüĀHounkpe conceded ŌĆŗdefeat earlier ŌĆŗon Monday.

item.title: Man charged after Molotov cocktail attack on OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's home
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - A Texas man was charged with hurling a Molotov cocktail at the home of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and attempting to set fire to ŌĆŗthe AI firm's headquarters.
Daniel Moreno-Gama, 20, was captured on surveillance video ŌĆīthrowing an incendiary device outside Altman's San Francisco residence, according to a Federal Bureau of Investigation affidavit filed in federal court on Monday.
They said Moreno-Gama was carrying a ŌĆŗself-authored "anti-AI" document when the San Francisco Police Department arrested him.
According to ŌĆŗthe FBI, the document Moreno-Gama wrote - which was cited as ŌüĀevidence of motive - contained threats directed at Altman.
"We are only at the ŌĆŗbeginning of this investigation, but if the evidence shows that Mr. Moreno-Gama executed ŌĆŗthese attacks to change public policy or to coerce government and other officials, we will treat this as an act of domestic terrorism and together with our law enforcement ŌĆŗpartners prosecute him to the fullest extent allowed by law," U.S. Attorney ŌĆŗCraig Missakian said in a statement.
OpenAI faces growing scrutiny for its role in national security, ŌĆīincluding ŌüĀcriticism of a proposed deal with the U.S. government to allow its artificial intelligence technology to be used in classified military operations.
Moreno-Gama is charged with attempted damage and destruction of property by means of explosives and possession of ŌĆŗan unregistered firearm.
Police ŌĆŗsaid he traveled ŌüĀfrom Texas to California before carrying out the attacks and they recovered multiple incendiary devices, kerosene and a lighter ŌĆŗat the time of his arrest.
Moreno-Gama told security personnel ŌĆŗat the ŌüĀcompany's headquarters that he intended to burn the building and harm the people inside, according to court records.
If convicted, he faces a mandatory minimum sentence of ŌüĀfive years ŌĆŗand up to 20 years in prison ŌĆŗon the explosives charge, and up to 10 years in prison on the unregistered firearm charge, ŌĆŗcourt filings showed.

item.title: US kills two men in strike in Eastern Pacific
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. ŌĆŗmilitary said on ŌĆīMonday that it had ŌĆŗcarried out ŌĆŗa strike in the ŌüĀEastern ŌĆŗPacific that killed ŌĆŗtwo men.
The U.S. Southern Command wrote ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗX: "Intelligence confirmed the vessel ŌĆīwas ŌüĀtransiting along known narco-trafficking routes in ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗEastern ŌüĀPacific and was ŌĆŗengaged in ŌĆŗnarco-trafficking ŌüĀoperations."

item.title: The commodities supercycle is here. How might investors participate?
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - Commodities supercycles can reshape markets for years, if not decades. We appear to be in one again, as the Iran war has amplified several long-term bullish trends. The question for many investors has shifted from whether to engage with the supercycle to how.
Following the initial U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran on ŌĆŗFebruary 28, oil recorded its largest monthly gain in history. Combine that with copper and gold's substantial gains since mid-2025 ŌĆō as well as the ongoing race for energy, metals and minerals to fuel the artificial intelligence arms ŌĆīrace ŌĆō and a commodities supercycle appears to be underway.
Commodities supercycles are long, powerful waves driven by major structural shifts. Just think of the oil shocks of the 1970s and China's urbanisation boom in the early 2000s.
The question, as always, is how investors can participate.
Commodities are often treated as a single asset class, but a bull market is not necessarily a synchronous move across the entire complex. Instead, leadership tends to shift. So rotation within the asset class can be as important as its overall direction.
For example, a geopolitical shock may lift oil prices immediately, yet copper or gold may lag ŌĆŗor even fall as investors cut crowded positions or reassess growth risks.
This occurred in 2022 when energy prices surged after Russia's invasion of Ukraine , while some industrial metals retraced as recession concerns mounted.
WeŌĆÖre seeing such divergence again today.
Oil prices spiked 64% ŌĆŗin March after the Iran war began as energy supply was squeezed, functioning as a good portfolio hedge against equity market volatility. Yet gold fell by 12%, recording its worst month since ŌüĀ2008 , largely because it entered the shock inflated by months of heavy speculative buying and was one of the few liquid assets investors could sell to meet margin calls.
Given these uneven shifts ŌĆō and the massive geopolitical uncertainty ŌĆō what are some of the ŌĆŗkey points that investors should keep in mind as they consider riding the latest commodity wave?
First, investors should remember that small shifts in commodity allocations can have a massive impact on price.
Commodities are enormously important to the real economy, but as ŌĆŗinvestable markets they are small relative to stocks and bonds. Within the S&P 500, energy and materials together account for only about 6% of the index, compared with more than 30% for the "Magnificent Seven" tech giants.
Size matters because price is set at the margin. If even a modest amount of capital rotates out of broad equities or bonds and into commodities ŌĆō whether through physical exposure or listed derivatives ŌĆō the impact on prices could be outsized. A relatively small market can absorb only so much new capital before price adjusts.
Interconnectedness is another key issue: one commodity's move can reshape the ŌĆŗoutlook for others.
Many commodities are linked through potential demand substitution or as raw materials for each other. For example, high natural gas prices can lead to more oil consumption. Rising energy prices feed into fertiliser, freight and food prices. Copper and aluminium ŌĆŗcan substitute for each other in some industrial applications.
Another point to consider is inventories. While firms may prize cost-efficiency when supply is unconstrained, geopolitical shocks can spur a move towards higher inventory levels.
The Iran conflict is a case in point. TehranŌĆÖs ability to disrupt roughly one-fifth of ŌĆīthe worldŌĆÖs energy ŌüĀsupply by blocking the narrow Strait of Hormuz has underscored how vulnerable energy and other goods are to chokepoints.
Moving forward, governments and companies are likely to prioritise security of supply of many goods, producing higher inventory levels across a wide set of strategic materials. That could create a broader structural premium across the complex.
The next decision is whether to own the underlying commodities or commodities-related equities.
While physical commodities primarily respond to current and near-term supply-demand imbalances, commodity-related equities are impacted by a host of other factors, including each companyŌĆÖs hedging policy, capital allocation decisions, project pipelines, position in the commodity value chain, location and even geological issues.
Consider the energy industry. When crude prices spike, upstream energy firms ŌĆō those involved in exploration and production ŌĆō tend to benefit, while refiners and petrochemical firms ŌĆŗmay suffer from higher input costs.
Not all upstream producers benefit ŌĆŗequally, of course.
During the current conflict, U.S. shale production has ŌüĀbeen insulated from direct damage. These producers also have more flexibility to ramp up or down than most of their Middle Eastern counterparts, due to differing geological profiles and production methods.
Moreover, many U.S. oil companies were well adapted to the pre-Iran war price regime of roughly $70 per barrel, generating healthy free cash flow at those levels. At current prices of around $100, the windfall could be substantial.
Of course, ŌĆŗenergy companies ŌĆō no matter where theyŌĆÖre located ŌĆō also come with their own bespoke risks related to management, debt issues, long-term strategic concerns and other issues ŌĆō something one doesnŌĆÖt have to ŌĆŗconsider when investing in the commodity itself.
Finally, investors should not underestimate one additional feature of commodities' return profile: positive convexity. Price upside is often much larger than downside.
This is rooted in commoditiesŌĆÖ physical nature and direct links to productive activities that are necessary to keep economies humming.
Investors can delay purchases of financial assets or short sell them when prices rise.
The same logic rarely applies to physical commodities, where consumption is hard to postpone or reduce quickly. Demand destruction may eventually emerge as prices increase, but often not fast enough to prevent a sharp squeeze first.
That asymmetry is one ŌüĀreason commodity cycles, ŌĆŗonce they take hold, can prove more forceful than many investors expect. Understanding this can be the difference between being well positioned for a cycle ŌĆŗand being run over by it.
(The views expressed here are those of the author. Taosha Wang is a portfolio manager and creator of the ŌĆ£Thematically ThinkingŌĆØ newsletter at Fidelity International.)

item.title: OPEC lowers second-quarter global oil demand forecast on Iran war
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - OPEC on Monday lowered its forecast for world oil demand in the second quarter by 500,000 barrels per day, the producer group's monthly oil report showed in its first public assessment of the Iran war's impact ŌĆŗon the market.
OPEC sees a smaller hit to oil demand from the war this year ŌĆŗthan some other forecasters, such as the U.S. government's Energy Information Administration . OPEC also made ŌüĀno change to its full-year outlook as it sees consumption rebounding in later months.
The war has effectively ŌĆŗclosed the Strait of Hormuz, the worldŌĆÖs most important oil route, shutting in millions of barrels of ŌĆŗMiddle East production and sending fuel prices soaring. The price surge is pressuring consumers and businesses across the globe, and triggering government action to conserve supplies.
Global oil demand is projected to average 105.07 million bpd in the second quarter, OPEC's ŌĆŗreport said, down from the 105.57 million bpd forecast in last month's report.
"The demand growth for the ŌĆŗsecond quarter of 2026 is revised down for both the OECD and non-OECD, driven mainly by slight transitory weakness ŌĆīin oil ŌüĀdemand growth, given ongoing developments in the Middle East," OPEC said, referring to countries inside or outside the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development grouping of industrialised nations.
OPEC kept unchanged its forecast that world oil demand will rise by 1.38 million bpd in 2026, in contrast to ŌĆŗthe EIA which halved ŌĆŗits prediction in an ŌüĀApril 7 report.
OPEC+, which includes the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries plus other producers such as Russia, had agreed to resume oil production hikes as ŌĆŗof April, although the report highlighted how steeply output has fallen since the ŌĆŗIran conflict ŌüĀbegan at the end of February.
Crude oil output by OPEC+ averaged 35.06 million bpd in March, down 7.70 million bpd from February, citing secondary sources, the report showed, with Iraq and Saudi Arabia making the biggest cuts.
OPEC+ agreed ŌüĀon ŌĆŗApril 5 to raise its oil output quotas by 206,000 bpd ŌĆŗfor May, a modest rise that will largely exist on paper as long as key members are unable to raise production ŌĆŗdue to the effective Hormuz blockage.
